Conclusion: A geometry-aware, differentiable back-projection approach with attention-guided projection and bidirectional long-range dependencies provides a viable solution for low-dose, sparse-view pediatric skull CT reconstruction and facilitates the creation of a pediatric skull CT dataset for internal evaluation.

Abstract: Computed Tomography (CT) is essential for diagnosing pediatric craniofacial abnormalities, yet poses radiation risks to developing anatomies. Reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays offers a low-dose alternative but is severely ill-posed. Existing methods employ geometry-agnostic feature lifting, naively projecting 2D features into 3D without explicit spatial modeling, causing depth ambiguity and degraded osseous boundaries. We present PSCT-Net, a geometry-aware framework with differentiable back-projection. Differentiable back-projection establishes a spatially faithful volumetric prior, alleviating depth ambiguity. An Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) module then learns non-linear voxel-wise correspondences between 2D regions and 3D locations. A Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) module captures long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity. We further curate a private institutional pediatric skull CT cohort, PedSkull-CT, comprising normal and pathological cases for internal evaluation, addressing the gap in adult-centric, trunk-focused datasets.

</details>


### [27] [Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19882)
*Tongqing Shi,Ge Yan,Tuomas Oikarinen,Tsui-Wei Weng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MM-CBM extends Concept Bottleneck Models to a multimodal CLIP-like framework with dual Concept Bottleneck Layers, enabling interpretable zero-shot classification and image retrieval; shows up to 51.26% average accuracy gain across four benchmarks and maintains within ~5% of black-box performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CBMs struggle to generalize beyond a fixed set of concepts and risk leaking non-concept predictive signals. A multimodal, interpretable framework is needed to generalize to new tasks while preserving interpretability.

Method: Introduce dual Concept Bottleneck Layers to align both image and text embeddings into a shared, interpretable concept space and integrate CBMs with CLIP, enabling zero-shot classification and image retrieval with interpretable intermediates.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows up to 51.26% average accuracy improvement across four standard benchmarks; performance remains within ~5% of black-box models, indicating favorable interpretability-accuracy trade-offs.

Conclusion: MM-CBM demonstrates that concept bottlenecks can be effectively extended to multimodal models, enabling interpretable zero-shot vision tasks and retrieval with competitive accuracy.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance the interpretability of deep learning networks by aligning the features extracted from images with natural concepts. However, existing CBMs are constrained in their ability to generalize beyond a fixed set of predefined classes and the risk of non-concept information leakage, where predictive signals outside the intended concepts are inadvertently exploited. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) to address these issues and extend CBMs into CLIP. MM-CBM utilizes dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) to align both the image and text embeddings into interpretable features. This allows us to perform new vision tasks like zero-shot classification or image retrieval in an interpretable way. Compared to existing methods, MM-CBM achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks. Our method maintains high accuracy, staying within ~5% of black-box performance while offering greater interpretability.

</details>


### [28] [SurgVista: Long-Horizon Surgical World Modeling with Plausible Instrument-Tissue Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19889)
*Wentao Pan,Wuyang Li,Shengyuan Liu,Xinyu Liu,Hengyu Liu,Yixuan Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SurgVista introduces two training recipes to build a robust surgical world model that maintains spatial interaction coherence and long-horizon temporal fidelity, validated on a new SurgWorld-Bench with decoupled metrics, achieving superior visual and interaction performance as horizon grows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safety and data-efficiency challenges in learning autonomous surgical policies by improving world-model predictions of instrument-tissue interactions over long rollouts.

Method: Deformation Consistency Regularization: extract scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforce cross-frame coherence via latent contrastive learning to ensure physically plausible tissue deformation; Drift Adaptation Training: perturb conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations tuned to long-horizon drift to preserve visual fidelity.

Result: SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with larger gains at longer horizons.

Conclusion: The two linked training strategies enhance both spatial coherence of instrument-tissue dynamics and resilience of long-horizon predictions, and the SurgWorld-Bench enables robust, decoupled evaluation of instrument motion and tissue response.

Abstract: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is challenging, as expert demonstrations are expensive and in vivo exploration poses substantial safety risks. Surgical world models address this by generating realistic, action-conditioned future frames from an initial observation, but existing methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: spatial interaction incoherence, where visible instrument contact fails to induce spatially consistent tissue deformation, and temporal fidelity collapse, where prediction errors compound across autoregressive rollouts and progressively corrupt visual quality. We present SurgVista, a surgical world model that mitigates both failures through two training recipes. Deformation Consistency Regularization extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence through latent contrastive learning, strengthening physically consistent instrument-tissue dynamics. Drift Adaptation Training mitigates long-horizon drift by perturbing conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics, sustaining visual fidelity over extended rollouts. To enable rigorous evaluation, we further introduce SurgWorld-Bench, featuring diverse procedure types, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument-motion accuracy and tissue-response fidelity. Extensive experiments show that SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with gains widening as the prediction horizon grows.

</details>


### [29] [Linear Recurrent Unit with Semantic Modulation for Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19901)
*Mingyu Choi,Woo Kyoung Han,Sunghoon Im,Kyong Hwan Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces an LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) to adapt stable linear recurrence for 2D image restoration, achieving state-of-the-art performance with comparable computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LRU has strong stable linear recurrence for long-range dependencies but suffers from static parameterization and a single-scan limitation, hindering 2D vision tasks like single-image super-resolution (SR). A network that dynamically modulates LRU and leverages 2D spatial information could improve SR efficiency and accuracy.

Method: Propose a LRU-based restoration network augmented by a semantic modulating unit (SMU). The SMU performs LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement via learned prototypes, enabling dynamic, spatially-aware use of LRU for SR.

Result: Extensive experiments show quantitative and qualitative improvements over recent state-of-the-art methods, with superior performance while maintaining computational complexity on par with existing approaches.

Conclusion: The LRU-based restoration network with SMU achieves a favorable balance between performance and efficiency for single-image SR and demonstrates the potential of SMU-driven LRU modulation for 2D vision tasks; code and models are publicly available.

Abstract: Linear recurrent unit (LRU), designed with a principled formulation for stable linear recurrence, has demonstrated promising accuracy and robustness on long-range dependency tasks. However, its static parameterization and single-scan method limits its applicability to 2D vision tasks. In this study, we propose a LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) to achieve a harmonious balance between performance and efficiency in single-image super-resolution. The SMU plays three key roles: LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement through learned prototype. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our approach achieves superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing methods. The source code and models are available at https://github.com/MingyuChoi-run/LSM

</details>


### [30] [Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder for Endoscopic Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19908)
*Ivan De Boi,Xinxing Shi,Xiaoyu Jiang,Tim J. M. Jaspers,Francisco Caetano,Mauricio A. Alvarez,Fons van der Sommen,Sam Van der Jeught*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GPVAE: a Gaussian Process prior-guided VAE for endoscopic video restoration that exploits temporal coherence, uses EndoVAE + GastroNet-5M encoders, and two scalable GP approximations (HPA, SPA) with DUCKNet masking; achieves notable RMSE improvements and provides per-frame uncertainty on a colonoscopy dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Endoscopic videos suffer transient corruptions (specular reflections, motion artifacts, missing frames) that hinder restoration and downstream tasks; a temporally-aware, uncertainty-quantified approach is needed.

Method: Replace factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior in a VAE framework (GPVAE). Incorporates endoscopy-specific encoders (EndoVAE CNN backbone and GastroNet-5M ViT encoders). Employs two GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Uses DUCKNet-based masking to exclude corrupted pixels from the reconstruction objective. Evaluated on C3VDv2 colonoscopy data; downstream trajectory errors assessed with visual odometry and PoseNet.

Result: Best GPVAE variants reduce image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9% on average (up to 26.1% vs matched VAE baselines). Downstream trajectory RMSE reduced by 12.7% on average. Training time per epoch increases by 27.3% on average. GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty that reflects temporal support and offers a confidence signal for restorations.

Conclusion: Temporal GP priors within a VAE framework, combined with endoscopy-tailored encoders and masking, enable uncertainty-aware restoration of degraded endoscopic video and improved downstream performance, with manageable training overhead.

Abstract: Endoscopic video analysis is essential for gastrointestinal diagnosis and computer-assisted interventions, but video sequences are routinely degraded by specular reflections, motion artifacts, and missing frames. These transient corruptions can distract clinicians, reduce image interpretability, and disrupt downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and navigation. Effective restoration therefore requires methods that exploit temporal continuity rather than treating frames in isolation. We introduce a Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder (GPVAE) framework for endoscopic video restoration that replaces the standard factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior, enabling interpolation of missing frames with uncertainty-aware reconstruction.
  The framework combines endoscopy-specific encoders, including a convolutional EndoVAE backbone and pretrained Vision Transformer encoders from GastroNet-5M, with two scalable GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Specular reflections are handled using a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline that excludes corrupted pixels from the reconstruction objective. On the C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset, the best GPVAE variants reduced image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9\% on average, and by up to 26.1\%, relative to matched VAE baselines. Downstream trajectory RMSE was reduced by 12.7\% on average across classical visual odometry and a pretrained PoseNet, at an average increase of 27.3\% in training time per epoch. Finally, the GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty estimates that reflect temporal support and offer a confidence signal for restored frames.

</details>


### [31] [SpatialSV: Internalizing Interpretable 3D Spatial Awareness in MLLMs via Task-Oriented Visual Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19915)
*Jiayu Tang,Yuchen Zhou,Chao Gou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpatialSV internalizes 3D spatial awareness in MLLMs via 2D-to-3D lifting (depth maps, camera poses, point clouds) with task-oriented supervision, yielding interpretable 3D reconstructions and strong semi-supervised generalization, reducing reliance on external tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLM spatial understanding relies on external tools (high inference cost) or opaque latent feature distillation lacking fine-grained geometry; need intrinsic, interpretable 3D reasoning.

Method: Use task-driven visual supervision to actively lift 2D visual features into explicit 3D representations (depth, pose, point clouds); 3D reconstructions act as interpretable proxies for spatial knowledge; accommodates semi-supervised learning using unlabeled data.

Result: Experiments across models and benchmarks show improved spatial intelligence and interpretability; the approach generalizes well in semi-supervised settings.

Conclusion: SpatialSV delivers internal, interpretable 3D spatial reasoning within MLLMs, reduces dependence on external tools, and offers scalable, diagnosable representations via 3D reconstructions.

Abstract: Unlocking the spatial intelligence of multimodal large language model (MLLMs) is crucial for understanding and interacting with the 3D world. Prevailing approaches typically inject spatial priors via external tools, which impose significant inference overhead, or rely on latent feature distillation, which remains uninterpretable and lacks fine-grained geometric constraints. To address these issues, we propose SpatialSV, a framework designed to internalize robust 3D spatial awareness within MLLMs while simultaneously offering inherent interpretability. Deviating from passive feature imitation, SpatialSV employs task-oriented visual supervision, compelling the model to actively lift its 2D visual features into explicit 3D representations, including depth maps, camera poses, and point clouds. Crucially, this 2D-to-3D lifting process provides a transparent window into the model's representations: the resulting 3D reconstructions serve as an intuitive proxy for visualizing and diagnosing the quality of the model's intrinsic spatial knowledge. Extensive experiments across multiple models and benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of SpatialSV in enhancing and interpreting MLLMs' spatial intelligence. Furthermore, the framework exhibits strong generalization in semi-supervised settings, validating its potential to leverage unlabeled visual data for scalable, interpretable spatial representation learning.

</details>


### [32] [CARE: Competence-Aware Reward Shaping for Adaptive Reasoning Length in Video-MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19927)
*Chengwen Liu,Hao Peng,Jisheng Dang,Hong Peng,Bin Hu,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CARE is a competence-aware reward shaping framework for adaptive reasoning length in multimodal video reasoning. It uses an exponential moving average of pass rates to stage training from exploration-focused long-form reasoning to concise reasoning, normalizes reasoning effort with batch statistics to separate verbosity from difficulty, and employs a posterior amplifier to boost rewards for unexpectedly strong performance on hard samples. Integrated into the GRPO pipeline, CARE improves accuracy and token efficiency without extra inference cost, exhibiting an inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length and more informative concise traces at convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning-based multimodal video reasoning typically uses fixed, simplistic reasoning-length strategies that misalign with the model’s evolving competence, leading to under-exploration early and inefficient decoding later. A dynamic, competence-aware control of reasoning length is needed to balance exploration and efficiency while respecting task difficulty.

Method: CARE maintains a smoothed competence estimate via an exponential moving average of pass rates and uses it to route training into progressive stages that shift reward preference from long-form exploration to concise efficiency. It normalizes reasoning effort using batch-level statistics to decouple verbosity from intrinsic task complexity and introduces a posterior amplifier to strengthen signals on unexpectedly strong performance on historically hard samples. The approach is integrated into the GRPO training pipeline with no additional inference-time overhead.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks shows that CARE consistently improves reasoning accuracy, stabilizes reinforcement learning, and increases token efficiency. It also yields an inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length during training and shorter, more informative reasoning traces at convergence, indicating effective adaptive budgeting of reasoning resources.

Conclusion: CARE provides a principled, adaptive mechanism to allocate reasoning budget in multimodal RL-based video reasoning, improving performance and efficiency while maintaining training stability. The work is supported by open-source code enabling reproducibility.

Abstract: In multimodal video reasoning, reinforcement learning-based methods typically rely on simplistic and inflexible reasoning-length control strategies that fail to adapt to the model's evolving competence. This mismatch may suppress necessary exploration at early stages, while encouraging redundant reasoning and inefficient decoding once the model becomes more competent. In this paper, we propose CARE, a competence-aware reward shaping framework for adaptive reasoning length optimization in multimodal reasoning. Specifically, CARE maintains a smoothed competence estimate via an exponential moving average of pass rates, and uses it to route training into progressive stages that shift the reward preference from exploration-oriented long-form reasoning to efficiency-oriented concise reasoning. To avoid conflating verbosity with intrinsic task complexity, CARE further normalizes reasoning effort with batch-level statistics, and introduces a posterior amplifier to strengthen reward signals for unexpectedly strong performance on historically difficult samples. The proposed mechanism is seamlessly integrated into the GRPO training pipeline and incurs no additional inference-time overhead. Extensive experiments on multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that CARE consistently improves reasoning accuracy, stabilizes reinforcement learning, and significantly enhances token efficiency. Moreover, CARE exhibits a characteristic inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length during training, and yields shorter yet more informative reasoning traces at convergence, indicating effective adaptive allocation of reasoning budget. We provide the source code for our proposed CARE framework and experiments at https://github.com/1Pansy/Video-CARE.

</details>


### [33] [Spatial-Aware Reduction Framework: Towards Efficient and Faithful Visual State Space Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19932)
*Jindi Lv,Aoyu Li,Yuhao Zhou,Zheng Zhu,Xiaofeng Wang,Qing Ye,Yueqi Duan,Wentao Feng,Jiancheng Lv*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STORM is a plug-and-play, training-free token-reduction module that makes pruning spatially aware to preserve 2D structure in Mamba vision backbones, delivering state-of-the-art accuracy gains while maintaining performance on PlainMamba and VMamba.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing token-reduction methods are spatially agnostic and break the 2D structural premise required by Mamba's selective scanning, causing large performance drops when pruning. A structure-preserving reduction could maintain grid topology and neighborhood coherence during compression.

Method: Reformulates token reduction as a structured operation on spatial units. Enforces localized constraints to preserve grid topology and neighborhood coherence, preserving spatial structure during compression. Plug-and-play, requires no training, and can be appended to existing reduction pipelines.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy across diverse vision Mamba backbones in training-free settings. On VMamba, STORM yields up to 63.3% relative improvement in top-1 accuracy over prior methods. On PlainMamba, only ~1.0% accuracy drop, achieving performance comparable to ViT.

Conclusion: Spatially aware, structure-preserving reduction is an effective, training-free enhancement for pruning vision backbones like Mamba, with particularly strong gains on VMamba and minimal cost on PlainMamba.

Abstract: Mamba demonstrates strong efficiency in modeling long visual sequences. However, when token reduction is applied to structurally enhanced Mamba variants, these models exhibit a severe performance collapse. We attribute this degradation to the spatially agnostic nature of existing reduction methods, which violate the two-dimensional structural premise required by the selective scanning mechanism. In this work, we propose STORM, a spatial-aware token reduction framework designed to maintain structural integrity throughout the compression process. STORM reformulates reduction into a structured operation on spatial units, enforcing localized constraints to maintain both grid topology and neighborhood coherence. As a plug-and-play module, STORM equips existing reduction pipelines with explicit spatial awareness without any training. Empirical results demonstrate that STORM achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy across diverse vision Mamba backbones under training-free settings. Notably, STORM delivers a substantial accuracy recovery on VMamba, outperforming prior methods by up to 63.3\% in top-1 accuracy. Meanwhile, STORM incurs only a 1.0\% accuracy drop on PlainMamba, achieving performance comparable to ViT.

</details>


### [34] [Speeding up the annotation process in semantic segmentation industrial applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19934)
*Marta Fernandez-Moreno,Margarita Guerrero,Rosalia Rementeria,Pablo Mesejo,Raul Moreno*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unsupervised pre-annotation significantly accelerates pixel-wise semantic segmentation labeling in industrial materials science, reducing labeling time from 170 hours to 37 hours (~78%). The study contributes the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset to date under MIT license, compares labeling times with and without pre-annotation, and provides a trained DL model as an industrial benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotation bottlenecks in large, high-resolution semantic segmentation datasets—especially in industrial materials science—drive up time, cost, and human error. This work uniquely quantifies how unsupervised algorithms can accelerate labeling and delivers public data and a baseline model to advance the field.

Method: Apply unsupervised computer vision algorithms to pre-segment high-resolution steel microstructure images, enabling pre-annotation before expert labeling. Compare labeling times from scratch versus using pre-annotation. Assemble the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset (high-res images 1280x959 and 960x703) under MIT License with a permanent DOI. Train and validate a DL model on this dataset, and deploy a baseline in an industrial setting.

Result: Labeling time reduced from 170 hours (from scratch) to 37 hours when using unsupervised pre-annotation, a ~78% improvement. Created and shared the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset to date under MIT license with DOI. Provided a trained DL model validated by field experts and deployed industrially as an initial benchmark.

Conclusion: Unsupervised pre-annotation can substantially speed up pixel-level labeling for semantic segmentation in materials science, enabling large high-resolution datasets and practical industrial deployment. This work is the first to quantify labeling-time acceleration from unsupervised pre-annotation and establishes a public dataset and baseline model for the community.

Abstract: Current machine learning models commonly require large and well-annotated datasets. However, the annotation process often becomes a bottleneck, with increased complexity leading to higher chances of human errors. Within this context, our goal in this paper is to leverage unsupervised algorithms to improve data annotation efficiency for complex semantic segmentation problems in industrial materials science. Previous research has quantified labeling time and others explored unsupervised methods. However, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to quantify how much unsupervised algorithms accelerate the labeling process. We aim to validate the extent to which this laborious process can be accelerated, focusing on semantic segmentation tasks that involve annotating each pixel of high-resolution images, such as the microstructure characterization challenge in materials science. Specifically, we demonstrate that by using unsupervised computer vision algorithms, the time required for the labeling process can be reduced from 170 hours to 37 hours, achieving an approximate reduction of 78\%. The dataset we work with includes large images of dimensions 1280x959 and 960x703, which further increases the complexity of the annotation task. Despite these challenges, we create and share the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset to date, available under MIT License with permanent DOI, contributing a fully annotated, high-resolution dataset to the field. Additionally, this is the first work to compare the labeling time from scratch (a common approach in previous studies) to the labeling time when using these unsupervised algorithms as a pre-annotation step. Furthermore, we provide a Deep Learning model trained on this dataset, validated by field experts, and deployed in an industrial setting, serving as an initial benchmark for this public dataset.

</details>


### [35] [Triangular Consistency as a Universal Constraint for Learning Optical Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19938)
*Yi Xiao,Carlos Rodriguez Coronel,Jing Zhan,Haniyeh Ehsani Oskouie,Alex Wong,Dong Lao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A universal, geometry-based triangular consistency constraint for optical flow that improves training across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer settings, agnostic to architecture and data, applicable to image pairs and multi-frame sequences with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a principled geometric regularization for optical flow without extra annotations, leveraging the natural composition of flows to enforce self-consistency across image pairs, frames, and synthetic augmentations. Aims to be architecture- and dataset-agnostic and easy to integrate.

Method: Introduce triangular consistency by enforcing that composing two estimated flows yields a third flow that remains consistent with the originals. This yields three-component flows in different scenarios: (i) image pairs via cycle consistency; (ii) multiple frames via temporal chaining to extend motion; (iii) image pairs with synthetic transforms to enable data augmentation. The constraint is computationally light and annotation-free, acting as a universal plug-and-play regularizer for training optical flow models.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows consistent improvements across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings, with the constraint behaving as a universal, low-overhead regularizer that is agnostic to model architecture and datasets.

Conclusion: Triangular consistency offers a principled, universal constraint for optical flow training that can be readily integrated as a plug‑in component, providing performance gains across settings while adding negligible overhead.

Abstract: We propose triangular consistency as a first-principled constraint for optical flow, which is agnostic to network architecture, supervision type, and dataset, and applies to both image-pair and multi-frame settings. This simple but powerful constraint is to compose two flows to induce a third flow and enforce consistency among the three. The composed flows may arise from (i) image pairs, yielding cycle consistency; (ii) multiple video frames, producing longer-range motion through temporal chaining; or (iii) image pairs combined with controlled synthetic transformations, which becomes data augmentation. This triangular consistency introduces negligible computational overhead and requires no additional annotations. Since it is derived directly from the geometry of optical flow, it does not rely on model-specific assumptions and serves as a ``universal'' plug-and-play component for optical flow training. Experiments show consistent improvement across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings.

</details>


### [36] [DiffMath: Symbol- and Graph-Aware Latent Diffusion Transformer for Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19939)
*Wei Pan,Xuhan Zheng,Yilin Shi,Huiguo He,Hiuyi Cheng,Dezhi Peng,Minghui Liao,Lianwen Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiffMath is a diffusion-based framework for handwritten mathematical expression generation that eliminates explicit positional supervision by leveraging a RelAST triplet representation, a structure-preserving latent space via MathVAE, and a conditional denoising process in structured latent space (MathDiT) with symbol-count guidance, achieving structurally coherent expressions and benefiting OCR through synthetic data augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HMEG faces complex 2D layouts and long-range dependencies, with traditional methods relying on costly symbol-level bounding boxes. There is a need for scalable, structure-aware generation that minimizes annotation requirements while preserving spatial structure.

Method: 1) RelAST: a generation-oriented Relational Abstract Syntax Tree that encodes symbols, spatial relations, and nesting depth as [S, R, D] triplets derived from MathML trees. 2) MathVAE: learns a structure-preserving latent space using symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization to capture both character semantics and spatial topology. 3) MathDiT: conditional denoising in the structured latent space, guided by a global symbol-count prior via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) to enforce structural coherence.

Result: Experiments show DiffMath produces structurally consistent handwritten expressions, outperforms existing methods in accuracy, and improves downstream OCR performance when used to augment synthetic data.

Conclusion: DiffMath demonstrates that combining a structure-aware latent diffusion framework with an explicit relational representation and symbol-count conditioning can effectively generate high-quality, structurally coherent handwritten mathematical expressions without explicit positional supervision, and benefits OCR through data augmentation.

Abstract: Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation (HMEG) is challenging due to the complex two-dimensional layouts and long-range structural dependencies of mathematical expressions. Existing methods typically rely on explicit spatial supervision, such as symbol-level bounding boxes, which incurs high annotation costs and limits scalability. In this work, we propose DiffMath, a symbol- and graph-aware latent diffusion framework that leverages the hierarchical structure inherent in LaTeX as a structural prior, eliminating the need for positional supervision. First, we design a Relational Abstract Syntax Tree (RelAST), a generation-oriented representation that distills MathML trees into compact triplet sequences [S, R, D], where each token directly encodes a symbol identity, spatial relation, or nesting depth. Second, we introduce MathVAE, which learns structure-preserving latent representations through symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization, ensuring that the latent space captures both character semantics and spatial topology. Third, MathDiT performs conditional denoising in this structured latent space, further guided by a global symbol-count prior via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) to improve structural coherence. Experiments show that DiffMath produces structurally consistent handwritten expressions, achieves superior performance over existing methods, and improves the accuracy of downstream OCR models through synthetic data augmentation.

</details>


### [37] [Timage: A Generative Text-in-Image Paradigm for Fine-Tuning Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19944)
*Yifeng Wu,Huimin Huang,Ruiluo Wu,Chunyi Lin,Guanhua Chen,Xian Wu,Wang Song,Ruize Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Timage reframes multimodal reasoning as an input-level alignment problem by rendering a query as a typeset overlay on the image. The overlay is generated by a constrained Schrödinger Bridge with two stages (Region Search and Appearance Shaping) to target query-aligned regions while maintaining legible, balanced text. This approach yields strong performance on VMCBench with a small 7B backbone, surpassing larger systems and tuned baselines, highlighting input reconstruction as an effective, architecture-neutral lever for multimodal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs struggle with fine-grained spatial reasoning because text lacks explicit geometric anchors to pixels. Prior fixes either modify model weights or rely on verbose prompts, which can erode general competence and fail to pin language to visual coordinates.

Method: Timage uses a constrained Schrödinger Bridge (cSB) to generate a query-aligned overlay directly on the image in two coupled stochastic stages. First, Region Search transports noise toward image regions aligned with the query while respecting a hard occlusion barrier to protect salient foreground content. Second, Appearance Shaping adjusts glyph sizes under an ink-budget regularizer to ensure legibility and visual balance. The overlay acts as an explicit attention beacon guiding the model’s spatial focus.

Result: On VMCBench, Timage with a modest 7B backbone clearly surpasses far larger proprietary systems and parameter-tuned baselines, demonstrating strong gains in fine-grained spatial reasoning while preserving backbone competence.

Conclusion: Deliberate input reconstruction serves as a powerful, architecture-neutral lever to strengthen multimodal reasoning; aligning language to image coordinates via an input overlay can mitigate misalignment without compromising general capabilities.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often lose track of the right image regions during fine-grained spatial reasoning, because a textual query rarely carries any explicit geometric anchor into the pixel domain. Prevailing remedies either rewire the model's weights or pad the prompt with verbose instructions, yet neither reliably pins the language to the correct visual coordinates without eroding the backbone's general competence. We introduce Timage, a paradigm that recasts multimodal understanding as an alignment problem solved at the input: the query is drawn, as a typeset overlay, onto the image itself. The placement and appearance of this overlay are produced by a Constrained Schrödinger Bridge (cSB), an entropic optimal-transport sampler that factorizes layout synthesis into two coupled stochastic stages. The first stage, Region Search, transports noise toward query-aligned image zones while obeying a hard occlusion barrier that protects salient foreground content; the second stage, Appearance Shaping, sizes the glyphs through an ``ink-budget'' regularizer so that the rendered text stays legible and visually balanced. The resulting overlay behaves as an explicit attention beacon that channels the model's focus along spatial semantics. On the VMCBench suite, Timage paired with a modest 7B backbone clearly overtakes far larger proprietary systems as well as parameter-tuned baselines. The study positions deliberate input reconstruction as a powerful, architecture-neutral lever for strengthening multimodal reasoning.

</details>


### [38] [Confidence Calibration for Multimodal LLMs: An Empirical Study through Medical VQA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19950)
*Yuetian Du,Yucheng Wang,Ming Kong,Tian Liang,Qiang Long,Bingdi Chen,Qiang Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes MS-FBI with auxiliary expert LLM to calibrate confidence in Medical VQA; reduces ECE by ~40% across three datasets, improving reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs in medicine show confidence misalignment with accuracy, risking misdiagnosis; need domain-specific confidence calibration.

Method: Introduce Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation (MS-FBI) combined with an auxiliary expert LLM to assess and calibrate model confidence in medical VQA.

Result: Achieves a ~40% average reduction in Expected Calibration Error (ECE) across three Medical VQA datasets; improves reliability of medical MLLMs.

Conclusion: Highlights the need for domain-specific confidence calibration in healthcare AI to provide trustworthy AI-assisted diagnosis.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show great potential in medical tasks, but their elicited confidence often misaligns with actual accuracy, potentially leading to misdiagnosis or overlooking correct advice. This study presents the first comprehensive analysis of the relationship between accuracy and confidence in medical MLLMs. It proposes a novel method that combines Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation (MS-FBI) with auxiliary expert LLM assessment, aiming to improve confidence calibration in Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA). Experiments demonstrate that our method reduces the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by an average of 40\% across three Medical VQA datasets, significantly enhancing MLLMs' reliability. The findings highlight the importance of domain-specific calibration for MLLMs in healthcare, offering a more trustworthy solution for AI-assisted diagnosis.

</details>


### [39] [SketchKeyAnime: Reference-anchored Sparse Key-Sketch Animation Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19958)
*Meixi Li,Xianlin Zhang,Yue Zhang,Xueming Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based framework (SketchKeyAnime) enables controllable, appearance-consistent video animation from sparse key sketches and a single reference image, using dual-branch conditioning, Sketch Cross Attention with gating, and Adaptive Weighted Loss; it achieves strong temporal coherence and sketch fidelity, outperforming baselines on Sakuga-42M (Aesthetic) with notable EDMD and FVD gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap where existing animation methods require dense frame-level conditions (full sketches or boundary frames) and are not cost-effective for low-input setups; enable low-cost, sparse-input animation with reliable structure and appearance control.

Method: Dual-branch conditioning to encode local geometric constraints and semantic-temporal context; Sketch Cross Attention to fuse reference image and sketch conditions with learnable gating; Adaptive Weighted Loss to emphasize supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions; diffusion-based video generation with sparse inputs; evaluation on the Aesthetic subset of Sakuga-42M.

Result: Outperforms representative animation interpolation and sketch-guided generation baselines. On the Sakuga-42M Aesthetic subset, EDMD is reduced by 31.9% and FVD by 9.5% compared to the best baseline, indicating improved sketch fidelity and temporal coherence; achieves the best overall performance across most metrics.

Conclusion: The framework validates that sparse-key-sketch inputs can yield high-quality, controllable animation with good temporal consistency and sketch fidelity, highlighting potential for low-cost animation production.

Abstract: Traditional animation production relies heavily on manual drawing and iterative refinement, particularly for key-pose design, in-betweening, and character coloring. While existing animation and video generation methods have made notable progress, they typically depend on RGB boundary frames, dense frame-wise conditions, or complete sketch sequences, limiting their applicability under low-cost input conditions. We present SketchKeyAnime, a video diffusion framework for generating structurally controllable, appearance-consistent, and temporally coherent animations from sparse key-sketch inputs. Given a single reference RGB image and a few temporally indexed key sketches, SketchKeyAnime introduces a dual-branch conditioning mechanism to encode local geometric constraints alongside semantic-temporal context. It leverages Sketch Cross Attention to fuse reference image and sketch conditions with learnable gating, and incorporates an Adaptive Weighted Loss to strengthen supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions. Experimental results on the Aesthetic subset of Sakuga-42M show that our approach consistently outperforms representative animation interpolation and sketch-guided generation baselines. Compared to the best-performing baseline, SketchKeyAnime reduces EDMD by 31.9\% and FVD by 9.5\%, demonstrating superior sketch fidelity and temporal coherence, while achieving the best overall performance across most quantitative metrics. These results validate the proposed framework and highlight its potential for low-cost, highly controllable animation creation.

</details>


### [40] [Addressing Detail Bottlenecks in Latent Diffusion for RGB-to-SWIR Image Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19961)
*Kaili Wang,Martin Dimitrievski,Jose Maria Salvador,Ben Stoffelen,David Van Hamme,Lore Goetschalckx*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two lightweight, backbone-agnostic fixes (SCAE and LGE) improve LDM-based image-to-image translation by preserving spatial details and refining conditioning, yielding strong gains in detection and SOTA FID on RGB-to-SWIR tasks with zero-shot generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent diffusion models compress inputs via autoencoders that discard spatial information, and their conditioning path downscales signals, hurting downstream perception tasks. Need simple, general fixes that retain spatial fidelity and provide better conditioning.

Method: Introduce Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) with skip connections injecting high-res source features into the decoder; introduce Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) to replace naive downsampling with a learnable conditioning pathway. Evaluate across two denoisers (U-Net, DiT) on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes.

Result: Detection mAP improved up to 2x vs latent diffusion baseline; up to 3.4x improvement on small objects (COCO-small); achieved state-of-the-art FID; FID and detection are poorly correlated; results generalize zero-shot to RASMD; plan to release data, checkpoints, and code.

Conclusion: The proposed SCAE and LGE provide backbone-agnostic improvements to latent diffusion-based image-to-image translation, enhancing downstream perception and evaluation robustness, with strong zero-shot generalization and public release.

Abstract: Latent diffusion models (LDMs) enable efficient image-to-image translation but discard fine spatial details during compression, degrading downstream perception tasks. We identify two bottlenecks: the autoencoder, which loses spatial information, and the conditioning pathway, which further degrades the source signal through naive downsampling. We propose two lightweight, backbone-agnostic fixes: a Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) that injects high-resolution source features into the decoder via skip connections, and a Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) that replaces naive downsampling with a learned conditioning signal. Evaluated on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes with two denoiser backbones (U-Net and DiT), our approach improves detection mAP by up to 2x over the latent diffusion baseline, with up to 3.4x gains on small objects (COCO-small, <32^2 px^2), while achieving state-of-the-art FID. We further show that FID and detection performance are poorly correlated, motivating multi-axis evaluation. Results generalise zero-shot to the public RASMD benchmark. We will publicly release test data with annotations, all checkpoints, and training code.

</details>


### [41] [ROSE: Benchmarking the Perception-to-Action Gap in Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19965)
*Yihao Wang,Zijian He,Jie Ren,Keze Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ROSE provides a controlled benchmark to test how multimodal LLMs turn the same visual evidence into different actions under contextual constraints; results show large drops in performance when moving from counting to region-conditioned actions across nine models, revealing a bottleneck beyond basic visual grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs often must translate shared visual evidence into task-specific actions, but little is known about reliability under changing context. ROSE isolates the effect of region constraints and symbolic outputs on action execution while keeping visual input fixed.

Method: ROSE constructs a fixed visual scene and varies region constraints and required symbolic outputs. It uses coupled counting and coordinate-action tasks to assess whether models infer a majority reference and apply fine-grained evidence to context-specific actions. The evaluation spans nine recent MLLMs and includes analyses with paired scenes/regions, global-click, and local controls to separate grounding from other bottlenecks.

Result: Across nine MLLMs, performance drops up to 44.5 percentage points when shifting from counting-oriented tasks to region-conditioned actions, with human performance at 98.8%. The gap persists even when the count is correct on paired scenes/regions. Global-click and matched local controls indicate that coordinate grounding explains only part of the loss, revealing a distinct, model-dependent bottleneck in turning shared visual evidence into context-specific actions.

Conclusion: ROSE reveals that reliable action in context is not guaranteed by correct counting or basic grounding; models exhibit a bottleneck in mapping contextually constrained evidence to the appropriate actions. The benchmark exposes a specific failure mode in current MLLMs and suggests a need for mechanisms to infer and apply implicit majority references to actionable outputs.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly expected to act on visual information, yet the same scene may require different actions under different task contexts. How reliably can a model turn the same visual evidence into the action required by the current context? To answer this question, we introduce \textsc{ROSE} (\textbf{R}eference-conditioned \textbf{O}ddity and \textbf{S}ymbolic \textbf{E}xecution), a controlled benchmark that holds the visual scene fixed while varying region constraints and required symbolic outputs. Through coupled counting and coordinate-action tasks, \textsc{ROSE} tests whether models can infer an implicit majority reference and act on the resulting fine-grained visual evidence under changing contexts. Across nine recent MLLMs, performance drops by as much as 44.5 percentage points from counting-oriented tasks to region-conditioned action, despite 98.8\% human performance. The gap persists on paired scenes and regions for which the same model returns the correct count, while global-click and matched local controls show that coordinate grounding explains only part of the loss, revealing a distinct, model-dependent bottleneck in turning shared visual evidence into context-specific actions.

</details>


### [42] [Semantic-Anchored Evidential Fusion for Domain-Robust Whole-Slide Survival Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19966)
*Yucheng Xing,Ling Huang,Pei Liu,Jingying Ma,Jiaqing Xu,Kai He,Mengling Feng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAEFS introduces semantic anchors from VQA for WSI-based survival prediction, achieving strong zero-shot cross-center generalization and robustness, with a 10.2% C-index gain and lower cross-center divergence for semantic vs pixel features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shift in WSIs due to staining and scanner differences degrades pixel-based models; high-level pathology semantics offer a domain-invariant representation aligned with human diagnostic reasoning.

Method: Semantic-Anchored Evidential Fusion Survival (SAEFS): derive semantic anchors from WSIs via Visual Question Answering; dual-stream WSI evidence extraction; Dirichlet-based Subjective Logic for uncertainty; cautious conjunction fusion to combine semantic and visual evidence; trained on one domain, evaluated zero-shot on four unseen domains.

Result: Average C-index improves by 10.2% over state-of-the-art; semantic features show lower cross-center divergence than pixel features, indicating better cross-center robustness; improvements in both prediction accuracy and reliability.

Conclusion: Semantic anchors extracted through VQA provide a domain-invariant semantic representation that aligns with clinical reasoning; the evidential fusion strategy mitigates overconfident fusion from correlated sources and supports robust cross-center prognosis.

Abstract: Whole-slide images (WSIs) are widely used for computational cancer prognosis. However, most existing methods primarily focus on in-domain performance and fail to generalize across clinical centers. This limitation stems from their reliance on pixel-derived representations that are highly susceptible to domain-specific artifacts caused by staining protocols and scanner hardware. We hypothesize that high-level pathology semantics, such as tumor grade and micro-environmental architecture, provide a domain-invariant semantic representation that mirrors the robust diagnostic logic of human pathologists. Therefore, we propose a Semantic-Anchored Evidential Fusion Survival (SAEFS) framework, where SAEFS derives semantic anchors from WSIs via Visual Question Answering (VQA), employs a dual-stream WSI evidence extraction architecture, uses Dirichlet-based Subjective Logic to model uncertainty, and fuses semantic and visual evidence through a cautious conjunction rule to avoid overconfident fusion from correlated sources. Trained exclusively on one source domain and evaluated zero-shot across four unseen domains, SAEFS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models both in prediction accuracy and reliability, improving the average C-index by 10.2%. Quantitative analyses further show that VQA-derived semantic features exhibit significantly lower cross-center divergence than pixel-derived features, highlighting their robustness for cross-center clinical applications.

</details>


### [43] [CrossFlow: One-Step Generation Across Latent and Pixel Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19970)
*Xiyuan Wang,Xiao Zhang,Yang Li,Ruoxi Jiang,Zhao Zhong,Liefeng Bo,Muhan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CrossFlow introduces cross-space flow by mapping noisy latent inputs directly to pixel-space images, using a velocity-free one-step objective that trains in latent space but supervises in image space. It removes the need for a separate decoder in latent diffusion pipelines and achieves competitive ImageNet-1k 256x256 results (FID 1.62 with one function evaluation).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent-diffusion pipelines suffer from a mismatch: training targets are latent representations while final image quality depends on how a decoder handles latent inputs. A cross-space approach aims to align latent-space training with pixel-space supervision, enabling end-to-end efficiency without a separate decoder at inference.

Method: Propose CrossFlow, a cross-space flow model that maps noisy latent inputs directly to pixel-space images. Introduces a velocity-free one-step objective: the latent trajectory defines the training path, but supervision targets an image rather than a latent displacement. Trains with latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual and adversarial losses to improve fidelity; yields a one-step latent-to-pixel generator that can replace the decoder in latent diffusion pipelines.

Result: Empirical results on class-conditional ImageNet-1k at 256×256: CrossFlow-XL achieves 1.62 FID with a single function evaluation. Ablations show the latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual/adversarial losses are crucial for fidelity.

Conclusion: Cross-space flow objectives can combine latent-space efficiency with direct pixel-space supervision, eliminating the need for a separate decoder at inference and enabling a unified latent-to-pixel generation framework.

Abstract: Most diffusion and flow-matching generators define the prior, probability path, and prediction target in the same representation space. Latent diffusion improves efficiency by moving this path into an autoencoder latent space, but the final sample is still produced by a separately trained decoder. This separation creates a mismatch: the generator is optimized for latent-space prediction, while final quality depends on how the decoder handles generated latents that may differ from clean encoder outputs. We introduce CrossFlow, a cross-space flow formulation that maps noisy latent inputs directly to pixel-space images. The key technical step is a velocity-free one-step objective: the latent trajectory defines the training path, but the supervised prediction is an image rather than a latent displacement. This lets one model act both as a one-step latent-to-pixel generator and as a decoder replacement for latent diffusion pipelines. On class-conditional ImageNet-1k at $256\times256$, CrossFlow-XL achieves 1.62 FID with one function evaluation. Ablations show that the latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual and adversarial losses are important for fidelity. These results indicate that cross-space flow objectives can combine the efficiency of latent representations with direct pixel-space supervision, without requiring a separate decoder at inference.

</details>


### [44] [Vision-Reasoning-Guided Occlusion Removal from Light Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19985)
*Mohamed Youssef,Oliver Bimber*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A vision-language model–guided light-field occlusion removal framework combines physics-based light-field integration with semantic priors and multi-sample fusion to robustly recover occluded scenes, achieving state-of-the-art SSIM on synthetic benchmarks and good generalization across acquisition setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occlusions from dense vegetation severely hinder scene visibility in natural environments, necessitating methods that integrate physical imaging constraints with semantic reasoning to robustly recover lost detail.

Method: 1) Use light-field integration (LFI) to fuse multi-view observations and suppress foreground occlusions, creating an initial visibility-enhanced representation. 2) Introduce a vision-language model (VLM) as a conditional semantic prior to restore degraded structures and fine details guided by observed measurements. 3) Apply a multi-sample fusion strategy to combine multiple generated hypotheses into a consistent, hallucination-reduced estimate.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance, with the highest average SSIM across four synthetic light-field benchmark scenes (4-Syn) and strong generalization to both structured and unstructured acquisition settings, indicating robust perception under heavy occlusion.

Conclusion: Combining physical imaging constraints with vision-language reasoning yields robust occlusion-robust scene recovery, with potential applications in search-and-rescue and exploratory robotics where visibility is severely limited.

Abstract: Occlusion-robust scene recovery remains a major challenge in computational imaging, particularly in natural environments where dense foreground vegetation severely limits visibility. We propose a vision-reasoning-guided light field occlusion removal framework that combines the visibility recovery capability of light field integration (LFI) with the semantic reasoning capacity of vision-language models (VLMs). Multi-view observations are first integrated via LFI to suppress foreground occlusions and produce an initial visibility-enhanced representation. A VLM is then incorporated as a conditional semantic prior to restore degraded structures and recover fine details, guided by the observed measurements. To improve recovery consistency and reduce hallucination artifacts, we introduce a multi-sample fusion strategy that aggregates multiple generated hypotheses into a unified estimate. Experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving the highest average SSIM across four synthetic light field benchmark scenes (4-Syn) and strong generalization across structured and unstructured acquisition settings. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining physical imaging constraints with vision-language reasoning for robust perception under severe occlusion, with applicability to search-and-rescue and exploratory robotic navigation.

</details>


### [45] [QG-MIL: A Gated Transformer Aggregator for Domain-Agnostic Multiple Instance Learning in Medical Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20027)
*Luca Zedda,Davide Antonio Mura,Cecilia Di Ruberto,Maurizio Atzori,Muhammed Furkan Dasdelen,Carsten Marr,Andrea Loddo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QG-MIL introduces a gated transformer MIL aggregator that stabilizes training and distributes attention more evenly across instances without auxiliary losses, achieving stronger cross-domain performance on six medical imaging benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attention-based MIL aggregators often concentrate attention on a few instances, leading to overconfident and unstable predictions. A more balanced aggregation is desirable for robust medical imaging analysis across different MIL scales.

Method: A gated Transformer-based MIL aggregator (QG-MIL) with four synergistic components: RMSNorm-based pre-normalization, per-head QK normalization, fine-grained attention output gating, and SwiGLU-style feed-forward modules. The approach avoids auxiliary losses or multi-stage regularization and does not rely on masking. It includes ablation studies and cross-domain benchmarks across whole-slide pathology and cell-level hematology.

Result: Across six benchmarks (two MIL scales), QG-MIL variants achieve the best performance, averaging +6.1 macro F1 points over baselines. Attention overlays and mass analysis show more distributed instance weighting. Ablations indicate components contribute to cross-domain performance and variance reduction; no single component is strictly dominant across all datasets.

Conclusion: QG-MIL provides improved stability and more uniform attention distribution, delivering consistent cross-domain gains without auxiliary losses. A configurable implementation is released to aid reproducibility.

Abstract: Attention-based Multiple Instance Learning aggregators in medical imaging are prone to attention concentration, producing overconfident and unstable predictions. We introduce QG-MIL, a gated transformer aggregator that addresses this through four synergistic architectural components: RMSNorm-based pre-normalization, per-head QK normalization, fine-grained attention output gating, and SwiGLU-style feed-forward modules. Together, these design choices stabilize training and distribute attention more uniformly across instances without auxiliary losses, masking, or multi-stage regularization. We evaluate QG-MIL across six benchmarks spanning whole-slide pathology and cell-level hematology, covering two fundamentally different MIL scales. The best-performing QG-MIL variants outperform leading baselines on all six benchmarks, with an average improvement of +6.1 mean macro F1 points. Attention overlays and attention mass analysis confirm more distributed instance weighting. Ablation studies show that while individual components can match the full model on specific datasets, the QG-MIL design provides the most consistent cross-domain performance and tightest variance when compared to selected baselines. We release a configurable implementation to support reproducibility at: https://github.com/unica-visual-intelligence-lab/QG-MIL

</details>


### [46] [ReA-OVCD: Reliability-Aware Open-Vocabulary Change Detection via Semantic and Spatial Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20032)
*Hongming Zhu,Huaji Chen,Bowen Du,Sicong Liu,Qin Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReA-OVCD presents a training-free framework for open-vocabulary change detection that locates candidate changes based on pixel-level semantic discrepancies and then refines them via reliability-aware SCR and boundary-aware BCR, achieving state-of-the-art F1^C gains (2.13%–9.75%) with improved efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-Vocabulary Change Detection requires flexible, fine-grained change localization without relying on predefined category vocabularies, while ensuring reliability and clean boundaries. Existing instance-level or pixel-wise methods trade semantic detail for stability, and often incur artifacts or inefficiency.

Method: A two-stage, training-free framework: (1) derive candidate change regions from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies to enable detailed localization; (2) apply collaborative refinement with Semantic Change Reasoning (SCR) to analyze distributional divergence and response variation, and Boundary-aware Change Refinement (BCR) to verify interior-pixel support and suppress boundary artifacts.

Result: Extensive experiments on LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, DSIFN, and SECOND show consistent improvements in F1^C over state-of-the-art methods, with gains ranging from 2.13% to 9.75% and higher computational efficiency. Code is publicly available at the linked repository.

Conclusion: The framework delivers reliable, open-vocabulary change detection without training by integrating semantic-spatial reliability reasoning (SCR) and boundary-aware refinement (BCR), achieving strong cross-dataset performance and efficiency; it advances practical OVCD deployment in remote sensing.

Abstract: Unlike traditional remote sensing change detection that relies on predefined categories, Open-Vocabulary Change Detection (OVCD) identifies land cover changes flexibly using arbitrary text prompts. However, existing methods suffer from an inherent trade-off when modeling changes: instance-level comparison overlooks fine-grained semantic variations (e.g., partial building extensions), while direct pixel comparison proves unreliable, yielding unstable responses and boundary artifacts due to semantic ambiguity and spatial inconsistency. To this end, we propose an efficient training-free Reliability-Aware Open-Vocabulary Change Detection (ReA-OVCD) framework. It first derives candidate change regions from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies to ensure flexible and detailed localization. To ensure reliability, it subsequently introduces a collaborative refinement strategy to explicitly model change validity from both semantic and spatial perspectives. Specifically, we develop a Semantic Change Reasoning (SCR) module that reassesses changes by jointly analyzing distributional divergence and response variation, enabling the suppression of incidental inconsistencies while preserving reliable semantic shifts. In addition, a Boundary-aware Change Refinement (BCR) module is designed to mitigate artifacts stemming from boundary misalignment and uncertainty through validating whether candidate regions are supported by reliable interior pixels. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets (LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, DSIFN, and SECOND) demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving $\mathrm{F}_{1}^{C}$ improvements of 2.13\% to 9.75\% with higher computational efficiency. The code is publicly available at \https://github.com/Funny0101/ReA-OVCD

</details>


### [47] [PU-UNet: Stable Multiplicative Interactions for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20035)
*Ziyuan Li,Osamah Sufyan,Uwe Jaekel,Babette Dellen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PU-UNet introduces stable product-unit residual blocks into a U-Net to enable explicit multiplicative feature modeling in medical image segmentation, achieving improved Dice/IoU with negligible overhead and reduced false positives, especially when placed in low-resolution stages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dense prediction networks rely on additive interactions; product units offer explicit multiplicative modeling but suffer from numerical instability due to log-exp formulation. A stable design is needed to harness multiplicative interactions in deep segmentation models.

Method: Replace or augment residual blocks in a U-Net with product-unit residual blocks, integrate them into low-resolution stages of a residual U-Net; apply smooth positivity mapping and log-domain clipping to stabilize multiplicative learning; evaluate on ISIC 2018, Kvasir-SEG, and BUSI; compare to matched Residual U-Net; conduct ablations on placement and stabilization design.

Result: Dice scores: ISIC 2018 0.942; Kvasir-SEG 0.959; BUSI up to 0.925; consistent improvements in Dice and IoU over matched Res U-Net; negligible changes in parameters, FLOPs, latency; reduced image-level false-positive rate on normal BUSI cases from 0.077 to zero; ablations indicate benefits from product-unit interactions and low-resolution placement with stabilization.

Conclusion: Stable product-unit residual learning is an effective enhancement for U-Net–style segmentation networks, enabling explicit multiplicative interactions with minimal overhead.

Abstract: Many dense prediction networks rely on additive feature transformations and model higher-order feature interactions only implicitly. Product units provide an explicit mechanism for multiplicative feature modeling, but their logarithmic--exponential formulation can cause numerical instability, which has limited their use in deep dense prediction networks. In this work, we propose Product-Unit U-Net (PU-UNet), a residual U-Net that integrates stable product-unit residual blocks into rich low-resolution stages for medical image segmentation. The proposed formulation combines smooth positivity mapping with log-domain clipping, enabling stable multiplicative feature learning with negligible computational overhead. On ISIC 2018, Kvasir-SEG, and BUSI, PU-UNet achieves Dice scores of 0.942, 0.959, and up to 0.925, respectively. Compared with a matched Residual U-Net baseline, PU-UNet consistently improves Dice and IoU while keeping parameters, FLOPs, and inference latency nearly unchanged, and reduces the image-level false-positive rate on normal BUSI cases from 0.077 to zero. Ablation studies suggest that the gains are associated with product-unit interactions, are strongest under low-resolution placement, and benefit from the proposed stabilization design. These results suggest that stable product-unit residual learning can be an effective way to enhance U-Net-style segmentation networks with explicit multiplicative interactions.

</details>


### [48] [FUSE: Frequency-domain Unification and Spectral Energy Alignment for Multi-modal Object Re-Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20044)
*Xuanhao Qi,Tom H. Luan,Yukang Zhang,Jinkai Zheng,Zhou Su,Shuwei Li,Lei Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FUSE introduces a frequency-domain, two-stage framework for multi-modal ReID, featuring Spectral Decomposition Module (SDM) and Cross-Modal Alignment Module (CAM) with learnable frequency modulation; achieves notable improvements on RGBNT201/100 and MSVR310.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-modal ReID methods overemphasize low-frequency cues (color, illumination, coarse appearance) and neglect mid/high-frequency structures that encode geometry, texture, and identity-discriminative details, leading to incomplete spectral representations and unstable cross-modal alignment.

Method: SDM adaptively partitions features into low, mid, and high-frequency subspaces for hierarchical spectral modeling; CAM enforces energy alignment and subspace complementarity across modalities via frequency-consistency regularization; learnable frequency modulation enhances robustness under varying illumination and heterogeneous sensor conditions.

Result: On RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310, FUSE yields sizable improvements, reported as 9.1 percentage points in mAP and 9.5 percentage points in Rank-1 over baselines (relative improvements as stated in the abstract).

Conclusion: FUSE establishes an interpretable frequency-domain paradigm for multi-modal representation learning in ReID, demonstrating robust cross-modal alignment and superior performance across multiple datasets.

Abstract: Despite significant progress in multi-modal Re-Identification (ReID), existing methods tend to emphasize low-frequency cues. Consequently, they focus on attributes such as color, illumination, and coarse appearance, while overlooking mid and high-frequency structures that encode geometric, textural, and identity-discriminative details. This imbalance leads to incomplete spectral representations and unstable cross-modal alignment. To overcome these limitations, we introduce FUSE, a frequency-domain framework that reformulates multi-modal ReID as a two-stage process of spectral disentanglement and energy alignment. The proposed Spectral Decomposition Module (SDM) adaptively partitions features into low, mid, and high-frequency subspaces, enabling hierarchical spectral modeling. The Cross-Modal Alignment Module (CAM) further enforces energy alignment and subspace complementarity across modalities via frequency-consistency regularization. In addition, FUSE incorporates learnable frequency modulation to enhance robustness under varying illumination and heterogeneous sensor conditions. Extensive experiments on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310 show that FUSE achieves 9.1\% mAP and 9.5\% Rank-1 improvements, establishing an interpretable frequency-domain paradigm for multi-modal representation learning.

</details>


### [49] [See-and-Reach: Precise Vision-Language Navigation for UAVs within the Field of View](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20045)
*Fanfu Xue,En Yu,Yantian Shen,Zhikun Hu,Hongjun Wang,Yang Yang,Xindi Wang,Jiande Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes UAV-VLN-FOV to isolate see-and-reach in UAV navigation, and 3DG-VLN to improve fine-grained grounding with dynamic 3D cues; introduces a high-res benchmark and reports strong gains and real-world validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of holistic UAV-VLN formulations which conflate long-range discovery with precise 3D reaching; need a diagnostic, see-and-reach capability for aerial embodied agents.

Method: Introduce UAV-VLN-FOV to isolate the see-and-reach stage in target-visible navigation. Propose 3DG-VLN, a vision-language waypoint predictor guided by dynamic 3D direction cues, processing high-resolution front-view and downward-view inputs, and online updates of target-relative direction during closed-loop navigation. Build a high-resolution benchmark with 2,717 trajectories, high-res observations, and continuous 3D waypoint annotations.

Result: 3DG-VLN outperforms competitive UAV-VLN baselines, with a 13.82% improvement in success rate. Real-world trials validate practical see-and-reach navigation. A dedicated benchmark and code are released (2,717 trajectories; high-res data; 3D waypoints).

Conclusion: The work enables diagnostic evaluation of the see-and-reach capability in UAV navigation and demonstrates practical effectiveness for precise target reaching, supported by a high-res benchmark and publicly available code.

Abstract: UAV Vision-Language Navigation (UAV-VLN) is typically formulated as a holistic search-and-reach problem, where long-range target discovery and final target approach are optimized and evaluated jointly. This formulation makes it difficult to assess a critical capability of aerial embodied agents, namely whether a UAV can accurately ground a visible target and translate vision-language evidence into precise 3D motion once the target enters its field of view. To address this limitation, we introduce UAV-VLN-FOV, a target-visible navigation task that isolates the see-and-reach stage and enables a more diagnostic evaluation of terminal reaching ability. We further propose 3DG-VLN, a vision-language waypoint prediction framework guided by dynamic 3D direction cues to enhance fine-grained visual grounding and spatial direction alignment for precise target reaching. Specifically, 3DG-VLN adaptively processes high-resolution front-view and downward-view observations to preserve fine-grained visual and geometric details for target grounding. It also updates the target-relative direction online during closed-loop navigation, allowing the agent to maintain spatial alignment with the target and reduce accumulated direction drift. To support this task, we construct a dedicated high-resolution benchmark which contains 2,717 trajectories with target-oriented high-level instructions, high-resolution front-view and downward-view egocentric observations, and continuous 3D waypoint annotations. Experiments show that 3DG-VLN outperforms competitive UAV-VLN baselines, achieving a 13.82\% improvement in success rate. Real-world trials further demonstrate the potential of 3DG-VLN for practical see-and-reach navigation. The source code and benchmark are available at https://github.com/xuefanfu/3DG-VLN.

</details>


### [50] [Variable-Length Tokenization via Learnable Global Merging for Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20076)
*Dong Hoon Lee,Seunghoon Hong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Merge-based variable-length tokenizer with learnable global merging for diffusion transformers, addressing cross-length misalignment of truncation-based VLTs, achieving better gFID-compute on ImageNet 256x256.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent Diffusion Models rely on a fixed compression tokenizer; variable-length tokenizers promise adaptive quality/compute. However, truncation-based length modulation makes token semantics length- and position-dependent, causing cross-length distribution shifts that hinder a single VLT-enabled diffusion model.

Method: Introduce a token merging mechanism for variable-length tokenizers. Encourage merging of similar tokens to align representations across lengths; implement learnable global merging that is data-independent to ensure compatibility with diffusion transformers.

Result: On ImageNet 256×256 generation, merging-based VLT with a diffusion transformer achieves a superior gFID-compute trade-off compared to prior VLT methods; code released.

Conclusion: Merging-based VLT with global merging enables cross-length representation alignment, enabling effective single diffusion model across varying lengths and improving efficiency; a practical path for adaptive compression in LDMs.

Abstract: Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) have become dominant in visual synthesis, but their quality-compute trade-off is largely constrained by the tokenizer's fixed compression ratio. Variable-length tokenizers (VLTs) promise adaptive compression by varying token counts, allowing diffusion models to flexibly balance quality and compute. However, conventional VLTs modulate length by truncating ordered token sequences, which makes token semantics depend on token position and breaks representational alignment across lengths. This leads to a cross-length shift in the latent distribution that hinders a single variable-length diffusion model from operating effectively. To address this, we propose a novel variable-length tokenizer that modulates length by merging tokens. We show that encouraging similar tokens to merge enables direct cross-length representation alignment when the diffusion transformer operates according to the merging pattern. Since conventional merging methods are data-dependent, making the merging pattern inaccessible during generation, we introduce learnable global merging, which is data-independent, to ensure compatibility with diffusion transformers. On ImageNet 256$\times$256 generation, our merging-based variable-length tokenizer integrated with a diffusion transformer achieves a superior gFID-compute trade-off compared to prior VLT methods. Code is available at [this https URL](https://github.com/movinghoon/lgm)

</details>


### [51] [The Hidden Evolution of Disguised Visual Context inside the VLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20077)
*Wish Suharitdamrong,Tony Alex,Muhammad Awais,Sara Atito*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A controlled, cross-paradigm study comparing in-context visual prompts versus layer-wise visual injection in VLMs, under identical training, across image and video tasks. It reveals that visual tokens are progressively reshaped inside the LLM in a paradigm-dependent way, each capturing different frequency components of the visual signal, which determines what features are usable and how well representations align with language space; attention alone is insufficient—the quality of visual representations at each layer drives performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how integration architecture shapes the internal transformation of visual tokens in VLMs, and establish a fair, controlled comparison to reveal how in-context prompts vs layer-wise injection influence representation evolution, feature utilization, and task performance across single-image, multi-image, and video settings.
