Method: Fair, controlled comparison under identical training conditions between two integration paradigms: (1) in-context prompts where visual tokens are treated as prompts within the input sequence, and (2) layer-wise injection where visual tokens are injected into intermediate layers. Evaluations span single-image, multi-image, and video benchmarks. Analysis focuses on how tokens are transformed across LLM layers, their frequency characteristics, alignment with language space, and reliance on layer-wise representation quality versus attention.

Result: Visual tokens undergo a hidden evolution inside the LLM, becoming increasingly shaped by the integration paradigm. They emerge as disguised visual context or raw representations lacking linguistic structure, with each paradigm preserving different frequency components of the visual signal. This evolution constrains which visual features the VLM can leverage, affects alignment with the language space, and leads to paradigm- and task-specific performance differences. Merely reallocating attention is insufficient to explain performance; the decisive factor is the quality of visual representations at each layer.

Conclusion: Integration architecture critically determines how visual information is transformed within LLMs. For robust VLM performance, it is essential to consider the progressive refinement of visual representations across layers and how this interacts with language-space alignment, rather than relying on attention shifts alone. Different tasks and visual modalities may benefit from different integration strategies due to their distinct frequency characteristics and representational requirements.

Abstract: Visual tokens enter Large Language Models (LLMs) as raw, foreign signals. How they are transformed into meaningful representations and interact with the language space depends entirely on the integration architecture. Whether by treating visual tokens as in-context prompts within the input sequence or injecting them directly into the LLM's intermediate layers. A controlled comparison and understanding of how these architectural choices affect visual information and its internal transformation to integrate with the LLM remains underexplored. We provide a fair comparison by evaluating in-context and layer-wise injection VLM integration paradigms under identical training conditions across single image, multi-image, and video benchmarks. In doing so, we uncover a hidden evolution where visual tokens enter the LLM as disguised visual context, raw representations lacking linguistic structure, but are progressively reshaped depending on the integration paradigm, each capturing fundamentally different frequency characteristics of the visual signal. We show that this evolution inside the LLM determines what visual features the VLM can utilize effectively, how visual representations align with the language space, and ultimately how each paradigm performs across different tasks. We further demonstrate that attention allocation alone is insufficient, and that performance is driven by the quality of visual representations at each layer.

</details>


### [52] [Holo-World: Unified Camera, Object and Weather Control for Video World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20083)
*Xiangchen Yin,Wenzhang Sun,Jiahui Yuan,Zijie Liu,Yinda Chen,Wei Li,Dachun Kai,Chunfeng Wang,Xiaoyan Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Holo-World is a unified controllable video world model that starts from a single image to both preserve a scene and transfer it to diverse weather states, using a dedicated HoloStateData dataset and a two-branch scene adapter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable fine-grained control of camera, object motion, and weather in video synthesis starting from a single frame, addressing the limitation that weather usually relies on source video or pre-defined future structure.

Method: 1) Build HoloStateData to convert diverse videos into control samples for camera, object, weather. 2) Propose Holo-World with Unified Scene Adapter that factorizes world preservation and weather transfer into separate parameter subspaces, employing rendered background, geometry buffers, and object controls to maintain structure while modeling weather-dependent appearances and particle effects. 3) Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG to separate scene and weather residuals, strengthen target weather effects without amplifying full condition.

Result: Experiments show precise camera/object control and consistent scene structure; successful weather transfer to diverse states; outperforms video-to-video weather editing baselines in weather-state generation; qualitative and quantitative results.

Conclusion: Holo-World demonstrates stable preservation of scene structure under weather changes and effective weather transfer, offering a unified framework for controllable video generation from a single image. A project page is available.

Abstract: Video world models are moving toward preserving an observed world under controllable camera and object motion while allowing its environmental state to change. Yet these controls remain isolated, and weather generation typically relies on a source video or reconstructed scene that already specifies future structure. We study a first-frame-anchored source-to-state setting, where the model starts from a single image and follows explicit camera and object controls and an optional weather instruction, then generates a video that either preserves the source world or transfers it to a target weather state. To address these challenges, we first build HoloStateData, a state video dataset that turns diverse videos into unified control samples for camera, object, and weather supervision. Second, we introduce Holo-World, a unified controllable video world model that jointly controls scene from a single image. Its Unified Scene Adapter factorizes world preservation and weather transfer into distinct parameter subspaces, using rendered background, geometry buffers, and object controls to maintain controlled scene structure while modeling weather-dependent appearance and particle effects. Additionally, Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG guides scene and weather residuals separately, strengthening target weather effects without over-amplifying the full condition. Quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that Holo-World maintains precise camera and object control with consistent scene structure while transferring scenes into diverse target weather state, outperforming video-to-video weather editing baselines on weather-state generation. Our project page is available at \url{https://xiangchenyin.github.io/Holo-World/}.

</details>


### [53] [EventVLA: Event-Driven Visual Evidence Memory for Long-Horizon Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20092)
*Ganlin Yang,Zhangzheng Tu,Yuqiang Yang,Sitong Mao,Junyi Dong,Tianxing Chen,Jiaqi Peng,Jing Xiong,Jiafei Cao,Jifeng Dai,Wengang Zhou,Yao Mu,Tai Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EventVLA introduces sparse visual evidence memory with foundational anchors and a dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM) to store task-critical visual events, enabling foresight-driven recall for long-horizon manipulation; it achieves ~40% higher success on memory-demanding tasks compared to prior VLA methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon robotic manipulation suffers from memory bottlenecks as task-relevant cues become occluded or unobservable. Existing memory-augmented VLAs underperform due to information bottlenecks, high latency fromdual systems, or unselective buffers with redundant data.

Method: An end-to-end framework with two components: (1) foundational visual anchors to retain initial and short-term context, and (2) KEM that directly predicts future keyframe probabilities from the VLA latent embeddings to sparsely store task-critical visual events. The approach enables foresight into the future causal utility of current observations and preserves transient cues. It is complemented by RoboTwin-MeM, a diagnostic benchmark for non-Markovian manipulation with interactive visual evidence, and validated across 17 simulation tasks and 4 real-world bimanual tasks.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows an average success-rate improvement of 40% over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLAs across 21 tasks (17 simulations + 4 real-world).

Conclusion: The foresight-driven, sparse memory design effectively mitigates non-Markovian challenges by preserving essential transient visual evidence, improving robustness and efficiency of memory-based VLA policies; the RoboTwin-MeM benchmark provides a targeted evaluation for non-Markovian manipulation scenarios.

Abstract: Memory remains a critical bottleneck for long-horizon robotic manipulation, as standard Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies often fail when task-relevant cues become occluded or unobservable over time. While existing memory-augmented methods utilize historical context, they either suffer from severe information bottlenecks, incur high latency via decoupled dual systems, or rely on unselective buffers that accumulate massive visual redundancies. To address these limitations, we introduce EventVLA, an end-to-end framework founded on the concept of sparse visual evidence memory that comprises two core components: foundational visual anchors to retain initial and short-term contexts, and a dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM) module. Specifically, KEM directly predicts future keyframe probabilities from the VLA's latent embeddings to autonomously capture and store sparse, task-critical visual events. This foresight-driven mechanism empowers the policy to dynamically evaluate the future causal utility of current observations, preserving transient visual evidence before it becomes unobservable. Furthermore, we propose RoboTwin-MeM, a diagnostic benchmark specifically designed to evaluate non-Markovian manipulation tasks with interactive visual evidence. Extensive evaluations show that across 17 memory-requiring simulation tasks and 4 real-world bimanual tasks, EventVLA achieves an average success rate improvement of +40% over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLAs.

</details>


### [54] [MakeupMirror: Improving Facial Attribute Preservation in Diffusion Models for Makeup Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20094)
*Nefeli Andreou,Angel Martínez-González,Sabine Sternig,Matthieu Guillaumin,Epameinondas Antonakos,Michael Opitz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based makeup transfer with MakeupMirror improves identity preservation and skin-tone stability over Stable-Makeup using geometry-conditioned ControlNets, region-specific transfer, skin-tone modulation, and a LM Langevin sampler, achieving fast inference (~0.7s) and high expert acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of existing diffusion-based makeup transfer (identity preservation and skin tone shift) to enable production-level virtual try-on for makeup shopping.

Method: Integrates facial geometry conditioning with ControlNets to maintain facial fidelity; region-specific makeup transfer control across facial regions (skin, eyes, lips); skin tone-based transfer modulation to prevent cross-subject skin tone changes; Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler to speed up inference while preserving quality; evaluated on CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, and MakeupSelfies datasets.

Result: Relative facial recognition similarity improved by +60% vs Stable-Makeup; relative skin tone difference reduced by -50%; latency ~0.7s; expert acceptance rate 94% across core facial identity preservation criteria.

Conclusion: MakeupMirror advances diffusion-based makeup transfer toward production-level quality by combining geometry-aware conditioning, region-aware transfer, and skin-tone modulation with accelerated sampling, showing strong identity preservation and skin tone stability on diverse datasets; further work needed on broader demographics, lighting variations, and reproducibility.

Abstract: Makeup transfer models enable fun augmented reality (AR) experiences as well as virtual try-on (VTO) for online makeup shopping. While recent state-of-the-art diffusion based solutions such as Stable-Makeup dramatically improve the accuracy and realism of makeup transfer, they still face limitations in identity and skin color preservation, making production-level VTO for makeup shopping unrealistic. In this work, we propose MakeupMirror, a diffusion-based approach to makeup transfer that makes significant progress towards preserving facial features and skin tone. We introduce several technical innovations over Stable-Makeup: (1) integration of facial geometry conditioning with ControlNets to maintain facial fidelity; (2) region-specific makeup transfer control to enable precise makeup application across facial regions such as skin, eyes and lips; (3) skin tone-based makeup transfer modulation that prevent skin tone alteration in cross-subject transfer scenarios; and (4) integration of a Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler to speed up inference while maintaining generation quality. Our experiments on CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, and (herein newly collected, more diverse) MakeupSelfies datasets show that MakeupMirror improves relative facial recognition similarity by +60%, reduces relative skin tone difference by -50% over Stable-Makeup, with a latency of 0.7s, while achieving expert acceptance rate of 94% across core facial identity preservation criteria.

</details>


### [55] [Stitching and dimensionality effects on large artificially generated volume datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20095)
*Lucas von Chamier,Jan Philipp Albrecht,Dagmar Kainmüller*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Stitching artifacts in patch-based image generation can harm downstream biomedical segmentation, and perceptual metrics like FID may fail to reflect this impact; 3D stitching offers marginal gains at high cost, 2D training is more stable, and ensembling helps only for low-quality outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how patch-based generation with different stitching strategies and 2D vs 3D patching affects downstream biomedical segmentation, and whether perceptual metrics align with task performance.

Method: Train cycleGANs on cryo-EM datasets using three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D and 3D). Evaluate perceptual quality (FID) and downstream mitochondria segmentation performance. Test three-way orthogonal ensembling of predictions. Compare 2D vs 3D training stability and computational cost.

Result: FID metrics fail to detect subtle stitching artifacts that degrade segmentation performance. 3D models with artifact-free stitching provide marginal improvement on downstream segmentation over 2D but not enough to justify higher computational cost. 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes. Orthogonal ensembling improves low-quality volumes but offers no benefit for high-quality outputs.

Conclusion: Stitching artifacts significantly affect domain-specific biomedical imaging tasks and perceptual metrics inadequately capture this; careful mitigation of stitching artifacts is essential for large-scale generative models in biomedical contexts, and relying solely on perceptual scores is insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation quality.

Abstract: Generating large images via deep learning requires patching input data to accommodate hardware memory limitations, then assembling output patches, a process that can introduce stitching artifacts when neighboring patches do not align at borders. While these artifacts are known to affect segmentation tasks, their impact on generative models for style-transfer remains poorly understood. We investigated three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D vs 3D) using cycleGAN models trained on cryo-electron microscopy datasets. We evaluated both perceptual quality and performance on downstream mitochondria segmentation. Our key findings reveal that: (1) FID scores fail to detect subtle stitching artifacts that significantly impact downstream segmentation performance, (2) 3D models with artifact-free stitching marginally outperform 2D models on downstream tasks, though the improvement barely justifies the computational cost, and (3) 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes. Additionally, we demonstrate that ensembling predictions from three orthogonal directions can improve low-quality volumes but provides no benefit for high-quality outputs. These results demonstrate that maximizing generative model performance on large scientific datasets requires careful consideration and mitigation of stitching artifacts, and that perceptual metrics alone are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation quality in biomedical imaging.

</details>


### [56] [WeGenBench: A Multidimensional Diagnostic Benchmark towards Text-to-Image Model Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20100)
*Qian Liang,Xiaomin Li,Ying Zhang,Jia Xu,Lihao Ni,Hongrui Li,Jingjing Li,Jing Lyu,Chen Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WeGenBench: a comprehensive, bilingual benchmark (Chinese-English) for text-to-image generation with 4,000 prompts, multi-dimensional tagging, cross-dimensional evaluation, and novel Vision-Language Model-based metrics plus reasoning traces to diagnose model deficiencies across categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks inadequately capture multi-dimensional performance and language-specific deficiencies in text-to-image generation, hindering fine-grained analysis of models. There is a need for a bilingual, category-aware benchmark with robust metrics and traceable reasoning to identify limitations.

Method: 1) Compile 4,000 test prompts across two primary categories, balanced for Chinese and English. 2) Annotate prompts with multi-dimensional language-specific tags to enable fine-grained sub-categories. 3) Use cross-dimensional evaluation via scene classifications and tags to pinpoint deficiencies. 4) Design/validate novel evaluation metrics by integrating Vision–Language Models (VLMs) assessing domain-specific tasks across three core aspects. 5) Provide assessment results with detailed reasoning trajectories to verify results. 6) Benchmark current SOTA models and analyze limitations.

Result: A new benchmark framework, WeGenBench, enabling precise, cross-linguistic, multi-dimensional evaluation of text-to-image generation. It yields both performance scores and reasoning traces, facilitates pinpointing model weaknesses by category, and includes VLM-based metrics validated on domain-specific tasks across three aspects. Systematic benchmarks for SOTA methods and an in-depth analysis of existing limitations are provided.

Conclusion: WeGenBench offers a holistic, transparent evaluation toolkit for T2I generation, combining bilingual prompts, fine-grained task tagging, cross-dimensional analysis, and VLM-based metrics with traceable reasoning, enabling rigorous verification of model capabilities and limitations.

Abstract: Recent text-to-image generation models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in synthesizing highly realistic images from text inputs alone. Although existing benchmarks can evaluate the generation capabilities of various models to some extent, they struggle to comprehensively and accurately measure performance across multiple dimensions, often failing to reveal the inherent deficiencies of models in specific categories. To address these limitations, we propose WeGenBench, a novel benchmark designed for the comprehensive, multi-perspective evaluation of text-to-image generation capabilities. Our benchmark comprises a total of 4,000 test prompts across two primary categories, meticulously balanced between Chinese and English to evaluate bilingual and cross-cultural generation capabilities. Beyond macroscopic scene classification, we annotate each prompt with multi-dimensional tags tailored to the distinct content and challenges of each language, thereby refining the generation tasks into more specific sub-categories. Through a cross-dimensional evaluation mechanism leveraging both scene classifications and multi-dimensional tags, WeGenBench can precisely pinpoint model shortcomings in specific generation categories. Furthermore, to measure generation quality more accurately, we design and validate several novel evaluation metrics by integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs), which assess model performance on domain-specific tasks from three core aspects. Crucially, our approach yields both the assessment outcomes and the detailed reasoning trajectories, facilitating a rigorous verification of the accuracy and soundness of the evaluation results. Finally, we conduct systematic benchmarking on current state-of-the-art methods and provide an in-depth analysis of the limitations present in existing models.

</details>


### [57] [Geometry-Preserving in 3D Gaussian Splatting for LiDAR-Camera Extrinsic Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20103)
*Kyoleen Kwak,Daeho Kim,Jeong Woon Lee,Hyoseok Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Targetless LiDAR-camera calibration that preserves Gaussian Splatting geometry by using multi-view LiDAR depth supervision while blocking photometric updates to the Gaussian parameters, yielding higher calibration accuracy than prior targetless methods on driving datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate proxy geometry drift and over-reliance on photometric cues by enforcing metric consistency and directly leveraging multi-view depth information.

Method: Aggregate multi-view LiDAR observations to produce dense depth supervision for the Gaussian proxy, and block photometric gradients from updating Gaussian spatial parameters, within a differentiable LiDAR-camera calibration framework built around 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS).

Result: The approach consistently achieves higher calibration accuracy than existing targetless methods on public driving datasets.

Conclusion: Preserving the metric geometry of the Gaussian proxy while exploiting multi-view depth supervision improves targetless LiDAR-camera calibration and reduces geometry drift compared to prior methods that optimize on photometric cues.

Abstract: Accurate LiDAR-camera calibration is essential for robust multi-modal perception. Targetless approaches avoid manual setup but remain limited by the scarcity of discriminative cross-modal features. Recent methods address this by reconstructing the scene within a differentiable model, enabling extrinsic optimization through dense photometric supervision. Among these, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has been widely adopted as a geometric proxy that bridges LiDAR and camera within a single differentiable framework. However, since 3DGS was originally designed for novel view synthesis, existing methods tend to prioritize rendering quality, causing the proxy geometry to drift from the true LiDAR structure. We propose a framework that preserves the metric geometry of the Gaussian proxy by aggregating multi-view LiDAR observations for dense depth supervision and blocking photometric gradients from updating the Gaussian spatial parameters. We validate our method on public driving datasets, where it consistently outperforms existing targetless methods in calibration accuracy.

</details>


### [58] [EFIQA: Explainable Fundus Image Quality Assessment via Anatomical Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20108)
*Pengwei Wang,José Morano,Qian Wan,Hrvoje Bogunović*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EFIQA proposes a label-free, spatially explicable image quality assessment for fundus images, using a two-stage approach that learns what should be there via anatomical priors to produce quality maps, and outperforms supervised methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning image quality assessment methods rely on dataset-specific quality labels, which limits generalization to different labeling criteria and prevents spatial explainability. A label-free approach with spatial feedback is desirable.

Method: Stage 1: train an unsupervised anomaly detector via masked anatomical inpainting to identify regions with missing vasculature. Stage 2: distill this anatomical prior knowledge into a shallow adapter that maps features from a frozen foundation model to precise, per-pixel quality maps.

Result: External-dataset evaluation shows that EFIQA, a label-free approach with minimal adaptation, achieves better performance and explainability than supervised methods across benchmarks with varying quality criteria.

Conclusion: EFIQA demonstrates that label-free, explainable image quality assessment with spatial maps is feasible and can outperform supervised baselines, indicating strong potential for real-world applications.

Abstract: Image quality control is vital for a wide range of downstream applications. Deep learning-based image quality assessment methods typically train classifiers on dataset-specific quality labels, inheriting two limitations: (1) generalization is tied to the labeling criteria of the training set and (2) these methods cannot provide spatial feedback on where the quality is degraded, lacking explainability. In this work, we propose EFIQA, a framework that requires no quality-related supervision and produces spatial quality maps by design. Rather than learning ``what is degradation" from human-annotated labels, EFIQA learns ``what should be there" by leveraging anatomical priors. For fundus photography, we instantiate this as a two-stage approach, by first training an unsupervised anomaly detector via masked anatomical inpainting to identify regions of missing vasculature, and then distilling this prior knowledge into a shallow adapter mapping features of a frozen foundation model to precise quality maps. External-dataset evaluation demonstrates that this label-free approach with minimal adaptation achieves better performance and explainability compared with supervised methods across benchmarks with different quality criteria, highlighting its potential for real-world applications.

</details>


### [59] [FrozenDrive: Zero-Shot Text-Guided Driving Scene Generation and Data Augmentation with Parameter-Free Frozen Diffusion Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20110)
*Yuhwan Jeong,Hyeonseong Kim,Daehyun We,Seonkyu Song,Jinnyeong Yang,Hyun-Kurl Jang,Youngho Yoon,Kuk-Jin Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FrozenDrive preserves pretrained diffusion knowledge while achieving cross-view alignment and temporal coherence without fine-tuning, using a parameter-free frozen backbone with spatio-temporal attention and an object-focused constraint to improve fidelity for rare categories; it yields robust multi-view driving scene synthesis under adverse conditions and benefits downstream AD models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion-based synthetic driving data struggle with maintaining multi-view/temporal consistency without backbone fine-tuning, risk eroding pre-trained knowledge, and failing under adverse weather or rare classes. A method that preserves pretrained knowledge while enforcing consistency and improving rare-object fidelity would enhance data usefulness for AD models.

Method: A parameter-free frozen diffusion backbone augmented with knowledge-preserving spatio-temporal attention to enforce cross-view alignment and temporal coherence in a single pass. Conditioning on rich driving-stack signals plus text prompts, plus an object-focused constraint to boost fidelity for rare categories; no weather- or scene-specific fine-tuning.

Result: On nuScenes, FrozenDrive-augmented data significantly improves autonomous driving models, with notable gains in night and rain scenarios, demonstrating stronger robustness when trained with scenario-targeted data; it surpasses prior baselines without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: FrozenDrive enables globally coherent, multi-view driving scene synthesis under adverse conditions without fine-tuning, maintaining pretrained diffusion knowledge and improving downstream AD robustness and label fidelity.

Abstract: Synthetic data for autonomous driving is surging, powered by diffusion models that promise scalable scene generation. Yet key obstacles remain, as enforcing multi-view and temporal consistency often relies on backbone fine-tuning or added layers, which erodes pre-trained knowledge and weakens text alignment. Models also stay close to the training distribution, struggling under adverse weather and unseen configurations, and fidelity favors frequent over rare classes. We address these gaps with FrozenDrive, a controllable generative framework that preserves a pretrained diffusion models knowledge while achieving strong consistency. FrozenDrive conditions on rich driving-stack signals and text prompts, and introduces knowledge-preserving spatio-temporal attention to impose cross-view alignment and temporal coherence in a single pass within a parameter-free frozen diffusion backbone. An additional object-focused constraint improves per-object fidelity for rare categories. Without any weather- or scene-specific fine-tuning, our model synthesizes globally coherent multi-view driving scenes from text, particularly under adverse and rare conditions, and surpasses prior baselines. On nuScenes, FrozenDrive augmented data significantly improves AD models performance, especially at night and in rain, demonstrating stronger robustness when trained with our scenario-targeted data.

</details>


### [60] [Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer: Scalable 3D CT Volume Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20112)
*Zhenkai Zhang,Markus Hiller,Krista A. Ehinger,Tom Drummond*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PRDiT is a two-stage voxel-level diffusion framework for high-resolution 3D medical volumes. It uses a local MLP-based patch denoiser to separate low-frequency content, followed by a global residual diffusion transformer with memory-efficient attention to refine high-frequency details, achieving superior metrics over HA-GAN, 3D LDM, and WDM-3D on LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generating high-resolution 3D CT volumes is computationally demanding and difficult to optimize with existing generative models, often suffering from loss of subtle structures. A scalable, stable, autoencoder-free approach is needed to preserve fine details without bottlenecks.

Method: Two-stage training: (1) local denoiser via MLP-based blind estimator on overlapping 3D patches to extract low-frequency structures; (2) global residual diffusion transformer with memory-efficient attention to model and refine high-frequency residuals across full volumes. Coarse-to-fine strategy reduces optimization complexity and improves stability, avoiding autoencoder bottlenecks.

Result: PRDiT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models (HA-GAN, 3D LDM, WDM-3D) on LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT, achieving significantly lower 3D FID, MMD, and Wasserstein distances.

Conclusion: A scalable, stable voxel-level diffusion framework for high-resolution 3D medical volumes that preserves subtle structures better than prior methods, validated across two large CT datasets.

Abstract: Generating high-resolution 3D CT volumes with fine details remains challenging due to substantial computational demands and optimization difficulties inherent to existing generative models. In this paper, we propose the Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer (PRDiT), a scalable generative framework that synthesizes high-quality 3D medical volumes directly at voxel-level. PRDiT introduces a two-stage training architecture comprising 1) a local denoiser in the form of an MLP-based blind estimator operating on overlapping 3D patches to separate low-frequency structures efficiently, and 2) a global residual diffusion transformer employing memory-efficient attention to model and refine high-frequency residuals across entire volumes. This coarse-to-fine modeling strategy simplifies optimization, enhances training stability, and effectively preserves subtle structures without the limitations of an autoencoder bottleneck. Extensive experiments conducted on the LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT datasets demonstrate that PRDiT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, such as HA-GAN, 3D LDM and WDM-3D, achieving significantly lower 3D FID, MMD and Wasserstein distance scores.

</details>


### [61] [SAM3 Self-Distillation for Fine-Grained GOOSE 2D Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20130)
*Xuesong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 4th-place ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D fine-grained semantic segmentation entry using SAM3 encoder with a lightweight decoder; adds self-distillation from SAM3 with GT boxes, image-level multi-scale TTA by input rescaling, and shows aggressive photometric distortion from a prior winning entry as the main gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To push performance in fine-grained semantic segmentation by leveraging a recent visual foundation model (SAM3) and investigating training-time and test-time strategies to maximize accuracy on a 1,815-image test set.

Method: Adapt the SAM3 image encoder with a lightweight decoder; implement self-distillation where SAM3 serves as teacher for classes where it outperforms the student, using ground-truth boxes as prompts; apply image-level multi-scale test-time augmentation by rescaling images instead of model inputs; apply aggressive photometric distortion inspired by a prior winning entry.

Result: Achieved a mean IoU of 69.73% on the official test set and ranked 4th in the competition.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective adaptation of a large foundation-model encoder for fine-grained segmentation, with self-distillation and input-rescaled TTA providing notable gains; photometric distortion from a prior winning entry is identified as the dominant performance booster.

Abstract: We describe our 4th-place entry to the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge, which reached a composite mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU) of 69.73% on the official 1,815-image test set. Our model adapts the image encoder of a recent visual foundation model, Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3), with a lightweight decoder. Beyond this, we contribute two techniques and one empirical finding: (i) a self-distillation scheme that re-uses SAM3 itself, prompted with ground-truth boxes, as a teacher on the classes where it outperforms our own model; (ii) an image-level multi-scale test-time augmentation scheme that restores multi-scale inference for a fixed-input-size model by rescaling the image rather than the model input; and (iii) the finding that an aggressive photometric distortion from a winning 2025 GOOSE 2D entry, transplanted onto our pipeline, is its single largest source of improvement.

</details>


### [62] [TriFlow: Generating Artist-Like 3D Mesh Topology via Nearest-Vertex Vector Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20131)
*Haoxuan Li,Ziya Erkoç,Daniele Sirigatti,Vladislav Rosov,Lei Li,Angela Dai,Matthias Nießner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TriFlow introduces a nearest-vertex field on surfaces to encode topology, learned via a flow-based model conditioned on input geometry, and extracted through NVF-guided clustering and topology-aware QEM; it achieves better topology quality, generalization, and speed over prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative 3D mesh methods struggle to produce coherent, artist-friendly topology that also faithfully conforms to input geometry; a topology-centric, learnable representation can improve both quality and controllability.

Method: Represent topology with a nearest-vertex field (NVF) defined on the surface; train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize NVF conditioned on geometry; cluster surface regions using the NVF; apply a topology-aware constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification to extract a coherent mesh.

Result: TriFlow demonstrates stronger generalization and significantly improved topology quality compared to state-of-the-art learning-based approaches, with a Chamfer Distance that is 90% lower and an 8x speedup.

Conclusion: A topology-centric generative pipeline (NVF + flow-matching) can produce artist-like topology meshes that faithfully approximate input geometry more efficiently and with better topology than prior methods; suggests a promising direction for controllable 3D mesh generation.

Abstract: We present TriFlow, a new generative approach for producing compact 3D meshes with artist-like triangle topology directly from input geometry conditions such as signed distance fields. Our key insight is to represent mesh topology as a nearest-vertex vector field (NVF) defined over the surface, where each point encodes its association to the nearest triangle vertex in the local barycentric frame. We train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize this field, enabling topology generation conditioned on the input geometry. To extract a coherent mesh, we cluster surface regions using the generated NVF and guide a constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification with topology-aware optimization. This yields output meshes that closely match the input geometry while exhibiting structured, artist-like connectivity. Experiments demonstrate that TriFlow achieves stronger generalization and significantly improved topology quality compared to state-of-the-art learning-based approaches, alongside 90% lower Chamfer Distance and an 8x speedup.

</details>


### [63] [SA-VIS: Sparse frame Annotations for training Video Instance Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20140)
*Edoardo Mello Rella,Ajad Chhatkuli,Shipra Jain,Ender Konukoglu,Luc Van Gool*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SA-VIS introduces Past-frames Feature Propagation to enable flexible, low-compute training of video instance segmentation with sparse annotations, achieving near-parity with densely annotated training and boosting performance on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current online VIS methods rely on densely annotated video sequences, which are expensive in computation and labeling. Reducing annotation density without sacrificing performance remains a key challenge.

Method: Introduce Past-frames Feature Propagation (PFP) that propagates low-dimensional encoder features across multiple frames to provide temporal context. Pair this with light-weight, frame-specific Instance Queries. Train end-to-end using sparsely annotated frames (SA-VIS).

Result: SA-VIS substantially improves over baseline with sparse labels; using annotations on only 1/5 of the images incurs merely ~0.4% AP drop. It yields strong gains on YouTube-VIS 2019/2021/2022 and Occluded VIS (OVIS), and more than 1% AP improvement in limited-annotations scenarios.

Conclusion: A simple, low-cost module (PFP) can bridge the accuracy gap between sparse and dense video annotations in VIS, enabling effective end-to-end training with far fewer labeled frames while maintaining competitive performance.

Abstract: Recent online video instance segmentation (VIS) methods have achieved impressive results, thus becoming the preferred approach to segment instances in videos. Despite the resurgence of impressive single image models, the online (or semi-online) VIS approaches outperform single-image models (e.g., based on SAM) by using long sequences of densely annotated frames during training. However,such a training setup of VIS is expensive in the sense of compute as well as dense annotations required. In order to solve these major flaws, we argue that the effective modeling of the instances and their evolution in videos do not require densely annotated frames. To that end, we propose a simple and effective module, called Past-frames Feature Propagation (PFP) which aggregates low-dimensional features from the image encoder of multiple frames. This simple low-compute module provides tremendous learning capability in using sparse video frame labels for end-to-end training. Combined with a light-weight frame-specific Instance Queries, our Sparse frame Annotation VIS (SA-VIS) significantly improves performance over its baseline. Most interestingly, our simple design that avoids complexities effectively bridges the gap in accuracy between training on sparsely and densely annotated video sequences. This translates to a mere 0.4% drop in performance of SA-VIS when using annotations for only 1/5 of the images in the dataset. Empirically, SA-VIS shows strong improvements over the baseline on YouTube-VIS 2019/2021/2022 and Occluded VIS (OVIS) and an over 1% improvement in AP on the state-of-the-art in a limited annotations scenario.

</details>


### [64] [HEad and neCK TumOR (HECKTOR) 2025: Benchmark of Segmentation, Diagnosis, and Prognosis in Multimodal PET/CT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20143)
*Numan Saeed,Salma Hassan,Shahad Hardan,Lishan Cai,Xinglong Liang,Moona Mazher,Abdul Qayyum,Yansong Bu,Mengye Lyu,Yue Lin,Mingyuan Meng,Chuanyi Huang,Lisheng Wang,Dalal Chamseddine,Shamimeh Ahrari,Beining Wu,Yifei Chen,Fuyou Mao,Hao Zhang,Baixiang Zhao,Surajit Ray,Muzi Guo,Lei Xiang,Jakob Dexl,Michael Ingrisch,Adrien Depeursinge,Arman Rahmim,Mathieu Hatt,Vincent Andrearczyk,Mohammad Yaqub*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HECKTOR 2025 benchmarks automated head-and-neck cancer analysis on multimodal PET/CT, including segmentation of GTVp/GTVn, recurrence-free survival prediction, and HPV status classification; >1,100 patients from 10 centers; 35 teams; top metrics: Dice 0.75, C-index 0.66, balanced accuracy 0.56.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address variability and time cost of manual segmentation and the clinical value of noninvasive outcome and HPV prediction; establish a comprehensive, multi-institutional benchmark to accelerate clinically translatable automated HNC analysis.

Method: Collaborative benchmark study analyzing 15 final submissions for 3 tasks on a held-out test set; evaluation across lesion characteristics; dataset with multimodal PET/CT and EHRs (>1,100 patients, 10 centers); metrics: Dice for segmentation, C-index for survival, balanced accuracy for HPV; cross-team comparative analysis.

Result: Top-performing algorithms achieved mean Dice 0.75 in segmentation, C-index 0.66 for RFS, and balanced accuracy 0.56 for HPV; 35 registered teams, 15 final submissions; comprehensive methodology analysis and performance stratified by lesion characteristics; implications for deployment in automated oncology workflows.

Conclusion: The HECKTOR 2025 benchmark advances multimodal HNC analysis benchmarking, highlights current performance gaps and generalization challenges, and informs future dataset expansion, methodological innovations (e.g., multimodal fusion, EHR integration), and translation of automated tools into clinical practice.

Abstract: Head and neck cancers (HNC) represent a significant global health burden, with accurate tumor delineation being essential for effective radiotherapy planning. The complexity of the oropharyngeal anatomy, combined with the heterogeneous appearance of tumors on imaging, makes manual segmentation time-intensive and subject to inter-observer variability. Beyond segmentation, predicting long-term clinical outcomes, such as recurrence-free survival (RFS), and determining human papillomavirus (HPV) status from noninvasive imaging, remain challenging yet clinically valuable goals. The HECKTOR 2025 challenge addresses these needs by establishing a comprehensive benchmark for automated HNC analysis using multimodal PET/CT imaging and electronic health records. Building on previous editions (2020-2022), this challenge features an expanded multi-institutional dataset comprising over 1,100 patients from 10 centers worldwide. Participants were tasked with three complementary objectives: (1) segmenting primary gross tumor volumes (GTVp) and metastatic lymph nodes (GTVn), (2) predicting recurrence-free survival, and (3) classifying HPV status. The challenge attracted 35 registered teams, with 15 final submissions evaluated on a held-out test set. Top-performing algorithms achieved a mean Dice similarity coefficient of 0.75 for segmentation, a concordance index of 0.66 for survival prediction, and a balanced accuracy of 0.56 for HPV classification. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the submitted methodologies, evaluates their performance across different lesion characteristics, and discusses their implications for clinical translation in automated oncology workflows and decision support systems.

</details>


### [65] [NAMESAKES: Probing Identity Memorization in Text-to-Image Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20155)
*Morris Alper,Vasudha Varadarajan,Moran Yanuka,Angelina Wang,Hadar Averbuch-Elor*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A black-box behavioral probe distinguishes memorized vs fabricated faces in text-to-image models, using the NAMESAKES dataset; it predicts identity memorization without reference photos or training data, and reveals differences across model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy concerns when T2I models can generate recognizable likenesses of public figures. Current memorization detection requires ground-truth data or access to internals; a fully black-box approach enables practical auditing.

Method: Develop a black-box probing procedure that uses prompts with names and perturbed variants to elicit identity signals from T2I models, without any reference photos or training data. Construct NAMESAKES, a dataset of 1,000+ public figure names and faces plus perturbed names. Evaluate state-of-the-art T2I models to predict memorization and separate memorized from unrecognized names, analyzing differences across model families.

Result: The probe substantially predicts identity memorization and successfully separates memorized from unrecognized names. It provides insights into how different model families handle memorization.

Conclusion: A fully black-box probing framework can detect memorization in T2I models and benchmark memorization across models without requiring access to training data or model internals, with implications for privacy and model evaluation.

Abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) models generate realistic likenesses of some individuals when prompted with their names, raising privacy concerns. However, distinguishing whether a generated face is memorized or fabricated currently requires ground-truth photos, access to training data, or white-box access to model internals, limiting applicability. We introduce a fully black-box behavioral probe that distinguishes between these regimes while requiring no reference photos or prior knowledge of training data. To benchmark this task, we present the NAMESAKES dataset of over one thousand names and faces of public figures spanning a wide range of fame levels, along with perturbed, less famous names. Experiments on state-of-the-art T2I models show that our probe substantially predicts identity memorization and separates memorized from unrecognized names, with further insights into differences across model families.

</details>


### [66] [ARTEMIS: Agent-guided Reliability-aware Temporal Mask Evolution for Imperfectly Supervised Video Polyp Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20161)
*Tong Wang,Siwen Wang,Yaolei Qi,Jinxing Zhou,Yuting He,Guanyu Yang,Yutong Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ARTEMIS is a unified framework for imperfectly supervised video polyp segmentation that uses agent-guided reliability-aware temporal mask evolution. It initializes masks with SAM2 and dense anchors, selects reliable temporal anchors via a debate-and-judge vision-language agent, propagates masks bidirectionally with SAM2, and trains a reliability-aware robust segmenter with a Reference Prototype Transport Module and reliability-guided loss, achieving state-of-the-art results on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 under scribble/point/limited-label supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VPS under weak or scarce pixel-level supervision is clinically valuable but challenging due to weak contrast, boundary ambiguity, motion blur, and specular highlights. Current methods (e.g., SAM2) can produce boundary leakage and ignore temporal reliability; there is a need to leverage temporal consistency and reliability signals to improve dense, temporally coherent masks.

Method: 1) Initialize coarse masks from supervision: SAM2 converts points/scribbles; dense labels serve as anchors. 2) A debate-and-judge vision-language agent selects reliable temporal anchors under weak supervision. 3) Propagate anchors bidirectionally with SAM2 to refine unreliable/unlabeled frames. 4) Train the segmenter with temporal reliability-aware robust learning, featuring: reliability-guided reference selection; Reference Prototype Transport Module; reliability-aware robust loss. These components evaluate mask reliability, evolve anchors over time, transport target identity across frames, and down-weight noisy supervision instead of discarding difficult samples.

Result: Empirical evaluation on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 under scribble, point, and limited-label settings shows state-of-the-art performance for imperfectly supervised VPS.

Conclusion: ARTEMIS demonstrates that reliability-aware temporal mask evolution, anchored by a vision-language agent and SAM2-assisted propagation, can effectively leverage weak supervision for accurate, temporally consistent VPS, with a robust learning scheme that down-weights noisy labels rather than discarding difficult samples; code will be released.

Abstract: Imperfectly supervised video polyp segmentation (VPS) aims to learn dense, temporally consistent masks from inexpensive supervision, including weak annotations (points, scribbles) and semi-supervision with few densely labeled frames. This setting is clinically valuable but challenging due to weak contrast, ambiguous boundaries, motion blur, and specular highlights, compounded by sparse pixel-level guidance. While SAM2 can generate dense masks from sparse inputs, direct pseudo-labeling often yields geometry-degraded masks with boundary leakage, underutilizes temporal consistency, and ignores reliability. To address these issues, we propose ARTEMIS, a unified framework for imperfectly supervised VPS driven by agent-guided reliability-aware temporal mask evolution. ARTEMIS initializes coarse masks from available supervision: SAM2 converts points/scribbles, while dense labels serve as reliable anchors. A debate-and-judge vision-language agent selects reliable temporal anchors under weak supervision, which are propagated bidirectionally with SAM2 to refine unreliable or unlabeled frames. Finally, ARTEMIS trains the segmenter using temporal reliability-aware robust learning, incorporating reliability-guided reference selection, a Reference Prototype Transport Module, and reliability-aware robust loss. These components assess mask reliability, evolve anchors over time, transport target identity across frames, and down-weight noisy supervision instead of discarding difficult samples. Experiments on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 under scribble, point, and limited-label settings demonstrate that ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code will be released at https://github.com/wangtong627/ARTEMIS.

</details>


### [67] [Evaluating and Enhancing Negation Comprehension in Remote Sensing MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20177)
*Haochen Han,Jue Wang,Alex Jinpeng Wang,Fangming Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RS-Neg: a benchmark for negation understanding in multimodal RS LLMs across region- to scene-level tasks; introduces NeFo test-time learning; leverages ~5% unlabeled data; aims to close negation gap; code/data to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Negation understanding is underexplored in RS MLLMs, leading to hallucinations and unreliable decisions in critical tasks; real-world scenarios require identifying what is false or absent.

Method: Data generation with LLMs to synthesize diverse negation queries from RS imagery; dynamic visual focus module for verification; NeFo: test-time learning optimizing negation's logical role using small unlabeled sample; evaluation on RS MLLMs.

Result: RS MLLMs struggle with negation; hallucinations; performance drop; NeFo significantly improves negation understanding and generalizes to unseen tasks with ~5% unlabeled data.

Conclusion: RS-Neg provides a first benchmark for negation in RS MLLMs; NeFo offers an effective adaptation method; code/data release planned; potential impact for emergency response and other RS applications.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable success in various Remote Sensing (RS) tasks. However, their ability to comprehend negation remains underexplored, limiting deployment in real-world applications where models must explicitly identify what is false or absent, e.g., emergency responders need to locate non-flooded routes for evacuation. To comprehensively study this limitation, we introduce RS-Neg, the first benchmark to evaluate negation understanding across region-level to scene-level tasks. Specifically, we design an automated data generation pipeline for RS imagery, using LLMs to synthesize diverse negation queries, and introduce a dynamic visual focus module for verification. Our evaluation reveals that advanced RS MLLMs struggle with negation, exhibiting hallucinations and substantial performance degradation. To close this gap, we propose NeFo, a novel test-time learning method that explicitly incorporates the logical role of negation into the model optimization. Remarkably, using about 5\% unlabeled test samples, NeFo significantly improves the negation understanding of models and shows strong generalization to unseen tasks. Code and data will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [68] [HilDA: Hierarchical Distillation with Diffusion for Advancing Self-Supervised LiDAR Pre-trainin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20189)
*Maciej Wozniak,Jesper Ericsson,Hariprasath Govindarajan,Truls Nyberg,Thomas Gustafsson,Patric Jensfelt,Olov Andersson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HilDA is a self-supervised pretraining framework for LiDAR backbones that uses hierarchical distillation (multi-layer) and global context distillation, plus a temporal occupancy diffusion objective to enforce spatiotemporal consistency, achieving state-of-the-art results in cross-modal distillation and improved downstream 3D tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the data scarcity and the limitations of using Vision Foundation Models as black-box teachers. Existing methods rely on frame-wise feature similarity and fail to leverage layer-wise semantic structure and temporal information present in LiDAR sequences.

Method: HilDA introduces hierarchical distillation with multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment and global context distillation for scene-level semantics, combined with a temporal occupancy diffusion objective that enforces spatiotemporal consistency in LiDAR representations; self-supervised pretraining for LiDAR backbones aimed at driving tasks.

Result: Pretraining with HilDA yields state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks and surpasses prior distillation methods on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction.

Conclusion: HilDA effectively leverages hierarchical semantic structure and temporal information to produce richer LiDAR representations, improving cross-modal distillation and key downstream driving tasks; code is available.

Abstract: Leveraging Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) for camera-to-LiDAR knowledge distillation offers a promising solution to the scarcity of annotated data needed to represent the immense geometric and kinematic diversity of real-world autonomous driving (AD). However, current approaches typically treat VFMs as black-box teachers, relying exclusively on frame-wise feature similarity. Consequently, they do not fully exploit the teacher's layer-wise semantic structure and global context, as well as the rich spatiotemporal information inherent in LiDAR sequences. We propose HilDA, a self-supervised pretraining framework for LiDAR backbones that better captures the semantic what and geometric where needed for driving tasks. HilDA combines hierarchical distillation comprising multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment and global context distillation for scene-level semantics, with a temporal occupancy diffusion objective promoting spatiotemporal consistency. Models pre-trained with HilDA achieve state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks and outperform models trained via prior distillation approaches on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction. Code available at: https://maxiuw.github.io/hilda.

</details>


### [69] [Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long: Exploring Dataset Distillation for Continual Test-Time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20196)
*Hyun-Kurl Jang,Jihun Kim,Hyeokjun Kweon,Kuk-Jin Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DO-ALL distills the source distribution into a small set of anchors and uses them during continual test-time adaptation to provide stable, privacy-preserving source information, improving long-term robustness without retaining raw data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy constraints and instability in source-free CTTA under long-term distribution shifts; avoid compounding self-training errors and catastrophic forgetting.

Method: Precompute distilled anchors via Dataset Distillation. During deployment, match each target sample to its semantically closest anchor; use this anchor for source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization within existing CTTA frameworks.

Result: Reported as improved long-term robustness on CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and CCC benchmark across multiple CTTA baselines; code released.

Conclusion: Dataset Distillation enables stable, continual adaptation without raw source data, and DO-ALL serves as a plug-in to existing CTTA methods.

Abstract: Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) aims to maintain model performance under evolving target domains by adapting online without labeled data. However, practical deployments often cannot retain the source dataset due to privacy or licensing constraints, and purely source-free CTTA methods tend to become unstable under long-term distribution shift, suffering from compounding self-training errors and catastrophic forgetting. We introduce DO-ALL (Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long), a plug-and-play framework that revisits source information in a compact and privacy-conscious form via Dataset Distillation (DD). Before deployment, DO-ALL performs DD to produce a small set of synthetic distilled anchors that summarize the source distribution. During adaptation, each target sample is matched with its most semantically aligned anchor, which provides a stable reference for various CTTA via source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization. DO-ALL can be seamlessly integrated into existing CTTA algorithms, consistently improving long-term robustness across CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and the CCC benchmark. This demonstrates the potential of leveraging DD to enable stable and continuous adaptation without retaining raw source data. The code is available at https://github.com/blue-531/DOALL.

</details>


### [70] [Evaluation of Image Matching for Art Skills Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20199)
*Asaad Alghamdi,Michael Poor,Trung-Nghia Le,Tam V. Nguyen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes measuring drawing skill by comparing a hand-drawn image to its reference template using computer vision; compares SIFT-based keypoint matching and a Siamese network, finding SIFT more effective for skill detection and demonstrating feasibility of automated art-skill assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for objective, scalable assessment of drawing skill beyond subjective judgment; existing methods are complex, and computer vision can enable human-like comparison.

Method: Implement and analyze two approaches for image similarity: (1) SIFT feature-based keypoint matching between the drawing and the template, and (2) a Siamese network trained to measure similarity between the two images.

Result: SIFT-based keypoint matching provides a more effective signal for detecting drawing skills than the Siamese network; overall, the study demonstrates feasibility of automated skill assessment through image similarity analysis.

Conclusion: Computer-vision-based image similarity can be used to assess drawing skill, with SIFT features offering superior performance for this task compared to a Siamese network; suggests potential for automated, objective evaluation of art ability.

Abstract: While some individuals possess a natural talent for drawing, mastering this skill requires dedicated training and practice. Determining one's skill in the art of drawing requires proper comprehensive assessment. In this paper, we propose a method to measure drawing skill by by matching the hand-drawn image with the original template. Existing techniques often involve complex processes. However, advancements in computer vision allow us to train computers to perform these comparisons at a human-like level, thereby resolving the tedious and overwhelming traditional process. Using computer vision applications, determining image similarity involves identifying the level of similarities in an image with a reference image. We have implemented and analyzed the SIFT feature and Siamese network to measure image similarity. Our results indicate that it is feasible to assess art skill levels. Through feature analysis, we found that SIFT-based key point matching provides a more effective means of detecting drawing skills.

</details>


### [71] [DeepForestVisionV2: Ecology-Driven Taxonomy Expansion for Camera-Trap Monitoring in African Tropical Forests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20223)
*Hugo Magaldi,Theau d'Audiffret,Etienne Francois Akomo-Okoue,Bala Amarasekaran,Naomi Anderson,Claire Auger,Noemie Cappelle,Daniel Cornelis,Raphael Cornette,Tobias Deschner,Gabriel Dubus,Davy Fonteyn,Rosa M. Garriga,Jennifer Hatlauf,Innocent Kasekendi,Raymond Katumba,Aram Kazandjian,Alfred Ngomanda,Stephan Ntie,Simone Pika,Xavier Rufray,Harold Rugonge,John Justice Tibesigwa,Peter van Lunteren,Hadrien Vanthomme,Joeri A. Zwerts,Sabrina Krief*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DeepForestVisionV2 expands DeepForestVision from 35 to 64 classes (61 animals + human/vehicle/blank) for African tropical forests, addressing vertical stratification, openness, and anthropogenic interfaces, while preserving offline workflow and improving taxonomic resolution and robustness across sites.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome coarse 35-class predictions that fail when encountering arboreal species, birds, semi-aquatic taxa, and human-associated confounders in open and edge habitats, by extending to a richer 64-class taxonomy and new deployment gradients.

Method: Developed an expanded classifier trained on 1,535,010 photos and 243,354 videos from multi-country African forests. Maintains the original offline workflow. Evaluation uses a cross-country cropped-photo validation set and three held-out Uganda video benchmarks spanning vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces.

Result: Validation metrics: 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, 0.81 balanced accuracy. Deployment benchmarks show maintained or improved accuracy, taxa identified increasing from 22 to 29 in forest interiors and 4 to 9 at riverbanks; park-edge accuracy rises from 0.62 to 0.86 and false alarms drop from 11 to 0.

Conclusion: DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility and robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings while enabling finer taxonomic resolution for Africa's open and edge habitats.

Abstract: Camera-trap monitoring in African tropical forests increasingly extends beyond closed-canopy interiors to riverbanks, clearings, and park edges. Among available open tools for African forest camera-trap classification, DeepForestVision is the only one providing a matched offline workflow for both photographs and videos, and previous work showed that it outperformed other available baselines on a comparable benchmark. However, it was designed for closed-canopy, ground-level forest interiors and uses a 35-class prediction space that becomes too coarse when deployments encounter arboreal primates, birds, semi-aquatic taxa, or human-associated confounders such as livestock. We present DeepForestVisionV2, an ecology-driven expansion from 35 to 64 prediction classes (61 animal classes plus human, vehicle, and blank) designed to address three recurrent deployment gradients: vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces. DeepForestVisionV2 retains the same offline workflow and is trained on 1,535,010 photographs and 243,354 videos from multi-country African tropical-forest projects. Evaluation combines a cross-country cropped-photo validation set, used to assess robustness across sites and camera-trap settings, with three held-out Uganda video benchmarks spanning the targeted gradients. On the validation set, DeepForestVisionV2 reaches 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, and 0.81 balanced accuracy. On the deployment benchmarks, it preserves or improves baseline accuracy despite its harder classification task, while increasing the number of identified taxa from 22 to 29 in forest-interior videos and from 4 to 9 at riverbanks. In the park-edge use case, it raises accuracy from 0.62 to 0.86 and reduces false alarms from 11 to 0. These results show that DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility while preserving robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings.

</details>


### [72] [Cinematic Compositing Using Character-Environment-Harmonized Video Generation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20233)
*Tianyi Xiang,Mingming He,Li Ma,Jing Liao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based cinematic compositing method that jointly models character-to-environment and environment-to-character interactions, using a tri-mask RGB-D denoising architecture, data-driven relighting pair curation, and reference-conditioned control for prop replacement and scene synthesis; reports state-of-the-art cinema-quality results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of prior green-screen compositing approaches that treat lighting and physical interaction unidirectionally, failing to capture bidirectional C2E/E2C effects and interactive props in dynamic scenes.

Method: An end-to-end video diffusion framework that (1) jointly models C2E and E2C interactions; (2) uses a tri-mask-guided architecture with RGB-D joint denoising to enforce physical interaction constraints among character, props, and environment; (3) builds a data-curation pipeline using priors to generate high-quality relighting pairs without expensive rendering; (4) employs a reference-conditioned mechanism for controllable environment synthesis and precise prop replacement.

Result: Extensive experiments show the proposed framework significantly outperforms existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.

Conclusion: The framework enables physically consistent, controllable, and high-fidelity dynamic video compositing by integrating bidirectional lighting and interaction cues, and by efficiently producing relighting-ready data and controllable scene synthesis.

Abstract: Cinematic compositing aims to integrate green-screen characters into novel environments while maintaining physical and photometric realism. Previous methods often fail to capture the complex bidirectional interactions between characters and their surroundings, which we characterize as Character-to-Environment (C2E) physical interaction and Environment-to-Character (E2C) lighting harmonization. To address this, we propose an end-to-end video diffusion framework that jointly models C2E and E2C interactions, specifically handling the challenges of interactive props. Our approach introduces a tri-mask-guided architecture with RGB-D joint denoising to ensure physically consistent interactions among the character, props, and environment. We further develop an efficient prior-driven data curation pipeline to construct high-quality relighting pairs without expensive rendering. Finally, a reference-conditioned mechanism enables controllable environment synthesis and precise prop replacement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.

</details>


### [73] [BAFIS: Dataset + Framework to assess occupational Bias and Human Preference in modern Text-to-image Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20241)
*Thomas Klassert,Adrian Ulges,Biying Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Five text-to-image models (Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, FLUX.1-dev) exhibit systematic gender/ethnicity biases in occupation-related image generation. The study introduces BAFIS, a human-feedback platform, and a multilingual prompt dataset (21,140 images) to assess bias, image quality, and prompt alignment, revealing partial correlations between traditional metrics and subjective ratings and highlighting the need to integrate human preferences for fairer models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address biases in generative image models, including inherent and language-induced biases, particularly in occupation contexts; extend evaluation beyond automatic metrics by incorporating human preferences; contextualize findings with real-world employment statistics.

Method: Develop BAFIS to collect human bias feedback on generated images; create a multilingual prompt-based dataset (21,140 synthetic images); evaluate five models on gender and ethnicity bias, image quality, and prompt alignment; compare results with official employment statistics.

Result: Systematic biases detected across models; traditional evaluation metrics show partial correlation with subjective human judgments; a large multilingual synthetic image dataset and a human-feedback platform enable bias analysis; results advocate for incorporating human preferences to build fairer models.

Conclusion: Incorporating human feedback is essential for reducing bias and improving fairness in text-to-image generation; biases are pervasive across models and language prompts, and aligned evaluation should blend objective metrics with subjective human preferences, possibly informed by societal statistics.

Abstract: Generative artificial intelligence has the potential to improve productivity and transform the production of creative content. However, existing research indicates that image generation models are significantly influenced by biases. This work investigates the inherent biases and language-induced biases present in text-to-image models within the context of occupation-related image generation, complementing established metrics with human preference feedback. We present a comprehensive evaluation of five current text-to-image models: Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, and FLUX.1-dev , focusing on gender and ethnicity bias, image quality, and prompt alignment. To facilitate this evaluation, we developed the "Battle-Arena for Fair Image Synthesis" (BAFIS), a platform designed to collect human feedback on bias in generated images. Furthermore, we created a dataset comprising 21,140 synthetic images generated using multilingual prompts, which serves as a basis for our analysis. We further place our results within a broader social context by comparing them to official statistics from the German Federal Employment Agency. Our findings reveal systematic biases in text-to-image models, with established evaluation metrics in partial correlation with subjective user ratings. Thus, our research emphasizes the need for including human preferences to develop fairer and more inclusive text-to-image models.

</details>


### [74] [SPOT-E: Test-Time Entropy Shaping with Visual Spotlights for Frozen VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20244)
*Bo Yin,Xiaobin Hu,Chengming Xu,Ruolin Shen,Mo Yang,Jiangning Zhang,Peng-Tao Jiang,Cheng Tan,Shuicheng YAN*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPOT-E is a plug-and-play test-time spotlighting method using entropy-shaping anchors to improve evidence readout and robustness in vision–language models without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evidence-intensive tasks reveal that decisive visual cues are small and easily overlooked. Existing inference-time interventions are open-loop and lack a mechanism to verify that highlighted evidence is actually utilized by the model.

Method: The authors treat answer-span prediction entropy as a model-internal feedback signal but show naive entropy minimization is ambiguous. They propose low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective to reduce answer uncertainty while preserving confident tokens. SPOT-E implements question-conditioned spotlights, optimized per instance via lightweight tuning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

Result: SPOT-E yields consistent gains across benchmarks and VLM families and improves robustness under visual corruptions.

Conclusion: Entropy shaping with anchor-based low-entropy prompts resolves ambiguity in entropy-based feedback and enhances evidence grounding at test time. SPOT-E demonstrates strong, class-agnostic gains across models, with publicly available code.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) often underperform on evidence intensive tasks because decisive visual evidence are small, localized, and easy to overlook, leading to failures in evidence readout even when high-level reasoning is intact. Prior inference-time visual interventions can improve grounding without retraining, but they are largely open-loop and lack a mechanism to verify whether highlighted evidence is actually used. We study answer-span prediction entropy as a model-internal feedback signal and show that naive entropy minimization is ambiguous, since low entropy may arise from evidence-grounded confidence or shortcut collapse. To resolve this ambiguity, we introduce low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective that reduces answer uncertainty while preserving baseline high-confidence tokens. We instantiate this principle in SPOT-E, a plug-and-play test-time method that produces question-conditioned spotlights, optimized per instance via light-weight tuning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Across all benchmarks and different VLM families, SPOT-E yields consistent gains and improved robustness under visual corruptions. Code is publicly available at: \url{https://github.com/YinBo0927/SPOT-E}

</details>


### [75] [Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification for Weakly Supervised Histopathology Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20250)
*Duc T. Nguyen,Hoang-Long Nguyen,Thanh-Ha DO,Huy-Hieu Pham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification (SSHR) for weakly supervised semantic segmentation in histopathology, integrating feature rectification into the forward pass via Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses core limitations of multi-stage WSSS in computational pathology: high training cost and error propagation from shallow CNN texture biases leading to false positives; aims to produce high-fidelity activation maps within a single training loop.

Method: Introduces HFRM that leverages deep global semantic context to filter local anomalies in shallow layers, purifying intermediate features during the forward pass to yield accurate CAMs without post-hoc refinement; single-stage training loop; evaluated on LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets; code released.

Result: SSHR outperforms state-of-the-art multi-stage WSSS methods on LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets and reduces training duration by 2–5x, suggesting improved efficiency and accuracy with single-stage rectification.

Conclusion: SSHR offers a simple yet effective reformulation for WSSS in computational pathology, enabling faster clinical translation for large-scale histopathology workflows; potential for broader applicability and future work on generalization and ablation studies.

Abstract: Existing weakly supervised semantic segmentation (WSSS) methods in computational pathology rely on a multi-stage paradigm: class activation map (CAM) generation, offline pseudo-mask refinement, and fully supervised retraining. While established, this decoupled approach presents fundamental limitations. The multi-stage process not only incurs high computational training costs but also suffers from error propagation: local texture biases in shallow CNN layers generate false-positive artifacts that subsequent refinement steps often fail to correct. To address these persistent challenges through a simple yet highly effective approach, we propose the Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification (SSHR) framework. Rather than passively refining CAMs post-hoc, our method proactively purifies intermediate feature representations during the forward pass. We introduce a Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) that utilizes deep global semantic context to filter out local anomalies in shallow layers. This mechanism generates high-fidelity activation maps directly within a single training loop. Experiments on the LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets demonstrate that SSHR outperforms state-of-the-art multi-stage methods. Furthermore, SSHR reduces training duration by 2 to 5 times. This efficiency minimizes computational overhead and accelerates clinical translation for large-scale histopathology workflows. The code is available at: https://github.com/trongduc-nguyen/SSHR

</details>


### [76] [U$^2$Mamba: A Two-level Nested U-structure Mamba for Salient Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20282)
*Junhui Li,Jialu Li,Youshan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces U^2Mamba, a U-structured SOD network that uses multiscale Mamba U-blocks (MMUBs) within a nested U-structure to capture multi-scale context, with hierarchical per-level supervision, achieving competitive results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in existing SOD models: insufficient modeling of contextual information and network depth, and the need to integrate multi-scale information across shallow and deep layers without loss of resolution.

Method: Proposes the U^2Mamba architecture with nested U-structures built from multiscale Mamba U-blocks (MMUBs); introduces hierarchical training supervision with loss computed at each level rather than only at the top level.

Result: Extensive experiments show highly competitive performance against state-of-the-art SOD methods; source code is provided at the specified GitHub repository.

Conclusion: The proposed nested U-structure with MMUBs and per-level supervision effectively enhances multi-scale contextual modeling for salient object detection, offering a competitive and scalable alternative to existing methods.
