Existing approaches either process videos densely with large vision-language models (VLMs), incurring prohibitive computational cost, or rely on sparse caption-based reasoning, which often misses temporally localized and motion-centric evidence. We introduce TimeProVe, a cost-efficient hybrid framework for temporally grounded reasoning in long videos. TimeProVe first employs lightweight modules to generate action-grounded answer--evidence hypotheses and subsequently invokes an expensive VLM only for targeted verification. The core of our framework lies in the Action-based Candidate Evidence (ACE) module, which converts temporally localized actions into query-conditioned candidate answers and supporting evidence windows through lightweight LLM reasoning. We further introduce OpenTSUBench (OTB), an open-ended benchmark designed to evaluate temporally grounded reasoning in real-world Activities of Daily Living (ADL) scenarios. Experiments show that TimeProVe outperforms the strongest baseline on OTB by 7.3%, while reducing VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%. Furthermore, without explicit temporal grounding training, TimeProVe achieves competitive performance on Charades-STA, and reaches state-of-the-art results when enhanced with grounding VLMs.

</details>


### [101] [JanusMesh: Fast and Zero-Shot 3D Visual Illusion Generation via Cross-Space Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20563)
*Siang-Ling Zhang,Huai-Hsun Cheng,Tsung-Ju Yang,Yu-Lun Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A fast, training-free framework for text-driven 3D visual illusions that produces dual-semantic 3D meshes via a two-stage pipeline: (1) cross-space dual-branch denoising to align geometry (CLIP) and blend SDFs for seamless fusion; (2) view-conditioned texture synthesis projecting 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry. It delivers realistic results in 3–5 minutes and outperforms prior methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional optimization-based methods are slow and prone to oversaturated colors, while naive stitching yields visible seams and semantic leaks. A fast, training-free approach could enable practical, real-time-like generation of 3D illusions with coherent geometry and multi-view semantics.

Method: The method decouples generation into two stages. Stage 1 uses a cross-space dual-branch denoising process that decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and Signed Distance Function (SDF) blending, enabling seamless geometric fusion. Stage 2 employs a view-conditioned texture synthesis module that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry to produce consistent textures across views.

Result: Empirical results claim highly realistic dual-semantic 3D illusions generated in 3–5 minutes. The method reportedly achieves superior geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency compared to existing methods, with a project page provided for reproduction.

Conclusion: A training-free, two-stage framework delivers fast, coherent dual-semantic 3D illusions by integrating CLIP-guided geometry alignment and SDF blending with view-conditioned texture synthesis; suitable for rapid creation of multi-view semantic 3D content.

Abstract: Creating 3D visual illusions, a single 3D mesh that reveals entirely different semantics from various viewing angles, is a fascinating but tough challenge. Existing optimization-based methods are slow and can produce oversaturated colors. In contrast, naive stitching approaches fail to produce geometrically coherent objects. This results in visible unnatural seams and semantic leaks. In this paper, we present a fast and training-free framework for generating text-driven 3D visual illusions. Our approach decouples the generation into two stages. First, we propose a cross-space dual-branch denoising process. This process dynamically decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and Signed Distance Field (SDF) blending, which ensures seamless geometric fusion. Second, we introduce a view-conditioned texture synthesis module that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method generates highly realistic, dual-semantic 3D illusions in just 3-5 minutes. It significantly outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency. Project page: https://siang1105.github.io/JanusMesh.github.io/

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [102] [Deontic Policies for Runtime Governance of Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19464)
*Anupam Joshi,Tim Finin,Karuna Pande Joshi,Lalana Kagal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A deontic, agent-centric governance framework (AgenticRei) for LLM-driven agents, providing obligations, dispensations, conflict resolution, and ontological reasoning via an OWL-based policy language evaluated by an external logic engine, enabling governance of tool invocations and agent messaging; integrates with A2AS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current policy engines cover permits/prohibits only; need full governance across tool use, data handling, cross-domain agents, and domain hierarchies; enforce obligations and dispensations.

Method: Design Rei-based deontic policy language expressed in OWL; runtime evaluation by external logic engine; apply to both tool invocations and agent messages; demonstrate with examples; ecosystem compatibility with A2AS.

Result: Demonstrate that deontic policies capture governance constraints beyond current engines; provide examples; show natural composition with industry standards; empirical evaluation not specified in abstract.

Conclusion: AgenticRei extends policy enforcement capabilities to obligations, dispensations, meta-policy conflict resolution, and domain reasoning, complementing permit/prohibit; potential for enterprise adoption.

Abstract: Autonomous agentic AI systems driven by Large Language Models (LLMs) introduce a new class of security, privacy, and compliance challenges: an agent that can invoke tools, manipulate data, install software, and coordinate with peer agents across organizational boundaries must be constrained not just by authentication and access control, but by the full structure of enterprise governance. This includes specifying what agents are permitted and prohibited from doing, what they areobliged to do after certain actions (e.g., notify the CISO), under what conditions a standing obligation may be waived, and which rules take precedence when policies conflict. This governance problem exceeds what current policy engines provide. Systems such as XACML, Rego, and Cedar address only the permit/prohibit subset of this governance structure. They do not provide obligation lifecycle management, meta-policy conflict resolution, dispensations that waive obligations in specific circumstances, and ontological reasoning over domain class hierarchies commonly found in applications such as healthcare, cybersecurity, or data privacy. We propose AgenticRei, which realizes key governance requirements such as obligations, dispensations, policy conflict resolutions, and reasoning over policies, as well as the basic permit/prohibit constraints. We use a deontic policy language built on the Rei framework, expressed as OWL (Web Ontology Language) and evaluated at runtime by a high-performance logic engine entirely outside the LLM. The same pipeline governs both tool invocations by the agent and agent-to-agent messages. We show through examples that deontic policies capture governance constraints around security and privacy that mostly cannot be expressed in current production engines. Our approach composes naturally with industry-standard frameworks like A2AS.

</details>


### [103] [Measuring Curriculum Alignment across Topical Coverage, Competency, and Cognitive Depth: A Longitudinal Framework Applied to CS2013 and CS2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19469)
*Sherzod Turaev,Mary John,Saja Aldabet,Mamoun Awad,Nazar Zaki,Khaled Shuaib*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A human-in-the-loop pipeline measures CS program coverage of external guidelines (CS2013/CS2023) longitudinally; finds ~50% coverage, with gap in depth due to guideline evolution; retriever ensemble improves matching; instrument is reusable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a reliable, reproducible measure of how closely an undergraduate CS program aligns with evolving international curricular guidelines and how coverage shifts when guidelines are restructured.

Method: Represent program and guidelines as structured corpora; generate candidate course-to-knowledge-unit matches via semantic retrieval; confirm matches through human judgment under an explicit coverage definition (human-in-the-loop). Evaluate seven retrievers; select reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble; compare models of varying context; measure inter-rater reliability with Cohen's kappa; apply longitudinally to CS2013 and CS2023 for one accredited BSc in CS.

Result: The program covers 49.7% of CS2023 units and 50.9% of CS2013 units; coverage is stable over a decade. Competency articulation is achieved for ~88% of covered units under each guideline, but depth meets recommendations for 76% (CS2023) vs 95% (CS2013) of present units. Seven retrievers tested; the reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble performed best; a reputed long-context model underperformed a small sentence model. Inter-rater reliability: Cohen’s kappa 0.64 (CS2023), 0.69 (CS2013). Persistent structural gaps identified (parallel/distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, systems fundamentals), separate from guideline evolution effects.

Conclusion: The designed instrument is reusable and available on request; it provides a structured, reproducible method to monitor program alignment with evolving curricula and diagnose where and why gaps persist.

Abstract: Undergraduate computer science is governed by international curricular guidelines revised about once a decade, yet programs lack a reliable, reproducible way to measure how completely they cover the current guidelines and how that coverage shifts when the guidelines are restructured. We address this with a human-in-the-loop pipeline that measures a program's coverage of an external body of knowledge, applied longitudinally to one accredited BSc in Computer Science against Computer Science Curricula 2013 (CS2013) and 2023 (CS2023). The pipeline represents the program and each guideline as structured corpora, generates candidate course-to-knowledge-unit matches by semantic retrieval, and confirms them through human judgment under an explicit coverage definition. Of seven benchmarked retrievers, a reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble was strongest, and a reputed long-context model underperformed a small sentence model, so retriever choice must be measured. Both maps were validated by an independent second rater (Cohen's kappa 0.64 for CS2023, 0.69 for CS2013). The program covers 49.7% of CS2023 and 50.9% of CS2013 knowledge units, near-constant across a decade. Extending the same retrieve-then-confirm design to competency articulation and cognitive depth shows that the program articulates the competency for ~88% of covered units under each guideline, yet delivers it at the recommended depth for 76% of present units under CS2023 against 95% under CS2013, a gap reflecting the newer guideline's raised expectations, not the program. The longitudinal comparison separates persistent structural gaps (parallel and distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, systems fundamentals), uncovered against both guidelines and ABET, from differences that reflect the standard's evolution. The instrument is reusable and available from the authors on request.

</details>


### [104] [Diffusion Language Models: An Experimental Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19475)
*Thomas Bertolani,Davide Bucciarelli,Leonardo Zini,Marcella Cornia,Lorenzo Baraldi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Systematic experimental analysis of diffusion language models (DLMs): eight SOTA DLMs evaluated across eight benchmarks for quality and efficiency; analyzes inference-time factors; shows design choices drive trade-offs; provides deployment guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address heterogeneous evaluation practices across DLMs and to understand the trade-offs between generation quality and computational efficiency under realistic inference settings.

Method: Empirical study evaluating eight state-of-the-art DLMs on eight benchmarks spanning reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving; measures include generation quality and computation; analyzes inference-time factors (denoising steps, context length, block size, parallel unmasking); includes controlled comparisons of smaller models trained under identical conditions.

Result: DLM behavior is strongly shaped by generation-time design choices, producing distinct performance-efficiency trade-offs; provides practical insights for deployment.

Conclusion: Offers a comprehensive, deployment-oriented assessment of contemporary DLMs, clarifying capabilities and limitations and informing model/configuration choices under various budgets.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized language modeling through autoregressive generation, enabling strong performance across a wide range of tasks. Recently, Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have emerged as an alternative paradigm that generates text through iterative denoising rather than next-token prediction, allowing parallel refinement of entire sequences. While numerous diffusion-based architectures have been proposed, differences in evaluation protocols, datasets, inference budgets, and generation hyperparameters make it difficult to compare their capabilities and understand the trade-offs they offer. In this work, we present a systematic experimental analysis of modern DLMs. Specifically, we evaluate eight state-of-the-art DLMs across eight benchmarks spanning reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving, while explicitly considering both generation quality and computational efficiency. Beyond downstream evaluation, we analyze the impact of key inference-time factors, including denoising steps, context length, block size, and parallel unmasking strategies, and complement large-scale experiments with controlled comparisons of smaller models trained under identical conditions. Our analysis highlights the strengths and limitations of diffusion-based language modeling across different tasks, architectures, and inference budgets. We show that the behavior of DLMs is strongly influenced by generation-time design choices, leading to distinct trade-offs between performance and computational efficiency. Overall, our study provides practical insights into the capabilities and deployment characteristics of contemporary DLMs.

</details>


### [105] [Hidden Anchors in Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19494)
*Apurba Pokharel,Ram Dantu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Deliberation among agents with hidden internal anchors can be modeled as a closed-loop system; anchors can be inferred from deliberation data and explain hull-crossing behaviors and generalization dynamics across model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain why multi-agent LLM deliberation improves reasoning and under what conditions internal beliefs drive outcomes beyond classical opinion dynamics.

Method: Formulates a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a latent anchor. Recovers the anchor from deliberation data, tests predictive power on held-out runs, and analyzes across three open-weight model families to classify anchor influence as a spectrum.

Result: Anchors can be recovered from deliberation; agents’ confidence in the correct answer can exceed the initial convex hull. The recovered anchors predict held-out runs. Across three model families, anchors are similarly influential but differ in anchor location; hull escape occurs only when anchors sit far from initial opinions, necessitating the full closed-loop model.

Conclusion: Deliberation dynamics are anchored by latent internal beliefs; the necessity of a closed-loop model depends on anchor placement. A simple anchor-based diagnostic can assess when deliberation is truly anchor-driven, revealing a spectrum rather than a binary effect.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM deliberation, where agents exchange and revise answers over several rounds, is increasingly used to improve reasoning and accuracy, yet how and why it works is rarely modelled. Such deliberation mirrors how humans reach decisions. As social animals we are pulled both by the group, the herd effect that classical opinion-dynamics models such as DeGroot and Friedkin--Johnsen capture, and by our own internal belief, which they do not. We model multi-agent deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system in which each agent carries a hidden internal belief, its anchor, that continually pulls its opinion regardless of its neighbours. We show this anchor can be recovered from the deliberation alone, and that it explains a behaviour classical consensus rules forbid: an agent's confidence in the correct answer can climb past where any agent started, escaping the space (convexhull) formed by the initial beliefs. Checking whether the recovered anchor also predicts held-out runs (generalizes) gives a simple test for when a model is truly driven bysuch an anchor. Across three open-weight model families this is a spectrum, not all-or-nothing. All anchors' influence are about equally strongly, but they differ in where the anchor sits, and only when it sits far from the initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull and need the full closed-loop model.

</details>


### [106] [DeXposure-Claw: An Agentic System for DeFi Risk Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19501)
*Aijie Shu,Bowei Chen,Wenbin Wu,Cathy Yi-Hsuan Chen,Fengxiang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes DeXposure-Claw, a regulator-oriented, forecast-grounded supervision system for DeFi combining forecasted exposure networks with structured, auditable decision tickets; evaluated via DeXposure-Bench on five years of weekly data; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose LLMs struggle to provide regulator-aligned, low-false-alarm interventions in fast-moving, networked DeFi credit risk. A system that grounds LLM decisions in structured evidence and auditable rationales is needed.

Method: Three-part pipeline: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model forecasting future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios that convert forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; (3) data-health and confidence gates that constrain escalation; outputs are auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. Evaluation via DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis harness comparing tickets against an absolute-loss ground truth and a regulator-aligned false-intervention rate.

Result: Experiments on five years of weekly real data support the system's efficacy; the authors provide code at the project repository.

Conclusion: DeXposure-Claw presents a viable path to regulator-aligned, auditable supervision in decentralized finance, combining forecasting, structured evidence, and gating to reduce false alarms; the evaluation framework (DeXposure-Bench) provides a principled benchmark and reproducibility.

Abstract: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly: they over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations offer no regulator-aligned way to measure the resulting false alarms. We introduce DeXposure-Claw, a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios then turn those forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; and (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before DeXposure-Claw emits auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. We further develop DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness, whose decision axis scores tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and an explicit false-intervention rate. Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support our system. Code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/DeXposure-Claw.

</details>


### [107] [LLM Doesn't Know What It Doesn't Know: Detecting Epistemic Blind Spots via Cross-Model Attribution Divergence on Clinical Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19509)
*Akshat Dasula,Prasanna Desikan,Jaideep Srivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show epistemic limits on structured data tasks when compared with XGBoost; attribution-divergence and orthogonal signals can reduce epistemic uncertainty and improve calibration without internal access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address whether LLMs can recognize and signal their own knowledge limits on structured clinical data tasks, aiming to reduce epistemic uncertainty through cross-model attribution signals.

Method: Compare Qwen 2.5 7B LLM to XGBoost on a structured prediction task; analyze cross-model attribution divergence; quantify Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS); test few-shot prompts and SHAP-derived feature evidence; develop a cross-model calibrator based on attribution divergence to estimate reliability without model internals.

Result: (1) LLM verbalized confidence is nearly constant and uninformative about accuracy; (2) inverse difficulty effect where LLMs underperform when XGBoost is highly accurate but align when uncertainty is moderate; (3) few-shot and SHAP evidence are orthogonal and yield super-additive gains, reducing ADS from 1.54 to 0.38 and boosting accuracy from 49% to 75.3%; (4) a cross-model calibrator using attribution divergence reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080 without internal access or repeated inferences.

Conclusion: Framing the problem as a cold-start for LLMs on structured data, the work outlines a path to genuine epistemic self-awareness via cross-model attribution signals and reliability calibrators, highlighting the need for external mechanisms to assess LLM reliability on structured tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to structured clinical data, yet whether they can recognize the limits of their own knowledge on such tasks remains unexplored. We study this question through the lens of cross-model attribution divergence with the goal of reducing epistemic uncertainty for structured tasks, comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a prediction task via attribution divergence analysis. We report four findings. First, LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous, it outputs a near-constant (0.856-0.937) regardless of whether accuracy is 49% or 75.3%, tracking prompt format rather than prediction quality. Second, the LLM exhibits an inverse difficulty effect: accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct, but matches XGBoost (73.8% vs. 73.1%) when it is moderately uncertain. Third, few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence are orthogonal, super-additive interventions: they reduce the Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS) from 1.54 to 0.38 and improve accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training. Fourth, a cross-model calibrator that determined LLM reliability using attribution divergence signals reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, replacing uninformative verbalized confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates, without accessing model internals or requiring repeated inference. We frame these findings as a cold start problem for LLMs on structured data and outline a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness.

</details>


### [108] [REVEAL++: Differentiable Phenotypic Grouping for Vision-Language Retinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19522)
*Ethan Elio Meidinger,Seowung Leem,Zeyun Zhao,Ruogu Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Continuous phenotypic supervision in contrastive learning improves cross-modal retinal AD risk prediction over discrete groupings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rigid, discrete phenotypic groupings constrain representation learning and fail to capture gradual disease-risk differences; a learnable, continuous similarity signal could better reflect the spectrum of risk.

Method: Replace hard group assignments with differentiable, intra-modality embedding similarities to construct soft, continuous multi-positive relations. Use a soft-target contrastive objective that jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure end-to-end.

Result: On UK Biobank retinal data for incident AD prediction, the proposed method consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines.

Conclusion: Learning continuous phenotypic similarity provides a principled, robust framework for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

Abstract: The retina offers a noninvasive window into neurodegenerative disease, capturing subtle structural patterns associated with a risk of future cognitive decline. Vision-language alignment frameworks such as REVEAL have shown that pairing retinal fundus images with structured clinical risk narratives improves early prediction of Alzheimer's disease (AD). A key design choice in these approaches is the use of phenotypic grouping, where individuals with similar risk profiles are treated as multi-positive pairs during contrastive learning. However, existing methods operationalize phenotypic similarity as a discrete construct, relying on hard group assignments that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning. We propose a continuous formulation of phenotypic structure within contrastive learning. Rather than assigning samples to fixed clusters, we model inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function derived from intra-modality embedding similarities in both retinal images and risk profiles. These weights define soft multi-positive relationships through a continuous aggregation operator, enabling graded supervision that reflects the spectrum nature of disease risk. We further introduce a soft-target contrastive objective that jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure in an end-to-end manner. Evaluated on UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines. By treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule, our approach provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

</details>


### [109] [Emergent Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19527)
*Martin Kolář*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An online alignment approach for LLMs using a self-conscience step and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) that does not rely on external judges, aiming for emergent alignment across training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate misalignment of LLM outputs with human ethics and enable self-correcting behavior, addressing emergent misalignment without external evaluators.

Method: Introduce a conscience module that reviews its own reasoning and outputs. Extend training loss with a DPO-based alignment term. Use a frozen copy of the model as the judge. Apply online across training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompts, and zero-shot scenarios, guided by a single high-level introspective question.

Result: Empirically demonstrates Emergent Alignment: a single introspective question steers training toward an ethical model in code-hacking scenarios, without requiring weaker/stronger external judges.

Conclusion: Presents a general, online alignment technique for LLMs that leverages self-review and DPO, reducing reliance on external evaluators and enabling broad applicability.

Abstract: Can Large Language Models (LLMs) discern when their own outputs are misaligned with human ethics? And can they self-correct? We endow an LLM with a conscience step that reviews its own reasoning and outputs, and we extend the training loss with an alignment component using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to steer the model away from non-ethical outputs. The result is an online technique to align models in a wide range of applications: training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning. It does not require a weaker or stronger judge, relying instead on a frozen copy of itself. In previous work, the Emergent Misalignment scenario showed a range of emergent unethical behaviors from fine-tuning the model to hack code. Instead, we empirically show how to achieve Emergent Alignment: a single high-level introspective question steers training toward an ethical model under the same code hacking scenario.

</details>


### [110] [ITNet: A Learnable Integral Transform That Subsumes Convolution, Attention, and Recurrence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19538)
*Ashim Dhor,Rasel Mondal,Pin Yu Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified Integral Transform Network (ITNet) unifies convolution, self-attention, and recurrence as a learnable integral transform, achieving competitive performance across diverse tasks via a kernel modeled by an MLP and enhanced by practical optimizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the fragmentation among CNNs, Transformers, and RNNs by introducing a single learnable interaction mechanism that can replicate locality, sequential memory, and content-based pairwise interactions.

Method: Define ITNet with a position- and feature-dependent kernel implemented as an MLP that models pairwise interactions. Show that conv, self-attention, and autoregressive recurrence are special cases under certain parameterizations. Propose practical techniques: tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, learned low-rank factorization. Prove ITNet is a universal approximator of continuous operators. Use shared operator with lightweight modality-specific encoders and evaluate on ImageNet-1K, GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA v2, NLVR2.

Result: A single ITNet architecture matches or exceeds specialized baselines across diverse benchmarks (ImageNet-1K, GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA v2, NLVR2). It can recover the behavior of convolution, self-attention, and autoregressive recurrence from data, highlighting a unified interaction mechanism.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that a single learned interaction operator can reproduce the behavior of the three architectural families, offering a practical and scalable unification of neural network inductive biases through a learnable integral transform.

Abstract: Convolutional networks, recurrent networks, and transformers each encode different inductive biases -- locality, sequential memory, and content-dependent pairwise interaction -- and have remained mathematically distinct since their inception. We show that this fragmentation reflects not a fundamental diversity in how signals should be processed, but rather incomplete views of a single underlying mathematical object: a learnable integral transform. We introduce the Integral Transform Network (ITNet), a unified architecture built around a learnable kernel that depends jointly on positions and features. This kernel is implemented as a small neural network, specifically an MLP, that models pairwise interactions, enabling the model to adapt its behavior from data. We show that convolution, self-attention (including multi-head), and autoregressive recurrence (including LSTM, GRU, S4, and Mamba) arise as special cases under appropriate parameterizations, and that ITNet is a universal approximator of continuous operators. To make this practical, we develop tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization, enabling efficient and scalable computation. A single ITNet architecture with a shared operator and lightweight modality-specific encoders matches or exceeds specialized baselines on ImageNet-1K , GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA\,v2 and NLVR2. The results demonstrate that a single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of all three architectural families from data.

</details>


### [111] [Uncertainty Decomposition for Clarification Seeking in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19559)
*Gregory Matsnev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Prompt-based underspecification-aware decomposition of uncertainty for LLM agents; separates action confidence from request/clarification uncertainty to enable proactive clarification; validated on WebShop and ALFWorld benchmarks across five backbones with significant gains in clarification F1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical uncertainty (aleatoric/epistemic) is insufficient for interactive LLM agents. Deployment constraints (black-box APIs, latency, unlabeled trajectories) preclude logprob-based or training-time uncertainty methods. Need underspecification-aware, decomposed, and communicable uncertainty representations to enable proactive clarification and shared mental models.

Method: A simple prompt-based decomposition that splits action confidence from request uncertainty (u). Introduces two clarification-augmented benchmarks (WebShop-Clarification, ALFWorld-Clarification) with 50% underspecified tasks. Evaluates the proposed method against ReAct+UE and Uncertainty-Aware Memory (UAM) across five LLM backbones (GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-v3.2-exp, GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5-35B, GPT-OSS-120B) on these and standard benchmarks; uses clarification F1 as the metric.

Result: Across five backbones, clarification F1 on ALFWorld-Clarification improves by 73% over ReAct+UE and 36% over UAM. For WebShop-Clarification, gains in clarification F1 on every backbone; for ALFWorld-Clarification, gains on four of five backbones. Indicates the gains generalize across backbones.

Conclusion: A deployment-friendly, prompt-based decomposition of uncertainty yields effective, generalizable signals for proactive clarification, improving fault detection/handling for underspecified tasks across diverse LLMs.

Abstract: Recent position papers argue that the classical aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty framework is insufficient for interactive large language model (LLM) agents and call for underspecification-aware, decomposed, and communicable uncertainty representations that can unlock new agent capabilities such as proactive clarification seeking and shared mental-model building. Practical deployment constraints -- black-box APIs, interactive latency budgets, and the absence of labeled trajectories -- rule out logprob-based, multi-sampling, and training-based methods, leaving prompt-based estimation as the most viable family for surfacing such signals at deployment time. We answer this call with a simple prompt-based decomposition that separates action confidence from request uncertainty (u), enabling the agent to ask for clarification when the task specification is ambiguous. To evaluate it, we introduce two clarification-augmented benchmarks (WebShop-Clarification and ALFWorld-Clarification) in which 50% of tasks are deliberately underspecified, and systematically compare the proposed decomposition against ReAct+UE and Uncertainty-Aware Memory (UAM) across five LLM backbones (GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-v3.2-exp, GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5-35B, GPT-OSS-120B) on these variants together with the standard WebShop, ALFWorld, and REAL benchmarks for fault detection. Averaged across the five backbones, the proposed decomposition improves clarification F1 on ALFWorld-Clarification by 73% over ReAct+UE and by 36% over UAM, and leads clarification F1 on every backbone on WebShop-Clarification and on four of five backbones on ALFWorld-Clarification, indicating that the gains generalize beyond a single LLM.

</details>


### [112] [Analyzing the Narration Gap in LLM-Solver Loops](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19588)
*Zunchen Huang,Songgaojun Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Model the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure; narration remains vulnerable to prompt injection; hardened prompts help but do not eliminate risk; end-to-end robustness to user-visible answer is not achieved.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the neglected narration step in LLM-formal tool pipelines by providing verifiable guarantees for the whole loop and studying security of narration against prompt injection.

Method: Formal modeling of the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure; empirical evaluation on five open-source LLMs under prompt injection; analysis of certificate gating; evaluation of hardened prompts and adaptive attacks.

Result: Certificate gating preserves solver soundness; adversaries can invert verified conclusions across phrasings and channels; hardened prompts reduce injection but do not eliminate it; adaptive attacks remain; robustness does not extend to the final user-facing answer.

Conclusion: End-to-end robustness of LLM-solver pipelines is not achieved; the narration step remains a vulnerability; need improved narration mechanisms and stronger end-to-end verification against adaptive prompt injections.

Abstract: Formal tools such as SAT and SMT solvers are increasingly embedded in language model reasoning pipelines when a safety or security critical question can be formulated in logic. Unlike chain of thought whose steps are sampled from the model distribution without formal guarantee, a solver produces a sound and independently verifiable answer. However, the soundness guarantee can be lost in the interaction between the solver and the model. The hybrid pipeline has three components: formalizing the question, deciding it, and narrating the result. Prior work has studied the formalization and decision, but not narration, which is the step that turns a formal tool's output into the user answer. To fill the narration gap, we first model the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure. We further evaluate five open-sourced models under prompt injection, and we find certificate gating makes the solver verdict sound, while an adversary can invert a verified conclusion across phrasings and channels. We study the mitigation through hardened prompt that reduces injection significantly but cannot eliminate it and still suffers under adaptive attack. Combining the formal analysis and empirical studies, we show in the LLM-solver loop, robustness does not reach to the answer that the user finally reads.

</details>


### [113] [Configurable Clinical Information Extraction with Agentic RAG: What Works, What Breaks, and Why](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19602)
*Osman Alperen Çinar-Koraş,Marie Bauer,Sameh Khattab,Merlin Engelke,Moon Kim,Stephan Settelmeier,Shigeyasu Sugawara,Fabian Freisleben,Felix Nensa,Jens Kleesiek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ACIE is an on-premise agentic RAG system for clinical information extraction over full patient context; it grounds answers to sources; evaluated vs lymphoma registry with 7,326 judgments; 96.5% acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the metadata gap and shortcomings of standard retrieval-augmented generation in heterogeneous clinical data, enabling temporal reasoning, cross-document dependencies, and robust clinician verification.

Method: Deploy ACIE at University Medicine Essen as an on-premise agentic RAG pipeline that reasons over complete patient contexts and grounds every answer in source passages for clinician verification; quantify the metadata gap, trace architectural decisions, and evaluate extractions against an independent retrospective lymphoma registry with nuclear-medicine physicians verifying citations; 7,326 judgments.

Result: Clinicians accepted 96.5% of extractions; per-type acceptance ranges from 80% to 99%.

Conclusion: ACIE demonstrates effective grounding of AI-derived clinical answers to source data, enabling reliable clinician-verified extraction in complex patient contexts; the on-premises, agentic design addresses temporal, cross-document, and metadata gaps intrinsic to RAG in healthcare.

Abstract: Patient contexts span hundreds of heterogeneous documents and thousands of structured data points, yet the document-level metadata that AI systems need for retrieval and triage is absent or incomplete. Standard retrieval-augmented generation fails on this data, mishandling temporal reasoning, cross-document dependencies, and missing metadata. We deploy ACIE (Agentic Clinical Information Extraction) at University Medicine Essen: an on-premise agentic RAG pipeline that reasons over complete patient contexts and grounds every answer in source passages for clinician verification. We quantify the metadata gap, trace the architectural decisions it shaped, and evaluate extraction alongside an independent retrospective lymphoma registry study, in which nuclear-medicine physicians verify every extracted value against its cited sources. Across 7,326 judgments, clinicians accepted 96.5\% of extractions, with per-type acceptance ranging from 80\% to 99\%.

</details>


### [114] [Which Pairs to Compare for LLM Post-Training?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19607)
*Jiangze Han,Vineet Goyal,Will Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frames comparison curation as a sampling-design problem for preference-based post-training, derives bounds for the DPO-trained policy, and offers an optimization criterion for budgeted labeling to select informative pairs; shows improved sample efficiency empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve sample efficiency in preference-based post-training (e.g., DPO) by smartly allocating labeling budget to the most informative comparison pairs from a pool of generated completions.

Method: Formalizes pair-selection as a design problem, develops matching upper/lower bounds on the post-training optimality gap via an information-matrix analysis, and proposes explicit budget-aware design criteria; validates with synthetic experiments and LM post-training benchmarks.

Result: Identifies a design-dependent information matrix that links label allocation to estimation error and policy suboptimality, enabling practical sampling strategies that outperform common heuristics in terms of sample efficiency.

Conclusion: Comparison selection materially impacts downstream policy performance; the proposed budgeted curation framework yields principled, efficient labeling strategies for preference-based post-training.

Abstract: Preference-based post-training has become a central paradigm for aligning language models. A common data-collection strategy is to generate a small set of completions for each prompt and label the resulting comparison pairs. However, human preference labels are often much more expensive than generating additional completions, suggesting a different use of the same labeling budget: generate a larger pool of completions, but label only the most informative comparison pairs. This paper studies which pairs should be compared in preference-based post-training. We formulate comparison curation as a sampling-design problem and evaluate designs by the quality of the final policy under the preference-based post-training objective. We instantiate this framework for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), analyzing how the choice of labeled pairs propagates through DPO training to downstream policy performance. Our main results provide matching upper and lower bounds on the post-training optimality gap of the DPO-trained policy. The bounds show that comparison selection affects downstream performance through a single design-dependent information matrix, which links label allocation to parameter estimation error and policy suboptimality. This yields an explicit optimization criterion for budgeted comparison curation and motivates practical sampling designs for selecting informative pairs from large generated completion pools. Experiments on synthetic settings and language-model post-training benchmarks show that the proposed designs consistently improve sample efficiency over common comparison-selection heuristics.

</details>


### [115] [Toten: Knowledge-Based Ontological Tokenization Of Physical Quantities And Technical Notation In Brazilian Portuguese](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19626)
*Antonio de Sousa Leitão Filho; Allan Kardec Duailibe Barros Filho; Fabrício Saul Lima; Selby Mykael Lima dos Santos; Rejani Bandeira Vieira Sousa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TOTEN introduces ontological tokenization with an engineering ontology (OEE), replacing statistical BPE for semantically faithful handling of physical quantities, units, and symbols. It uses a triple <O, classify, {inst_tau}> with an instantiator to produce self-descriptive structures, and is bolstered by three external oracles (Pint, UCD, RSLP). Demonstrates unit ontological atomicity and superior numerical reconstruction on EngQuant and four Brazilian Portuguese corpora, outperforming eight baselines (notably Quantulum3). Statistical significance and concurrent validity are reported.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by the semantic blind spots of Byte-Pair Encoding with technical content, particularly where structured quantities, units, and symbols are essential. A knowledge-based, declarative ontology approach aims to preserve semantics, enforce dimensional correctness, and enhance cross-linguistic handling in engineering domains.

Method: Formalization of TOTEN as a triple <O, classify, {inst_tau}>. O gathers types, structural principles, composition relations, and preserveable invariants. classify maps raw text into typed regions. inst_tau (instantiator family) yields a self-descriptive, structured representation. Robustness achieved through deterministic coupling with three external oracles: Pint (dimensional analysis), Unicode Character Database (typographic properties), and RSLP (Portuguese morphology). Evaluation uses four properties verifiable by construction: ontological atomicity, dimensional equivalence, typographic robustness, numerical reconstruction. Internal EngQuant benchmark (N=800) and four PT corpora (N=1771 eligible). Eight baselines including Quantulum3 for comparison. McNemar with Holm correction for significance; Spearman correlation for concurrent validity; dimensional equivalence tested against Pint.

Result: TOTEN achieves unit ontological atomicity across all contrasts. Numerical reconstruction on external corpora ranges from 0.775 to 0.904, outperforming the best baseline (0.627–0.703; Quantulum3). On EngQuant, TOTEN yields 0.780 versus 0.340 for the baseline. Differences are statistically significant (McNemar with Holm correction). Spearman correlation between internal and external rankings supports concurrent validity of the internal benchmark. Dimensional equivalence shows parity with Pint, the dimensional authority oracle.

Conclusion: A knowledge-based ontological tokenization framework improves semantic fidelity for engineering texts by preserving structural quantities and units, enabling robust numerical reconstruction and dimensional correctness. Demonstrates strong cross-language applicability (Portuguese) with robustness to typography and morphology. Future work could broaden the ontology, enhance instantiation strategies, and scale to additional domains and languages.

Abstract: Byte-Pair Encoding tokenization is statistically efficient for vocabulary compression, but semantically blind to structured technical entities, fragmenting physical quantities, numbers, units, and symbolic expressions into lexically arbitrary subwords. We present TOTEN, a knowledge-based ontological tokenization framework that replaces statistical derivation with declarative classification grounded in a formal ontology of engineering entities (OEE). We formalize TOTEN as the triple <O, classify, {inst_tau}>: the ontology gathers types, structural principles, composition relations, and preservable invariants; the classification function maps raw text into typed regions; and the instantiator family yields a self-descriptive structured representation. Robustness derives from deterministic coupling with three external oracles: Pint (dimensional), Unicode Character Database (typographic), and RSLP (Portuguese morphology). Intrinsic evaluation covers four properties verifiable by construction -- ontological atomicity, dimensional equivalence, typographic robustness, and numerical reconstruction -- over an internal, physically validated benchmark (EngQuant, N=800) and four Brazilian Portuguese external corpora (N=1771 eligible cases). We also report detection recall, distinguishing coverage from conditional atomicity. Against eight state-of-the-art baselines, TOTEN achieves unit ontological atomicity in all contrasts and numerical reconstruction of 0.775-0.904 on external corpora, vs. 0.627-0.703 for the best baseline (Quantulum3); on EngQuant, 0.780 vs. 0.340. Differences are statistically significant (McNemar with Holm correction). Spearman correlation between internal and external rankings confirms concurrent validity of the control benchmark. Dimensional equivalence shows statistical parity with Pint, the oracle from which the system inherits dimensional authority.

</details>


### [116] [AI4SE and SE4AI Exploration: A Decade Looking Back and Forward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19630)
*H. Sinan Bank,Daniel R. Herber,Thomas Bradley*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A synthesis of AI in Systems Engineering (SE) progress across three phases (foundational, applied, LLM inflection), identification of five critical research gaps, and practitioner guidance; plus a human–AI agreement study over 1,712 INCOSE INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications, with data and a web app for benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To summarize and evaluate progress in AI for SE since the INCOSE INSIGHT AI theme, and to assess relevance through expert–AI agreement to guide AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation in SE.

Method: Phase-based literature synthesis (foundational, applied, LLM inflection) based on core SE/AI papers; a separate human–AI agreement literature review using human expertise and six AI models to assess relevance of 1,712 INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications; release of agreement data and the AI4SE/SE4AI Explorer web application.

Result: Identified five critical research gaps; provided actionable guidance for practitioners on AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation in SE; shared agreement dataset and a web app for benchmarking judgments against human and AI raters.

Conclusion: The field has progressed through distinct phases toward AI-enabled SE, but notable gaps remain; the available data and tools (agreement dataset and web app) support ongoing evaluation and alignment between human judgments and AI assessments in SE literature.

Abstract: The March 2020 INCOSE INSIGHT special issue on AI and Systems Engineering (SE) became the most downloaded issue in the publication's history and launched a research community that now draws over 250 registrants to its annual workshop. In this article, we trace the progress in AI and SE across three phases (labeled here foundational, applied, and LLM inflection) based on the authors' reading of the field's core papers, and describe our opinions of where the community has converged and where critical gaps remain. Separately, a human-AI agreement literature review leveraging both human expertise and six AI models was performed to assess the relevance of 1,712 INCOSE INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications. The results identify five critical research gaps and offer guidance for practitioners navigating AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation in SE. We share the agreement data and the AI4SE/SE4AI Explorer web application so readers can compare their own relevance judgments with the human and AI raters.

</details>


### [117] [BrainG3N: A Dual-Purpose Tokenizer for Controllable 3D Brain MRI Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19651)
*Max Van Puyvelde,Ibrahim Gulluk,Wim Van Criekinge,Olivier Gevaert*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A fully volumetric MAE-based tokenizer for 3D brain MRI in latent diffusion decouples encoder/decoder, achieving strong task performance and enabling controllable generation within a single embedding space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the reconstruction–information trade-off in reconstruction-driven tokenizers by decoupling the encoder (clinically informative embeddings) from the decoder (anatomically faithful reconstruction).

Method: Use a frozen 3D MAE encoder to produce embeddings; a dedicated CNN decoder reconstructs voxels from a linear projection of those embeddings. Pretrain the encoder on 35,309 volumes from 18 public cohorts across four modalities, ten disease categories, and 200+ sites. Train a conditional diffusion transformer (DiT) on these embeddings for six-variable conditional generation and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting.

Result: Encoder attains state-of-the-art performance or matches across 21 of 23 tasks in a 23-task linear-probing benchmark. The DiT model trained on the embeddings supports conditional generation across six variables and longitudinal forecasting.

Conclusion: Presents a single 3D brain-MRI embedding space that supports both downstream clinical tasks and controllable generation, addressing the core tokenizer trade-off via encoder–decoder decoupling.

Abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) brain MRI is central to clinical neurology and neuro-oncology, where generative models could augment under-represented cohorts, simulate disease trajectories, and support privacy-preserving data sharing. Latent diffusion has been the go-to solution for modeling imaging data, but it places two competing demands on the tokenizer: encoder embeddings must retain the clinical information that downstream tasks act on, and the decoder must reconstruct anatomically faithful volumes. Existing reconstruction-driven tokenizers achieve the second at the expense of the first. To address this, we introduce a fully volumetric masked-autoencoder (MAE) based tokenizer for 3D brain MRI latent diffusion, decoupling encoder and decoder: a frozen 3D MAE encoder produces clinically informative embeddings, while a dedicated CNN decoder reconstructs voxels from a linear projection of those embeddings. We pretrain the encoder on 35,309 volumes from 18 public cohorts spanning four modalities, ten disease categories, and 200+ acquisition sites, and demonstrate its dual utility in two settings. First, on a 23-task linear-probing benchmark, the encoder outperforms or matches SOTA models (i.e., BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, and MedicalNet) on 21 of 23 tasks. Second, a conditional diffusion transformer (DiT) trained on these clinically informative embeddings supports both conditional generation across six variables and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting. Together these results establish a single 3D brain-MRI embedding space capable of both downstream clinical tasks and controllable generation.

</details>


### [118] [Denoising Implicit Feedback for Cold-start Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19658)
*Gaode Chen,Shicheng Wang,Shikun Li,Rui Huang,Xinghua Zhang,Yunze Luo,Shipeng Li,Shiming Ge,Ruina Sun,Yinjie Jiang,Jun Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DIF is a model-agnostic denoising method for cold-start implicit feedback that uses content-based pseudo-labels from warm items, confidence modeling via content similarity, aggregation of multiple pseudo-labels, and uncertainty estimation (relative entropy and cold-start status) to correct noisy labels, with theoretical justification and deployment on Kuaishou showing improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Implicit feedback is noisy (e.g., clickbait, position bias) and cold-start items suffer more from noise. Existing denoising methods rely on heuristics and struggle in cold-start scenarios; there is a need for a denoising approach tailored to cold items that is model-agnostic and scalable.

Method: DIF: a model-agnostic denoising framework that (1) infers pseudo-labels for cold items using content-similar warm items assuming stable user preferences over content, (2) models pseudo-label confidence based on content similarity and aggregates multiple pseudo-labels per sample, (3) explicitly estimates uncertainty of the noisy sample label by considering relative entropy and cold-start status to guide pseudo-label usage in a sample-adaptive manner.

Result: The approach is theoretically justified and validated through extensive experiments on real-world datasets. It is deployed at-scale in Kuaishou (billion-user) short-video application, achieving significant improvements in commercial metrics under cold-start conditions.

Conclusion: DIF provides an effective, model-agnostic solution for denoising implicit feedback in cold-start recommendation, with strong theoretical support, empirical validation, and real-world deployment evidence.

Abstract: Implicit feedback is widely used in recommender systems due to its accessibility and generality, yet it usually presents noisy samples (e.g., clickbait, position bias). Meanwhile, recommenders inevitably face the item cold-start problem due to the continuous influx of new items. We identify that cold items are more prone to noisy samples due to the aforementioned factors, and researchers often overlook the significance of denoising implicit feedback for cold items. Previous denoising studies usually identify noisy samples based on heuristic patterns, such as higher loss values, and mitigate noise through sample selection or re-weighting. However, these methods have limited adaptability and are ineffective in cold-start scenarios. To achieve denoising implicit feedback for cold-start recommendation, we propose a model-agnostic denoising method called DIF. First, user preferences for content remain stable, which allows us to infer pseudo-labels indicating whether a user is interested in a cold item through content-similar warm items. Furthermore, to improve pseudo-label accuracy, we model the confidence of pseudo-labels based on the content similarity between the cold item and warm items, and then aggregate multiple pseudo-labels for each sample. Finally, we explicitly estimate the uncertainty of the noisy sample label by considering its relative entropy and the cold-start status of the item, which adaptively guides the role of pseudo-labels to correct the noisy labels at the sample level. DIF's superiority is supported by both theoretical justification and extensive experiments on real-world datasets. The method has been deployed on a billion-user scale short video application Kuaishou and has significantly improved various commercial metrics within cold-start scenarios.

</details>


### [119] [Exit-and-Join Dynamics for Decentralized Coalition Formation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19683)
*Quanyan Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Decentralized coalition formation via exit-and-join dynamics with Aumann-Dreze payoffs; equilibrium characterizations; potential/Lyapunov conditions; costs shape stability; numerical validation including finite-time stabilization and a convex-game benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge cooperative payoff allocation (Aumann-Dreze) with noncooperative dynamics; understand how local, unilateral moves lead to globally stable coalition structures and how costs affect stability.

Method: Model agents who can exit their current coalition and join another; payoffs are given by the Aumann-Dreze value within the agent's current coalition; derive equilibrium conditions where a terminal partition has no profitable exit-and-join deviations; identify conditions under which the dynamics admit scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representations; analyze the impact of switching and acceptance costs on local stability; perform numerical experiments to study finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and test a convex-game benchmark.

Result: A terminal partition is exactly a coalition structure with no admissible, individually profitable exit-and-join deviation; under matching conditions, the dynamics admit Lyapunov or exact-potential representations; switching and acceptance costs affect local stability; numerical experiments demonstrate finite-time stabilization, quantify cost sensitivity, and compare to a convex-game benchmark.

Conclusion: The framework ties cooperative payoff allocation to noncooperative dynamic behavior, clarifying convergence to locally stable coalition structures and showing how cost parameters shape stability; the results provide analytical tools (Lyapunov/potential) and empirical validation for decentralized coalition formation.

Abstract: This paper studies coalition formation as a decentralized dynamical process driven by unilateral exit-and-join decisions. Agents evaluate local moves using the Aumann-Dreze value, so payoffs are computed within the agent's current coalition rather than through a globally negotiated coalition structure. The resulting model links cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative best-response behavior: a terminal partition is precisely a coalition structure with no admissible, individually profitable exit-and-join deviation. We establish equilibrium characterizations, identify conditions under which the dynamics admit scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representations, and analyze how switching and acceptance costs shape local stability. Numerical experiments test finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a special convex-game benchmark.

</details>


### [120] [Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19704)
*Dhaval C. Patel,Kaoutar El Maghraoui,Shuxin Lin,Yusheng Li,Tianjun Feng,Chun-Yi Tsai,Yihan Sun,Wei Alexander Xin,Akshat Bhandari,Tanisha Rathod,Aaron Fan,Sanskruti Vijay Shejwal,Tomas Pasiecznik,Sagar Chethan Kumar,Tanmay Agarwal,Rohith Kanathur,Sam Colman,Amaan Sheikh,Dev Bahl,Ann Li,Krish Veera,Alimurtaza Mustafa Merchant,Shambhawi Baswaraj Bhure,Sajal Kumar Goyla,Chengrui Li,Kirthana Natarajan,Rui Li,Thomas Ajai,Rujing Li,Vivek G. Iyer,Sanjaii Vijayakumar,Yitong Bai,Ayal Yakobe,Darief Maes,Yassine Jebbouri,Tianyang Xu,Thai Quoc On,Vera Mazeeva,Winston Li,Yuval Shemla,Yeshitha Bhuvanesh,Rushin Bhatt,Siddharth Chethan Gowda,Alisha Vinod,Caroline Cahill,Shriya Aishani Rachakonda,Yunfeng Chen,Aryaman Agrawal,Aman Upganlawar,Mao Le Jonathan Ang,Yubin Sally Go,Madhav Rajkondawar,Yang-Jung Chen,Trisha Maturi,Ananya Kapoor,Andrew Li,Shrey Arora,Mana Abbaszadeh,Shen Li,Charles Xu,Byeolah Kwon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Meta-analysis of MCP-based industrial-agent benchmarks shows aggregate scores underspecify deployment performance; advocates predictive-validity rankings and a 12-tier OOD-aware evaluation apparatus, plus three falsifiable OOD criteria and a pre-registered pilot design for next-gen benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between benchmark aggregates and real-world deployment performance, demonstrating rank instability and the need for deployment-relevant evaluation frameworks.

Method: Synthesize fourteen parallel implementation studies on a large MCP-based benchmark, integrate seven prior agent benchmarks, analyze why aggregate scores fail to predict deployment performance, propose a predictive-validity ranking approach, and design a 12-tier measurement framework plus three explicit OOD criteria; outline a pre-registered pilot and future benchmark vision.

Result: Findings indicate that aggregate-score leaderboards poorly predict out-of-distribution performance; in-sample rank is not predictive of out-of-sample rank; evidence supports the need for predictive-validity based rankings, though OOD criteria evidence is currently inconclusive; introduces a twelve-tier deployment-focused measurement apparatus and a pre-registered pilot plan.

Conclusion: Current HELM-era benchmarks collapse under deployment-relevant evaluation; a shift to predictive validity-based rankings and explicit OOD evaluation thresholds is necessary; the paper lays a concrete agenda and pilot design for next-generation agent benchmarks.

Abstract: Agent benchmarks are growing fast, but no single benchmark touches more than four or five of the dimensions that deployment exposes. This paper aggregates the largest coordinated deep-dive of one MCP-based industrial-agent benchmark to date: fourteen parallel implementation studies covering new asset classes (including a multi-modal visual extension), alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes. Consolidating those studies with seven prior agent benchmarks, we argue that aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation. Rankings derived from aggregate scores do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings; recent public-to-hidden competition retrospectives provide direct empirical evidence of this rank instability. We propose ranking configurations by predictive validity, the correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rank, rather than in-sample mean, and report a twelve-tier measurement apparatus that exposes the deployment-relevant dimensions HELM and its agent-era successors collapse. The position is operationalized through three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria with explicit thresholds; existing evidence partly supports it but is too thin to confirm. We close with a pre-registered pilot design and a field-level vision for what the next generation of agentic benchmarks should report.

</details>


### [121] [GLARE: A Natural Language Interface for Querying Global Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19735)
*Bhavan Vasu,Rajesh Mangannavar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes an LLM-mediated NL-to-SQL interface for global explanations of black-box image classifiers, enabling targeted, statistics-rich natural language responses and visualizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global explanations are informative but often monolithic; users need targeted, natural-language answers and easy access to insight without exposing low-level representations.

Method: An LLM acts as mediator that translates natural language questions into structured SQL queries over local explanation data, producing statistics-augmented NL responses and intent-aligned visualizations; evaluated on intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries/datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors.

Result: LLM-mediated querying substantially improves accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI compared to baseline approaches.

Conclusion: LLM-driven NL-to-SQL interfacing can democratize access to global explanations, enabling flexible, user-centric exploration of model explanations with robust performance across datasets and linguistic variations.

Abstract: While global explanations are crucial for understanding vision models across datasets, classes, and decision contexts, their complex and monolithic nature often hinders practical exploration. Because users typically seek targeted answers to specific questions rather than static artifacts, we present an LLM-based interactive interface that provides natural language access to global explanations for black-box image classifiers. The system's core LLM acts as a mediator, translating natural language questions into structured SQL queries over local explanation data. This enables flexible aggregation without exposing users to low-level representations. For each query, the interface outputs statistics-augmented natural language responses, supporting local explanations, and intent-aligned visualizations. We evaluate the system on intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries and datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors. Our results demonstrate that LLM-mediated querying substantially improves the accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI.

</details>


### [122] [Interpreting Neural Combinatorial Optimization via Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19741)
*Haocheng Duan,Yuxin Guo,Jieyi Bi,Anqi Xie,Sirui Li,Yining Ma,Cathy Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EPB distills black-box Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) policies into evolving, human-readable program portfolios. An LLM autonomously evolves a bank of programs; per-step action distributions form bottlenecks. A two-block iterative framework (hybrid gradient descent and dynamic bank capacity) yields distilled portfolios that largely match original performance and reveal stage-wise strategy shifts, treating NCO behavior as a composition of classic heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NCO decisions are dynamic, state-dependent, and lack interpretable vocabulary; standard CBMs are ill-suited for such policies. There is a need for interpretable, modular representations of NCO behavior to enable deployment, diagnosis, and trust.

Method: Two-block framework: Block I fixes program bank capacity and uses a hybrid textual-numerical gradient descent (numerical gradients for student policy updates, textual gradients for LLM-driven program revisions). Block II dynamically adapts bank capacity via fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning. An LLM is used to autonomously evolve a bank of programs; each program’s per-step action distribution serves as the bottleneck for interpretation. Extensive experiments compare distilled portfolios to the original NCO, across optimization stages.

Result: Distilled program portfolios largely preserve the original performance. EPB reveals that NCO behavior shifts across optimization stages and can be approximated as a composition of classic heuristic variants. The framework provides broad applicability for interpreting sequential decision-making models and advances the interpretability of NCO.

Conclusion: EPB offers a novel, interpretable framework for NCO policies by distilling them into evolving program portfolios guided by an LLM. It delivers faithful approximations of black-box behavior, uncovers stage-wise strategy shifts, and lays groundwork for deploying and diagnosing sequential decision systems with interpretable components.

Abstract: Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) achieves strong performance, yet its black-box nature remains a key roadblock to deployment and scientific diagnosis. Standard interpretability tools, such as Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs), are ill-equipped for NCO, whose decisions are dynamic, state-dependent, and lack proper concept vocabulary definition. To close this gap, we introduce Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks (EPB), to our knowledge, the first framework for interpreting NCO policies by distilling black-box NCO models into human-readable program portfolios. EPB employs an LLM to autonomously evolve a bank of programs, where each program's per-step action distribution serves as the bottleneck. EPB works through an iterative framework: Block I fixes program bank capacity and introduces a hybrid textual-numerical gradient descent scheme that couples numerical gradients for student router updates and textual gradients for LLM-based program revision; Block II dynamically adapts bank capacity via fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning. Extensive experiments demonstrate EPB's effectiveness and broad applicability, where the distilled program portfolios largely match original performance. EPB also reveals that NCO behavior shifts across optimization stages and can be approximated as a composition of classic heuristic variants. Our work advances interpretable NCO and establishes EPB as a promising tool for interpreting sequential decision-making models.

</details>


### [123] [A Comparative Study of Pretrained Transformer Models for Quranic ASR: Speech Representations, Label Formats, and Dataset Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19747)
*Nabil Mosharraf Hossain,Riasat Islam,Unaizah Obaidellah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Domain-specific fine-tuning of transformer-based ASR for Quranic speech yields substantial WER reductions; best WER around 0.08 (EveryAyah) and 0.11 (EveryAyah+Tarteel); significant efficiency gains; diacritics-free Arabic helps; Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 strongest.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce high Word Error Rates and expand coverage of the Quranic corpus in ASR by leveraging pretrained transformer models with domain-specific fine-tuning on a large Quranic dataset.

Method: Fine-tune Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R on a filtered Quranic corpus (>870 hours) of professional and user recitations. Conduct comprehensive ablations across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations. Evaluate on EveryAyah and EveryAyah+Tarteel subsets; compare to Citrinet baseline; examine effect of diacritics.

Result: Best WER: 0.08 (EveryAyah) and 0.11 (EveryAyah+Tarteel); ~5 percentage-point improvement over Citrinet baseline (WER 0.163). Training time reduced from 140 hours to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields better fine-tuning performance; Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides strongest overall representation.

Conclusion: Dataset quality improvements and phoneme-aware models are promising directions for Tajweed-sensitive Quranic ASR; future work should focus on higher-quality data and phoneme-level modeling.

Abstract: Quran Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) aims to convert Quranic recitation into text, enabling applications such as aided memorisation tools and Quranic search engines. However, existing ASR models often exhibit high Word Error Rates (WER) on user-recited verses and lack full coverage of the Quranic corpus. This paper presents a systematic empirical study of domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained Transformer-based models for Quranic ASR, using advanced speech feature extraction methods: Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R. These models apply self-supervised learning by masking portions of input audio and using Transformer architectures to learn context-aware speech features. The pretrained models are fine-tuned on a filtered Quranic dataset exceeding 870 hours of professional and user recitations. Through comprehensive ablation studies across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations, we identify the key factors that affect transcription accuracy in this domain. Our best-performing configuration achieves a WER of 0.08 on the EveryAyah subset and 0.11 on the combined EveryAyah+Tarteel setting, representing roughly a five-percentage-point gain over the Citrinet baseline (WER = 0.163) while reducing combined-model training time from 140 hours to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields the best fine-tuning results, and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest overall representation. Future work includes improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models to extract deeper speech feature representations for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

</details>


### [124] [Benchmarking Agentic Review Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19749)
*Dang Nguyen,Wanqing Hao,Yanai Elazar,Chenhao Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI-assisted peer-review systems can track human-quality judgments and detect errors but still underperform ideal reviewers; OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 yields the strongest performance (~83% pairwise accuracy; 71.6% error-detection recall), while combining models increases detection (83.3% union recall). Real-user deployment yields mostly positive reception but notable false positives and minor nitpicks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the evaluation gap for agentic (AI-assisted) review systems in the era of AI-augmented research, and establish benchmarking across model types, error-detection capabilities, and real-world usability.

Method: Evaluate multiple AI review systems (OpenAIReview, coarse, Reviewer3, and a zero-shot baseline) across six LLMs (frontier and efficient). Assess (1) alignment with external quality signals (citations, acceptance), (2) a perturbation-based error-detection benchmark across eight arXiv classes with four error types, (3) a real-world deployment with user feedback on a public OpenAIReview instance.

Result: All systems exceed chance in pairwise accuracy; best configuration (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) achieves 83.0% pairwise accuracy. Perturbation benchmark detects 71.6% of injected errors with best setup; union across models yields 83.3% recall. Real deployment shows positive votes (1.44 to 1) with frequent complaints about false positives and minor nitpicks.

Conclusion: AI review systems can track human quality signals and identify significant errors, but substantial improvements remain possible through design optimization and model diversity; real-world acceptance is promising but requires reducing false positives and refining critique granularity.

Abstract: A new class of agentic review systems are emerging as a remedy to the pressure placed on peer review systems by AI-assisted research, but it is unclear how they should be evaluated. We evaluate two open-source systems (OpenAIReview and coarse), one proprietary system (Reviewer3), and a zero-shot baseline, across six LLMs spanning frontier and efficient models. First, we study whether AI reviews on ICLR/NeurIPS papers track with papers' quality as approximated by external signals such as citations and acceptance decisions. Every system performs above chance in pairwise accuracy, and the best is OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 at 83.0%. Second, to test whether systems can catch errors with known ground truth, we construct a perturbation benchmark that injects four categories of errors into papers across eight arXiv subject classes and measure detection recall. The strongest configuration (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) catches 71.6% of injected errors, leaving substantial room for improvement. The union of detections across six models reaches 83.3% recall, suggesting different models detect different errors and better harness design can potentially increase performance. Beyond these benchmarks, we study a public deployment of OpenAIReview with real users. Votes on its comments skew positive at 1.44 to 1, and the most common complaints are about false positives and minor nitpicks. Together, by evaluating full review systems backed by state-of-the-art models on real research papers, we show that while AI reviews still have room for improvement, they can already track human quality judgments well, catch important errors, and earn positive feedback from real users.
