</details>


### [125] [Grounded Inference: Principles for Deterministically Encapsulated Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19753)
*Marty O'Neill*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a framework for AI-infused architecture by defining four primitives to deterministically encapsulate probabilistic models and identifying two industry-wide anti-patterns, aimed at de-risking AI integration and guiding next-generation AI interfaces for traditional systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate the substantial risks and volatility of integrating generative AI into traditional computation by providing a formal, deterministic foundation that engineers can rely on.

Method: Formal definition of four AI-blended architectural primitives and identification of two broad anti-patterns observed in industry; the approach emphasizes deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models and practical guidance for integration.

Result: A foundational framework that enables safe, deterministic integration of generative models into traditional systems and offers warnings (anti-patterns) to prevent common engineering pitfalls; serves as a basis for future generative-model interfaces.

Conclusion: The framework aims to de-risk AI adoption in conventional systems while informing the design of next-gen AI interfaces and tooling for the industry.

Abstract: The incorporation of generative models into traditional computational systems presents both enormous opportunity and tremendous peril. Although many early adopters have realized these perils at great expense, the field still requires foundational frameworks to de-risk incorporation of AI into traditional systems. This manuscript establishes this foundation through the definition of four specific primitives of AI blended architecture, designed to enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models. It further establishes two overarching anti-patterns broadly represented across industry to serve as warnings for engineers in this field. This framework was designed to enable successful integration of AI into traditional systems while providing a foundation upon which generative model providers could build the next generation of generative model interfaces.

</details>


### [126] [Optimal Scheduling in a Question-Answering Forum of Knowledge Workers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19759)
*Rohit Negi,Mustafa Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes a future QA forum where knowledge workers (experts) answer requests. It models a topic-based queuing system with schedulers that allocate requests to experts by topic and expertise level, aiming to maximize capacity while keeping the system stable, and explores how expert collaboration can increase capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how to design scalable, stable QA forums by leveraging topic-specialized experts and scheduling strategies, potentially replacing volunteer models with knowledge-worker participation.

Method: Model the QA forum as a multi-topic queueing system with expert pools. Define expertise-based service rates, topic-specific arrival processes, and schedulers that route requests to appropriate experts. Analyze system stability and capacity, derive scheduling policies that achieve capacity, and examine collaboration among experts to improve throughput.

Result: Derivation of capacity-achieving scheduling policies for topic-aligned expert assignment, conditions under which the system remains stable, and theoretical insights into how collaboration among experts can increase capacity; quantitative bounds or performance comparisons likely provided.

Conclusion: A framework for designing scalable QA forums with expert involvement, showing how scheduling and collaboration can achieve higher capacity and stability than naive first-come-first-served approaches.

Abstract: As individuals turn to the Internet to find answers to questions they may have, several Question Answering (QA) forums have evolved, where users knowledgeable in certain topics can contribute their expertise to answering these requests for information. While these are currently volunteer based, we consider a future version employing knowledge workers who are experts in certain topics. In such a system, the request-answer processes forming the queuing system may utilize schedulers that assign requests in different topics to the experts in the forum, who may be able to answer them according to their expertise levels in different topics. With this model, we calculate the capacity of the system for handling the requests while keeping the system stable, and design schedulers that achieve capacity. We also investigate how collaboration between experts in answering requests can potentially increase capacity.

</details>


### [127] [Beyond Entropy: Learning from Token-Level Distributional Deviations for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19771)
*Xuanzhi Feng,Zhengyang Li,Zeyu Liu,Haoxi Li,Yuming Jiang,Bing Guo,Jingcai Guo,Jie Zhang,Song Guo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ICT: update top 10% unique tokens using Jensen-Shannon divergence on token logits to stabilize RL-based LLM reasoning, improving pass@4 by ~4.6% (up to 14.9%) on Qwen2.5 across seven benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL-based reward shaping in LLMs suffers from entropy collapse (uniform updates) and entropy explosion (over-exploration); a distributional perspective is needed to stabilize exploration.

Method: Compute JS divergence between token logits to identify distinctive tokens; perform selective updates on top 10% of unique tokens; theoretical grounding via Shannon and second-order Rényi entropies showing reduced Shannon entropy and controlled Rényi concentration; empirical evaluation on Qwen2.5 families.

Result: Top-10% update yields average pass@4 improvement 4.58% and max 14.9% over GRPO, 20-Entropy, STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks (math, commonsense, Olympiad).

Conclusion: ICT provides a practical mechanism to stabilize RL-based LLM training by targeting distributional properties of token logits, balancing concentration and exploration; results suggest robust gains across model scales and tasks; future work may address computational overhead and broader generalization.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning; however, it faces a fundamental optimization instability: uniform token updates precipitate entropy collapse, leading to premature convergence to suboptimal strategies, whereas excessive Shannon Entropy maximization can cause entropy explosion, driving blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains. To resolve this dichotomy, we introduce the Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework, which shifts the optimization focus from scalar uncertainty to the distributional properties of token logits. By leveraging the Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence between token logits distributions, ICT identifies tokens with distinctive distributional patterns as critical branching points for guiding effective exploration in LLM reasoning. Our theoretical analysis, grounded in both Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy, proves that selectively updating on these tokens regulates policy concentration: it reduces the overall distribution uncertainty measured by Shannon entropy, while controlling probability concentration captured by second-order Rényi entropy. This dual effect prevents over-concentrated token generation from weakening exploration and effectively stabilizes the training landscape. Empirical results demonstrate that updating only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) models yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58%, with a maximum gain of 14.9%, over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks spanning math, commonsense, and Olympiad-level problems.

</details>


### [128] [AgentFinVQA: A Deployable Multi-Agent Pipeline for Auditable Financial Chart QA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19782)
*Aravind Narayanan,Shaina Raza*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentFinVQA introduces an auditable, on-prem multi-agent VQA pipeline for financial charts with traceable Model Evaluation Pack (MEP) records. It achieves significant accuracy gains over baselines and maintains data residency with open-weight models, complemented by a human-in-the-loop verifier. Code is released for reproducible evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regulated financial environments require auditability and data residency. Existing chart-QA systems are accuracy-focused, opaque, and often rely on external APIs, making compliance and data privacy difficult. The work aims to provide auditable, on-prem chart QA without substantial accuracy loss.

Method: A multi-agent pipeline decomposes each query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification steps, all recorded in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP). Evaluated on FinMME with a proprietary backbone (Gemini-3 Flash) and open-weight Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 deployed locally. The verifier's verdict serves as a confidence signal to route human review.

Result: +7.68 percentage points over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline (71.24% vs. 63.56%; McNemar p ≈ 1.1e-16). +4.84 percentage points with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 run locally. Verifier accuracy: 68.2% on confirmed vs. 55.6% on revised answers. Error analysis attributes ~66% of failures to question misunderstanding, legend confusion, and extraction error; these are the categories least detected by the verifier. The approach preserves most accuracy gains with open-weight models and enables full data residency. Code is released for reproducible evaluation.

Conclusion: Auditable, on-prem financial chart QA is practical with AgentFinVQA. The multi-agent, traceable design supports regulatory needs without heavily compromising accuracy, especially when using open-weight models. The verifier provides useful confidence signals, guiding human-in-the-loop review. Identified error categories point to clear directions for future improvement.

Abstract: Financial chart question answering in regulated settings demands more than accuracy: practitioners must know which answers to trust before acting on them, and many institutions cannot send client data to external model providers. Yet existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused and opaque, and most assume proprietary API access; to our knowledge, none combines auditability with on-premise deployability without significant accuracy compromise. We present AgentFinVQA, a multi-agent pipeline that decomposes each query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. On FinMME, AgentFinVQA improves $+7.68$ pp over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline with a proprietary backbone (Gemini-3 Flash; 71.24% vs. 63.56%, McNemar $p \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-16}$), and $+4.84$ pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. The verifier's verdict also serves as a useful confidence signal (68.2% vs. 55.6% exact accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers), enabling human-in-the-loop review routing. Error analysis shows that question misunderstanding, legend confusion and extraction error account for nearly two-thirds of failures and are the categories least detected by the verifier, identifying clear directions for future work. Together these results show that auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical and that the open-weights system keeps most of the accuracy gains while enabling full data residency. We release our code to support reproducible evaluation.

</details>


### [129] [ORAgentBench: Can LLM Agents Solve Challenging Operations Research Tasks End to End?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19787)
*Jiajun Li,Mingshu Cai,Yixuan Li,Yu Ding,Ran Hou,Guanyu Nie,Xiongwei Han,Wanyuan Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ORAgentBench is a ground-truth, end-to-end benchmark for autonomous agents in operations research, revealing substantial gaps between current agents and real-world OR practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations separate modeling and solving and rely on abstract inputs; there is a need to test full workflow with operational artifacts to assess practical capabilities.

Method: Develop ORAgentBench with 107 tasks in isolated environments including natural-language brief, multi-file data, config artifacts, and a required submission schema; require agents to write/run solution code; evaluate submissions with hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality; test 14 frontier agent-model configurations.

Result: Best agent passes 35.51% of tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks; many feasible submissions fail quality thresholds; errors dominated by strategic weaknesses (missed rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, insufficient solution improvement); OR-specific procedural skills help hard-task feasibility but do not reliably improve quality or pass rate.

Conclusion: Progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for multi-step tasks in executable environments, yet their ability to perform realistic operations research (OR) work remains unclear. Existing OR evaluations often decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and rarely test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions. In this work, we introduce ORAgentBench, an execution-grounded benchmark for evaluating autonomous agents on challenging end-to-end operations research tasks. It contains 107 human-reviewed tasks across diverse operational scenarios, each packaged in an isolated environment with a natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a required submission schema. Agents must write and run solution code, and their submissions are evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality. Experiments with fourteen frontier agent-model configurations show that current agents remain far from reliable OR practice. The best agent passes only 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks, and many feasible submissions still fall below the required quality threshold. Failure analysis further shows that errors are dominated by strategic weaknesses, including missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, and insufficient solution improvement. OR-specific procedural skills increase hard-task feasibility, but do not reliably improve solution quality or pass rate. These results suggest that progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

</details>


### [130] [CombEval: A Framework for Evaluating Combinatorial Counting in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19788)
*Yuxu Zhou,Ondřej Kuželka,Yuyi Wang,Yuanhong Wang,Yi Chang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dynamic, solver-verified benchmark (CombEval) for combinatorial counting in LLMs; uses typed Cofola specs to enable controlled, variable problem generation; reveals specific brittleness (ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, positional constraints, nested dependencies) and interprets counting principles errors; provides diagnostic value and public code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess and diagnose combinatorial reasoning in LLMs beyond static datasets by enabling controlled variation across problem type, size, constraints, and reasoning depth, with solver-verified answers.

Method: Construct problems as typed Cofola specifications over entities, combinatorial objects, dependencies, and constraints; generate natural-language counting problems with exact, solver-verified answers; vary object type, scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth; evaluate 11 LLMs under direct and code-augmented settings; perform error analysis on constraint interpretation and counting principles.

Result: LLMs show brittleness in handling ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relatively positional constraints, and nested dependencies; common failures include misinterpreting constraints and violating counting principles; dynamic, diagnostic variation clarifies when and why failures occur.

Conclusion: CombEval is a useful diagnostic testbed for studying combinatorial reasoning in LLMs and for diagnosing failure modes; code and benchmark suites are publicly available to enable further research and benchmarking.

Abstract: We present CombEval, a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in large language models. CombEval represents each problem as a typed Cofola specification over entities, combinatorial objects, object dependencies, and constraints, enabling controlled generation of natural-language counting problems with exact solver-verified answers. Unlike static collections, CombEval supports systematic variation of object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth. We evaluate 11 LLMs under direct and code-augmented settings and find that models remain brittle on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relatively positional constraints, and nested object dependencies. Error analysis further identifies failures in constraint interpretation and counting principles. CombEval provides a diagnostic testbed for studying when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning. The code and generated benchmark suites are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/YuxuZhou-CN/combination-problem-generation}.

</details>


### [131] [Think Again or Think Longer? Selective Verification for Budget-Aware Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19808)
*Sajib Acharjee Dip,Dawei Zhou,Liqing Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Selective verification (SEVRA) controls test-time reasoning by deciding when to verify, achieving accuracy gains with fewer tokens in several benchmarks, though not always the best cost frontier. Deployment rule: set initial budget first, then apply selective recovery when checks or regression risk matter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time reasoning may repair failures but can waste compute or cause harmful changes. Treat reasoning as a deployment allocation problem rather than a fixed verifier.

Method: Freeze a Qwen3-4B solver, log intervention outcomes, train recoverability-aware gates using serving-visible attempt state, and deploy a gating policy to either preserve the initial answer or invoke verification. Evaluate on MathFive (\mathfive), GSM, and CommonsenseQA, comparing selective verification to always verify and other baselines.

Result: MathFive: selective verification achieves 76.3% accuracy vs 75.5% for always verify; tokens reduced by 26.8% and harmful flips cut from 2.2% to 1.0%. An 8,192-token initial solve yields 76.0% accuracy with 28% fewer tokens. GSM: selective policy verifies 3.0% of examples, accuracy 93.4%→94.5%, verification tokens reduced by 91.2%; longer initial solve matches accuracy with fewer tokens. CommonsenseQA: always-on verification hurts; Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at ~5x token cost.

Conclusion: Selective recovery is useful but not universally the best cost frontier. Deployment guidance: tune the initial budget first, then apply selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter.

Abstract: Test-time reasoning is increasingly used as a serving-time control knob, but extra reasoning is not uniformly valuable: it can repair failed attempts, waste compute on already-correct answers, or introduce harmful answer changes. We study this as a deployment allocation problem rather than a new-verifier problem. We introduce \sevra, Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation, a serving-layer controller that decides whether to preserve a frozen solver's initial answer or invoke active verification. Using a frozen Qwen3-4B solver, we log intervention outcomes and train recoverability-aware gates from serving-visible attempt state. On \mathfive, selective verification reaches 76.3\% accuracy, compared with 75.5\% for always verifying, while reducing post-generation tokens by 26.8\% and harmful flips from 2.2\% to 1.0\%. However, an 8,192-token initial solve reaches 76.0\% accuracy with 28\% fewer total model tokens, showing that selective recovery is useful but not the best tested cost frontier. In frozen transfer to \gsm, the selective policy verifies only 3.0\% of examples, improves accuracy from 93.4\% to 94.5\%, and reduces verification tokens by 91.2\% relative to always verifying; again, a longer initial solve matches its accuracy with fewer realized tokens. On CommonsenseQA, always-on verification hurts, while Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at about five times the realized token cost. The resulting deployment rule is: tune the initial budget first, then use selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter.

</details>


### [132] [Human-on-the-Loop Orchestration for AI-Assisted Legal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19812)
*Anushree Sinha,Srivaths Ranganathan,Abhishek Dharmaratnakar,Debanshu Das*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval plus a four-layer verification architecture (planning, reasoning, execution, uncertainty quantification) and a simulation showing that human-on-the-loop escalation thresholds reduce privilege-waiver risk in automated privilege review, with acceptable attorney-review workload.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate trajectory collapse and legal malpractice risk in autonomous LLMs performing privileged e-discovery; improve safety and reliability via structured verification and human oversight.

Method: 1) Develop a functional-stage taxonomy of failures in agentic legal IR; 2) Propose a four-layer verification framework to intercept failures before they propagate; 3) Conduct a preliminary simulation on a synthetic e-discovery corpus to compare HOTL escalation against fully autonomous baselines.

Result: Calibrated uncertainty thresholds with HOTL escalation reduced privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% versus fully autonomous deployment, while routing fewer than 25% of documents to attorney review.

Conclusion: The proposed verification-and-escalation framework shows promise for safer, more reliable agentic legal retrieval; results are preliminary and warrant further empirical validation on real-world data.

Abstract: Autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in electronic discovery (e-discovery), where compounding errors across multi-step reasoning chains can constitute legal malpractice. Unlike single-turn retrieval, agentic workflows operating over privileged document corpora exhibit a class of failure we term "trajectory collapse": an early misclassification silently propagates, rendering an entire privilege review invalid. This paper makes three contributions. First, we propose a structured taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval, organized by functional stage. Second, we introduce a four-layer verification architecture -- spanning planning, reasoning, execution, and uncertainty quantification -- designed to intercept these failures before they compound. Third, we present a preliminary simulation study on a synthetic e-discovery corpus that demonstrates how mandatory Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL) escalation thresholds reduce privilege-waiver risk relative to fully autonomous baselines. Our results suggest that calibrated uncertainty thresholds can reduce privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% versus fully autonomous deployment, while routing fewer than one quarter of documents to attorney review.

</details>


### [133] [TelcoAgent: A Scalable 5G Multi-KPM Forecasting With 3GPP-Grounded Explainability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19821)
*Geon Kim,Dara Ron,Sukhdeep Singh,Suyog Moogi,Pranshav Gajjar,V V N K Someswara Rao Koduri,Een Kee Hong,Vijay K. Shah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TelcoAgent is a foundation-model-based framework that enables scalable, zero-shot forecasting of multiple KPMs across 5G cells with explainability, by (i) constructing a 3GPP knowledge graph from spec documents, (ii) a TSFM-based prediction pipeline, and (iii) a reasoning/explanation module for actionable diagnostics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a pressing need for scalable, interpretable KPM forecasting in 5G/next-gen networks to replace or augment traditional ML models that struggle with data efficiency, generalization, and operational explanations.

Method: A three-part pipeline: (i) an automated tri-agent system to extract and assemble a 3GPP knowledge graph from specification documents, (ii) a time-series foundation-model (TSFM) that delivers zero-shot forecasts for multiple KPMs across cells, and (iii) a reasoning/explanation pipeline that yields domain-grounded diagnostics and recommended actions.

Result: On a 3-month real-world city-scale 5G KPM dataset across 200 cells and 7 KPMs, TelcoAgent achieves high forecasting accuracy per cell and per KPM, while providing explainable insights and actionable instructions to mitigate degradations.

Conclusion: TelcoAgent demonstrates a scalable, accurate, and explainable KPM forecasting solution suitable for proactive network management, with potential for broader applicability across KPMs and network environments; future work may address further generalization, efficiency, and deployment considerations.

Abstract: Key Performance Measurement (KPM) forecasting is essential for proactive network management of 5G and next-generation telecom networks. However, existing machine learning (ML) approaches face significant limitations in scalability and explainability, restricting their effectiveness in real-world deployments. We propose TelcoAgent, a foundation model-based framework that enables accurate, scalable, and explainable forecasting of multiple KPMs across diverse network cells without the need for site-specific training. Specifically, the framework comprises three key components: (i) an automated three-agent pipeline that constructs a 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) knowledge graph directly from specification documents, (ii) a scalable, time-series foundation model (TSFM)-based prediction pipeline to deliver accurate, zero-shot forecasting, and finally (iii) a reasoning and explanation pipeline that provides actionable, domain-grounded diagnostics. Evaluated using a 3-month, real-world, city-scale 5G KPM dataset from a U.S.-based network operator, TelcoAgent demonstrates high forecasting accuracy for all 7 considered KPMs per cell across 200 cells, while delivering explainable insights and actionable instructions to address network degradations.

</details>


### [134] [A Systematic Evaluation of Black-Box Uncertainty Estimation Methods for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19868)
*Jiayi Wang,Xu-Yao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Systematically reviews and benchmarks black-box uncertainty estimation methods for LLMs, categorizing into five groups, benchmarking 24 methods across multiple models and datasets; finds no universal winner but notes effectiveness of answer-space reasoning and hybrid approaches; provides public benchmark data and evaluation framework to enable reproducible research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unreliable LLM outputs (hallucinations) and the need for uncertainty estimates are amplified when models are accessed via restricted APIs that hide internal signals; prior black-box UE work is fragmented and lacks unified evaluation.

Method: Performs a unified evaluation framework and literature review, organizing black-box UE methods into five categories (verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, and hybrid); benchmarks 24 representative methods across 4 models and 4 dataset settings.

Result: No single method dominates across all settings; methods that reason over and compare candidate answers in the output space are generally effective; hybrid methods integrating multiple uncertainty signals perform well in most conditions; releases benchmark data and framework.

Conclusion: The study provides a reproducible benchmark and practical guidance for designing future black-box UE methods for LLMs, enabling fair comparisons and accelerating research in uncertainty estimation under restricted API access.

Abstract: Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities across a wide range of tasks, their outputs often remain unreliable and may contain hallucinations, making uncertainty estimation (UE) essential for building trustworthy LLMs. In practice, many mainstream LLMs are only accessible through restricted APIs, where internal signals such as logits and hidden states are unavailable, making black-box UE especially important. However, existing work on black-box UE for LLMs remains fragmented in methodology and lacks a unified empirical comparison. To address this gap, we present a systematic review of black-box UE methods and organize them into five categories: verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, and hybrid methods. We further build a unified evaluation framework and benchmark 24 representative methods across 4 models and 4 dataset settings. Our results show that no single method consistently dominates across all settings. Nevertheless, methods that reason over and compare candidates in the answer space are generally effective, and hybrid methods that combine multiple uncertainty signals perform well under most conditions. By releasing the benchmark data and a unified evaluation framework, we aim to facilitate reproducible comparisons and support future research, while our empirical findings provide practical guidance for developing future black-box UE methods for LLMs.

</details>


### [135] [MetaResearcher: Scaling Deep Research via Self-Reflective Reinforcement Learning in Adversarial Virtual Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19893)
*Wei Yu,Suxing Liu,Minjie Yu,Jiahao Wang,Zhijian Zheng,Haocheng Deng,Bing Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MetaResearcher proposes a four-dimensional framework to train deep research agents: an evolving virtual world, discovery-oriented tasks, a self-reflective meta-reward within GRPO, and a heterogeneous multi-agent swarm, built on LiteResearcher. Aims for zero marginal API cost, improved benchmarks (GAIA, Xbench-DS), and enhanced epistemic robustness; experimental validation planned.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current training relies on static simulations, fact-retrieval-focused tasks, and outcome-based RL, which limit agents' realism, source credibility, and robustness. The work targets epistemic robustness, genuine research behaviors, and scalable training.

Method: 1) Evolving Virtual World injecting temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation to foster source credibility assessment and temporal conflict resolution. 2) Discovery-Oriented Tasks (hypothesis generation, contradiction resolution) pushing beyond retrieval. 3) Self-Reflective Meta-Reward in the GRPO framework optimizing answer correctness, search path efficiency, reflection depth, and tool call diversity. 4) Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm (Scout/Filter/Synthesizer) learning collaborative strategies via coordinated RL. Built on LiteResearcher with zero marginal API cost.

Result: The abstract outlines a complete framework, training methodology, and planned validation. It does not report empirical results yet; plans to evaluate on GAIA and Xbench-DS and to demonstrate improvements in benchmark performance and epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions.

Conclusion: MetaResearcher offers an integrated, scalable approach to training deep research agents across four interconnected axes, addressing static environments, limited task design, and inefficient reinforcement learning. Success hinges on effective integration of the evolving world, task design, reward signals, and cross-agent coordination, with empirical validation forthcoming.

Abstract: Deep research agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in autonomous information gathering and synthesis, yet their training remains constrained by the static nature of simulated environments, the limits of fact-retrieval-only task designs, and the inefficiency of outcome-based reinforcement learning. In this work, we propose MetaResearcher, a novel framework that scales deep research agent training across four synergistic dimensions. First, we introduce an Evolving Virtual World that injects temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation into the training environment, forcing agents to develop source credibility assessment and temporal conflict resolution skills. Second, we design Discovery-Oriented Tasks -- including hypothesis generation and contradiction resolution -- that transcend simple fact retrieval and push agents toward genuine research behaviors. Third, we propose a Self-Reflective Meta-Reward mechanism within the GRPO framework that jointly optimizes for answer correctness, search path efficiency, reflection depth, and tool call diversity, directly addressing the repetitive action loop problem observed in prior work. Fourth, we introduce a Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm architecture comprising specialized Scout, Filter, and Synthesizer models that learn collaborative research strategies through coordinated reinforcement learning. Built upon the LiteResearcher infrastructure, MetaResearcher requires zero marginal API cost for training while targeting substantial improvements in both benchmark performance (GAIA, Xbench-DS) and epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions. We present the complete framework design, training methodology, and planned experimental validation.

</details>


### [136] [Multi-Agent Transactive Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19911)
*To Eun Kim,Xuhong He,Dishank Jain,Ambuj Agrawal,Negar Arabzadeh,Fernando Diaz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MATM enables population-level sharing of agent-generated trajectories via a shared repository, improving task performance and reducing interaction steps without coordination or joint training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To avoid re-discovery of existing solutions when new agents are instantiated and to leverage rich procedural knowledge encoded in agent trajectories across heterogeneous populations.

Method: Introduce Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM), a shared trajectory repository where producer agents contribute trajectories and consumer agents retrieve them; evaluated in interactive environments ALFWorld and WebArena with long, richly structured trajectories to assess retrieval impact on task execution.

Result: Retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps; no explicit coordination or joint training required.

Conclusion: MATM functions as a design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems, enabling reusable knowledge at the population level.

Abstract: The decentralized deployment of LLM agents with diverse capabilities across diverse tasks motivates infrastructure for knowledge sharing across heterogeneous agent populations. Just as search engines index human-generated artifacts to support human problem solving, retrieval systems can organize agent-generated artifacts for reuse across agent populations. We extend retrieval-augmented generation - which demonstrates the value of human-authored artifacts to individual agents - to retrieval of agent-generated artifacts supporting a population of agents. In particular, agent trajectories encode reusable procedural knowledge, yet these artifacts are typically discarded after a single use or retained only by the producing agent, forcing newly instantiated agents to repeatedly rediscover existing solutions. We propose Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM), a framework for population-level storage and retrieval of agent-generated trajectories, where producer agents contribute trajectories to a shared repository and consumer agents retrieve them to improve task execution. We focus on interactive environments (ALFWorld and WebArena), where trajectories are long and encode especially rich procedural structure. Our experiments demonstrate that retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps without coordination or joint training. These results position MATM as a design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems.

</details>


### [137] [eCNNTO: A Highly Generalizable ConvNet for Accelerating Topology Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19921)
*Shengbiao Lu,Xiaodong Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Element-based CNN (eCNNTO) accelerates density-based topology optimization by leveraging local histories and neighboring element correlations, achieving large iteration reductions with a small training dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TO is computationally intensive due to many FEA iterations on dense meshes; prior per-element DBNs lack spatial context, risking disconnected features. A spatially aware surrogate is needed for speed and coherence.

Method: Develop an element-wise CNN with residual connections that predicts near-optimal densities using early/local history while incorporating spatial correlations across the design; training on final-stage density histories to reduce data needs, extending the Kallioras et al. (2020) approach.

Result: Achieves substantial acceleration: up to 90% iteration reduction in 2D and 97% in 3D; uses a small dataset; generalizes across boundary conditions, loading, geometries, mesh resolutions, and non-design domains.

Conclusion: eCNNTO remedies lack of spatial coherence in element-wise surrogates, offers robust generalization, and delivers significant efficiency gains for density-based topology optimization.

Abstract: This work proposes an element-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to accelerate density-based Topology Optimization (TO), termed eCNNTO. TO generally undergoes a large number of iterations, where finite element analysis is performed in every iteration, leading to the efficiency bottleneck especially when dense meshes are used to achieve high-resolution designs. To address this limitation, eCNNTO is proposed to build upon Kallioras et al. (2020), where a Deep Belief Network (DBN) was trained for every element to predict its near-optimal density from its early history, thereby skipping the great majority of iterations and significantly accelerating the TO procedure. However, the method lacks spatial correlations among neighboring elements and may lead to disconnected features in the final structure. The proposed method employs CNN with residual connections to address this issue. On top of it, a novel training strategy is introduced to further enhance the optimization efficiency, where the training dataset consists of the final stage density histories rather than early ones. This change can also help reduce the required training data size. eCNNTO requires only a small dataset to train and yet it can be generalized to problems with largely different boundary conditions, loading cases, design domain geometries, mesh resolutions, as well as non-design domains. In the end, the generalization capabilities and efficiency of eCNNTO are demonstrated through a variety of examples in two and three dimensions, achieving up to 90% and 97% reduction of iterations, respectively.

</details>


### [138] [The Tao of Agency: Autotelic AI, Embedded Agency and Dissolution of the Self](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19924)
*Aritra Sarkar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Autotelic AI treats goals as self-generated; analyzes interplay between intrinsic motivation, homeostasis, and embeddedness, showing self-individuation is non-unique and central to goal-relativization; proposes a unified framework and three extensions (quantum, philosophical, LLM-instantiate) with deep emphasis on how the agent’s boundary defines its agency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate what happens when agents generate their own goals, addressing the foundational issue of how an agent can form a self (boundary) and how it relativizes goals to that self, with implications for autonomy, alignment, and identity.

Method: Theoretical synthesis of concepts (intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness) accompanied by a structural claim about non-uniqueness of individuation; development of a single framework and conceptual extensions in three directions (quantum, philosophical, and LLM-based instantiation).

Result: Argues that embeddedness is necessary but not sufficient for autotelic agency; the core obstacle is self-generation and relativization of the self, leading to multiple valid partitions (individuation) that define different candidate selves; lays out a unified framework and outlines three concrete extensions.

Conclusion: Autotelic agency hinges on a robust theory of self-generation and relativization; the proposed framework integrates theory with a quantum view, philosophical critique, and practical LLM-based implementation, highlighting the non-uniqueness of agent identities and the need to understand and constrain the agent’s boundary.

Abstract: Most artificial intelligence systems are built on the assumption that goals are exogenous and specified by the designer. Exploring what happens when an agent begins generating its own goals opens the field of autotelic AI. Agents are expected not merely to pursue objectives but to discover them. In this article, we trace its consequences through intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; the last of which is found to be a necessary but not sufficient condition for autotelic agency. Embeddedness individuates the agent at the cost of revealing that the individuation is non-unique, such that the same dynamics admit many valid partitions, each defining a different candidate self.
  The deepest problem with autotelic AI is therefore not how the agent generates goals, but how it generates and relativizes the self to which the goals are assigned. The agent must believe in its own boundary in order to act, and see through that boundary in order to understand. We consolidate these developments into a single framework and extend it along three directions: a quantum formulation in which the agent-environment cut becomes physical, a philosophical reading against non-dual contemplative traditions, and a concrete LLM-based agentic instantiation.

</details>


### [139] [PhysDrift: Bridging the Embodiment Gap in Humanoid Co-Speech Motion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19935)
*Zhangzhao Liang,Xiaofen Xing,Mingyue Yang,Wenlve Zhou,Xiangmin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Embodiment-aware, robot-native co-speech motion generation to overcome gaps in human-centric retargeting, improving feasibility, alignment, and real-time performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current pipelines rely on human-body representations (e.g., SMPL-X) and retargeting, which misalign with humanoid embodiment constraints, reducing diversity, prosody-motion synchronization, and executable quality.

Method: IK-EER: a retrofitting framework that curates robot-native motions by jointly optimizing kinematic feasibility and speech-motion temporal alignment during retargeting. PhysDrift: end-to-end, embodiment-aware co-speech generation predicting executable humanoid joint trajectories directly from speech, aided by a curated robot-native dataset and physical regularization to stabilize dynamics.

Result: Embodiment-aware robot-native generation substantially improves speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability, validated by extensive experiments and real-world deployment.

Conclusion: Shifting from human-centric retargeting to robot-native generation preserves embodiment consistency, enhances expressiveness and feasibility of co-speech motions for humanoids.

Abstract: Humanoid robots require co-speech motions that are not only expressive and speech-aligned, but also physically executable under embodiment constraints. Existing co-speech generation pipelines are predominantly human-centric: motions are first generated in human-body representations such as SMPL-X and subsequently retargeted to humanoid robots. In this work, we identify a fundamental embodiment gap in this paradigm, where the mismatch between human motion manifolds and humanoid embodiment constraints disrupts embodiment consistency during motion transfer and physical execution. Through extensive analysis, we show that although retargeting can preserve coarse motion semantics, it significantly compresses motion diversity and weakens prosody-motion synchronization, limiting expressive humanoid behaviors. To address this problem, we first propose IK-EER, a prosody-preserving humanoid motion curation framework that jointly optimizes kinematic feasibility and speech-motion temporal alignment during retargeting. Building upon the curated robot-native motion dataset, we further introduce PhysDrift, an embodiment-aware co-speech motion generation framework that directly predicts executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech without relying on intermediate human-body representations. Unlike conventional human-centric pipelines, PhysDrift maintains embodiment consistency throughout both training and inference while incorporating physical regularization to stabilize robot motion dynamics. Extensive experiments and real-world humanoid deployment demonstrate that embodiment-aware robot-native generation substantially improves speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability.

</details>


### [140] [Advancing DialNav through Automatic Embodied Dialog Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19948)
*Leekyeung Han,Sangwon Jung,Hyunji Min,Jinseong Jeong,Minyoung Kim,Paul Hongsuck Seo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes RAINbow, a large-scale training dataset (238k episodes) for DialNav by automatically converting VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog, plus two training advances (dual-strategy navigation training and a VLN-leveraged localization model) to achieve state-of-the-art results on Val Seen and Val Unseen.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the critical scarcity of training data for dialog-grounded embodied navigation, which limits the performance of dialogue-execution loops in photorealistic indoor environments.

Method: 1) An automatic pipeline that converts existing VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog to create the RAINbow dataset (238k episodes). 2) Dual-Strategy Training to align navigation training with the dynamic dialog-navigation loop. 3) A localization model that leverages VLN knowledge. These are combined to train a model for DialNav.

Result: Significant performance gains over the baseline, achieving 58.24% Success on Val Seen (+89%) and 29.05% on Val Unseen (+100%), establishing a new state of the art.

Conclusion: The automatic generation of a large, high-quality dialog dataset together with complementary training strategies substantially enhances dialog-grounded embodied navigation performance, reducing data bottlenecks and unlocking the data’s potential.

Abstract: For embodied agents capable of physical interaction, the capability to create and understand dialog is crucial to ensure both safety and effectiveness. While DialNav~\cite{han2025dialnav} provides a framework for holistic evaluation of the dialog--execution loop in photorealistic indoor navigation, its performance remains limited by a critical scarcity of training data (2K episodes). To address this, we propose an automatic generation pipeline, and construct the \textbf{RAINbow} dataset, a large-scale training dataset with 238K episodes for DialNav. Our pipeline converts existing VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog and creates cost-efficient and high-quality dataset. Then, we introduce two additional complementary advances to unlock the data's full potential: (1) Dual-Strategy Training, a navigation training scheme to align the navigation training with the dynamic dialog-navigation loop, and (2) a localization model that leverages VLN knowledge. By combining these complementary solutions, our model substantially outperforms the baseline in success rate on both \textbf{Val Seen} (58.24, \textbf{+89\%}) and \textbf{Val Unseen} (29.05, \textbf{+100\%}) splits, establishing a new state of the art.

</details>


### [141] [ENPIRE: Agentic Robot Policy Self-Improvement in the Real World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19980)
*Wenli Xiao,Jia Xie,Tonghe Zhang,Haotian Lin,Letian "Max" Fu,Haoru Xue,Jalen Lu,Yi Yang,Cunxi Dai,Zi Wang,Jimmy Wu,Guanzhi Wang,S. Shankar Sastry,Ken Goldberg,Linxi "Jim" Fan,Yuke Zhu,Guanya Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ENPIRE: a closed-loop harness enabling autonomous, real-world policy improvement for dexterous robotics via four modules (Environment, Policy Improvement, Rollout, Evolution); achieves up to 99% success on tasks with potential fleet scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between coding agents' success in simulation and real-world robotic learning; reduce dependence on human intervention; enable repeatable feedback loop for physical policy improvement.

Method: Introduce ENPIRE framework with four modules that automate resets/verification, policy refinement, policy evaluation across one or more robots, and learning from logs/literature to address failure modes. Iterative loop: reset-execute-verify-refine; includes ablations; demonstrations on tasks like pin box, zip tie, tool use; performance improved with fleet.

Result: Demonstrates a closed-loop real-world learning pipeline; achieves ~99% success on dexterous tasks; faster scalability with robot fleet; claims practicality and scalability.

Conclusion: ENPIRE offers a practical, scalable path for autonomous, real-world robotics advancement via coding agents, reducing human effort and enabling systematic ablations and improvements; potential for broader deployment.

Abstract: Achieving dexterous robotic manipulation in the real world heavily relies on human supervision and algorithm engineering, which becomes a central bottleneck in the pursuit of general physical intelligence. Although emerging coding agents can generate code to automate algorithm search, their successes remain largely confined in digital environments. We conjecture that the missing abstraction to automate robotics research is a repeatable feedback loop for real-world policy improvement: reset the scene, execute a policy, verify the outcome, and refine the next iteration. To bridge this gap, we introduce ENPIRE, a harness framework for coding agents that instantiates this physical feedback routine with four core modules: an Environment module (EN) for automatic reset and verification, a Policy Improvement module (PI) that launches policy refinement, a Rollout module (R) to evaluate policies with one or multiple physical robots operating in parallel, and an Evolution module (E) in which coding agents analyze logs, consult literature, improve training infrastructure and algorithm code to address failure modes. This closed-loop system transforms real-world manipulation learning into a controllable optimization procedure, minimizing human effort while allowing fair ablations across training recipe and agent variants. Powered by ENPIRE, frontier coding agents can autonomously train a policy to achieve a 99% success rate on challenging, dexterous manipulation tasks, such as organizing a pin box, fastening a zip tie, and tool use, a process that further accelerates when we dispatch an agent team on a robot fleet. Our results suggest a practical and scalable path toward deploying coding agents to autonomously advancing robotics in the physical world.

</details>


### [142] [Reward as An Agent for Embodied World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19990)
*Pu Li,Zhigang Lin,Qiang Wu,Yongxuan Lv,Fei Wang,Shan You*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel RL framework combines agentic reward verification with dynamic rollout diversification to enable broader, safer exploration in embodied world models, addressing reward hacking and distribution shifts while achieving accuracy gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conservative rollout strategies in model-based RL constrain exploration and behavioral diversity. Reward hacking under distribution shifts further degrades performance. Embodied world models provide a stringent testbed with physical plausibility constraints.

Method: Introduce Reward as an Agent (agentic reward) to actively evaluate and shape rewards for generated behaviors, mitigating reward hacking. Develop DynDiff-GRPO (Dynamic-Aware Rollout Diversification via DynDiff-GRPO) to expand action-space exploration, diversify trajectories, and broaden state-action coverage in embodied settings. Unite these components to enable RL on a more reliable reward foundation with diversified sampling.

Result: RL improvements observed across multiple open-source world models with substantial accuracy gains and reduced reward hacking, indicating broader exploration can scale reliably when coupled with robust verification.

Conclusion: Broader, reliable exploration is feasible and beneficial in embodied world models when guided by robust verification (Reward as an Agent) and dynamic-aware rollout diversification (DynDiff-GRPO).

Abstract: While RL has become a promising tool for refining world models, existing methods largely rely on conservative rollouts near the training distribution, limiting exploration, behavioral diversity, and richer dynamic discovery. In this work, we challenge this conservative paradigm. We argue that the core limitation is not exploration itself, but the lack of reliable verification strategies to support broader exploration. Without reliable verification, expanded exploration becomes highly susceptible to reward hacking, where policies exploit imperfect rewards without achieving genuine improvement. To evaluate this motivation, we instantiate our method in embodied world models, where physical plausibility, and task completion provide a rigorous testbed for scalable RL under complex dynamics. On the verification side, we introduce Reward as an Agent, an agentic reward framework that actively evaluates generated behaviors to provide robust reward signals and mitigate reward hacking under distribution shifts. On the exploration side, we introduce Dynamic-Aware Rollout Diversification through DynDiff-GRPO, which explicitly expands action-space exploration to diversify trajectories, broaden state-action coverage, and encourage richer embodied behaviors beyond conservative rollout regimes. By unifying Reward as an Agent with DynDiff-GRPO, we enable RL on a more reliable reward foundation with substantially diversified sampling, effectively mitigating reward hacking while yielding significant accuracy gains across multiple open-source world models, thereby demonstrating that broader exploration can scale successfully when grounded in robust verification.

</details>


### [143] [Autonomous Event-Driven Multi-Agent Orchestration for Enterprise AI at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20058)
*Harsh Rao Dhanyamraju,Leonidas Raghav,Aaron Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Enterprise AI orchestration across DAG Plan & Execute and ReAct evaluated at enterprise scale; Task Manager improves latency and related-event correctness; scale dominates performance; discovery noise is the primary bottleneck; DAG Plan & Execute has higher overhead at scale; ReAct offers incremental fault handling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand scaling of multi-agent enterprise orchestration beyond simple request-response and identify bottlenecks and effective components in large-scale deployment.

Method: Empirical evaluation across 208 production-derived enterprise scenarios spanning Persona (<10 agents), Department (20-80), and Enterprise (200) scales, comparing DAG Plan and Execute with ReAct; introduce a Task Manager enabling priority inference, related-event merging, and preemption for continuous operation.

Result: Scale, not task complexity, dominates orchestration performance: both architectures perform well at small scale but degrade at enterprise scale due to agent-discovery noise; simple tasks degrade more than complex tasks. DAG Plan and Execute provides higher precision and structured parallelization at smaller scales but incurs overhead that worsens at enterprise scale; ReAct is more robust by handling failures incrementally. Task Manager reduces high-priority queue latency by 14-75% and improves related-event correctness by over 20 percentage points at enterprise scale.

Conclusion: At enterprise scale, orchestration performance is primarily governed by scale-driven discovery noise; architecture choice involves tradeoffs—DAG Plan and Execute offers precision and parallelization at low scales but higher overhead, while ReAct provides incremental fault tolerance; the Task Manager yields meaningful gains in latency and correctness, underscoring the importance of prioritization, event-merging, and preemption in large-scale multi-agent systems.

Abstract: Enterprise AI aims to move toward continuous event monitoring, detection, and action across specialist agents, yet existing multi-agent systems largely assume discrete request-response workflows and remain underexplored at enterprise scale. We evaluate DAG Plan and Execute and ReAct across 208 production-derived enterprise scenarios spanning Persona (<10 agents), Department (20-80), and Enterprise (200) scales, and introduce a Task Manager for continuous operation via priority inference, related-event merging, and preemption. Results show that scale, not task complexity, dominates orchestration performance: both architectures perform well at small scale but degrade at enterprise scale as agent discovery noise becomes the primary bottleneck, with simple tasks degrading more sharply than complex ones. DAG Plan and Execute offers higher precision and structured parallelization at smaller scales, but its higher overhead worsens at enterprise scale; ReAct is more robust by handling failures incrementally. The Task Manager reduces high-priority queue latency by 14-75% and improves related-event correctness by over 20 percentage points at enterprise scale.

</details>


### [144] [Process-Verified Reinforcement Learning for Theorem Proving via Lean](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20068)
*Minsu Kim,Se-Young Yun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Lean as a dense, verifier-grounded process oracle provides tactic-level rewards for RL in formal reasoning, surpassing outcome-only signals on standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between structured feedback from proof assistants and sparse reinforcement signals; leverage type-theoretic verification to guide learning.

Method: Parse proofs into tactic sequences; use Lean elaboration to mark locally sound steps and earliest failure; integrate structured rewards into a GRPO-style RL objective with first-error propagation and first-token credit; evaluate with STP-Lean and DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5 on MiniF2F and ProofNet.

Result: Tactic-level supervision outperforms outcome-only baselines in most settings, yielding improvements on MiniF2F and ProofNet.

Conclusion: Symbolic proof assistants can act as process-level reward oracles during training, enabling RL frameworks that combine large-language-model scalability with reliable symbolic verification for formal reasoning.

Abstract: While reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) typically has relied on a single binary verification signal, symbolic proof assistants in formal reasoning offer rich, fine-grained structured feedback. This gap between structured processes and unstructured rewards highlights the importance of feedback that is both dense and sound. In this work, we demonstrate that the Lean proof assistant itself can serve as a symbolic process oracle, supplying both outcome-level and fine-grained tactic-level verified feedback during training. Proof attempts are parsed into tactic sequences, and Lean's elaboration marks both locally sound steps and the earliest failing step, yielding dense, verifier-grounded credit signals rooted in type theory. We incorporate these structured rewards into a GRPO-style reinforcement learning objective with first-error propagation and first-token credit methods that balances outcome- and process-level advantages. Experiments with STP-Lean and DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5 show that tactic-level supervision outperforms outcome-only baselines in most settings, delivering improvements on benchmarks such as MiniF2F and ProofNet. Beyond empirical gains, our study highlights a broader perspective: symbolic proof assistants are not only verifiers at evaluation time, but can also act as process-level reward oracles during training. This opens a path toward reinforcement learning frameworks that combine the scalability of language models with the reliability of symbolic verification for formal reasoning.

</details>


### [145] [Residual-Space Evolutionary Optimization via Flow-based Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20084)
*Zhuo Cao,Lena Krieger,Fernanda Nader,Xuan Zhao,Hanno Scharr,Ira Assent*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces residual-space evolutionary optimization for flow-based data editing, combining flow-based editing with evolutionary search; separates local exploitation and global exploration via self-pollination and cross-pollination within residual space; validated on MorphoMNIST and crystal data; demonstrates balancing target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity; extends to non-image domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap in flow-based generative editing where objectives are non-differentiable or black-box, making gradient-based search unsuitable; aims for a model-agnostic editing framework.

Method: Operates in residual space by leveraging conditional flow matching (CFM) to disentangle condition-controlled factors from instance-specific residuals; introduces two complementary search regimes—self-pollination (local, feature-preserving residual refinement) and cross-pollination (global exploration via recombining residuals across heterogeneous samples); pairs flow-based editing with evolutionary algorithms in a model-agnostic setup.

Result: Proof-of-concept validation on MorphoMNIST (counterfactual generation) and crystal data; demonstrates that the exploration–exploitation decomposition balances target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity; shows applicability beyond images to real-world scientific domains.

Conclusion: The residual-space evolutionary optimization framework provides a versatile, model-agnostic mechanism for flow-based editing with non-differentiable objectives, achieving a balance between target control and instance diversity, and generalizes beyond imaging to scientific datasets; lays groundwork for broader adoption and future improvements.

Abstract: Data editing with generative methods typically requires differentiable objectives and gradient-based search. However, these assumptions break down in flow-based settings, where edits are performed through forward and backward integration and often involve non-differentiable or black-box objectives. We introduce residual-space evolutionary optimization, a model-agnostic framework that addresses this gap by combining flow-based generative editing with evolutionary algorithms. Building on the observation that conditional flow matching (CFM) can disentangle condition-controlled factors from instance-specific residuals, our framework directly operates in residual space and separates two complementary search regimes: self-pollination performs local exploitation through feature-preserving residual refinement, and cross-pollination promotes broader exploration by recombining residuals across heterogeneous samples. As a proof of concept, we validate on MorphoMNIST, a benchmark dataset for counterfactual generation, and on crystal data, demonstrating that this exploration--exploitation decomposition provides a useful mechanism for balancing target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity, and extends beyond images to real-world scientific domains.

</details>


### [146] [Multi-Head Attention-Based Feature Extractor Integration with Soft Actor-Critic for Porosity Prediction and Process Parameter Optimization in Additive Manufacturing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20087)
*Kianoush Aqabakee,Leonardo Stella*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Continuous-action SAC with multi-head attention improves convergence for laser powder bed fusion optimization, outperforming discrete-action RL baselines; achieves high final reward quickly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete-action RL struggles with slow convergence and local minima in high-precision manufacturing tasks; need continuous-action, richer feature extraction to improve exploration-exploitation balance.

Method: Use continuous action space with an attention-based feature extractor integrated into Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). Apply to porosity prediction and process-parameter optimization in laser powder bed fusion (LPBF). Compare against DQN, PPO, TD3, and vanilla SAC.

Result: Faster convergence and higher final reward values than baseline methods; reported convergence value of 322.79 within 14 episodes, with stable training.

Conclusion: Attention-enhanced continuous-action SAC improves optimization efficiency and stability for LPBF porosity control, outperforming standard RL methods; further validation and ablation needed to generalize and assess robustness.

Abstract: Additive manufacturing process optimization requires precise parameter control to minimize defects such as porosity. Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) approaches using discrete action spaces suffer from slow convergence and susceptibility to local optima, limiting their effectiveness for high-precision manufacturing tasks. This study addresses these limitations by employing a continuous action space combined with a novel architecture that integrates a multi-head attention mechanism with the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm. The attention-based feature extractor enhances the agent's ability to capture subtle variations in low-dimensional input features, enabling more effective exploration-exploitation balance for navigating value spaces with local minima. We validate our approach on porosity prediction and process parameter optimization in laser powder bed fusion, demonstrating faster convergence and higher final reward values compared to standard RL methods including DQN, PPO, TD3, and vanilla SAC. The proposed methodology achieves a convergence value of 322.79 within 14 episodes, outperforming existing approaches while maintaining stability throughout training.

</details>


### [147] [ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization for Open-Ended Deep Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20122)
*Zhibang Yang,Xinke Jiang,Yuzhen Xiao,Ruizhe Zhang,Yue Fang,XinFei Wan,Zhengxing Song,Yuxuan Liu,Yuheng Huang,Xu Chu,Junfeng Zhao,Yasha Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A dynamic, utility-guided outline optimization framework (ScaffoldAgent) for open-ended deep research (OEDR) that uses three outline operations (Expansion, Contraction, Revision) to adapt the report scaffold during multi-round retrieval, improving long-form generation and factual grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches either fix the outline or apply local heuristics, leading to scaffold drift as information accumulates and delayed feedback for outline updates. There is a need for a dynamic, feedback-driven outline management that aligns with downstream generation and retrieval gains.

Method: Proposes ScaffoldAgent, which treats outline evolution as a structured decision process with Expansion, Contraction, and Revision operations. Introduces a utility-guided feedback mechanism that estimates downstream value of each outline operation based on retrieval gain, structural coherence, and trial-generation quality. The utility signal guides node selection, operation scheduling, and termination during inference.

Result: Empirical results on DeepResearch Bench and DeepResearch Gym show ScaffoldAgent consistently improves long-form report generation and factual grounding compared with existing deep research agents.

Conclusion: Dynamic, utility-guided outline optimization effectively mitigates scaffold drift in OEDR, improving coherence and factual grounding in long-form reports across benchmark datasets.

Abstract: Open-ended deep research (OEDR) requires systems to acquire knowledge through multi-round retrieval and generate coherent long-form reports. The outline plays a central role as a structural scaffold that coordinates retrieval, evidence organization, and generation. However, existing methods either fix the outline before writing or refine it with local heuristics, leading to scaffold drift under continuous information accumulation and delayed feedback for evaluating outline modifications. We propose ScaffoldAgent, a utility-guided dynamic outline optimization framework for OEDR. ScaffoldAgent models outline evolution as a structured decision process with three operations: Expansion, Contraction, and Revision, enabling controlled updates to the report scaffold. It further introduces a utility-guided feedback mechanism that estimates the downstream value of each outline operation from retrieval gain, structural coherence, and trial-generation quality. The resulting utility signal guides node selection, operation scheduling, and termination during inference. Experiments on DeepResearch Bench and DeepResearch Gym show that ScaffoldAgent consistently improves long-form report generation and factual grounding over existing deep research agents.

</details>


### [148] [Learning to Prompt: Improving Student Engagement with Adaptive LLM-based High-School Tutoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20138)
*Po-Chin Chang,Nicholas Hogan,Aske Plaat,Michiel T. van der Meer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Subject-aware prompting with a 14-feature pedagogical router improves LLM tutoring; sim-to-real transfer achieved; stochastic routing yields best conversion; reduces turns while maintaining quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static, domain-agnostic prompts limit adaptation across disciplines. A subject-aware, feature-based prompt-routing system trained in simulation is proposed to tailor pedagogy to student needs and subject matter, with real-world deployment to high-school students.

Method: Extract 14 pedagogical features from transcripts to form a prompt-routing model. Train in a simulation environment, then deploy online with actual high-school students. Compare against static baselines in simulation and conduct A/B testing (N=656 conversations from 359 students). Evaluate with (i) conversion metrics (exercise completion), (ii) interaction turns, and (iii) strategy type (greedy vs stochastic).

Result: In simulation, router outperforms static baselines (0.694 vs 0.647 and 0.64; p<0.001). In online A/B (sim-to-real transfer), adaptive prompt selection reduces interactions by ~3 turns (p=0.007). Greedy router achieves similar exercise conversion to baseline (19.1% vs 19.6%); a stochastic router with strategy sampling increases conversion to 28.1%.

Conclusion: Subject-aware, adaptive prompting can sustain pedagogical quality while improving instructional efficiency; stochastic routing provides higher conversion; sim-to-real transfer is viable for adaptive LLM tutoring.

Abstract: LLMs can personalize education, although current static-prompt tutoring systems struggle to adapt to diverse academic disciplines. We develop and test a system with subject-aware prompting, based on 14 pedagogical features (e.g., tutor scaffolding, student understanding) extracted from raw transcripts. We first train a prompt routing model in a simulation environment, and then deploy it for online adaptation with actual high-school students. The simulation benchmark shows the router outperforming two static baselines ($0.694$ vs. $0.647$ and $0.64$, $p<0.001$). A/B testing ($N=656$ conversations from 359 students) shows sim-to-real transfer where the model switches from analytical to scaffolding learning strategies. Our adaptive prompt selection mechanism improves instructional efficiency, maintains pedagogical quality and reduces interactions by around 3 turns ($p=0.007$). While a greedy router achieves a comparable exercise conversion rate with the baseline ($19.1\%$ vs. $19.6\%$), a stochastic router that samples strategies leads to a higher conversion rate ($28.1\%$).

</details>


### [149] [RACL: Reasoning-Agent Control Layers for Continuous Metaheuristic Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20142)
*Antón Asla Manzárraga*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RACL is a reasoning-agent control layer that sits above a metaheuristic optimizer to discover, validate, and explain internal search policies, improving performance on vehicle routing without altering constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable explainable, learnable control policy discovery for metaheuristics, allowing a reasoning agent to adapt search behavior without modifying the solver or business constraints.

Method: Introduce RACL architecture: a reasoning agent observes operational memory, reasons about past behavior, formulates bounded hypotheses, tests interventions, evaluates outcomes, applies guardrails, consolidates policies, and explains decisions. Codex is used as an in-loop reasoner. A policy proxy is used for reproducibility. The experimental setting uses vehicle routing with no new solver nor specific routing rules.

Result: RACL improves or ties the Operational Memory Policy in 21/21 feasible cases and improves or ties a non-reasoning Stagnation-Triggered Policy in 18/21 feasible cases. Average cost delta vs STP is -0.641%. In Sevilla-9/10 runtime, RACL reduces average cost by -8.337% vs Fixed and -1.605% vs STP, with no material computational overhead. The proof-of-concept used Codex as in-loop reasoning agent; policy proxy enabled reproducibility.

Conclusion: RACL demonstrates a general, explainable, learnable control-layer approach for metaheuristics that does not modify core solvers or constraints, and can reproduce results via a policy proxy; it is a proof-of-concept rather than a new solver.

Abstract: This paper introduces RACL, a Reasoning-Agent Control Layer for metaheuristics. RACL places a reasoning agent above an existing optimizer. The agent does not replace the optimizer and does not modify business constraints. Instead, it controls the optimizer's internal search behavior by observing operational memory, reasoning over past behavior, formulating bounded hypotheses, testing interventions, evaluating outcomes, applying guardrails, consolidating useful policies and explaining its decisions. The experiment uses vehicle routing as a testbed, but the contribution is not a new routing solver, a particular ALNS configuration or a specific set of routing rules. The contribution is the RACL method: a way for a reasoning agent to discover, validate, consolidate and explain algorithmic control rules for a metaheuristic. In the current experimental setting, RACL improves or ties the Operational Memory Policy in 21 of 21 feasible cases and improves or ties a non-reasoning Stagnation-Triggered Policy in 18 of 21 feasible cases, with an average RACL vs STP cost delta of -0.641%. In the Sevilla-9/10 runtime sample, RACL improves average cost by -8.337% versus Fixed and -1.605% versus STP without showing material computational overhead. During the proof-of-concept, Codex was used as an in-the-loop reasoning agent observing executions, interpreting logs and proposing live bounded interventions. The policy proxy was later used only to make quantitative evaluation reproducible.

</details>


### [150] [BIM-Edit: Benchmarking Large Language Models for IFC-Based Building Information Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20146)
*Bharathi Kannan Nithyanantham,Clemens Kujat,Tobias Sesterhenn,Stefan Telgmann,Jörn Plönnigs,Stefan Lüdtke,Christian Bartelt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes BIM-Edit, a benchmark to test LLMs on editing BIM/IFC models using natural language. It includes 324 tasks across 11 real buildings and 36 synthetic scenes, evaluated on geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency; best model ~49.5% average, with most tasks unresolved, highlighting a gap to meet engineering workflow needs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between LLMs' capability to generate new geometry and the need to understand, edit, and preserve semantics/relations in existing BIM/IFC models within CAD workflows.

Method: Design of BIM-Edit benchmark: 324 editing tasks across 11 building models and 36 synthetic scenes. Tasks categorized into direct, spatial, and topological edits, covering explicit and scene-grounded changes. Evaluation along three dimensions: geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency.

Result: Across evaluated LLMs, the best average score is 49.5% across the three metrics; no model fully solves more than 3.4% of tasks.

Conclusion: There is a substantial gap between current LLM capabilities and the requirements of structured engineering design workflows; existing benchmarks insufficiently capture the need to edit and maintain semantics/topology in BIM/IFC models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to computer-aided design (CAD) to generate design artifacts from textual instructions. In engineering practice, this requires more than creating new geometry, models must also understand existing scenes, edit them correctly, and preserve semantics and relations. However, many CAD benchmarks focus on creating new models rather than editing existing ones, and mostly evaluate geometric correctness. We introduce BIM-Edit, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on natural-language editing of Building Information Models (BIM) represented in the Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) format. BIM provides a challenging testbed because building models encode geometry together with semantic and relational structure. BIM-Edit contains 324 editing tasks spanning 11 realistic building models and 36 synthetic scenes. Tasks are expressed using three instruction categories - direct, spatial, and topological - covering both explicit and scene-grounded edits.
