We evaluate outputs along three dimensions: geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency. Across evaluated LLMs, the best-performing model achieves only 49.5% average score across the three metrics, and no model fully solves more than 3.4% of tasks. These results demonstrate a substantial gap between current LLM capabilities and the requirements of structured engineering design workflows.

</details>


### [151] [Modularity-Free Conflict-Averse Training for Generalized PINNs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20156)
*Heejo Kong,Beomchul Park,Sung-Jin Kim,Seong-Whan Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Overparameterized PINNs can self-partition into task-exclusive modules that reduce cross-objective coupling, causing training failures. The authors propose Modular-Sparsity Synchronization (ModSync) to penalize exclusive connections while preserving interaction pathways, improving robustness and accuracy across PDE benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While conflict-averse optimization reduces gradient interference in PINNs, its effectiveness declines with increasing model capacity due to capacity-induced modularity and suppressed cross-objective interaction. A remedy is needed to maintain cross-task coupling as networks grow.

Method: Introduce ModSync, a framework that integrates structural optimization into conflict-averse PINN training. It penalizes task-exclusive connections to discourage modularity while preserving pathways that promote cross-objective interactions, effectively synchronizing sparse structures across tasks.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse PDE benchmarks show ModSync prevents capacity-driven failures, sustains cross-objective coupling, and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy. Code is released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Capacity-aware structural sparsity, enforced via ModSync, counteracts functional modularity in overparameterized PINNs and yields robust, high-accuracy multi-objective optimization on PDEs; the approach is a promising direction for stable physics-informed multi-task learning.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have become a powerful framework for solving PDEs by embedding physical laws into differentiable objectives. Despite their advances, training PINNs remains fragile: recent conflict-averse optimization schemes alleviate gradient interference between residual and boundary losses, but we show that their effectiveness deteriorates as model capacity increases. In this paper, we identify a capacity-induced failure mode, where overparameterized networks undergo functional modularity, self-partitioning into task-exclusive modules that suppress cross-objective interaction and hinder convergence toward Pareto-stationary points. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework, Modular-Sparsity Synchronization (ModSync), which integrates structural optimization into conflict-averse training by penalizing task-exclusive connections while preserving interaction-promoting pathways. Extensive experiments across diverse PDE benchmarks demonstrate that ModSync consistently prevents capacity-driven failures, sustains robust cross-objective coupling, and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy. Codes are available at \url{https://github.com/heejokong/ModSync}.

</details>


### [152] [Implicit Semantic-Aware Communication Based on Hypergraph Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20162)
*Yiwei Liao,Shurui Tu,Yong Xiao,Yingyu Li,Guangming Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hypergraph-based semantic reasoning (HISR) uses hypergraphs to model higher-order relationships among semantic entities, improving robustness and interpretation accuracy in semantic communication, outperforming pairwise-graph methods by up to 36.6%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based representations capture only pairwise relationships, missing higher-order correlations (e.g., group interactions, multi-entity contexts). This limits semantic expressiveness, increases ambiguity under noise, and degrades performance. A hypergraph framework aims to better model complex relations and enhance robustness when information is partially lost.

Method: Propose HISR, mapping semantic entities and higher-order relations into dedicated subspaces per relational context to disentangle interactions and mitigate over-smoothing; leverage hypergraph structures to support robust semantic inference under partial information loss.

Result: Numerical results report up to 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over state-of-the-art benchmarks, indicating enhanced semantic expressiveness and noise robustness.

Conclusion: Hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning yields a more expressive and robust semantic communication framework, addressing the limitations of pairwise graphs and enabling improved performance in realistic noisy channel conditions.

Abstract: Semantic-aware communication has emerged as a transformative paradigm for next-generation communication systems, shifting the fundamental goal from transmitting bit-level symbols to reliably recovering and understanding the semantic meaning of information. Previous studies have demonstrated that representing the semantic content of source messages as graph-based structures can significantly improve communication efficiency and the accuracy of semantic inference at the receiver. However, existing solutions typically employ graphs that capture only pairwise relationships, thereby neglecting higher-order implicit correlations commonly observed in real-world scenarios, such as group interactions, multi-entity associations, and complex relational contexts. This limitation reduces semantic expressiveness and makes semantic inference susceptible to ambiguity and performance degradation, particularly under noisy or corrupted channel conditions. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning framework, HISR, which leverages hypergraphs to represent complex multi-entity relationships among semantic knowledge entities. In HISR, entities and their associated higher-order relations are mapped into dedicated semantic subspaces tailored to distinct relational contexts. This design not only disentangles diverse semantic interactions to mitigate the over-smoothing effects commonly found in traditional graph embedding methods but also enables robust semantic inference even when partial information loss occurs during transmission. Numerical results show that the proposed HISR achieves up to a 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over the state-of-the-art benchmarks.

</details>


### [153] [Apparent Psychological Profiles of Large Language Models are Largely a Measurement Artifact](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20205)
*Jelena Meyer,David Garcia,Dirk U. Wulff*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs’ psychological profiles produced by human psychometrics are mostly artifacts of directional response bias rather than true traits; 81-90% of between-model variation is due to bias, which declines with capability but is not eliminated; reliability hinges on response orthogonality (trait-bias alignment), and profiles shift with item selection, suggesting instruments may be invalid for LLMs and urging bias-centered assessments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether standard human psychometrics yield genuine personality/risk profiles for instruction-tuned LLMs or merely artifacts, and to evaluate measurement validity across models and humans.

Method: Administer a battery of self-report and behavioral-variance instruments to 56 instruction-tuned LLMs and human references; perform variance decomposition to separate trait vs. bias contributions; assess how bias changes with model capability; introduce and quantify 'response orthogonality'; examine how instrument choice and item selection affect apparent profiles.

Result: Between-model differences are driven predominantly by directional bias rather than target traits (81-90% of variance); bias decreases with model capability but persists; instrument reliability correlates with response orthogonality; profiles shift with item sets and can be manufactured via item selection; biases suggest artifacts rather than stable LLM traits.

Conclusion: Human-psychometric instruments may be inappropriate for LLMs due to non-orthogonal responses; dedicated assessments focusing on response orthogonality are needed to obtain valid measurements of LLM behavior and preferences.

Abstract: Psychological instruments designed for humans are increasingly used to assign large language models (LLMs) stable psychological profiles that affect their usability, safety assessment, and use as proxies for human participants in research. Using a formal psychometric framework, we show that these profiles are largely a measurement artifact. Administering a battery of personality and risk-preference instruments spanning self-reports and behavioral tasks to 56 instruction-tuned LLMs alongside large human reference samples, we report four findings. First, differences between models are driven not by the traits an instrument targets but by a directional response bias, a tendency to respond toward one end of the scale, or one labeled option, regardless of item content; a variance decomposition attributes 81-90% of between-model variation to this bias, against 9-16% in humans. Second, the bias declines with model capability but is not eliminated by it. Third, because bias rather than trait drives responding, an instrument's apparent reliability is almost entirely predicted by its response orthogonality, a term we coin for the proportion of items for which trait and bias point in opposite directions. Fourth, the profile a model appears to have shifts with the items used and can be manufactured through item selection. These results demonstrate that the apparent psychological profiles of LLMs are artifacts of the instrument used to measure them, not properties of the models themselves. As instruments borrowed from human psychology are rarely fully orthogonal and may inherently lack validity for LLMs, we call for dedicated assessments centered on response orthogonality.

</details>


### [154] [Beyond Accuracy: Measuring Logical Compliance of Predictive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20208)
*Guillaume Olivier Delplanque,Pierre Genevès,Nabil Layaïda,Zephirin Faure*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new metric, Rule Violation Score (RVS), quantifies how well a model complies with predefined logical rules, in addition to predictive performance, using distinct handling for hard vs. soft rules. It is applicable to any relational model and can be computed via automatically generated SQL for Horn rules. RVS can also evaluate training data and rule quality, and reveals differences in logical compliance among models with similar accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard predictive metrics ignore whether model outputs respect logical or domain-specific constraints, which is critical in high-stakes domains. RVS provides a complementary assessment of logical consistency and dataset/rule quality.

Method: Define RVS with separate treatment of hard (strict) and soft (statistical) rules. Enable evaluation on any dataset and relational vocabulary. Generate SQL queries automatically to compute violation scores for Horn rules. Extend assessment to training data to diagnose rule definitions. Validate on benchmarks including knowledge graph link prediction and relational regression across rule-based, embedding-based, and neuro-symbolic models.

Result: RVS can be computed on a given model and dataset to yield a score reflecting logical compliance independent of predictive accuracy. Empirical evaluation shows that models with similar predictive performance can have substantially different RVS, exposing behavioral differences not captured by standard metrics. RVS also helps identify poorly defined rules and quality issues in datasets.

Conclusion: RVS is a valuable complementary metric to predictive accuracy, enabling assessment and debugging of logical consistency in models and data, with broad applicability to relational domains and compatibility with Horn-rule-based evaluation.

Abstract: Machine learning models are predominantly evaluated through predictive performance metrics such as ranking quality, prediction error, or classification accuracy. While these metrics effectively quantify how closely predictions match the ground truth, they do not assess whether model outputs respect predefined logical or domain-specific constraints. In high-stakes applications, including healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems, logical consistency can be as critical as predictive accuracy, yet no standard metric captures this dimension. We introduce the Rule Violation Score (RVS), a complementary evaluation metric that quantifies the extent to which a predictive model respects a given set of logical rules, independently of predictive accuracy. RVS treats hard rules (strict constraints) and soft rules (statistical regularities) differently, can be evaluated on any dataset and on any predictive model expressed over a relational vocabulary, and can be computed using SQL queries that are automatically generated for Horn rules. Beyond evaluating models, RVS can also evaluate the logical consistency of training datasets and help identify poorly defined rules. We evaluate RVS on three benchmarks covering knowledge graph link prediction and relational regression, including rule-based, embedding-based, and neuro-symbolic predictive models. Our results demonstrate that two models achieving comparable predictive accuracy can exhibit substantially different levels of logical compliance, revealing differences in model behavior that standard metrics fail to capture.

</details>


### [155] [Augmenting Game AI with Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20210)
*Alessandro Sestini,Joakim Bergdahl,Amir Baghi,Jean-Philippe Barrette-LaPierre,Florian Fuchs,Linus Gisslén*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Advocates using reinforcement learning (RL) to enhance game AI, presenting a framework of game-specific requirements, RL-augmented examples, deployment considerations, and a roadmap of bottlenecks and research directions for broader adoption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current hand-coded game AI struggles to capture complex, human-like behavior; ML, particularly RL, offers the potential to learn from interaction data or player data to create more believable and relatable characters, increasing immersion.

Method: Proposes a framework for training RL models tailored to game AI, outlines requirements for such frameworks, showcases examples of RL-augmented games, and discusses the practicalities of deploying player-facing ML agents; identifies bottlenecks and hard problems as directions for future work.

Result: A conceptual framework and narrative survey rather than new empirical results; provides examples of RL-augmented game AI and a set of deployment considerations, bottlenecks, and promising research directions.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning holds promise to improve realism and engagement in game AI; realizing this potential requires a structured training framework, addressing domain-specific constraints, and targeted research to overcome bottlenecks and enable cross-genre applicability.

Abstract: Immersion in video games depends not only on graphics, audio, and game mechanics, but also on the quality of in-game characters. Producing believable characters, or game AI, remains a significant challenge as behavioral complexity is hard to capture with hand-coded systems. Game AI is a source of immersion and engagement; however, the limitations stemming from the challenges of creating game AI often lead to frustration and the breaking of the illusion of realism within the game. The introduction of machine learning models opens the door to creating more believable, authentic, and relatable characters in games. The promise is that they either learn from interacting with the game, or from player data, to develop true human-like behavior. In this paper, we envision more applications of reinforcement learning for game AI in the future. For this to materialize, current research limitations are prohibitive to broad deployment across game genres. Therefore, we propose a framework for training reinforcement learning models with a set of requirements in mind that are suited towards game AI and game development. We present examples of games with reinforcement learning-augmented game AI and describe the practicalities of deploying player-facing machine learning agents in modern games. Furthermore, we identify bottlenecks and hard problems in these areas, which we believe offer promising research directions to accelerate the adoption of machine learning in game AI for the video game industry.

</details>


### [156] [QMFOL: Benchmarking Large Language Model Reasoning via Quantifiable Monadic First-Order Logic Test Case Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20227)
*Xinyi Zheng,Ling Shi,Tianlong Yu,Yongxin Zhao,Lorenz Goette,Kailong Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes QMFOL framework to generate controllable monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks and QMFOLBench benchmark; uses pattern-based structures and external prover verification; shows model performance degrades with logical complexity and is sensitive to semantic variation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address lack of fine-grained control over logical complexity in reasoning benchmarks and the trade-off between semantic diversity and logical consistency.

Method: Construct formal logical structures using conjunction/disjunction patterns to control depth, width, label types, and distractors; translate to natural language with LLMs; verify consistency with external prover via round-trips; build 2880 instances across 960 configurations; evaluate on LRMs and LLMs.

Result: Performance deteriorates as logical complexity increases; models perform better on True-labeled tasks; semantic variation affects results; framework is scalable and provides reliable benchmarks.

Conclusion: QMFOL offers a scalable, controllable benchmark for deductive reasoning in LLMs, enabling precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in reasoning, particularly in deductive reasoning, which is crucial for high-stakes decision-making. As models improve, evaluation benchmarks should evolve to keep pace. However, existing benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency.
  To address these issues, we propose QMFOL, an automated framework for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with quantifiable and controllable complexity. It constructs formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns, enabling precise control over reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors. These structures are then translated into natural language via LLMs, with logical consistency ensured through round-trip verification using an external prover. Based on our framework, we build QMFOLBench, a benchmark comprising 2880 instances with 960 configurations across diverse logical and semantic dimensions. Evaluations on six large reasoning models (LRMs) and two LLMs show that performance degrades and computational overhead increases with rising logical complexity. Models perform better on True-labeled tasks than on False or Unknown ones, and exhibit sensitivity to semantic variation. Overall, QMFOL offers a scalable and reliable approach for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling more precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

</details>


### [157] [Thermodynamic Measure of Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20231)
*Ishanu Chattopadhyay*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Intelligence is defined as the lawful, thermodynamic amplification of rare but admissible futures; recursive self-simulation is argued to be necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence, enabling a universal, cross-substrate measure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a universal, quantitative framework for intelligence that spans physical systems to humans, grounded in thermodynamics and the manipulation of rare but valid futures, enabling cross-domain measurement and comparison.

Method: The paper formalizes a definition of intelligence around rare-valid lift, and proves a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement linking recursive self-simulation to a thermodynamic measure of lift. It analyzes how high fidelity internal simulation and effective policy drive lift toward actuation-limited optima, arguing for a universal framework that applies from passive matter to intelligent agents.

Result: Key result: high rare-valid lift is impossible without high fidelity internal simulation. Conversely, with high fidelity simulation and a viable policy, lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum. This establishes recursive self-simulation as a necessary and nearly sufficient condition for high thermodynamic intelligence under the stated assumptions.

Conclusion: The authors propose a universal scale to measure intelligence across substrates, connecting information processing, thermodynamics, and agency, and extending the concept to systems ranging from simple physical processes to sophisticated agents like LLMs and humans, as well as Maxwell-demon-like engines.

Abstract: Can intelligence be measured? We propose that intelligence can be defined as the lawful amplification of rare but valid futures: a system increases the probability of outcomes that would be unlikely under passive dynamics but remain admissible under the constraints of the domain. We start with the premise that an intelligent system must model the world and its own place within it. Because the system is part of the world it models, this leads naturally to recursive self-simulation: the system represents futures in which its own actions are part of the trajectory. Our central results give a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement connecting this architecture to a precise thermodynamic measure of lawful amplification of rare-valid futures: high rare-valid lift is impossible unless the internal simulation identifies rare-valid futures with high fidelity; conversely, when rare-valid fidelity is high and the simulation contains an effective policy, the achievable lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum. Thus recursive self-simulation is not merely a plausible feature of intelligence but, under the stated assumptions, is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence. The resulting framework makes intelligence measurable on a universal scale, from passive matter and feedback controllers, large language models, and humans as text generators to Maxwell-demon-like information engines.

</details>


### [158] [A Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20236)
*Federica Filippini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes MAMO, a multi-agent RL approach to learning reward weights for constrained optimization, decoupling constraint handling from execution to adapt to dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses sensitivity to manually tuned Lagrangian-like weights in RL-based constrained optimization, which impedes robust performance in non-stationary settings.

Method: Introduces MAMO (Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization), a multi-agent RL framework that treats reward weight selection as a learnable problem, thereby decoupling objective design from task execution.

Result: Conceptual framework; a proposed algorithmic approach without reported empirical results in the abstract.

Conclusion: MAMO represents a first step toward autonomous, robust RL-based constrained optimization in dynamic environments; future work includes validation and extension to non-stationary contexts.

Abstract: Many decision-making problems in computing and networking systems can be naturally formulated as cost-minimization problems under performance constraints. In dynamic environments, reinforcement learning (RL) is often used to solve such problems at runtime by embedding both costs and constraint violations into a single scalar reward through weighted penalty terms, following a Lagrangian-inspired formulation. However, in this context the behavior of the learned policy critically depends on the choice of these weights, which are typically selected manually. This makes it difficult to identify an appropriate trade-off between optimizing the primary objective and effectively avoiding constraint violations, particularly in non-stationary environments where their relative importance may change. This paper presents MAMO (Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization), an approach to tackle this balancing problem through multi-agent RL. MAMO decouples task execution from objective design by formulating the selection of reward weights as a learning problem, providing a !rst step towards more autonomous and robust RL-based solutions for constrained optimization problems in dynamic environments.

</details>


### [159] [Navigating Unreliable Parametric and Contextual Knowledge: Explicit Knowledge Conflict Resolution for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20245)
*Huang Peng,Jiuyang Tang,Weixin Zeng,Hao Xu,Xiang Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-agent MACR framework resolves conflicts between internal and external knowledge in LLMs via adaptive assessment, knowledge externalization/retrieval, and three specialized agents for rules, analysis, and resolution, achieving superior performance and interpretable explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches assume reliability of at least one information source and resolve conflicts by privileging one side, ignoring that both internal parameters and external prompts may be erroneous. There is a need for explicit, interpretable conflict resolution that integrates multiple sources.

Method: An adaptive knowledge assessment using a modified semantic entropy estimates the model's confidence. If confident, internal knowledge is externalized as textual representations; if not, relevant external knowledge is retrieved to create basic contexts. An inductive multi-agent framework with three agents induces explicit rules, analyzes conflicts, and resolves inconsistencies across all available contexts.

Result: MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on benchmarks and provides interpretable resolutions of explicit conflicts.

Conclusion: The framework advances knowledge integration in LLMs by moving beyond binary accept/reject decisions to explicit, multi-agent conflict resolution, improving performance and interpretability.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across a wide range of language-based tasks by leveraging both extensive parametric knowledge and in-context learning ability, enabling them to incorporate external information provided in the input prompt. However, the integration of external knowledge can introduce conflicts, not only between the model's internal parametric knowledge and the external information, but also among multiple pieces of external contexts. Existing approaches typically assume that either the model or the provided context is reliable, overlooking the possibility that both sources may contain errors, and avoid conflicts by privileging one source over the other, rather than actively resolving inconsistencies. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework MACR for LLM knowledge conflict resolution that moves beyond the conventional binary choice paradigm and incorporates an explicit conflict-resolution mechanism based on a multi-agent reasoning approach. Specifically, we first propose an adaptive knowledge assessment and retrieval approach that employs a modified semantic entropy measure to quantify an LLM's confidence in its answer to a given query. Based on this confidence estimation, MACR either externalizes the model's internal knowledge as textual representations or retrieves relevant external knowledge when internal knowledge is insufficient, generating basic contexts for subsequent reasoning. Then we introduce an inductive multi-agent reasoning framework with three specialized agents that, respectively, induce explicit rules, analyze potential conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies across all available contexts. Empirical results demonstrate that MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across benchmarks, while also providing interpretable resolutions of explicit conflicts.

</details>


### [160] [Confidence-Aware Automated Assessment of Student-Drawn Scientific Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20264)
*Luyang Fang,Yingchuan Zhang,Jongchan Park,Zhaoji Wang,Ping Ma,Xiaoming Zhai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a confidence-aware Vision Transformer approach for automated scoring of student-generated scientific drawings, enabling selective automation by scoring high-confidence responses and deferring uncertain cases to humans; validated on six NGSS-aligned items, showing improved reliability and a practical trade-off between automated coverage and risk.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To scale the assessment of students' conceptual understanding in modeling-based tasks by automating scoring of complex drawings while maintaining trustworthiness through confidence estimates.

Method: Use a Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation for scoring drawings. Develop a confidence-aware scoring framework that derives response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions, enabling selective automation (auto-score high-confidence responses) and human review for uncertain cases.

Result: Experiments on six NGSS-aligned middle school items show improved scoring reliability and a practical balance between automated coverage and scoring risk, highlighting the usefulness of confidence-aware methods in educational assessment.

Conclusion: Confidence-aware ViT-based scoring can make large-scale, trustworthy assessment feasible by automating straightforward cases and flagging uncertain ones for human review.

Abstract: Student-generated drawings are widely used in science education to assess learners' conceptual understanding in modeling-based tasks aligned with the Next Generation Science Standards (NGSS). However, scoring such drawings requires expert human judgment to interpret complex visual representations, making large-scale assessment costly to implement and sustain in classroom settings. In this work, we study automated scoring of student-generated scientific drawings using a vision-based model. We evaluate a Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation and propose a confidence-aware scoring framework that derives response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions. This confidence signal enables selective automation by scoring high-confidence responses automatically while deferring uncertain cases for human review. Experiments on six NGSS-aligned middle school assessment items show that the proposed approach improves scoring reliability while supporting a practical trade-off between automated coverage and scoring risk, highlighting the value of confidence-aware methods for trustworthy educational assessment.

</details>


### [161] [Lagrange: An Open-Vocabulary, Energy-Based Sparse Framework for Generalized End-to-End Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20274)
*Shihao Ji,HongXi Li,Zihui Song,Mingyu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Lagrange is a sparse, open-vocabulary driving framework that uses Masked Latent Fields encoded by vision-language models to generate a continuous energy field for kinematically valid planning, addressing efficiency-generalization trade-offs in autonomous driving and showing strong offline results on nuScenes and CODA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-world autonomous driving requires robust generalization to anomalous scenarios and trajectories that respect vehicle kinematics. Dense perception models struggle with computational costs and semantics, while sparse planners (closed-set) struggle with out-of-distribution events. Open-vocabulary reasoning (VLMs) remains difficult to integrate with continuous control.

Method: Lagrange introduces Masked Latent Fields (MLF) where VLMs encode class-agnostic object proposals into continuous semantic tokens. An intent-driven masked cross-attention module filters irrelevant entities, decoding tokens into an implicit continuous energy field over spatial coordinates. Decision-making is cast as a Lagrangian action minimization problem spanning this energy field, enforcing kinematic constraints and collision avoidance.

Result: Extensive offline evaluations on nuScenes and CODA show that Lagrange provides a robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world driving framework, demonstrating promise for generalization and efficient computation.

Conclusion: The work presents a novel integration of open-vocabulary perception with energy-based, kinematics-aware planning via Masked Latent Fields, offering a promising path toward robust, open-world autonomous driving with favorable interpretability and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Scaling end-to-end autonomous driving to complex, open-world environments requires perceptual models that generalize to anomalous scenarios and planners that produce kinematically valid trajectories. Existing paradigms face a distinct dichotomy between representational efficiency and generalization capacity. Dense models (e.g., occupancy networks), while geometrically robust, incur critical computational bottlenecks and struggle with high-level semantic reasoning. Conversely, sparse, query-based planners are efficient but reliant on closed-set definitions, rendering them vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) events. Although recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer open-vocabulary reasoning, their autoregressive, discrete token generation fundamentally conflicts with the continuous, high-frequency control requirements of vehicle dynamics. To address this, we propose Lagrange, an open-vocabulary, computationally sparse driving framework based on Masked Latent Fields (MLF). Rather than relying on dense volumetric reconstructions or closed-set query mechanisms, Lagrange exploits Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to encode class-agnostic object proposals into continuous semantic visual tokens. We introduce an intent-driven masked cross-attention module that temporally filters irrelevant entities, decoding the attended tokens into an implicit continuous energy field defined over spatial coordinates. By framing decision-making as a Lagrangian action minimization problem spanning this energy field, we enforce strict compliance with vehicle kinematics while executing collision avoidance. Extensive offline evaluations on both standard (nuScenes) and long-tail (CODA) benchmarks demonstrate that Lagrange establishes a promising framework for robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world autonomy.

</details>


### [162] [Leveraging systems' non-linearity to tackle the scarcity of data in the design of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20323)
*Giancarlo Santamato,Andrea Mattia Garavagno,Massimiliano Solazzi,Antonio Frisoli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a data-scarcity-aware deep transfer learning approach for vibration-based fault diagnosis, using a periodic multi-excitation procedure to generate image representations for pre-trained CNNs, with a novel data visualization/augmentation strategy; validated on a railway pantograph.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DTL methods in fault diagnosis often require large labeled datasets, which are hard to obtain for machinery faults in real-world settings; the work tackles data scarcity and aims to leverage pre-trained models.

Method: Introduce a periodic multi-excitation level procedure that leverages intrinsic non-linearities to produce image representations suitable for pre-trained CNNs; develop a new data visualization method and augmentation technique to mitigate scarce data; validate on vibration data from a railway pantograph.

Result: Experimental validation on a railway pantograph shows that the proposed approach effectively supports fault diagnosis under data-scarce conditions, demonstrating practical viability.

Conclusion: The approach addresses data scarcity in vibration-based intelligent fault diagnosis systems using deep transfer learning and image-based CNN analysis, with potential applicability to other industrial systems and non-linear dynamics.

Abstract: Deep Transfer Learning (DTL) allows for the efficient building of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems (IFDS). On the other hand, DTL methods still heavily rely on large amounts of labelled data. Obtaining such an amount of data can be challenging when dealing with machines or structures faults. This document proposes a novel approach to the design of vibration-based IFDS using DTL in condition of strong data scarcity. A periodic multi-excitation level procedure leveraging intrinsic non-linearities of real-world systems is used to produce images that can be conveniently analysed by pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to diagnose faults. A new data visualization method and its augmentation technique are proposed in this paper to tackle the typical lack of data encountered during the design of IFDS. Experimental validation on a railway pantograph structure provides effective support for the proposed method.

</details>


### [163] [SoftSkill: Behavioral Compression for Contextual Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20333)
*Xijia Tao,Yihua Teng,Xinyu Fu,Ziru Liu,Kecheng Chen,Yuzhi Zhao,Suiyun Zhang,Rui Liu,Lingpeng Kong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SoftSkill proposes representing agent skills as compact continuous context prefixes (latent soft skills) learned by next-token prediction on a frozen base model, instead of relying on long natural-language Markdown tokens. This latent approach yields substantial performance gains across tasks, reduces prompt token requirements, and suggests that some skills are better implemented as latent priors that control how a frozen model enters a task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce reliance on lengthy natural-language skill artifacts (Markdown) and to improve efficiency and portability by encoding task skills as compact latent controls that steer a frozen model without changing its weights.

Method: Train a length-32 SoftSkill prefix via next-token prediction on a frozen backbone (e.g., Qwen3.5-4B). Deploy these as latent behavioral priors at inference. Compare against no-skill prompting and Markdown-based SkillOpt across single-round tasks; also examine agentic execution as a harder boundary case.

Result: SoftSkill yields improvements: +8.3 points on SearchQA, +42.1 points on LiveMath, +1.3 points on DocVQA vs no-skill prompting. Relative to SkillOpt: +5.2 points (SearchQA) and +12.5 points (LiveMath). Also reduces required token tokens from hundreds-thousands to a few virtual tokens. In agentic execution, sparse trajectory imitation provides signals but does not robustly compress long-horizon behavior.

Conclusion: Some task skills are better implemented as compact latent controls that guide a frozen model’s entry into a task rather than as extensive natural-language Markdown artifacts; latent soft skills enable strong performance while maintaining a frozen backbone and reducing prompt size.

Abstract: Agent skills are commonly deployed as natural-language Markdown files that encode answer policies, evidence-use habits, and task procedures. These files are readable and portable, but they are consumed indirectly: for each task instance, a frozen language model must translate a long textual artifact into generation-time behavior. This paper asks whether a natural-language skill can instead initialize a compact continuous context object, refined by a trainable soft delta while the base model remains frozen. We propose SoftSkill, a frozen-backbone method that tunes such soft skills with next-token prediction and deploys them as latent behavioral priors at inference time. In our main single-round setting, a length-32 SoftSkill prefix on Qwen3.5-4B improves over no-skill prompting by 8.3 points on SearchQA, 42.1 points on LiveMath, and 1.3 points on DocVQA. Relative to SkillOpt, SoftSkill improves accuracy by 5.2 points on SearchQA and 12.5 points on LiveMath, while replacing hundreds to thousands of Markdown skill tokens with a few virtual tokens. We further study agentic execution as a harder boundary case, where sparse trajectory imitation provides useful signal but does not yet robustly compress long-horizon procedural behavior. More broadly, the results suggest that some task skills are better treated not as additional Markdown to be reinterpreted at inference time, but as compact latent controls over how a frozen model enters the task.

</details>


### [164] [Automating SKILL.md Generation for Computer-Using Agents via Interaction Trajectory Mining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20363)
*Yuexing Hao,Xiaomin Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Trajectory mining yields readable skill clusters but does not meaningfully improve cross-domain policies; readability does not imply transfer; current components limit transferability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether explicitly mined skill libraries from interaction data can translate into improved downstream policies, bridging inspectability with transferability in GUI-task agents.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: segment GUI trajectories, cluster segments into candidate skills, train a skill-aware policy from the resulting annotations; evaluate readability against labels and transfer performance across domains.

Result: Clusters achieve high label purity (five of eight clusters ≥0.95), but transfer yields only modest gains (IW: 18.5%→20.5% in skill-step accuracy; BrowseComp+ unchanged; outperforms neither and is beaten by simple frequency priors).

Conclusion: Trajectory mining reveals inspectable skill structure but the boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement; the approach is diagnostic rather than practical for transfer.

Abstract: Explicit skill libraries make computer-using agents easier to inspect, but it remains unclear whether such libraries can be mined from interaction data in a way that improves downstream policies. We study this question through a three-stage pipeline that segments GUI trajectories, clusters segments into candidate skills, and trains a skill-aware policy from the resulting annotations. The mined clusters are readable on the source benchmark: five of eight clusters have at least 0.95 purity against InteraSkill Workflows labels. However, readability does not imply transfer. GRPO improves IW skill-step accuracy only from 18.5\% to 20.5\%, leaves BrowseComp+ essentially unchanged, and underperforms trivial frequency priors on key source-domain metrics. We therefore present the method as a diagnostic study: trajectory mining can expose inspectable skill structure, but the current boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement.

</details>


### [165] [Rethinking Shrinkage Bias in LLM FP4 Pretraining: Geometric Origin, Systemic Impact, and UFP4 Recipe](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20381)
*Qian Zhao,Kunlong Chen,Changxin Tian,Zhonghui Jiang,Haitao Zhang,Chaofan Yu,Peijie Jiang,Mingliang Gong,Jia Liu,Ziqi Liu,Zhiqiang Zhang,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Non-uniform E2M1 FP4 formats suffer Shrinkage Bias causing training instability; a uniform E1M2/INT4-based FP4 (UFP4) with Random Hadamard Transform (RHT) on all GEMMs improves quantization quality and stability, outperforming E2M1 baselines across Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address substantial memory/computation savings from FP4 in LLM pretraining while resolving grid-geometry-induced bias (Shrinkage Bias) that hinders training stability in current E2M1-centric FP4 hardware paths.

Method: Theoretically identify Shrinkage Bias as a consequence of non-uniform E2M1 bin geometry, show multiplicative error accumulation across layers, and its amplification by Random Hadamard Transform (RHT). Propose UFP4 with uniform E1M2/INT4 grids applying RHT to all three training GEMMs and restricting stochastic rounding to dY. Empirically evaluate on Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B long-run pretraining; perform scaling-law analysis and ablations.

Result: UFP4 achieves lower BF16-relative loss degradation than strong E2M1 baselines across the tested models; uniform grids mitigate grid-geometry error and better convert RHT benefits into quantization quality, as supported by scaling analyses and ablation results.

Conclusion: Future accelerators should treat E1M2/INT4-style uniform 4-bit grids as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1 to enable more stable and efficient FP4-based LLM pretraining.

Abstract: FP4 training promises substantial reductions in memory and computation cost for LLM pretraining, yet current FP4 hardware paths and recipes, including NVIDIA Blackwell/Rubin-class systems and AMD MI350-series GPUs, remain centered on E2M1 data elements. In this study, we identify a fundamental limitation of that choice: non-uniform formats such as E2M1 inherently suffer from Shrinkage Bias, a systematic negative rounding error caused by the geometric asymmetry of their representable bins. We show that this bias accumulates multiplicatively across layers and is amplified by the Random Hadamard Transform (RHT), providing a unified explanation for the training instability observed in existing E2M1-based FP4 recipes. In contrast, uniform grids (E1M2/INT4) bypass this grid-geometry error and better convert the improved bucket utilization from RHT into higher quantization quality. Based on this finding, we propose UFP4, a uniform 4-bit training recipe that applies RHT to all three training GEMMs while restricting stochastic rounding to dY alone. On Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B long-run pretraining, UFP4 consistently achieves lower BF16-relative loss degradation than strong E2M1-based baselines, supported by scaling-law analysis and ablation studies. Our results suggest that future accelerators should support E1M2/INT4-style uniform 4-bit grids as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1.

</details>


### [166] [Interpretable Sperm Morphology Classification via Attention-Guided Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20438)
*Zahra Asghari Varzaneh,Reza Khoshkangini,Thomas Ebner,Lars Johansson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Attention-guided EfficientNet-B0 framework with CBAM for sperm morphology classification; achieves ~90-94% accuracy on SMIDS and HuSHem with Grad-CAM++ interpretation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sperm morphology abnormalities are a key factor in male infertility. There's a need for accurate yet interpretable deep learning tools to aid clinical decision-making.

Method: Pretrained EfficientNet-B0 backbone with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to emphasize sperm head regions. Trained/evaluated on SMIDS and HuSHem datasets. Compared against SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0. Interpretability via Grad-CAM++ visualizations.

Result: Accuracies: 90.2% (SMIDS) and 93.9% (HuSHem). Macro F1: 0.913 and 0.948, respectively. Outperforms baseline models. Grad-CAM++ highlights features driving decisions.

Conclusion: An accurate and transparent framework suitable for automated sperm morphology analysis, with potential clinical application in fertility clinics.

Abstract: Male infertility is a major cause of couple infertility, often linked to abnormal sperm morphology. While deep learning models offer automated analysis, most lack interpretability, limiting their clinical adoption. This study proposes an attention-guided deep learning framework for sperm morphology classification. We combine a pretrained EfficientNet-B0 with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to focus on key areas of the sperm head, improving both accuracy and interpretability. Evaluated on the SMIDS and HuSHem public datasets, our model achieves accuracies of 90.2% and 93.9% (macro F1 scores of 0.913 and 0.948), outperforming SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0. Furthermore, we use Grad-CAM++ visualizations to highlight features influencing the model's decisions. The results demonstrate that this accurate and transparent framework is a practical tool for automated sperm analysis in fertility clinics.

</details>


### [167] [Context-Aware Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of IVF Laboratory Environmental Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20459)
*Zahra Asghari Varzaneh,Reza Khoshkangini,Pia Saldeen,Lars Johansson,Thomas Ebner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Context-aware laboratory environmental features improve IVF outcome predictions and transferability across clinics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution incubator data are underutilized for IVF outcomes; leveraging dynamics of microenvironments can enhance predictive accuracy and generalizability.

Method: Engineer 55 temporal features capturing stability, adherence, stress, and recovery; train cross-validated predictors; use hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression with partial pooling across Asian and Northern European clinics while preserving site baselines.

Result: Cross-validated error reduced to 1.27% (vs 3–5% with raw averages). On held-out Northern European data, R2 = 0.86; 64% error reduction for ages 35–39 vs naive baseline; evidence of transferable, clinically meaningful signal.

Conclusion: Structured environmental monitoring in IVF labs yields transferable predictive signal, enabling cross-site generalization and improved outcome modeling.

Abstract: IVF pregnancy rates are routinely modeled using patient-level variables, while high-resolution laboratory environmental data remain underutilized. We show that this is a missed opportunity. Rather than relying on raw sensor averages, we engineer 55 context-aware temporal features, including rolling thermal stability, simultaneous temperature-humidity adherence, peak stress duration, and post-stress recovery speed, that capture the dynamics of incubator microenvironments. On 61 weeks of data from an Asian IVF clinic, these features reduce cross-validated prediction error to 1.27%, compared to 3-5% for raw averages. We then train a hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression model that shares environmental effects across an Asian and a Northern European clinic via partial pooling, while preserving site-specific baselines. On held-out data from the Northern European clinic, the model achieves R2 = 0.86 and a 64% error reduction for the 35-39 age group over a naive baseline, demonstrating that structured environmental monitoring contains clinically meaningful, transferable signal.

</details>


### [168] [What Do Safety-Aligned LLMs Learn From Mixed Compliance Demonstrations?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20508)
*Sihui Dai,Mann Patel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Benign and harmful compliance demonstrations are not interchangeable; model risk depends on content, order, and training; optimization and refusal behaviors shape how in-context prompts influence harmful output.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To dissect how in-context demonstrations influence harmful compliance and to identify mechanism-level factors (content, order, training) that drive jailbreaking risks across models.

Method: Empirical study across four models; construct datasets with mixtures of benign (non-harmful request, helpful response) and harmful (harmful request, helpful response) demonstrations; manipulate demonstration content and order; analyze effects on compliance to harmful requests; examine role of preference optimization and refusal interactions; assess recency bias with ordering.

Result: Demonstrations are not interchangeable: benign prompts can reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model. Preference optimization is key to preventing harm; strong recency bias in demonstration order; models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning—some align with demonstrated formatting when refusing, others override in-context cues.

Conclusion: Understanding jailbreaking risk requires considering demonstration content, order, and training method. This nuance informs robust defense: curate benign/harmful prompts, consider training stages, model-specific refusal behavior, and ordering effects to mitigate risk.

Abstract: Prior work has shown that in-context demonstrations can jailbreak language models, but it remains unclear how models interpret different types of compliance demonstrations. We study this by mixing benign compliance demonstrations (non-harmful request, helpful response) with harmful compliance demonstrations (harmful request, helpful response) and testing three hypotheses about how demonstration composition drives harmful compliance. Across four models, we find that benign and harmful demonstrations are not interchangeable: benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model. We further show that preference optimization is the critical training stage that prevents benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance, that demonstration ordering exhibits strong recency bias, and that models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning: some adopt demonstrated formatting even when refusing, while others override all in-context signals upon refusal. Taken together, this work moves beyond showing that demonstration-based jailbreaking works to characterizing how it works: what models extract from compliance demonstrations depends on demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology.

</details>


### [169] [Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20517)
*Maria Ivanova,Pavel Zadorozhny,Rodion Levichev,Ivan Petrov,Adamenko Pavel,Ivan Lopatin,Alexey Kutalev,Dmitrii Babaev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multi-LCB extends the Python-centric LiveCodeBench to 12 languages by transforming Python tasks into equivalent tasks in other languages, preserving contamination controls and evaluation protocol. It enables cross-language code-generation evaluation and reveals cross-language gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address LCB's limitation of being Python-only and to assess whether LLMs generalize across diverse programming languages used in real-world software engineering.

Method: Repurpose Python tasks from LCB into 11 additional languages while maintaining LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol; keep compatibility with the original LCB format to track future updates; evaluate 24 LLMs on instruction and reasoning tasks.

Result: Evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance among 24 LLMs.

Conclusion: Multi-LCB provides a rigorous, cross-language benchmark that addresses LCB's primary limitation and highlights critical gaps in current LLM capabilities for multilingual code generation.

Abstract: LiveCodeBench (LCB) has recently become a widely adopted benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on code-generation tasks. By curating competitive programming problems, constantly adding fresh problems to the set, and filtering them by release dates, LCB provides contamination-aware evaluation and offers a holistic view of coding capability. However, LCB remains restricted to Python, leaving open the question of whether LLMs can generalize across the diverse programming languages required in real-world software engineering.
  We introduce Multi-LCB, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs across twelve programming languages, including Python. Multi-LCB transforms Python tasks from the LCB dataset into equivalent tasks in other languages while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Because it is fully compatible with the original LCB format, Multi-LCB will automatically track future LCB updates, enabling systematic assessment of cross-language code generation competence and requiring models to sustain performance well beyond Python.
  We evaluated 24 LLMs for instruction and reasoning on Multi-LCB, uncovering evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance. Our results establish Multi-LCB as a rigorous new benchmark for multi-programming-language code evaluation, directly addressing LCB's primary limitation and exposing critical gaps in current LLM capabilities.

</details>


### [170] [FlowEdit: Associative Memory for Lifelong Pronunciation Adaptation in Flow-Matching TTS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20518)
*Harshit Singh,Ayush Pratap Singh,Nityanand Mathur*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FlowEdit enables life-long adaptation of frozen flow-matching text-to-speech (TTS) by learning pronunciation corrections as latent text-embedding edits stored in a Modern Hopfield Network; retrieval during inference uses soft attention with a similarity gate to enable fuzzy morphological matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot flow-matching TTS often mispronounces out-of-vocabulary proper nouns; corrections require costly retraining. FlowEdit aims to provide continual, low-cost pronunciation adaptation during deployment without updating model weights.

Method: FlowEdit performs token-level perturbations in the text embedding space as corrective edits. Corrections are stored in a Modern Hopfield Network acting as content-addressable episodic memory. At inference, retrieved corrections are conditioned on input via soft attention with a similarity gate to enable fuzzy morphological matching.

Result: On a curated benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to the zero-shot baseline while preserving general speech quality. Corrections complete in approximately 15 seconds on a single GPU.

Conclusion: FlowEdit provides life-long, low-latency pronunciation adaptation for frozen flow-matching TTS, effectively handling OOV proper nouns with negligible impact on overall speech quality.

Abstract: Flow-matching text-to-speech systems achieve remarkable zero-shot quality but remain static after deployment: pronunciation errors on out-of-vocabulary proper nouns persist unless the model is retrained. We introduce FlowEdit, a life-long adaptation framework for frozen flow-matching TTS that learns pronunciation corrections as latent conditioning edits rather than weight updates. When corrective feedback is provided, FlowEdit optimizes a token-level perturbation in the text embedding space, then stores the correction in a Modern Hopfield Network serving as content-addressable episodic memory. At inference, corrections are retrieved via soft attention with a similarity gate, enabling fuzzy morphological matching. On our curated benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to the zero-shot baseline while maintaining identical general-speech quality. Corrections complete in approximately 15 seconds on a single GPU.

</details>


### [171] [DeepSWIP: Quotient-WMC Counterfactuals for Neural Probabilistic Logic Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20526)
*Saimun Habib,Vaishak Belle,Fengxiang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces DeepSWIP, a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog that enables causal reasoning by transforming neural predicates into ProbLog choices, applying SWIPs, and computing counterfactuals via weighted model counting on a single transformed program; under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, it is exact relative to the learned materialized FCM. Demonstrates speedups and calibration-related insights through experiments on MPI3D and SUMO HOV, with code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable principled counterfactual (causal) reasoning in neurosymbolic systems by integrating neural perception with probabilistic logic, addressing limitations of standard (associational) inference and handling interventions and evidence.

Method: Neural materialization converts neural predicates to ordinary ProbLog choices; apply Single World Intervention Programs (SWIPs); compute counterfactuals via weighted model counting (WMC) on a single transformed program. Under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, the approach is exact relative to the learned materialized FCM. The work analyzes how the quotient-WMC form of ProbLog conditionals identifies active neural probabilities, explains intervention cleaning, calibration sensitivity, and rare-evidence instability.

Result: Empirical validation on MPI3D with 12,000 queries confirms the transformation against a DeepTwin construction and yields a 2.14× inference speedup by avoiding endogenous duplication in DeepTwin. A SUMO HOV experiment shows that neural calibration degradation biases plug-in estimates, while a correctly scoped randomized-policy AIPW estimator removes most first-order bias for population mean and average treatment effect (ATE) estimands.

Conclusion: DeepSWIP provides an exact, more efficient counterfactual inference mechanism for DeepProbLog under specified assumptions, clarifies calibration and evidence sensitivity issues in neurosymbolic causal reasoning, and offers practical estimators (AIPW) to mitigate biases; code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

Abstract: Neurosymbolic systems such as DeepProbLog combine neural perception with probabilistic logic, but standard inference is associational. Counterfactual reasoning additionally requires a causal semantics for interventions and evidence. We introduce DeepSWIP, a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog programs. Using neural materialization, we reduce fixed-context neural predicates to ordinary ProbLog choices, apply Single World Intervention Programs (SWIPs), and compute counterfactuals by weighted model counting (WMC) over a single transformed program. Under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, DeepSWIP is exact relative to the learned materialized FCM. The standard quotient-WMC form of ProbLog conditionals identifies active neural probabilities and explains intervention cleaning, calibration sensitivity, and rare-evidence instability. Experiments on MPI3D confirm the transformation against a DeepTwin construction against 12,000 queries, as predicted and a 2.14$\times$ inference speedup from avoiding the Twin's endogenous duplication. A SUMO HOV experiment shows that neural calibration degradation biases plug-in estimates, while a correctly scoped randomized-policy AIPW estimator removes most first-order bias for population mean and ATE estimands. Code is at https://github.com/saibib/deep_SWIP.

</details>


### [172] [LedgerAgent: Structured State for Policy-Adherent Tool-Calling Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20529)
*Md Nayem Uddin,Amir Saeidi,Eduardo Blanco,Chitta Baral*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LedgerAgent introduces an inference-time ledger to maintain observed task states for policy-adherent tool-calling, reducing stale/missing information and preventing state-dependent policy violations; it yields improved pass@k across four customer-service domains with mixed-model weights, especially under stricter multi-trial consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In policy-adherent, tool-calling agents, task states are implicit and reconstructed from prompts each turn. This leads to state drift, stale information, and policy violations. A separate ledger to track observed states can improve grounding and enforcement of policies.

Method: Maintain a separate observable task-state ledger that records facts, identifiers, constraints, and conditions observed via user interactions and tool results. Render the ledger into prompts for decision making and use it to check state-dependent policies before executing environment-changing tool calls, thereby blocking violations. Evaluate across four customer-service domains using a mixed panel of open- and closed-weight models, comparing against a standard, prompt-based approach and reporting pass@k metrics.

Result: LedgerAgent yields higher average pass@k than standard prompt-based tool-calling across the tested domains and models, with the largest gains under stricter multi-trial consistency requirements.

Conclusion: An inference-time ledger for task-state management improves grounding, policy adherence, and reliability of tool calls in policy-adherent agents, suggesting broader applicability beyond the tested domains.

Abstract: Policy-adherent tool-calling agents in customer-service domains must maintain task states across turns while calling tools and obeying domain policies. Task states consist of relevant facts, identifiers, constraints, and conditions observed through user interaction and tool calls. In standard agents, task states are not represented separately. Observations, tool returns, and policy instructions are placed in the prompt, leaving agents to reconstruct the relevant states from the prompt each time they decide what to do next. This design makes state management implicit, creating two common failure modes. An agent may retrieve the right facts but later ground its decision in stale, missing, or incorrect information; and a syntactically valid tool call may still violate a domain policy that depends on the current task state. We introduce \textsc{LedgerAgent}, an inference-time method for tool-calling agents that maintains observed task states in a separate ledger and renders the states into the prompt. The ledger is also used to check state-dependent policy constraints before environment-changing tool calls are executed, blocking policy violations. Across four customer-service domains and a mixed panel of open- and closed-weight models, \textsc{LedgerAgent} improves average pass\textasciicircum{}k over a standard prompt-based tool-calling approach, with the largest gains under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.

</details>


### [173] [How Do Instructions Shape Speech? Cross-Attention Attribution for Style-Captioned Text-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20532)
*Nityanand Mathur,Hamees Sayed,Wasim Madha,Apoorv Singh,Sameer Khurana,Akshat Mandloi,Sudarshan Kamath*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cross-attention attribution is applied to a speech diffusion model (CapSpeech-TTS) by adapting DAAM; per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps reveal how style-caption words influence acoustics. Findings: style tokens exhibit global conditioning, correlate with F0/energy, peak early and in deep layers, with minimal attention entropy at layer 17 where style importance peaks. First study of natural language influence on cross-attention in speech diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how individual words affect acoustic output in style-conditioned TTS to diagnose failure modes and improve controllability.

Method: Adapt the DAAM attribution framework to a speech diffusion model (CapSpeech-TTS); compute per-token attribution heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps for 3,600 style-caption/text-transcript pairs (120 captions × 30 transcripts).

Result: Style tokens show lower temporal variance (global conditioning); style attention correlates with F0 and energy; conditioning peaks at early steps and in deep layers; attention entropy is minimized at layer 17, coinciding with the peak in style importance.

Conclusion: Presents the first study of natural language influence on cross-attention in speech diffusion models, providing insights for diagnosing failures and enhancing controllability in style-captioned TTS.

Abstract: Style-captioned text-to-speech systems use natural language to control voice characteristics, but how individual words influence acoustic output remains unclear. Understanding this is critical for diagnosing failure modes and improving controllability in expressive TTS. We propose cross-attention attribution for speech diffusion models, adapting the DAAM framework to the speech domain for the first time, and apply it to CapSpeech-TTS. Our method extracts per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps. We analyze 3,600 (style caption, text transcript) combinations comprising 120 style captions conditioning the generation of 30 text transcripts each, revealing how caption tokens shape waveforms. Results show: (1) style tokens have lower temporal variance than content/function tokens, confirming global conditioning; (2) style attention correlates with F0 and energy; (3) style conditioning peaks in early steps and deep layers; (4) attention entropy reaches its minimum at layer 17, co-occurring with the style importance peak, indicating maximal network selectivity at the most style-critical stage. This is the first study of how natural language influences cross-attention in speech diffusion models

</details>


### [174] [Toward Calibrated Mixture-of-Experts Under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20544)
*Gina Wong,Drew Prinster,Suchi Saria,Rama Chellappa,Anqi Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Expert calibration preserves overall MoE calibration under hard routing shifts; soft routing breaks calibration unless augmented; adversarial reweighting improves the accuracy–calibration tradeoff across settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify when calibrating individual predictors in mixture-of-experts (MoE) helps overall calibration under distribution shift and routing regimes; address gaps about MoE calibration behavior.

Method: Theoretically analyze MoEs under distribution shift, separating hard vs. soft routing; empirically evaluate across model classes, tasks, and shifts; introduce an adversarial reweighting objective that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift.

Result: Expert calibration is sufficient for calibrating the whole MoE under hard routing; insufficient for soft routing; the proposed adversarial reweighting improves the accuracy–calibration tradeoff on average and on hard subsets across settings.

Conclusion: Calibrating individual experts suffices for hard-routed MoEs under distribution shift, but soft routing requires calibration-aware objectives (e.g., adversarial reweighting) to achieve robust calibration; the approach generalizes across models, tasks, and shifts.

Abstract: Calibration aligns a model's predictive uncertainty with the frequencies of its empirical outcomes and is important for understanding and trusting reported probabilities. Recent work shows that enforcing calibration at the level of individual predictors can improve ensemble accuracy and calibration, with mixture-of-experts (MoE) models showing strong empirical improvements in particular; however, the conditions under which calibration helps MoE are not well understood. In this work, we study how MoE models behave under distribution shift, focusing on how routing mechanisms interact with expert-level calibration. We show that expert calibration is sufficient to ensure calibration of the overall model under a broad class of distribution shifts in hard-routed models, but is insufficient for calibrating soft-routed models. To address this, we propose an adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift, and we demonstrate that it improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff both on average and on difficult subsets of the data, across model classes, prediction tasks, and distribution shifts.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [175] [Fisher-Geometric Sharpness and the Implicit Bias of SGD toward Flat Minima](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20469)
*Md Sakir Ahmed,Kumaresh Sarmah,Hemen Dutta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Defines a reparametrization-invariant notion of flatness via the Fisher Information Metric and Riemannian geometry, proving invariance to smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations; links SGD noise to the FIM, derives a stationary distribution, and provides a PAC-Bayes bound that ties geometry to generalization, with empirical validation on MNIST/CIFAR-10 showing superiority over Euclidean sharpness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Euclidean flatness measures (trace, max eigenvalue) are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve the network function, undermining theoretical claims that flat minima generalize better.

Method: Introduce Riemannian sharpness based on the Fisher Information Metric (FIM); prove invariance under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations; model mini-batch SGD noise with covariance proportional to the FIM; derive the stationary distribution of the associated SDE; obtain a PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled by SR; compare Euclidean sharpness and SR empirically on MNIST and CIFAR-10; discuss invariance of the diagonal empirical estimator and the need for structured estimators like K-FAC.

Result: Riemannian sharpness is invariant under the stated reparametrizations, addressing the critique of Dinh et al.; diagonal empirical estimates are approximately invariant in practice; SR correlates with generalization and scales with η/B in line with theory; PAC-Bayes bound links SR to expected test performance; experiments show SR tracks generalization better than Euclidean sharpness across datasets.

Conclusion: The work provides a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize, grounding the intuition in the geometry of the statistical manifold induced by the FIM and connecting it to SGD dynamics and generalization bounds.

Abstract: A widely held intuition in deep learning is that stochastic gradient descent (SGD) implicitly favors flat minima and that flat minima generalize better, but standard Euclidean measures of flatness such as the trace or maximum eigenvalue of the loss Hessian are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve the network function, which undermines the theoretical foundations of this narrative. In this study we resolve this issue by grounding flatness in the Riemannian geometry of the statistical manifold induced by the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM). We define Riemannian sharpness mathematically and prove that it is invariant under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations, which directly addresses the critique of Dinh et al. in the paper ``Sharp minima can generalize for deep nets''.We note that this invariance is a property of the true FIM; the diagonal empirical estimator used in practice (and in all experiments below) inherits invariance only approximately, and exact invariance under arbitrary reparametrizations would require structured estimators such as K-FAC. We formalize the gradient noise of mini-batch SGD as having a covariance structure proportional to the FIM, derive the stationary distribution of the resulting stochastic differential equation, and then show that the probability mass is exponentially concentrated at Riemannian-flat minima. A PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled explicitly by SR formally links this geometric bias to test performance. Our experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 confirm that SR reliably tracks generalization in ways that Euclidean sharpness does not, and that its scaling with $η/B$ matches the theoretical predictions. Together these results provide a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize.

</details>


### [176] [Computational Identifiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19361)
*Lucius E. J. Bynum,Rajesh Ranganath,Kyunghyun Cho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes computational identifiability: a finite, data-driven search to empirically identify causal effects, contrasting with traditional theoretical identifiability under idealized conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between idealized, asymptotic identifiability and practical identification with finite data, ambiguous graphs, and mixed data, by introducing a computation-bound framework.

Method: Define a finite computational search procedure that seeks an empirical estimator within a specified error tolerance, conditioned on a prior over parameters and on the search process. Demonstrate via experiments that this approach can answer practical identification questions (small samples, ambiguous criteria, mixed data, counterfactuals); provide code for implementation (github.com/lbynum/metadentify).

Result: Empirical identifiability is achievable under finite data and specified search conditions; the framework yields practical identifiability answers in diverse scenarios and clarifies the role of the search procedure and prior in identification.

Conclusion: Computational identifiability complements theoretical identifiability by enabling practical, data-driven assessment of whether an estimand can be recovered; highlights dependence on search design and priors and offers a path for actionable identification in realistic settings.

Abstract: Identification conditions describe the computability of a target query or parameter of interest as a function of the type and amount of information available. In causal identification, this information is often expressed in the form of a causal graph, and data are observed or collected for some subset of variables in the graph. Target queries may be for a single effect alone or for a class of effects in a given model. The derivation of an identification algorithm then defines mathematically the process by which the desired causal effect(s) can be uniquely determined, theoretically, in expectation. Identifiability in expectation, or 'theoretical identifiability,' generally assumes asymptotic properties, infinite data, or other mathematically idealized conditions. In this paper, we explore a fundamental distinction between this theoretical, idealized notion of identifiability and a proposed alternative that is computation-bound. The framework we propose - 'computational identifiability' - is to instead define a finite computational search procedure for an empirical estimator. If this process finds an estimator empirically, within a desired error tolerance, then identifiability is satisfied, conditional on the specified assumptions of the search (i.e., a prior distribution over the parameters) and conditional on the search procedure itself.
