Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (ORL) offers the potential to improve the quality of clinical decision-making using historical electronic health record (EHR) data. Current training and evaluative practices in this field rely heavily on EHR datasets that have been temporally discretised into fixed, regular time intervals. Discretisation creates fictional representations of complex clinical scenarios and compromises the generalisability of retrospective model evaluations. In this paper, we introduce Insulin4RL, a healthcare ORL dataset featuring naturally irregular inputs and actions from real clinical trajectories. Derived from MIMIC-IV, Insulin4RL comprises over 375,000 labelled decisions across 12,209 patients requiring insulin infusion titration in the Intensive Care Unit. The dataset can thus be used for research into ORL model performance under realistic clinical sampling assumptions. We provide a description of the dataset's structure and characteristics, baseline performance metrics using model-free offline reinforcement learning, and a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation. We conclude with suggested areas for future research that could be addressed using this resource.

</details>


### [202] [Concept Flow Models: Anchoring Concept-Based Reasoning with Hierarchical Bottlenecks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19489)
*Ya Wang,Adrian Paschke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Concept Flow Models (CFMs) replace flat concept bottlenecks in CBMs with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree to localize discriminative concepts, reduce information leakage, and provide interpretable, stepwise reasoning without sacrificing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CBMs using large concept embeddings risk information leakage as the number of concepts grows, allowing spurious correlations to drive decisions and hurting interpretability. A hierarchical, localized approach could confine concept usage and improve auditability.

Method: Construct a hierarchical decision tree from visual embeddings, assign semantic concepts to different levels, and train differentiable concept weights via probabilistic tree traversal. The model makes predictions by progressively narrowing the scope through the hierarchy, yielding stepwise, interpretable decisions.

Result: CFMs achieve predictive performance on par with flat CBMs while substantially reducing information leakage by limiting effective concept usage. The hierarchical flow affords transparent, auditable reasoning with a clear, stepwise decision process.

Conclusion: Hierarchical concept-based models provide comparable accuracy to flat CBMs while enhancing interpretability and robustness to spurious correlations through localized concept usage and auditable decision flows.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance interpretability by projecting learned features into a human-understandable concept space. Recent approaches leverage vision-language models to generate concept embeddings, reducing the need for manual concept annotations. However, these models suffer from a critical limitation: as the number of concepts approaches the embedding dimension, information leakage increases, enabling the model to exploit spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations and undermining interpretability. In this work, we propose Concept Flow Models (CFMs), which replace the flat bottleneck with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree. Each internal node in the hierarchy focuses on a localized subset of discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing the prediction scope. Our framework constructs decision hierarchies from visual embeddings, distributes semantic concepts at each hierarchy level, and trains differentiable concept weights through probabilistic tree traversal. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that CFMs match the predictive performance of flat CBMs, while substantially mitigating information leakage by reducing effective concept usage. Furthermore, CFMs yield stepwise decision flows that enable transparent and auditable model reasoning with hierarchical class structures.

</details>


### [203] [Algebraic Dead Directions in LayerNorm Transformers: A Forward-Pass-Only Diagnostic at LLM Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19491)
*Tejas Pradeep Shirodkar,P. J. Narayanan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The authors derive a computable dead direction for LayerNorm transformers: the inverse-scale direction of the LayerNorm affine is an exact kernel of the post-final-norm activation covariance, enabling a parameter-only read of dead directions without forward passes or eigensolves. Validated across many models; distinguished LayerNorm from RMSNorm; training amplifies the effect and reveals blockwise spectral persistence; normalisation type can be inferred from parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify directions in parameter space along which Fisher information is degenerate (dead directions) in pretrained transformers, but existing methods require forward/backward passes or spectral computations; seeks a purely algebraic, parameter-based dead-direction for LayerNorm transformers.

Method: Derive that the LN inverse-scale direction gamma^{-1}/||gamma^{-1}|| is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm activation covariance for any input distribution. Readable solely from LN scale parameter, no forward/backward pass and no eigensolve. Empirically validate on 14 pretrained transformers (9 LayerNorm, 5 RMSNorm; 160M-35B; language and vision). Compare predicted direction to bottom singular direction via one forward pass + SVD; test across random initialization and trained checkpoints; examine covariance eigenvalues along direction and consequences.

Result: At random initialization, predicted direction matches bottom singular direction to 4 decimals on 9/9 LayerNorm models; absent on 5/5 RMSNorm models. At trained checkpoints, covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens ~10^3x and more dead directions appear; the random-init vs trained gap is measurable by one forward pass per checkpoint. Two consequences: (a) residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on 13/14 transformers; (b) the kernel direction's presence classifies transformer's normalisation from the parameters alone.

Conclusion: The inverse-scale LayerNorm direction provides a cheap, exact, architecture-specific dead-direction readout, enabling classification of normalisation and insight into Fisher geometry; this direction is a robust predictor of degeneracy across models and evolves with training, revealing persistent spectral structure in the residual stream.

Abstract: Pretrained transformers sit near singular minima of the loss, where the Fisher information metric degenerates along dead directions: directions in parameter space along which the directional Fisher vanishes. Locating such a direction normally needs a forward pass and an eigendecomposition of activations, or a sampling-based complexity estimate; none returns a direction computable from the network's parameters alone. We give one, for LayerNorm transformers. The inverse-scale direction $γ^{-1}/\|γ^{-1}\|$ of the LayerNorm affine is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm centred activation covariance, for any input distribution, and induces a corresponding dead direction in parameter space. It is read from the LN scale parameter alone, with no forward or backward pass and no eigensolve: the cheapest dead-direction read, specific to LayerNorm. We test it on $14$ pretrained transformers ($9$ LayerNorm, $5$ RMSNorm; $160$M-$35$B; language and vision objectives). At random initialisation the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction (one forward pass, direct SVD) to four decimal places on $9/9$ LayerNorm models, and is correctly absent on $5/5$ RMSNorm models, which lack the mean-subtraction projector that creates it. On the trained checkpoint the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by ${\sim}10^3\times$ and further dead directions open; the random-init-to-trained gap is a one-forward-pass, per-checkpoint readout of singular structure along the predicted coordinate. Two consequences follow in closed form: the residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on $13/14$ transformers measured on their own input distribution, the one exception (Gemma$4$-$31$B) a genuine dead direction the same read pinpoints; and the kernel direction's presence classifies a transformer's normalisation from the parameters alone.

</details>


### [204] [Calibrating Generative Models to Feature Distributions with MMD Finetuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19496)
*Nathaniel L. Diamant,Brian L. Trippe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: kCGM introduces kernel Calibrating Generative Models to steer generative models toward a target feature distribution using MMD with score-function gradient estimates and KL regularization to stay close to a pretrained model. It improves target feature alignment and chemical validity over naive finetuning, demonstrated on antibiotics and extended to protein/DNA generation across autoregressive and diffusion frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models often produce plausible samples that distort key feature distributions of interest. Direct finetuning on a target set can overfit and lacks control over which features are matched, leading to degraded validity or chemistry.

Method: Propose kCGM, optimizing an unbiased score-function estimator of MMD between generated and target feature distributions, with KL regularization to remain near the pretrained model. Applicable to autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models using feature-level supervision.

Result: On a target set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning reduces chemical validity while matching features; kCGM improves feature alignment without sacrificing validity. Additional demonstrations on protein and DNA generation show broad applicability across model classes.

Conclusion: kCGM provides a principled, scalable calibration method for generative models to match target feature distributions while preserving model validity, with broad applicability across modalities and model types.

Abstract: Generative models can produce individually plausible samples while deviating substantially from a target set in the distribution of key features. For example, a model pretrained on broad drug-like chemical space may generate molecules whose molecular features differ from those of a therapeutic class of interest, such as known antibiotics. Correcting such distributional miscalibration is challenging: direct finetuning on the target set can overfit and does not control which features are matched. To fill this gap, we introduce kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM). kCGM minimizes a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator, with KL regularization to remain close to the pretrained model. On a target set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning sacrifices chemical validity for feature-distribution matching, whereas kCGM improves target feature matching while increasing validity. We further demonstrate kCGM in protein and DNA generation tasks, showing it can adapt autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models using only feature-level supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/smithhenryd/cgm.

</details>


### [205] [Interactive Pareto navigation for deep multi-task learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19521)
*Augustina C. Amakor,Konstantin Sonntag,Sebastian Peitz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PPE is an interactive predictor-corrector framework for preference-guided Pareto-front exploration in multi-task learning, avoiding Hessian computations via Krylov subspaces, with automatic differentiation-based vector products; demonstrated on toy problems and deep learning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-task learning with many objectives makes weighted sums fail to capture user preferences and becomes computationally expensive to tune; there is a need for scalable, geometry-aware interactive exploration of Pareto-optimal trade-offs.

Method: A predictor step moves along the Pareto manifold tangential to it according to the decision maker's preference; a corrector step refines to a new trade-off. The tangent space is characterized using a Krylov subspace method that requires only matrix-vector products, computed efficiently via automatic differentiation, avoiding explicit Hessian calculations.

Result: The framework's functionality and performance are demonstrated on toy problems and deep learning examples, illustrating efficiency, robustness, and the ability to honor user preferences in Pareto-aware optimization.

Conclusion: PPE enables efficient, interactive, preference-guided exploration of Pareto-optimal solutions in multi-task learning, leveraging geometry-aware predictor-corrector steps and Hessian-free tangent-space computations.

Abstract: In multi-task learning, handling an increasing number of objectives can quickly become challenging, both in terms of the computational resources and the decision maker's capacity to choose appropriate trade-offs. A widely used approach is thus to aggregate the individual losses in a single loss function by a weighted sum. This often fails to capture either the decision maker's preferences as a result of the shape of the Pareto front, or requires multiple adjustments and computations which becomes prohibitively expensive in deep learning applications. To address these issues, we introduce a novel framework, Preference Pareto Exploration (PPE), which enforces the decision maker's preferences while accounting for the geometry of the Pareto set in an interactive exploration process. PPE is based on a predictor-corrector method that performs predictor steps tangential to the manifold of Pareto-optimal solutions, following the decision maker's preference. The subsequent corrector step results in a new trade-off reflecting this preference. To avoid explicit Hessian computations when characterizing the tangent space of the manifold, we employ a Krylov subspace method that relies solely on matrix-vector products. These products can be efficiently obtained via automatic differentiation, ensuring both efficiency and robustness throughout the optimization process. The method's functionality and performance are demonstrated using both toy problems and examples from deep learning.

</details>


### [206] [Techniques for Peak Memory Reduction for LoRA Fine-tuning of LLMs on Edge Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19528)
*Hassan Dbouk,Matthias Reisser,Prathamesh Mandke,Likhita Arun Navali,Christos Louizos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A suite of memory-saving techniques enables memory-efficient LoRA fine-tuning on consumer hardware, achieving substantial peak memory reductions and enabling personalization with privacy-preserving data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning large language models with LoRA on end-user data is privacy-preserving but memory-intensive, often exceeding consumer hardware limits for 3B+ models with long-context data.

Method: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization; (2) memory-efficient checkpointing using selective activation caching and disk offloading; (3) softmax approximation via semantically relevant token subsets; (4) logits masking. Demonstrated with Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B.

Result: Up to 26× and 28× reductions in peak memory, enabling fine-tuning on resource-constrained devices for the tested models.

Conclusion: The proposed combination of quantization, caching/offloading, softmax pruning, and masking yields substantial memory savings while preserving fine-tuning capability, enabling privacy-preserving personalized models on consumer hardware; future work could assess quality trade-offs and scale to larger models.

Abstract: Fine-tuning of Large Language Models (LLMs) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on an end-user's data offers personalized experiences while keeping data private, but faces severe memory constraints on consumer hardware. Peak memory during fine-tuning often exceeds device limits, especially for models with billions of parameters and long-context training data. This paper introduces a suite of complementary techniques to reduce memory footprint without sacrificing model quality: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization, (2) memory-efficient checkpointing combining selective activation caching and disk offloading, (3) softmax approximation using semantically relevant token subsets, and (4) logits masking. Experiments on Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B demonstrate up to $26\times$ and $28\times$ reduction in peak memory, enabling fine-tuning on resource-constrained devices.

</details>


### [207] [Tracking Representation Dynamics in Large Language Models with Persistent Homology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19542)
*Naman Malhotra,Jay Ambadkar,Abhinav Gupta,Kushal Kasivel,Abbas Schwarz,Kamillo Ferry,Anthea Monod*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fine-tuning LLMs causes rapid, early topology changes in activation spaces; a transient peak in topological activity precedes stabilization, with alignment objectives imprinting distinct trajectories; instruction-tuned and pretrained models diverge in evolution; persistent homology offers a complementary view beyond behavioral metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how internal representations evolve during alignment and to complement behavioral metrics by examining the topology of activation spaces.

Method: Apply persistent homology to monitor the topology of activation spaces during fine-tuning across four transformer LLMs (1B–7B) and three alignment objectives (helpful, harmless, mixed). Conduct dense checkpoint analysis to capture temporal topology changes and compare trajectories across objectives and model types.

Result: Most topological reorganization occurs in the early training phase. Dense checkpoint analysis reveals a transient peak in topological activity followed by rapid stabilization. Different alignment objectives yield distinguishable topological trajectories, and instruction-tuned versus pretrained models show qualitatively different evolutionary patterns.

Conclusion: Persistent homology provides a valuable, behavior-agnostic lens on alignment dynamics, revealing representation-level changes during fine-tuning that behavioral metrics may miss and highlighting objective- and model-dependent topology trajectories.

Abstract: Large language models are commonly aligned through supervised fine-tuning, yet little is known about how their internal representations evolve during this process. We study alignment dynamics using persistent homology by tracking the topology of activation spaces throughout fine-tuning. Across four transformer language models ranging from 1B to 7B parameters and three alignment objectives corresponding to helpful, harmless, and mixed training data, we find that the majority of topological reorganization occurs during the earliest stages of training. A dense checkpoint analysis reveals a transient peak in topological activity followed by rapid stabilization. We further show that different alignment objectives induce distinguishable topological trajectories, while instruction-tuned and pretrained models exhibit qualitatively different patterns of evolution. Our results suggest that persistent homology provides a complementary perspective on alignment, revealing representation-level changes that are not apparent from behavioral metrics alone.

</details>


### [208] [Predicting Mergeability of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19549)
*Lin Tang,Wei Zhang,Jing Li,Hongyu Chen,Ming Zhao,Yuxuan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRA adapters can be merged efficiently by predicting mergeability from early training signals using MergeProbe, enabling proactive decisions (merge, reweight, prune, route) and achieving strong retention on MERGE-PEFT across five domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-hoc evaluation of adapter merges is costly due to potential destructive interference; need early indicators of whether adapters will retain utility after merging.

Method: Extract signals from the first few percent of training: alignment of low-rank updates and their gradients across tasks, and the disturbance to shared representations. Train a lightweight predictor MergeProbe to estimate pairwise and set-level retention, and use its outputs to decide merging strategy (merge directly, reweight, prune, or route).

Result: MergeProbe achieves the best average and worst-case retention among strong interference-aware baselines on MERGE-PEFT, while adding far less deployment overhead than full task routing.

Conclusion: Transforms LoRA merging from a post-hoc engineering step into an anticipatory measurement problem, enabling proactive and efficient adapter management across domains.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) makes it cheap to train many domain- and task-specific language model adapters, but whether two adapters can be merged is usually discovered only after both have been fully trained and evaluated. This late feedback is costly: adapters that are strong in isolation can interfere destructively once their updates are combined. We ask whether this outcome can be anticipated. We formalize adapter mergeability as the degree to which an adapter preserves its single-task utility after merging, and show that it can be forecast from signals measured in the first few percent of training -- chiefly how the low-rank updates and their gradients align across tasks and how much they disturb shared representations. We package these signals into MergeProbe, a lightweight predictor that estimates pairwise and set-level retention and turns the estimate into a concrete decision: merge directly, reweight, prune, or route. On MERGE-PEFT, a five-domain benchmark spanning math, code, science, instruction following, and safety, MergeProbe attains the best average and worst-case retention among strong interference-aware merge baselines while adding far less deployment overhead than full task routing. This turns LoRA merging from a post-hoc engineering step into an anticipatory measurement problem.

</details>


### [209] [Displacement Is Not Direction: Evaluating Fidelity Metrics for Quantized LLM Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19558)
*Miloš Nikolić,Ali Hadi Zadeh,Enrique Torres Sanchez,Andreas Moshovos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KLD correlates with benchmark quality across large cohorts but collapses in near-baseline silent zones; it mainly measures disagreement volume rather than direction, with weak per-prompt predictive power and limited cross-model routing ability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether per-token KL divergence (KLD) to a high-precision reference is a reliable, low-cost proxy for benchmark quality across different models, benchmarks, and measurement variants.

Method: Empirical evaluation on 28-quant Qwen3.6-35B-A3B and 41-quant Devstral-Small-2-24B cohorts, across downstream benchmarks. Used multiple KLD aggregations and alternative fidelity formulations, calibration corpora, context lengths, top-1 agreement. Per-prompt evaluation on LiveCodeBench for code; assessed cross-model disagreement prompts. Investigated correlation with benchmark scores and predictive power for failures and routing.

Result: Strong negative correlations with benchmark scores across full cohorts (ρ = -0.72 for Qwen, ρ = -0.86 for Devstral, p<0.001). In the near-baseline silent zone, correlations collapse (ρ ≈ 0 for Qwen, ρ = -0.24, p=0.36 for Devstral). The collapse persists across 14 measurement variants. Per-prompt code tasks show weak failure-prediction (GM ratios 1.08–1.22) and cross-model disagreement routing accuracy of 42.3–49.4%. Structural decomposition reveals KLD primarily measures the volume of disagreement with the reference; silent-zone correlations are strong (ρ=+0.94, p<0.001 for Qwen; ρ=+0.55, p=0.03 for Devstral) while directionality of disagreements is weak and task-conditional.

Conclusion: KLD is only a partial proxy for benchmark quality: effective for capturing disagreement magnitude across broad ranges but unreliable when models are near baseline or when the direction and nature of disagreements matter. It fails as a robust per-prompt predictor and as a cross-model router. Implication: use KLD with caution, supplement with direction-sensitive and task-specific metrics, and account for silent-zone behavior.

Abstract: Fidelity metrics, such as per-token KL divergence (KLD) against a high-precision reference, are often used in practice as low-cost proxies for benchmark quality. We test this practice on a 28-quant cohort of Qwen3.6-35B-A3B and a 41-quant cohort of Devstral-Small-2-24B, evaluated across a suite of downstream benchmarks. We find that KLD is strongly correlated with benchmark score over the full cohort ($ρ=-0.72$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.86$ on Devstral, both with $p<0.001$). However, this relationship collapses to non-significance in the near-baseline silent zone ($ρ=+0.00$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.24$, $p=0.36$, on Devstral). This collapse persists across 14 measurement variants, including different KLD aggregations, perplexity formulations, top-1 agreement, calibration corpora, and context lengths. At the per-prompt level, KLD has only weak failure-prediction power on code, with failed-vs-passed geometric-mean ratios in $[1.08,1.22]$ across five models on LiveCodeBench, and fails as a cross-model router, achieving only $42.3\%-49.4\%$ accuracy on disagreement prompts. We trace the collapse to a structural decomposition: KLD primarily measures the volume of disagreement with the reference, with silent-zone composite $ρ=+0.94$ ($p<0.001$) on Qwen and $+0.55$ ($p=0.03$) on Devstral, while its relationship to the direction of those disagreements is weak and task-conditional.

</details>


### [210] [Understanding Key Features of Time Series Foundation Models from Epidemic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19560)
*Alireza Jafari,Judy Fox,Geoffrey C. Fox,Madhav Marathe,Aniruddha Adiga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A mixture-of-experts ensemble of multiple pretrained forecasters yields the strongest influenza forecasts across 1–4 weeks, with numerical transformers providing reliable forecasts; pretraining benefits are larger at longer horizons, particularly when aligned with influenza dynamics; LLM-based time-series methods underperform; hospitalization data can improve robustness as covariates or pretraining data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically compare modern forecasting architectures (classical neural nets, numerical transformers, pretrained time-series foundations, and LLM-based forecasting) for influenza surveillance, across temporal and spatial generalization, and horizons, to understand gains from pretraining, architecture type, and auxiliary signals.

Method: Evaluate regional influenza forecasting using influenza-like illness (ILI) surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series. Conduct 1–4 week ahead predictions under temporal and spatial generalization settings. Compare architectures including classical neural networks, numerical transformer-based models, pretrained time-series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting. Implement a mixture-of-experts that fuses multiple pretrained forecasters. Assess the role of hospitalization data as an auxiliary covariate and as a pretraining source across horizons.

Result: Mixture-of-experts combining multiple pretrained forecasters shows the strongest overall performance. Numerical transformer-based models provide reliable forecasts. Pretraining yields the largest gains at longer horizons, especially when domain alignment with influenza dynamics is strong. LLM-based time-series methods underperform relative to numerical forecasters. Hospitalization signals offer complementary improvements in selected settings and can enhance robustness when used as covariates or pretraining data.

Conclusion: For influenza preparedness, adopt a heterogeneous, pretrained ensemble (mixture-of-experts) for best performance, use numerical transformers as a robust baseline, and leverage pretraining primarily to boost longer-horizon forecasts. Incorporate hospitalization data when appropriate to improve robustness and auxiliary-signal utilization; LLM-based time-series approaches may require further adaptations to reach parity with specialized numerical forecasters.

Abstract: Seasonal influenza infects millions of people and causes substantial morbidity and mortality in the United States each year, making accurate short-term forecasting a core public-health need. Reliable forecasts of epidemic time series can inform vaccination timing, hospital staffing, and resource allocation, yet the comparative behavior of modern forecasting architectures on infectious-disease surveillance data remains insufficiently characterized. We address this gap through a systematic evaluation of regional influenza forecasting using influenza-like illness surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series under both temporal and spatial generalization settings for 1-4-week-ahead prediction. We compare classical neural network architectures, numerical transformer-based models, pretrained time series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting approaches. Across tasks, we demonstrate that a mixture-of-experts model that fuses multiple pretrained forecasters achieves the strongest overall performance, indicating that heterogeneous pretrained representations provide complementary predictive information. Our results further show that numerical transformer-based models produce reliable forecasts, while pretraining provides the largest gains at longer horizons, particularly when the pretraining domain is mechanistically aligned with influenza dynamics. In contrast, LLM-based time series methods underperform relative to numerical forecasters in this setting. Finally, we examine hospitalization information as both an auxiliary covariate and a pretraining source. Hospitalization signals provide complementary improvements in selected settings and clarify when additional surveillance streams enhance the robustness of multi-horizon forecasting. These findings provide actionable guidance on model selection, pretraining strategy, and auxiliary-signal use for influenza preparedness.

</details>


### [211] [Advances in Scientific Machine Learning for Coupled Fluid Flow and Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19562)
*Gabriel F. Barros,Rômulo M. Silva,Alvaro L. G. A. Coutinho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Survey of SciML for coupled incompressible Navier–Stokes and scalar transport; combines linear ROMs and nonlinear neural surrogates; introduces PINN-based turbidity-current surrogate and β-VAE extraction of nonlinear modes; uses HPC strategies (AMR/Coarsening, data compression); validates on turbidity currents and Rayleigh–Bénard convection; shows fast, accurate surrogates with substantial cost reduction; real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-fidelity simulations of coupled fluid flow and scalar transport are computationally expensive due to nonlinear coupling and multiscale dynamics. SciML surrogates aim to reduce cost while preserving essential physics.

Method: Review of state-of-the-art linear ROMs (e.g., SVD, DMD) and nonlinear neural approaches (PINNs, β-VAEs); integration with high-performance computing techniques (adaptive mesh refinement/coarsening, data compression); two new contributions: (i) PINN surrogate for turbidity currents; (ii) β-VAE-based extraction of disentangled nonlinear modes in thermal flows; governing equations and benchmarks (lock-exchange, Rayleigh–Bénard).

Result: Demonstrates that SciML surrogates can provide fast, accurate approximations with substantial cost reductions versus full-order simulations within the targeted data regimes; enables potential real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification, though these remain active research directions and problem-dependent.

Conclusion: SciML is a viable framework for efficient modeling of coupled fluid–transport systems, bridging math/physics foundations and computational strategies; the chapter highlights both capabilities and remaining challenges (real-time prediction, UQ) across relevant regimes.

Abstract: This chapter reviews recent advances in Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) for modeling coupled fluid flow and transport phenomena governed by the incompressible Navier-Stokes and scalar transport equations. Such systems, found in applications like turbidity currents and thermal convection, feature strong nonlinear coupling and multiscale behavior that make high-fidelity simulations computationally expensive. To address this, the chapter surveys state-of-the-art SciML methods for building efficient surrogate models, including linear reduced-order techniques based on Singular Value Decomposition (such as Dynamic Mode Decomposition) and nonlinear neural network approaches like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and $β$-Variational Autoencoders ($β$-VAEs). It first covers the authors' work combining these models with High Performance Computing strategies, including Adaptive Mesh Refinement/Coarsening (AMR/C) and scientific floating-point data compression. It then presents two new contributions: surrogate modeling of turbidity currents via PINNs, and the extraction of disentangled nonlinear modes from thermal flows using $β$-VAEs. Governing equations and representative benchmarks, including lock-exchange flows and Rayleigh-Bénard convection, illustrate these methodologies. The chapter is intentionally long, covering both the mathematical and physical foundations of coupled fluid flow and the computational aspects of state-of-the-art modeling. Overall, it demonstrates how SciML enables fast, accurate approximations of complex coupled systems within the specific data regimes and modeling assumptions considered, while substantially reducing computational cost relative to full-order simulations. Broader capabilities such as real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active research directions whose feasibility depends strongly on the problem at hand.

</details>


### [212] [On the QUEST for Uncertainty Quantification via Highest Density Regions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19569)
*Sam Goring,Tom Kuipers,Nicola Paoletti,David S. Watson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QUEST defines uncertainty as the Lebesgue volume of the highest-density regions of a predictive distribution, using a robustness parameter α, to address misalignment of traditional scoring-rule UQ with the target statistic and to provide axiomatic, distribution-shape-based uncertainty measures; empirically competitive on selective-prediction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar UQ measures based on proper scoring rules focus on pointwise risk and the conditional mean, which can misrepresent uncertainty when the target statistic differs. There is a need for distribution-structure–aware measures that satisfy desirable axioms and remain robust under transformations, especially for safety-critical regression.

Method: Introduce QUEST by quantifying uncertainty via the volume of the most probable subset (highest-density regions) of the predictive distribution, evaluated at one or more α-robustness levels. Establish axioms (monotonicity under spread, location invariance) and connect QUEST measures to information-theoretic and economic statistics. Compare to standard measures and demonstrate favorable selective-prediction performance.

Result: QUEST yields epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty measures that satisfy an axiomatic set (e.g., monotonicity under distributional spread, location invariance). Benchmark results on selective prediction show favorable performance relative to variance and differential entropy, indicating improved alignment with decision-critical uncertainty notions.

Conclusion: A principled, distribution-structure–based UQ framework (QUEST) that complements or surpasses traditional scoring-rule approaches, providing robust theoretical properties and practical improvements in selective-prediction tasks; it links information-theoretic and economic concepts to UQ in regression.

Abstract: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for reliable decision-making in safety-critical applications in probabilistic machine learning. For regression problems, dominant scalar UQ approaches - notably, those based on proper scoring rules - measure uncertainty via pointwise predictive risk. This can lead to counterintuitive results when the target statistic is not the conditional expectation. We propose an alternative framework, in which uncertainty is characterised by the volume of the most probable subset of a distribution's support. QUEST (Quantifying Uncertainty via highest dEnSiTy regions) is a novel approach to UQ based on the concentration of Lebesgue measure at a distribution's peak(s), evaluated at one or more values of a robustness parameter $α$. We establish connections between our measures and classical statistics from information theory and economics. We show that, unlike popular alternatives based on proper scoring rules, QUEST measures of epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty satisfy a set of axioms adapted from the UQ literature, including monotonicity under distributional spread and invariance to location shifts. Selective prediction benchmarks confirm that QUEST performs favourably against standard measures such as variance and differential entropy.

</details>


### [213] [Unsupervised Causal Abstractions Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19594)
*Théo Saulus,Simon Lacoste-Julien,Dhanya Sridhar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learn high-level causal abstractions directly from low-level measurements under a low-rank generative process: latent variables induced by a low-rank graph form a causal abstraction, with identifiability guarantees, plus a practical objective to recover the high-level SCM from data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practice relies on hypothesis testing of a proposed high-level model. There is a need for data-driven learning of the high-level causal abstraction from low-level measurements, leveraging low-rank structure to identify latent abstractions that capture interventional behavior.

Method: Assume observations come from a low-rank causal graph. Show that the resulting latent variables form a causal abstraction of the low-level system. Establish identifiability results for these latents. Propose a practical objective (optimization) to learn the high-level SCM from data.

Result: (i) Latents derived from a low-rank graph induce a causal abstraction of the low-level SCM. (ii) Identifiability results for these latent abstractions are provided. (iii) A practical learning objective to recover the high-level SCM from measurements is proposed.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that high-level causal abstractions can be learned directly from low-level data via latent causal discovery under low-rank structure, with theoretical identifiability guarantees and a concrete learning objective.

Abstract: Causal abstractions formalize when a high-level structural causal model (SCM) captures the interventional behavior of a lower-level SCM. Existing applications of this notion largely follow a hypothesis-testing paradigm: an expert proposes a candidate high-level model and then evaluates if the low-level system implements it. We study the complementary problem of learning a high-level model directly from low-level measurements. Our contributions leverage hypotheses from low-rank causal discovery, and can be summarized as follows: (1) we show that observations generated by a low-rank graph induce latents that form a causal abstraction, (2) we provide identifiability results about these latents, and (3) we propose a practical objective to learn this high-level SCM.

</details>


### [214] [IHBench: Evaluating Post-Interruption Recovery in Voice Agents with Structured Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19595)
*Ahmad Salimi,Wentao Ma,Yuzhi Tang,Dongming Shen,Mu Li,Alex Smola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IHBench is a benchmark for interruption-handling in voice agents executing state-machine workflows. It measures post-interruption recovery across 10 domains, using six interruption types mid-utterance, with per-interruption rubrics and dual scoring axes (task fulfillment, recovery quality). Evaluates 27 audio-language models (open vs closed weights). Key findings: closed-weight models are more robust, degrade slower with longer conversations, and show no audio-vs-text gap; open-weight models lag on all metrics. Human validation supports judgments; recovery quality appears as a distinct capability axis in cross-benchmark comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing speech benchmarks emphasize interruption timing (barge-in, endpointing, turn-taking) but ignore post-interruption recovery: whether the agent resumes at the correct step, handles the interjection, and avoids repeating already-heard content. IHBench addresses this gap by evaluating post-interruption recovery in state-machine-driven workflows across enterprise domains.

Method: Create IHBench across 10 enterprise domains with six interruption types injected at controlled points mid-utterance. Generate per-interruption evaluation rubrics. Score each interruption on task fulfillment and recovery quality. Evaluate 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and open-weight communities (comparing closed vs open weights). Include a human study validating the LLM judge against human annotators and perform a cross-benchmark analysis against AudioMultiChallenge to assess distinctiveness of recovery ability.

Result: Closed-weight models achieve higher task fulfillment, degrade ~3.3x more slowly as conversations lengthen, and exhibit no audio-vs-text modality gap. Open-weight models perform worse on all metrics. Recovery quality emerges as largely a distinct capability axis in cross-benchmark analyses. A human study confirms alignment between LLM judgments and human annotators.

Conclusion: IHBench provides a targeted, practical framework for evaluating post-interruption recovery in voice agents. The findings favor closed-weight models for robustness in interrupted workflows and highlight recovery quality as a distinct, critical capability demanding explicit assessment in future benchmarks and model development.

Abstract: Voice agents deployed in structured workflows (customer service, healthcare scheduling, account management) must handle frequent user interruptions while maintaining progress through multi-step procedures. Existing benchmarks for speech-capable models focus on the timing of interruptions: barge-in detection, endpointing, and turn-taking dynamics. They leave unmeasured what happens after the interruption: does the agent resume the workflow at the correct step? Does it address the user's interjection? Does it avoid re-delivering content the user already heard?
  We introduce IHBench (Interruption Handling Benchmark), a benchmark that evaluates post-interruption recovery in voice agents executing state-machine-driven workflows across 10 enterprise domains. Six interruption types are injected at controlled points mid-utterance, with per-interruption evaluation rubrics generated alongside the data. Each interruption is scored on two axes: task fulfillment and recovery quality.
  We evaluate 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and the open-weight community. Models vary widely, and recovery quality depends strongly on the interruption type. Across our experiments, closed-weight models are consistently more robust to interruptions than open-weight ones: they win far more often on task fulfillment, degrade roughly 3.3x more slowly as conversations grow longer, and show no audio-versus-text modality gap, whereas the open-weight models lose ground on all three. A human study validates the LLM judge against human annotators, and a cross-benchmark analysis against AudioMultiChallenge indicates that recovery quality is a largely distinct capability axis.

</details>


### [215] [Comparing Linear Probes with Mahalanobis Cosine Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19603)
*Zhuofan Josh Ying,Peter Hase,Nikolaus Kriegeskorte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MCS linearly predicts OOD AUROC for Gaussian-projected, balanced classes; a closed-form relation via signal-to-noise ratio, validated across models and layers; identifies regimes where linearity holds or fails; argues MCS is a principled alternative to Euclidean cosine for probe comparison.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether task-aware MCS provides a robust, theoretically grounded way to compare linear probes and predict out-of-distribution performance, extending prior empirical findings.

Method: Theoretical derivation under the assumptions of balanced classes and Gaussian projections. Demonstrates that both OOD AUROC and MCS are sigmoid functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR); hence, they are linearly related. Empirically verify the theory across models, layers, and concept domains and identify conditions under which linearity breaks.

Result: Shows a linear relationship between MCS and OOD AUROC (under stated assumptions), with high explained variance; provides conditions predicting when linearity fails; supports using MCS as a theoretically grounded alternative to Euclidean cosine for comparing linear probes.

Conclusion: MCS is a principled, effective metric for probe comparison with strong theoretical and empirical support; generalizes prior findings and offers guidance on when linearity between MCS and OOD performance holds.

Abstract: Linear probes are widely used in interpretability research and often compared by cosine similarity. The Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) between two directions, which reweights the inner product by test data covariance, is a natural task-aware refinement. Ying et al. (2026) report that a probe's MCS to a reference probe trained on the out-of-distribution (OOD) data near-perfectly linearly predicts the probe's OOD AUROC (R^2 = 0.98). Here, we extend this empirical finding across models, layers, and concept domains, and prove this general phenomenon in closed form: For balanced classes whose projections are Gaussian, OOD AUROC and MCS to the reference probe are linear because both are sigmoid-shaped functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on the test data. The theory also predicts when this linearity fails, which we verify empirically. MCS offers a theoretically grounded and empirically effective alternative to Euclidean cosine similarity for comparing linear probes.

</details>


### [216] [Latent Confounded Causal Discovery via Lie Bracket Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19610)
*Sridhar Mahadevan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: Recent work on Kan-Do-Calculus (KDC) has established that the boundary between passive observation and active intervention in causal inference is a category-theoretic bi-adjunction, with interventions modeled by left Kan extensions and conditioning by right Kan extensions. This paper introduces two causal discovery algorithms under latent confounding, building on the information-geometric and categorical consequences of KDC. In smooth statistical settings, Radon-Nikodym derivatives between observational and interventional measures induce local causal vector fields; failures of these fields to close under Lie brackets become computable Frobenius residuals, which we interpret as witnesses of failed visible integrability and possible latent or unmodeled structure. Our first algorithm, BRIDGE (Bracket Residuals for Interventional Discovery and Geometric Estimation), combines an interventional density or Radon-Nikodym-ratio engine with a geometric screen that proposes a high-recall family of admissible arrows, identifies non-closing visible pairs as latent-obstruction candidates, and passes the reduced family to downstream score-based or differentiable discovery routines. The second algorithmic contribution, Spectral Kan-Do Flow Matching (SKFM), learns amortized intervention fields and factors latent curvature spectrally, exposing the direct Lie-space endpoint toward which BRIDGE points. A detailed set of experiments show that both algorithms are capable of discovering causal models with latent confounders while collapsing the super-exponential space of possible DAGs by many orders of magnitude. This paper introduces a new paradigm in causal discovery, where latent structure is inferred directly from the geometry of intervention-induced flows.

</details>


### [217] [SEAGAN: domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19623)
*Antriksh Srivastava,Soumyashree Kar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph-based modeling of A-Ci curves (SEAGAN) improves biochemical limitation-state identification on leaf photosynthesis data; edge-aware graph attention with kNN connectivity yields strong performance on synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate identification of active biochemical limitation states along A-Ci curves is challenging and uncertain; leveraging graph representations of curve points can capture local dependencies and transitions to improve classification.

Method: Represent A-Ci curve points as graph nodes with edges from distance-based kNN and auxiliary-signal-guided connectivity; use edge attributes to encode pairwise relations. Apply SEAGAN (domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network) with process-aware node features, edge attributes, kNN topology, and graph attention; train with weighted cross-entropy loss.

Result: On a large synthetic dataset with known ground-truth states, graph-based models outperform baselines; best SEAGAN configuration achieves F1 = 0.857 and accuracy = 0.882, with edge-aware attention over local kNN neighborhoods providing the most gain, particularly near biochemical transition regions.

Conclusion: Graph representations of A-Ci curves enhance biochemical limitation-state analysis; edge-aware graph attention leveraging local neighborhoods is the most effective strategy, demonstrating promise for dynamic plant-process modeling.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) provide a flexible framework for learning from scientific data linked through physical, biological, or functional relationships. One promising domain is plant physiology, where measured responses often arise from multiple interacting processes whose exact separation remains difficult even with manual intervention. In plant physiology, a key example is the A-Ci curve, which relates net CO2 assimilation rate (Anet) to leaf intercellular CO2 concentration (Ci) and is used to estimate photosynthetic parameters in leaf and crop-canopy models. However, reliable estimation requires identifying the active biochemical limitation state at each curve point, which remains a major source of uncertainty. Here, we formulate limitation-state identification along A-Ci curves as a graph-based node classification problem, with curve points as nodes. Domain-specific graph representations are created using distance-based k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) and auxiliary-signal-guided (ASG) connectivity, with edge attributes encoding pairwise relations. The framework was evaluated against conventional learning baselines, graph-based architectures, and an automated fitting-based benchmark. Results on a large synthetic dataset with known ground-truth limitation states show that graph-based models improve classification, particularly near biochemical transition regions. The best-performing configuration, SEAGAN (domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes), integrates process-aware node features, edge attributes, kNN connectivity, and graph attention with weighted cross-entropy loss, achieving an F1-score of 0.857 and an accuracy of 0.882. The results show that representing A-Ci curves as graphs improves biochemical limitation-state analysis, with edge-aware attention over local kNN neighborhoods providing the most effective strategy.

</details>


### [218] [MassSpecGym in the Wild: Uncovering and Correcting Evaluation Pitfalls in AI-Driven Molecule Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19624)
*Hongxuan Liu,Roman Bushuiev,Ivy Lightheart,Mrunali Manjrekar,Anton Bushuiev,Magdalena Lederbauer,Filip Jozefov,Yinkai Wang,Soha Hassoun,Josef Sivic,James Taylor,Runzhong Wang,David Healey,Tomáš Pluskal,Connor W. Coley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Audits MS/MS benchmarks; identifies data leakage, shortcut learning, and bugs affecting MassSpecGym; provides v1.5 with fixes and recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable benchmarks are essential for MS/MS ML; current practice shows vulnerabilities that can mislead model evaluation.

Method: Review 26 papers using MassSpecGym, identify evaluation failures, perform replication experiments, quantify impact, and release an improved benchmarking suite.

Result: ≥17 papers affected; three failure classes (data leakage, shortcut learning, implementation bugs and metric divergence); issues distort evaluation and MassSpecGym’s intended reliability; MassSpecGym v1.5 addresses these failures.

Conclusion: Provides generalizable recommendations to improve MS/MS benchmarks and evaluation setups; release of MassSpecGym v1.5 to enhance trust and reproducibility in the field.

Abstract: Reliable benchmarking is critical for developing machine learning models for tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) based molecule discovery. Subtle issues in experimental design and model evaluation procedures can degrade the trustworthiness of such benchmarks and lead to erroneous conclusions. We conduct a thorough review of model evaluation issues in the recent MS/MS machine learning literature, using the standard MassSpecGym benchmark suite as a case study to illustrate the impact of these issues. We find evaluation issues in at least 17 of 26 papers reporting MassSpecGym benchmark results in the first year of its adoption. We isolate three classes of failures: (i) data leakage, (ii) shortcut learning, and (iii) implementation bugs and metric divergence. Through extensive experimentation and code replication, we quantify the impact of these issues and show how they corrupt the evaluation standards MassSpecGym was designed to enforce. We distill our findings into recommendations generalizable to MS/MS challenges, benchmarks, and custom evaluation setups. We also release MassSpecGym v1.5, an implementation of our recommendations in the MassSpecGym benchmarking suite which addresses the failure modes identified in this audit. MassSpecGym v1.5 is publicly available at https://github.com/pluskal-lab/MassSpecGym.

</details>


### [219] [Hard or Just Unreached? Diagnosing the Sampling Blind Spot in Math-Reasoning Difficulty Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19636)
*Luca Zhou,Sajel Shah,Emanuele Rodolà,Roberto Dessì*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pass@k underestimates hard math items; a six-chain deterministic regime with greedy decoding plus residual perturbations via activation grafting recovers a nontrivial fraction of previously seed-unsolved items, revealing a structurally identifiable hardness in the residual stream.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether pass@k is the sole proxy for example difficulty in math reasoning benchmarks; test if internal manipulations can unlock hard items without changing decoding strategy.

Method: Evaluate GSM8K/MATH across four open-weight models; compare six-chain deterministic regime (greedy + five cheap residual-stream perturbations via activation grafting) to standard sampling; quantify share of previously unsolved items that become solvable under matched compute; test perturbations' mechanistic distinctness via cross-kind fix-set Jaccard; treat activation grafting as diagnostic/diversification tool, not decoding.

Result: 10.3-22.9% of seed-unsolved examples become solvable under matched compute with the six-chain regime; greedy alone solves ≤6% on these cells; perturbations recovery scales with budget; perturbations are mechanistically distinct (Jaccard ≤ 0.47); results indicate pass@k=0% stratum is identifiable in the residual stream, not in ordinary inference.

Conclusion: Pass@k has blind spots; some hardest items are latent in internal representations and can be exposed via targeted interventions; this supports using internal diagnostics like activation grafting to understand and diversify model evaluation and robustness; the hardest items are structurally identifiable rather than unreachable by standard decoding.

Abstract: Math and science reasoning benchmarks rely on pass@k, the fraction of sampled chains that reach gold, as the canonical per-example difficulty signal. The same signal drives RL with verifiable rewards, math data curation, synthetic curricula, and verifier training. We show this proxy has a persistent blind spot on its hardest stratum: on the eight free-form math cells we test (GSM8K and MATH across four open-weight models), 10.3-22.9% of the examples that no sampling seed solves in six tries are instead solved at matched compute by a six-chain deterministic regime. These are greedy decoding plus five cheap residual-stream perturbations applied via activation grafting, while greedy alone solves at most 6% on these math cells. Recovery scales with the additional budget, across perturbations whose mechanistic distinctness we verify across all twelve cells (cross-kind fix-set Jaccard <= 0.47 in every setup). Activation grafting is used as an intervention on internal representations, not a decoding method; we use it purely as a diagnostic and diversification tool, and our recovered items show that the pass@k= 0 % stratum is structurally identifiable in the residual stream rather than that the unmodified model reaches them under ordinary inference.

</details>


### [220] [Convex training of Lipschitz-regularized shallow neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19652)
*Chao Yin,Antoine Lesage-Landry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Convexified post-training for shallow nets improves Lipschitz-regularized adversarial robustness with global-optimal guarantees and potential accuracy gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-convex Lipschitz-regularized training makes adversarial robustness hard to achieve; a tractable method with global guarantees that can refine an existing model is desirable.

Method: Introduce a convex restriction of the non-convex training objective and solve it to global optimality. The approach can be used as a post-processing step, taking a pre-trained network and solving the convex program to obtain a solution that is guaranteed to be no worse than the initial one.

Result: Experiments on real-world regression tasks under adversarial settings show the convex program yields lower values on the Lipschitz-regularized objective than existing methods. In some datasets, the networks produced are both more accurate and more robust to adversarial attacks.

Conclusion: The proposed convex training procedure provides a practical, guaranteed-no-worse refinement for pre-trained shallow networks, improving adversarial robustness and, in some cases, accuracy.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce a training procedure for shallow neural networks that promotes robustness against adversarial attacks. We solve a non-convex Lipschitz-regularized training program by introducing a convex restriction that can be efficiently solved to global optimality. Our approach can be employed as a post-processing step by taking a pre-trained network as an initial solution to then solving the convex program whose optimal network is guaranteed to be no worse than the initial one. We illustrate the improvements of our training procedure with experiments using real world datasets for regression tasks under an adversarial setting. We show numerically that solving our proposed convex program yields networks with lower objective values on the Lipschitz-regularized program compared to existing methods. Additionally, we show that on certain datasets, networks obtained using our convex training program are both more accurate and robust with respect to adversarial attacks.

</details>


### [221] [LOKI: Memory-Free Null-Space Constrained Lifelong Knowledge Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19679)
*Masih Eskandar,Miquel Sirera Perelló,Stratis Ioannidis,Jennifer Dy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LOKI enables flexible lifelong knowledge editing by dynamically selecting layers with HSIC and projecting gradient updates onto the null-space of weights, removing the need for previous knowledge data and improving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses rigidity of fixed-layer updates and reliance on previous data statistics, aiming to mitigate forgetting while efficiently incorporating new knowledge.

Method: Dynamically selects layers to update per sample using Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion; projects gradient updates into the null-space of the model weights to preserve prior capabilities; requires no access to prior data statistics.

Result: Outperforms baselines across diverse tasks, achieving up to 14% improvement in average accuracy and better forgetting control.

Conclusion: LOKI provides a flexible, data-efficient framework for lifelong knowledge editing with strong empirical gains and broad applicability.

Abstract: Lifelong knowledge editing aims to efficiently and sequentially update language models over time, as new knowledge becomes available or when the model makes mistakes, while preserving acceptable performance on past knowledge. One unresolved challenge is that existing methods modify a fixed set of layers for all new knowledge samples, reducing flexibility and increasing catastrophic forgetting. Another is requiring access to previous knowledge and extensive pre-processing to obtain data statistics. To address these challenges, we introduce LOKI, a novel approach that uses dynamic layer selection based on the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion and projects gradient updates onto the null-space of the model weights, bypassing the requirement for previous knowledge access. We show that LOKI achieves superior performance to existing approaches across a wide variety of experiments, achieving up to a 14\% improvement in average accuracy.

</details>


### [222] [Multi-Granular Attention-Driven Reinforcement Learning Framework for Web Intelligent Enhancement Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19690)
*Navin Chhibber,Deepak Singh,Anokh Kishore,Nikita Chawla,K. Anguraj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MGAR-WIES integrates semantic graph modeling, attention, and adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning to process heterogeneous web data for personalized, context-aware services, achieving 80% accuracy versus baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability challenges in continuously evolving web environments where data are heterogeneous and dynamic.

Method: Convert heterogeneous structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data into unified feature representations; build a dynamic semantic graph; apply attention-enhanced graph embeddings to capture local and global dependencies; deploy adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning utilizing attention-aware semantic states for actions (content recommendation, navigation optimization, service adaptation); incorporate online feedback to update graphs and policies in real time.

Result: Reported 80% accuracy improvement over existing approaches without detailed dataset or baseline descriptions.

Conclusion: MGAR-WIES offers a framework that combines semantic graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and adaptive reinforcement learning to achieve sustained adaptability and performance in web intelligence tasks; further details on datasets, ablations, and scalability are needed for assessment.

Abstract: From the past few years, web intelligent enhancement systems increasingly rely on heterogeneous and dynamic web data to deliver personalized, context-aware services. However, traditional machine learning, deep learning, and reinforcement learning models often struggle with semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability in continuously evolving web environments. In this research, a Multi-Granular Attention-based Reinforcement Web Intelligent Enhancement System (MGAR-WIES) is proposed to address the challenges by integrating semantic graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and adaptive reinforcement learning. Initially, heterogeneous web data comprising structured, semi-structured and unstructured sources are collected and preprocessed for generating unified feature representations. These representations are transformed into a dynamic semantic graph, where entities and their relationships are modeled by using graph embeddings enhanced by attention mechanisms for capturing both local relevance and global contextual dependencies. Subsequently, an adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning strategy leverages the attention-aware semantic states to optimize personalized web actions like content recommendation, navigation optimization, and service adaptation. Finally, the continuous online feedback is further integrated to update graph representations and learning policies in real time by ensuring sustained adaptability and performance. The proposed MGAR-WIES acheived better results in terms of accuracy (80%) when compared with existing approaches.

</details>


### [223] [Efficiently Representing Algorithms With Chain-of-Thought Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19697)
*Yanhong Li,Anej Svete,Ashish Sabharwal,William Merrill*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoT transformers can efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms with poly-log overhead, enabling near-optimal algorithmic performance (e.g., O(n log n) sort, O(E+V log V) for Dijkstra) under several model variants. This improves on prior Turing-machine-based simulations which require quadratic overhead. Results hold for finite-precision with poly-log width and rightmost unique hard attention, and extend to continuous CoT and hybrid architectures with RNNs. Overhead reduces to log^2 for flat instruction sets, and to log for multiplication-free flat instructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Turing machines are not the most convenient model for designing/analysing algorithms; Word RAM is the standard for such tasks. The question: can CoT transformers efficiently emulate Word RAM algorithms, not just simulate Turing machines?

Method: Theoretical analysis establishing equivalence (simulation) with poly-log overhead across three settings: (1) finite-precision transformers with poly-log width and rightmost unique hard attention; (2) continuous CoT (vectors); (3) hybrid architecture with transformer layers on top of a linear RNN. Construct encoding of Word RAM operations into CoT reasoning steps and analyze step complexity.

Result: CoT can efficiently simulate any Word RAM algorithm with poly-log overhead in n. Specific concrete results: sort in O(n log n), Dijkstra in O(E+V log V). Overhead is poly-log; reduces to log^2 for flat instruction sets; and to log if the flat set is multiplication-free.

Conclusion: This demonstrates that CoT reasoning, under plausible architectural assumptions, achieves algorithmic efficiency on Word RAM, surpassing prior Turing-machine-based simulations and aligning with practical algorithmic reasoning. It broadens the theoretical understanding of LLMs' computational capabilities and suggests directions for practical implementations.

Abstract: The increasing popularity of \emph{reasoning} models -- language models that output a series of reasoning or thought tokens before producing an answer -- is justified, in part, by theoretical results showing that chain-of-thought (CoT) transformers can simulate Turing machines, and thus perform arbitrary computation. However, the Turing machine, while suitable for complexity-theoretic analysis, is not convenient, intuitive, or efficient for discussing algorithms. Algorithms are typically designed and analyzed at a higher level of abstraction, captured by the \emph{Word RAM} model with random-access memory and unit-cost operations on $\bigO(\log n)$-bit words. As a result, Word RAM algorithms can be substantially more efficient than their Turing machine counterparts, raising the question: \emph{Can CoT transformers efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms?} For instance, can they sort $n$ items in $\bigO(n \log n)$ steps or run Dijkstra's algorithm in $\bigO(E + V \log V)$ steps? We answer affirmatively, up to poly-logarithmic overhead. We first establish this for finite-precision transformers with poly-logarithmic width and rightmost unique hard attention, then strengthen the result to two more practical settings with finite width and log-precision: \emph{continuous} CoT, where reasoning takes the form of vectors rather than tokens, and a \emph{hybrid} architecture in which transformer layers sit atop a recurrent (linear RNN) layer. In all three cases, we find that CoT \emph{can} efficiently simulate any Word RAM algorithm with only a poly-logarithmic overhead in $n$. This overhead reduces to log-square when the Word RAM has a ``flat'' instruction set, and only logarithmic for multiplication-free flat instructions -- in stark contrast to known CoT simulations of Turing machines, which require quadratic overhead over Word RAM.

</details>


### [224] [Efficient Neural Network Model Selection for Few-Class Application Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19712)
*Bryan Bo Cao,Abhinav Sharma,Lawrence O'Gorman,Michael Coss,Shubham Jain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a data-centric metric, 'few-class distinctiveness', to guide model selection for datasets with few classes, enabling faster evaluation and more compact models; demonstrates 6–29× speedups and up to 42% smaller models (vs YOLOv5-nano) in mobile/robot/IoT contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarking emphasizes large-class datasets; real-world tasks often involve fewer than ten classes, making traditional evaluation and selection inefficient. A data-side metric could streamline model choice and deployment.

Method: Define a classification difficulty measure based on data properties, termed 'few-class distinctiveness'. Use this metric to compare models and datasets without full retraining. Extend scaled model families to smaller architectures and validate across mobile robot, drone, and IoT settings.

Result: The metric enables 6–29× faster model/dataset comparisons and supports deploying models up to 42% smaller than YOLOv5-nano with comparable accuracy, achieving practical efficiency gains in resource-constrained applications.

Conclusion: Data-side properties can effectively guide efficient model selection for few-class problems. The 'few-class distinctiveness' metric reduces training/testing burden while preserving performance, facilitating deployment of compact models in mobile robotics and IoT contexts.

Abstract: While much effort has focused on developing and benchmarking high-performance neural networks, less attention has been given to how dataset properties, known to practitioners, can guide efficient model selection. Neural models are typically evaluated on datasets with thousands of classes, yet many real-world applications involve fewer than ten. To address this understudied but common setting, we develop a measure of classification difficulty based on data-side properties and show how it enables more efficient model selection for few-class datasets, where traditional approaches are less effective. We term this phenomenon "few-class distinctiveness". Our metric allows comparison of models and datasets 6 to 29$\times$ faster than repeated training and testing. Leveraging this insight, we extend scaled model families below the smallest published models, achieving greater efficiency at similar accuracy, for example models up to 42% smaller than YOLOv5-nano for a mobile robot task. Targeting resource-constrained applications, we demonstrate few-class model selection across mobile robot, drone, and IoT scenarios, highlighting practical gains in efficiency without sacrificing performance.

</details>


### [225] [OnDeFog: Online Decision Transformer under Frame Dropping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19721)
*Daiki Yotsufuji,Kenta Nishihara,Shoma Shimizu,Kento Uchida,Shinichi Shirakawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OnDeFog fuses DeFog with an online decision transformer to handle frame-dropping RL; it outperforms both the original ODT and DeFog under challenging dropping conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frame dropping due to delays or sensor failures causes missing states/rewards and degrades performance. Offline DeFog struggles to generalize to unseen states not well represented in training data.

Method: Integrate DeFog mechanisms into the online decision transformer (ODT) and evaluate through online interaction with the environment, focusing on high frame-drop-rate scenarios and datasets with many low-reward samples.

Result: OnDeFog outperforms ODT in high frame-drop-rate environments and surpasses DeFog on datasets containing a large amount of low-reward data.

Conclusion: Online integration of DeFog mechanisms with ODT yields robust performance under frame dropping and improves generalization to novel states, making OnDeFog more effective for real-world, unreliable sensing conditions.

Abstract: In challenging real-world reinforcement learning applications, communication delays or sensor failures often cause frame dropping, in which the agent cannot receive the dropped states and associated rewards. To address the performance degradation caused by frame dropping, the Decision Transformer under Random Frame Dropping (DeFog) was developed by incorporating additional mechanisms into the decision transformer to tackle frame dropping. Although DeFog can mitigate performance degradation in frame-dropping environments, since DeFog is an offline learning method, it struggles to effectively generalize to novel states not adequately represented in the training dataset. In this study, we propose OnDeFog, which integrates the mechanisms in DeFog with the online decision transformer (ODT), an online reinforcement learning method that learns policies through direct environmental interaction. Comprehensive experimental evaluation demonstrates that our proposed OnDeFog achieves superior performance compared to ODT in environments characterized by high dropping frame rate and outperforms DeFog on datasets containing a large amount of low-reward data.

</details>


### [226] [Federated Bilevel Performative Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19734)
*Liangxin Qian,Chang Liu,Xuanyu Cao,Jun Zhao,Kwok-Yan Lam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This work extends federated bilevel optimization to performative settings by defining a federated bilevel performatively stable (FBPS) point, proving existence/uniqueness under decoupled-risk assumptions, and offering two algorithms (FBi-RRM and FBi-SGD) with convergence guarantees and empirical validation across strategic regression, meta-learning, and CNN-based classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses distribution shifts caused by deployed decisions in federated bilevel learning (decision-dependent, client-specific distributions). Seeks a stable, well-posed solution concept and practical algorithms that guarantee convergence and good generalization.

Method: Introduce FBPS as a decoupled-risk-based stability concept for federated bilevel problems. Prove sufficient conditions for existence and uniqueness of FBPS. Propose two algorithms: FBi-RRM (linear convergence under a contraction condition) and FBi-SGD (communication-efficient stochastic method using federated hypergradient estimation; convergence under diminishing steps when sensitivities are small).

Result: Theoretical: conditions for FBPS existence/uniqueness and contraction-based linear convergence of FBi-RRM; FBi-SGD provides convergence guarantees in stochastic, federated settings with diminishing step sizes given small sensitivities. Empirical: experiments on strategic regression and meta strategic classification validate stability thresholds and improved meta-generalization; CNN-based results show practical effectiveness in nonconvex neural networks.

Conclusion: The work advances federated performative optimization by defining a stable FBPS point, establishing existence/uniqueness, and delivering two convergent algorithms with empirical validation across convex and nonconvex tasks, highlighting improved generalization under decision-dependent distribution shifts.

Abstract: Federated bilevel optimization is widely used for nested learning problems across distributed clients, such as federated hyperparameter tuning and meta-learning under privacy and communication constraints. Most existing formulations assume fixed client data distributions, which can be violated by performativity, where deployed decisions reshape client behavior and data collection, inducing client-specific, decision-dependent distribution shift. We study federated bilevel performative prediction, where both upper-level (UL) and lower-level (LL) objectives are evaluated under client-dependent, decision-dependent distributions. We formalize the federated bilevel performatively stable (FBPS) point under a decoupled-risk perspective and provide sufficient conditions for its existence and uniqueness. We then develop two federated methods to compute the FBPS solution: FBi-RRM, which converges linearly under a contraction condition, and FBi-SGD, a communication-efficient stochastic method based on federated hypergradient estimation with convergence guarantees under diminishing step sizes when sensitivities are sufficiently small. Experiments on strategic regression and meta strategic classification validate the predicted stability thresholds and demonstrate improved meta-generalization over non-performative baselines, and CNN-based classification further demonstrates the practical effectiveness of the proposed methods in nonconvex neural network settings.

</details>


### [227] [Manifold Bandits: Bayesian Curriculum Learning over the Latent Geometry of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19750)
*Darrien McKenzie,Nicklas Hansen,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG
