TL;DR: A structure-aware Bayesian curriculum for sampling problems in RL with LLMs, modeling problem space as a manifold and learning signals with a hierarchical task tree; shows that considering structure and problem type yields better downstream performance than difficulty-focused sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve training efficiency and reasoning in LLMs by smarter problem sampling; standard bandit treatments ignore latent task structure and non-stationarity in the learning signal.

Method: Introduce Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC), organize tasks into a hierarchical task tree, apply Bayesian updating to guide sampling over the manifold of problems, accounting for endogenous non-stationarity via latent representations.

Result: Empirical analysis reveals tradeoffs between productivity, diversity, and utility; structure-aware sampling improves downstream performance; simply maximizing difficulty is insufficient.

Conclusion: Emphasizes structure and type-awareness in problem sampling for RL with LLMs; BMC provides a principled framework for structured bandit-like learning.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a central approach for improving reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs), where training efficiency depends critically on how problems are sampled during optimization. Existing adaptive curriculum learning methods typically prioritize prompts of intermediate difficulty, treating problem selection as a standard bandit problem with independent arms and overlooking the structured, heterogeneous nature of the task space. In this work, we frame problem sampling as a manifold-structured bandit problem with endogenous non-stationarity: problems are related through the model's latent representation space, and sampling decisions can steer how learning signals evolve across that space. To operationalize this perspective, we introduce Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC), a structure-aware framework that organizes problems into a hierarchical task tree and applies Bayesian learning to guide sampling. Empirically, we find that different sampling strategies induce non-trivial tradeoffs between productivity (learning signal), diversity (coverage of the task manifold), and utility (evaluation relevance). These results show that prioritizing difficulty alone is insufficient for strong downstream performance, highlighting the importance of incorporating structure and type-awareness into problem sampling.

</details>


### [228] [Learning universal approximations for partial differential equations with Physics-Informed Broad Learning System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19754)
*Zhiwen Yu,Derong Yang,Liujian Zhang,Kaixiang Yang,Peilin Zhan,Jianmin Lv,Jane You,C. L. Philip Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PIBLS is a backpropagation-free, least-squares solver for PDEs with universal approximation; it yields 1–3 orders of magnitude faster performance and higher accuracy than PINNs on linear and nonlinear PDEs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional mesh-based PDE solvers are robust but costly; PINNs offer mesh-free solutions but suffer from slow convergence and instability. There is a need for a fast, accurate, mesh-free PDE solver.

Method: PIBLS reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization within a Physics-Informed Broad Learning System, eliminating backpropagation. The authors extend the method to nonlinear PDEs, provide a rigorous universal approximation proof, and validate with experiments on linear and nonlinear PDEs.

Result: PIBLS delivers 1–3 orders of magnitude speed improvements over conventional PINNs and achieves significantly higher solution accuracy; a universal approximation property is established for PIBLS in the nonlinear PDE setting.

Conclusion: PIBLS offers a computationally efficient framework for scientific machine learning, delivering a practical, high-speed alternative for real-time simulation and design optimization tasks.

Abstract: Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a central role in modeling complex physical, biological, and engineering systems. While traditional numerical solvers are robust, they often incur prohibitive computational costs due to mesh dependencies, whereas recent Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a mesh-free alternative but frequently suffer from slow convergence and optimization instability. To bridge this gap, this article proposes the Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), a novel backpropagation-free framework that reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization. We improved an algorithm within this framework to handle nonlinear PDEs efficiently and provide a rigorous mathematical proof establishing the universal approximation property of PIBLS for these equations. Experiments on linear and nonlinear PDEs demonstrate that PIBLS is one to three orders of magnitude faster than conventional PINNs while achieving significantly higher solution accuracy. This framework provides a computationally efficient paradigm for scientific machine learning, offering a practical, high-speed alternative for real-time simulation and design optimization tasks.

</details>


### [229] [An Information Theoretic Framework for Graph Novelty Generation via Latent Mixture Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19770)
*Itsuki Nakagawa,Kenji Yamanishi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An information-theoretic method for graph novelty generation using latent-space mixtures and MDL-based novelty and reliability constraints; it provides theory with convergence guarantees and empirical validation on synthetic and benchmark graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable principled generation of novel graph data that remain globally structurally consistent, while controlling the extent of novelty and impact on the underlying mixture model.

Method: Embed graphs into a latent space, model the latent distribution with finite mixture models, and generate novel samples by enforcing: (i) novelty — samples are poorly explained by all existing mixture components; (ii) reliability — samples minimally affect the overall mixture structure, constrained via the Minimum Description Length principle. Theoretical analysis yields convergence rates for misclassification probabilities under thresholding; experiments validate the approach.

Result: The paper proves that with appropriate thresholds, probabilities of misclassifying non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero at explicit rates. Empirical results on synthetic and benchmark graphs show principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk, demonstrating both theoretical soundness and practical utility.

Conclusion: The framework offers a principled, MDL-based approach to graph novelty generation with provable guarantees and empirical support. Limitations include threshold selection, scalability to very large graphs, and sensitivity to the chosen latent-mixture representation; future work could address automatic threshold tuning, alternative distance metrics, and application to other data modalities.

Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic framework for graph novelty generation, which aims to generate data that are distinct from existing patterns while preserving global structural consistency. Our approach embeds data into a latent space, models the latent distribution using finite mixture models, and generates novel samples by imposing explicit novelty and reliability conditions formulated in terms of description length. Specifically, novelty is enforced by requiring generated samples to be poorly explained by all existing mixture components, while reliability constrains their impact on the overall mixture structure under the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that, with appropriate threshold choices, the probabilities of misclassifying non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero with explicit rates. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed method enables principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.

</details>


### [230] [Flow Map Denoisers: Traversing the Distortion-Perception Plane for Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19802)
*Nicolas Zilberstein,Morteza Mardani,Santiago Segarra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A single flow map model yields a one-parameter family of denoisers that trades off MMSE vs perceptual quality (the DP frontier) via a lookahead t; theoretically optimal for Gaussian targets, empirically effective for natural images; extends to Plug-and-Play for general inverse problems; validated on CelebA 128x128 and AFHQ 256x256 with competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the distortion-perception tradeoff in image restoration without requiring multiple models or supervised data, by learning a unified flow-based denoiser that can traverse the DP frontier through a controllable parameter.

Method: Propose flow map models (extension of flow matching) that learn an average flow field; introduce a lookahead parameter t that yields a one-parameter family of denoisers spanning the DP frontier; provide a theoretical result for Gaussian targets; integrate into Plug-and-Play solvers for general inverse problems.

Result: Varying t on Gaussian targets recovers the optimal DP frontier; natural images show similar behavior empirically. A single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff and matches or surpasses specialized baselines at both extremes. Experiments on CelebA (128×128) and AFHQ (256×256) across multiple linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate the approach.

Conclusion: A single flow map can cover the DP tradeoff in image restoration and general inverse problems, simplifying deployment and potentially outperforming task-specific baselines at both ends of the spectrum; the t-parameter provides a principled control of perceptual fidelity vs data-consistency.

Abstract: Image restoration faces a fundamental tradeoff: methods that minimize error produce blurry reconstructions, while those that maximize perceptual quality yield sharp but less faithful images. Existing approaches either commit to a single operating point on this distortion perception (DP) frontier or require paired-data supervision, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning of the sampler to access different points. We show that flow map models, a recent extension of flow matching for few-step sampling that learns an average field, implicitly define a one-parameter family of denoisers that continuously spans the DP frontier. The lookahead parameter t acts as a control knob between the MMSE and perceptual regimes. For Gaussian targets, we prove that varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier; for natural images, we observe similar behavior empirically. Within a Plug-and-Play solver, the same mechanism extends to general inverse problems, where it controls a tradeoff between perceptual alignment and data consistency. Despite the lack of exact optimality guarantees in this setting, a single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff, matching or exceeding specialized baselines at both extremes. Extensive experiments on CelebA ($128\times 128$) and AFHQ ($256\times 256$) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate our findings.

</details>


### [231] [Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling for Stable RLHF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19818)
*Licheng Pan,Haocheng Yang,Haoxuan Li,Yichen Sun,Yunsheng Lu,Shijian Wang,Lei Shen,Yuan Lu,Zhixuan Chu,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM) enhances RLHF by calibrating reward-model uncertainty with quantile-based conformal prediction and reweighting GRPO advantages using heteroscedastic variance decomposition, reducing reward hacking and improving alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLHF relies on reward models that lack reliable uncertainty signaling and on policy optimization that can overemphasize unreliable rewards. As policies generate more diverse outputs, this creates vulnerability to reward hacking and miscalibration.

Method: Introduce quantile-based conformal prediction to calibrate reward-model uncertainty; apply heteroscedastic variance decomposition to reweight GRPO advantages; integrate into standard RLHF pipelines; evaluate on HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF.

Result: Reward model calibration improves, reward hacking is reduced, and downstream alignment quality improves against standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.

Conclusion: UARM offers a practical framework for uncertainty-aware reward modeling in RLHF, mitigating overreliance on unreliable rewards and enhancing alignment; future work could explore broader task settings and ablations of the calibration and reweighting components.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) aligns large language models by training reward models on preference data and optimizing policies to maximize predicted rewards. However, this pipeline faces two fundamental challenges: (1) reward models cannot signal when their predictions are unreliable, since they usually act as deterministic point estimators; and (2) modern group-based policy optimization can amplify unreliable reward signals, as exemplified by GRPO's uniform treatment of rewards during advantage computation. As policies explore increasingly diverse responses, these two limitations create a critical vulnerability: unreliable reward estimates may be granted disproportionate influence, triggering severe reward hacking. We propose Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM), which equips reward models with calibrated uncertainty via quantile-based conformal prediction and reweights GRPO advantages through heteroscedastic variance decomposition. Experiments across HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF demonstrate that UARM significantly improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared to standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.

</details>


### [232] [Enhancing Graph Neural Networks Using Proximity Graphs for Dust Source Emission Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19825)
*Maryam Sanisales,Zahed Rahmati,Ali Darvishi Boloorani,Ali Vefghi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proximity-graph–based GNNs improve dust source emission forecasting over random-graph baselines and LSTM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dust storms exhibit complex spatiotemporal dynamics that are hard to capture with traditional Forecasting methods; graph-structured representations can effectively model spatial dependencies.

Method: Construct input graphs from proximity graphs (Delaunay, Gabriel, k-NN, Yao) and apply GNNs (GraphSAGE, GCN, GAT) for message passing; compare against random graphs and LSTM baselines.

Result: GNNs with proximity graphs significantly outperform random-graph GNNs and LSTM in dust source emission forecasting.

Conclusion: Integrating proximity-graph representations with GNNs yields robust, accurate dust source forecasts; the choice of proximity graph topology critically impacts performance.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of dust source emissions is critical for mitigating the significant environmental and health hazards posed by dust storms. Traditional forecasting methods often struggle to capture the complex spatiotemporal dynamics of these phenomena.
  In this paper, we demonstrate that proximity graphs enable Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to effectively model the intricate spatial and temporal relationships between data points. Specifically, we use proximity graphs--such as Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel graph, k-Nearest Neighbor graph, and Yao graph--as the input for GNNs (including GraphSAGE, Graph Convolutional Networks, and Graph Attention Networks) to perform message passing.
  Our approach highlights the effectiveness of integrating proximity graphs with GNNs for robust and accurate dust source forecasting. To emphasize the importance of proximity graph representations, we compare our method against GNNs using random graphs for message passing. The results show that GNNs with proximity graphs significantly outperform those with random graphs and are also far superior to Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model in dust source emission forecasting.

</details>


### [233] [When, Where, and How: Adaptive Binning for Tabular Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19827)
*Daehwan Kim,Haejun Chung,Ikbeom Jang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive Binning introduces a training-adaptive, feature-wise discretization pretext for tabular self-supervised learning, coupling discretization with representation learning to improve downstream performance on medical tabular data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tabular SSL often relies on fixed global discretization and/or feature-agnostic supervision, while medical tabular data are abundant but labeled data are costly; a method that adaptively discretizes per feature during training can improve both representation quality and downstream performance.

Method: A per-feature coarse-to-fine curriculum that detects plateau to dynamically refine discretization; selects splits informed by representation; a heterogeneity-aware objective combining categorical reconstruction for discrete features with ordinal supervision for numerical features; training-loop couples discretization updates with representation learning.

Result: Experiments on public medical tabular datasets under unified evaluation protocols show consistent gains for linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning; a medical tabular SSL benchmark is introduced; code is available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: Adaptive Binning improves value-space concentration and representation-space coherence in tabular SSL, enabling reproducible progress in medical tabular learning and reducing the need for dataset-specific discretization tuning.

Abstract: Medical tabular data are ubiquitous in clinical research, but deep learning for tables remains underexplored because reliable labels often require costly expert adjudication, even though structured clinical variables are routinely available in tabular form. Self-supervised learning can leverage these unlabeled tables, and recent binning-based pretexts offer a promising inductive bias, but existing objectives fix a single global quantile discretization and apply feature-agnostic supervision. We propose Adaptive Binning, a training-adaptive discretization pretext for tabular SSL that couples discretization to learning through a feature-wise coarse-to-fine curriculum. Motivated by the spectral bias of neural networks and the principles of curriculum learning, our method progressively refines discretization per feature upon plateau detection and selects representation-aware splits to jointly improve value-space concentration and representation-space coherence. A heterogeneity-aware objective unifies categorical reconstruction with ordinal supervision for numerical features, and experiments on public medical tabular datasets under unified evaluation protocols show consistent gains for linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning. We further introduce a medical tabular SSL benchmark with standardized protocols to support reproducible progress in this underexplored domain. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/Adaptive-Binning.

</details>


### [234] [Neural Additive and Basis Models with Feature Selection and Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19850)
*Yasutoshi Kishimoto,Kota Yamanishi,Takuya Matsuda,Shinichi Shirakawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introducing a feature-selection layer in NAM and NBM to reduce computational cost and enable two-input neural interactions in high-dimensional data, yielding competitive performance with state-of-the-art GAMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NAM/NBM offer interpretability through GAM-style architectures, but modeling feature interactions with two-input NNs becomes computationally prohibitive in high-dimensional settings. A trainable feature-selection mechanism is proposed to prune inputs and interactions, addressing scalability while preserving interpretability.

Method: Add a trainable feature-selection layer to NAM and NBM; learn and update selection weights during training; enable two-input neural nets to capture feature interactions; aim for reduced compute, smaller model size, and scalability to high-dimensional data.

Result: The proposed models are more computationally efficient than vanilla NAM and NBM and achieve better or comparable performance with state-of-the-art GAMs on benchmark tasks.

Conclusion: Incorporating a feature-selection layer resolves computational bottlenecks in NAM/NBM, enabling scalable use of two-input interactions in high-dimensional data while maintaining competitive accuracy and interpretability.

Abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) exhibit attractive performance in various fields but often suffer from low interpretability. The neural additive model (NAM) and its variant called the neural basis model (NBM) use neural networks (NNs) as nonlinear shape functions in generalized additive models (GAMs). Both models are highly interpretable and exhibit good performance and flexibility for NN training. NAM and NBM can provide and visualize the contribution of each feature to the prediction owing to GAM-based architectures. However, when using two-input NNs to consider feature interactions or when applying them to high-dimensional datasets, training NAM and NBM becomes intractable due to the increase in the computational resources required. This paper proposes incorporating the feature selection mechanism into NAM and NBM to resolve computational bottlenecks. We introduce the feature selection layer in both models and update the selection weights during training. Our method is simple and can reduce computational costs and model sizes compared to vanilla NAM and NBM. In addition, it enables us to use two-input NNs even in high-dimensional datasets and capture feature interactions. We demonstrate that the proposed models are computationally efficient compared to vanilla NAM and NBM, and they exhibit better or comparable performance with state-of-the-art GAMs.

</details>


### [235] [Physics-Informed Neural Network with Squeeze-Excitation-like Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19853)
*Yun-Fei Song,Long-Gang Pang,Fu-Peng Li,Jun-Jie Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SEA-PINN integrates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into PINNs, providing stable initialization and improved convergence, functioning as a lightweight plug-in with notable gains on high-frequency problems and when combined with TSA-PINN.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address instability and initialization sensitivity in physics-informed neural networks and boost nonlinear representation with minimal overhead.

Method: Incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation–inspired attention module to recalibrate neuron importance across layers, enabling dynamic feature weighting; achieves highly stable initialization without Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activations; operates as a plug-in to existing PINNs.

Result: On 17 of 20 benchmark problems, SEA-PINN shows near-zero variance and reduced initial loss, enabling quasi-deterministic starting points. It achieves competitive accuracy without Fourier features, and, relative to TSA-PINN-a’s high-frequency approach, SEA-PINN attains ~83% (versus 90% improvement in a high-frequency case). Integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN yields a ~42.49% performance boost.

Conclusion: SEA-PINN is a lightweight, plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation, stabilizes optimization, and improves reliability of physics-informed learning, with pronounced benefits in high-frequency regimes and when combined with TSA-PINN.

Abstract: We introduce SEA-PINN, a novel architecture that incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into physics-informed neural networks to dynamically recalibrate the importance of neurons across layers. A key feature of SEA-PINN is its highly stable initialization. On 17 out of 20 benchmark problems, SEA-PINN exhibit nearly negligible variance and significantly reduced initial loss, establishing a quasi-deterministic and favorable starting point for optimization. Notably, without employing Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activation functions, SEA-PINN attained competitive accuracy (83\% vs. 90\% improvement relative to FNN-PINN on the high-frequency case 7) as compared with TSA-PINN-a model specifically engineered for high-frequency problems via learnable frequencies in sinusoidal activations. Furthermore, integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN boosted performance by 42.49\%. These results underscore SEA-PINN as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation power, promotes more robust and efficient convergence, and strengthens the overall reliability of physics-informed learning.

</details>


### [236] [Global Convergence of Gradient Descent for Score Matching in Gaussian Mixtures via Reverse Fisher Divergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19876)
*Alexander Tyurin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reverse Fisher divergence yields better optimization landscape for score matching, enabling global convergence guarantees for Gaussian mixtures under GD, with two settings (single Gaussian teacher and GMM student; and both GMMs with separation).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forward Fisher divergence can cause undesirable, initialization-dependent convergence even in simple Gaussian mixture models; the reverse objective aims to improve optimization properties and reliability of score-matching-based training.

Method: Theoretical analysis of gradient descent under the reverse Fisher divergence for Gaussian mixtures. Settings analyzed: (i) teacher is a single Gaussian, student is a GMM with fixed weights and identity covariances; (ii) both teacher and student are GMMs with a global random initialization and a separation assumption on means. A Lyapunov-based framework is developed to study the GD dynamics.

Result: Case (i) global convergence of GD from arbitrary initializations. Case (ii) with high probability, each student component converges near its closest teacher component; conditions for convergence in total variation. The reverse Fisher divergence exhibits a more favorable optimization landscape than the forward version.

Conclusion: The reverse Fisher divergence improves optimization properties of score-matching objectives, enabling strong convergence guarantees for Gaussian mixtures under gradient descent and clarifying why the reverse objective can be preferable in practice.

Abstract: The score matching problem is a central training objective in modern generative modeling, diffusion models, fitting unnormalized statistical models, and inverse problems. A standard approach is to minimize the forward Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the teacher distribution. However, recent results show that even in simple Gaussian mixture model settings, this objective can lead to undesirable and initialization-dependent convergence behavior. In this paper, we study an alternative objective: the reverse Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the student distribution. We analyze gradient descent (GD) for fitting Gaussian mixture models and show that this change in the objective leads to significantly better optimization properties. First, when the teacher distribution is a single Gaussian and the student is a Gaussian mixture model with fixed weights and identity covariances, we prove the global convergence of GD from arbitrary initializations. Second, we extend the analysis to the case where the teacher is also a Gaussian mixture model and prove global convergence guarantees under a global random initialization scheme and a $\widetildeΩ(1)$-separation assumption on the target means. In particular, with high probability, each student component converges near its closest teacher component, and we provide conditions under which the student distribution converges in total variation distance. Our proofs rely on a new Lyapunov-based analysis of the gradient descent dynamics, showing that the reverse Fisher divergence has a much more favorable optimization landscape than the forward Fisher divergence.

</details>


### [237] [On the Oracle Complexity of Interpolation-Based Gradient Descent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19878)
*Dongmin Lee,William Lu,Anuran Makur*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces PPI-GD, an inexact gradient method that uses piecewise polynomial interpolation of gradient samples over data-domain patches to approximate full gradients, achieving improved oracle complexity in ERM under data-domain smoothness, with polylog data-dimensionality and strong convexity/non-convexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard gradient descent can be costly due to evaluating the full gradient. If the ERM loss is smooth with respect to the data, one can reconstruct gradients via interpolation to reduce oracle calls while preserving convergence.

Method: Query the first-order oracle at equidistant points in the data domain within patches; build polynomial interpolants of the gradient samples (using d-variate tensor-product polynomials, extending techniques akin to bicubic spline error analysis); employ these interpolants to approximate the full gradient in each iteration; analyze oracle complexity under strong convexity and non-convexity with data dimension polylogarithmic in the number of samples.

Result: Oracle complexity of PPI-GD is shown to outperform several GD variants in key regimes when the loss is sufficiently smooth; the analysis extends interpolation error techniques from bicubic splines to d-variate tensor-product polynomial interpolants, which may be of independent interest.

Conclusion: PPI-GD demonstrates that exploiting data-domain smoothness via piecewise polynomial interpolation can reduce gradient query complexity in ERM for both strongly convex and non-convex losses, and connects optimization analysis with interpolation theory.

Abstract: Recent work on first-order optimizers for empirical risk minimization (ERM) has suggested that smoothness of ERM loss functions in the training data, rather than in the optimization parameters, can be leveraged to improve the oracle complexity of gradient descent (GD) methods. In this paper, we propose an inexact gradient method, piecewise polynomial interpolation-based gradient descent (PPI-GD), which approximates the full gradient in each iteration by querying the first-order oracle at equidistant points in the data domain to construct polynomial interpolants of the resulting gradient samples over appropriately sized patches of the data domain. We analyze the oracle complexity of PPI-GD for strongly convex and non-convex loss functions when the data space dimension is bounded by a polylogarithmic function of the number of training samples, and find it to outperform several GD variants in key regimes when the loss function is sufficiently smooth. Furthermore, our analysis extends several techniques from the error analysis of bicubic spline interpolants to the setting of $d$-variate tensor product polynomial interpolants which may be of independent interest in interpolation analysis.

</details>


### [238] [Matching Markets meet Cumulative Prospect Theory: Towards Optimal and Adversarially Robust Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19883)
*Ananya Kunisetty,Avishek Ghosh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies multi-agent multi-armed bandit in competitive two-sided markets with CPT-based risk preferences, derives player-optimal regret with CPT distortions, improves it via active arm selection, and extends to adversarial markets with known/unknown corruption budgets, achieving logarithmic regrets; optimal when K≫N.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Incorporate human-like risk preferences (CPT) into learning in competitive matching markets; address sub-optimal Δ-dependence and robustness to reward corruption.

Method: Model: CPT weight distortions with α-Hölder continuous weight; analyze existing CPT-weighted rewards; propose active exploration to reduce dependence on K; extend to adversarial markets; derive regret bounds; consider known/unknown corruption budgets.

Result: Player-optimal regret O(K log T (1/Δ)^{2/α}); improved to remove K factor from dominant term when K≫N; in adversarial settings, logarithmic regret in both known and unknown budget cases.

Conclusion: CPT-aware bandit learning yields near-optimal/optimal regret in competitive two-sided markets; robust to adversarial corruptions; effective especially for large K relative to N; opens avenues for human-centric decision-making in matching markets.

Abstract: We study a multi-agent multi-armed bandit problem in the competitive setup with two-sided matching markets under a human centric decision making model. To capture human preferences, we use cumulative prospect theory (CPT) that weighs the actions of the agent in a nonlinear fashion using a ($α$-Hölder continuous) weight function. CPT has been widely used in behavioral economics and risk sensitive machine learning to emulate human preferences. We analyze the state-of-the-art learning algorithm with CPT weight distorted rewards and obtain a player optimal regret of $\mathcal{O}(K\log T \left(\frac{1}Δ\right)^{2/α})$, where $K$ denotes the number of arms, $T$ is the learning horizon, and $Δ$ represents (suitably defined) players' minimum preference gap. Noticing the dependence on $Δ$ to be sub-optimal, we further improve this regret by judiciously selecting the active set of arms during exploration, which removes the dependence on $K$ in the dominant term and achieves an improved (optimal) regret guarantees in the setting where the number of arms $K$ is significantly larger than the number of players $N$. In addition, we consider adversarial markets where the observed rewards of the agents may be corrupted. We propose and analyze algorithms for robust markets with CPT as risk sensitive measure in both settings where the total corruption budget is known and where it is unknown, and establish logarithmic player-optimal regret guarantees in both cases.

</details>


### [239] [SL-S4Wave: Self-Supervised Learning of Physiological Waveforms with Structured State Space Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19888)
*Feng Wu,Harsh Deep,Eric Lehman,Sanyam Kapoor,Guoshuai Zhao,Rahul Krishnan,Gari Clifford,Li-wei H Lehman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces SL-S4Wave, a self-supervised, S4-based multichannel encoder with multiscale global convolutions for long-sequence physiological data; achieves state-of-the-art results in ECG arrhythmia detection and EEG tasks with strong label efficiency and cross-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long, multichannel medical time series (ECG, EEG) are high-dimensional, noisy, and sparsely labeled. Standard SSL with CNNs struggles with long-range dependencies; Structured State Space Models (S4) model long sequences well but lack multichannel adaptation. There is a need for a framework that captures long-range dependencies and multichannel waveform characteristics in a self-supervised setting.

Method: A tailored S4-based encoder augmented with multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels to capture local and long-range dynamics across channels; employs a contrastive self-supervised objective; evaluated on arrhythmia-detection and EEG benchmarks; analyzes label efficiency, robustness on long sequences, and transfer to unseen arrhythmia types.

Result: SL-S4Wave consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and SSL baselines on the arrhythmia task; achieves high performance with significantly fewer labels; robust on long waveform segments; transfers to unseen arrhythmia types; demonstrates generalizability to EEG tasks.

Conclusion: Integrating structured state-space long-sequence modeling with a multichannel, noise-robust encoder and SSL objective yields strong performance and generalization on physiological waveforms, indicating broad applicability to long-sequence biomedical signals.

Abstract: Modeling long-sequence medical time series data, such as electrocardiograms (ECG), poses significant challenges due to high sampling rates, multichannel signal complexity, inherent noise, and limited labeled data. While recent self-supervised learning (SSL) methods, based on various encoder architectures such as convolutional neural networks, have been proposed to learn representations from unlabeled data, they often fall short in capturing long-range dependencies and noise-invariant features. Structured state space models (S4) excel at long-sequence modeling, but existing S4 architectures fail to capture the unique characteristics of multichannel physiological waveforms. In this work, we propose SL-S4Wave, a self-supervised learning framework that combines contrastive learning with a tailored encoder built on structured state space models. The encoder incorporates multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels, enabling the capture of both fine-grained local patterns and long-range temporal dependencies in noisy, high-resolution multichannel waveforms. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that SL-S4Wave (1) consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and self-supervised baselines in a challenging arrhythmia detection task, (2) achieves high performance with significantly fewer labeled examples, showcasing strong label efficiency, and (3) maintains robust performance on long waveform segments, highlighting its capacity to model complex temporal dynamics in long sequences that most existing approaches fail to efficiently model, and (4) transfers effectively to unseen arrhythmia types, underscoring its robust cross-domain generalization. We additionally evaluate SL-S4Wave on multiple EEG tasks, achieving superior performance over strong baselines, demonstrating generalizability of our approach beyond cardiac waveforms.

</details>


### [240] [Adversarial Bandit Optimization with Globally Bounded Perturbations to Convex Losses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19891)
*Zhuoyu Cheng,Kohei Hatano,Eiji Takimoto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends globally budgeted post-action perturbation from linear losses to general convex and β-smooth losses in adversarial bandit optimization, deriving explicit regret bounds that quantify the perturbation budget's effect; in the no-perturbation limit, recovers standard bandit convex optimization guarantees with β-smooth losses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how adversarial, post-action perturbations with a global budget impact regret in bandit convex optimization beyond linear loss models, and to generalize existing results to a broader class of loss functions.

Method: Modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develop an analysis that bounds the additional regret incurred due to adversarial perturbations, under a global budget on their cumulative magnitude. The loss is a sum of a convex β-smooth component and an adversarial perturbation chosen after the learner's action.

Result: Derives expected regret guarantees that explicitly capture the dependence on the perturbation budget, showing how perturbations add to regret; when perturbations are absent, the bounds reduce to those for standard bandit convex optimization with β-smooth losses.

Conclusion: The framework generalizes prior models to a broader loss class (convex β-smooth plus adversarial perturbations) and demonstrates that sublinear regret can be maintained with an explicit budget-dependent trade-off; the no-perturbation case recovers classic BCO results.

Abstract: We study adversarial bandit optimization in which the loss functions may be non-convex and non-smooth. In each round, the learner selects an action and observes only the loss incurred at that action. The loss consists of an underlying convex and $β$-smooth component and an adversarial perturbation that may be chosen after observing the learner's action. The perturbations are subject to a global budget controlling their cumulative magnitude over time.
  This framework extends the globally budgeted, post-action perturbation model from underlying linear losses to general convex and $β$-smooth losses. For this broader class, we establish expected regret guarantees that explicitly characterize the effect of the perturbation budget.
  To establish these guarantees, we modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develop an analysis that controls the additional regret caused by the perturbations. In the absence of perturbations, our results reduce to regret guarantees for the standard bandit convex optimization setting with $β$-smooth losses.

</details>


### [241] [Score Approximation for Diffusion Models on Arbitrary Low-Dimensional Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19894)
*Xinhe Mu,Zaijiu Shang,Zhaoqi Zhou,Chuan Zhou,Qi Meng,Guiying Yan,Zhiming Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Universal score-approximation theorem for diffusion models on arbitrary compact supports with upper Minkowski dimension d; a discrete-mixture approach enables ReLU networks to approximate the score with complexity exponential in d, enabling diffusion models to adapt to irregular, non-smooth data structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current theoretical bounds require restrictive Lipschitz conditions or smooth manifolds, which fail for real-world perceptual data featuring singularities, sharp boundaries, and disconnected clusters. A theory that accommodates arbitrary compact supports and fractal-like structures would align theory with empirical success of diffusion models.

Method: Introduce a discrete-mixture formulation of distributions and prove that the score function can be approximated by a ReLU network. The network complexity grows exponentially with the upper Minkowski dimension d of the support. Combine this with existing results on solving the backward diffusion SDE for arbitrary compact distributions.

Result: Establishes a universal score-approximation theorem: score functions on any compact set with Minkowski dimension d can be approximated by ReLU nets with complexity exp(O(d)). This breaks the exponential curse of ambient dimensionality and validates diffusion models on irregular, non-smooth data structures when paired with backward SDE theory.

Conclusion: The work provides a theoretical foundation showing diffusion models naturally adapt to irregular data, explains their empirical success, and broadens applicability to real-world, non-smooth datasets through a dimension-sensitive approximation framework.

Abstract: The remarkable success of score-based diffusion models has spurred significant efforts to establish their theoretical foundations. However, existing complexity bounds for score approximation rely heavily on restrictive assumptions like Lipschitz continuous densities or smooth manifold supports, which are routinely violated by the singularities, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters inherent to real-world perceptual data. This work establishes a universal score approximation theorem that works for any distribution supported on any compact set of upper Minkowski dimension $d$. Using a novel discrete-mixture formulation, we prove that the score function can be approximated with a ReLU network whose complexity grows exponentially only with $d$, thus breaking the exponential curse of ambient dimensionality. Combined with existing theories on accurately solving the backward diffusion SDE for arbitrary compact distributions, our work shows that diffusion models readily adapt to irregular, non-smooth data structures, explaining their competence in real-world generative tasks.

</details>


### [242] [ADaPT: Token-Level Decoupling for Efficient Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19919)
*Tingyun Li,Zishang Jiang,Jinyi Han,Xinyi Wang,Sihang Jiang,Han Xia,Zhaoqian Dai,Shuguang Ma,Fei Yu,Jiaqing Liang,Yanghua Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADaPT presents a token-level dual-process framework that decouples efficiency incentives from correctness optimization, enabling tunable speed-accuracy trade-offs via a mode-selection token.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models incur high inference costs, and existing efficiency approaches often degrade reasoning by coupling efficiency and correctness at the sequence level. A decoupled, token-level approach aims to preserve long, correct reasoning while enabling efficiency gains.

Method: Introduce Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT) with a mode-selection token that governs fast vs. slow reasoning. Apply efficiency-related rewards only to this token, thus decoupling signals during training. At inference, control the trade-off by adjusting the generation probability of the mode token, enabling continuous navigation along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier.

Result: Experiments show substantial inference-cost reductions while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: Token-level decoupling of efficiency and correctness in a dual-process framework enables tunable, efficient reasoning without sacrificing accuracy, offering a practical Pareto-front navigation for large reasoning models.

Abstract: Large reasoning models rely on long chain-of-thought to achieve strong performance, but applying such reasoning uniformly incurs high computational cost. Existing efficiency-oriented methods attempt to shorten or mix reasoning strategies, yet often degrade reasoning capability. We identify the root cause as sequence-level coupling between efficiency incentives and correctness optimization, which implicitly penalizes long but correct reasoning trajectories. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT), a token-level dual-process framework that explicitly decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training. ADaPT introduces a mode-selection token to control fast and slow reasoning, applying efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token to avoid penalizing correct long reasoning while encouraging efficiency when appropriate. Moreover, ADaPT enables precise and continuous control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time: by adjusting the generation probability of the mode-selection token, a single trained model can smoothly move along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.

</details>


### [243] [Compositionality Emerges in a Narrow Depth-Connectivity Regime: Architecture Constraints and Solution Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19941)
*Dat H. Do,Rushi Shah,Duc V. Le,Dianbo Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compositionality in neural networks arises only within a narrow depth-connectivity regime; only specific sparse connectivity patterns and target depths yield compositional structure, while other configurations converge to fractured solutions. The work introduces similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and a depth predictor to locate where compositionality appears, supported by a theory based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why gradient-based optimization yields compositional internal structure only under certain conditions, and why compositionality is rare or fragile in standard training.

Method: Empirical study scanning network depth and connectivity; development of similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity; a heuristic depth predictor to estimate regimes where compositionality is likely; a theoretical framework using compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds to explain the emergence. 

Result: Compositionality emerges only in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot; specific sparse connections matter more than overall sparsity. When the depth or connectivity conditions are violated, gradient descent converges to fractured solutions. SP successfully recovers compositional connectivity, and the depth predictor identifies regimes where compositionology is likely. The theory accounts for the narrow regime via sparsity and interference bounds.

Conclusion: Compositional solutions are achievable only within a restricted depth-connectivity regime; the study provides practical methods (SP and depth predictor) to discover and exploit compositionality and offers a theoretical explanation for its fragile emergence.

Abstract: Compositionality is believed to be the foundation for generalization, enabling models to reuse meaningful primitives in novel combinations. Yet, models trained with standard gradient-based optimization rarely, and often only weakly, exhibit compositional internal structure, and it remains unclear how or why such compositionality forms. In this work, we show that compositionality emerges in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot. Along the connectivity axis, compositionality only appears in some specifically sparse networks, heavily depends on which connections remain rather than on weights' sparsity alone. Along the depth axis, compositionality emerges within a narrow, target-dependent regime, peaking at specific depths, while both shallower and deeper networks fail. When either the depth or connectivity condition is violated, gradient descent silently converges to fractured solutions rather than compositional ones. To discover and exploit this emergence, we introduce (i) similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and (ii) a heuristic depth predictor to estimate where compositionality is most likely to appear. Finally, we support these empirical findings with a theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds, explaining why compositional solutions are reachable only in a narrow depth-connectivity regime.

</details>


### [244] [Towards Graph-Based Deep Learning for Map Generalization: Insights from Building Footprints Simplification and Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19956)
*Yanning Wang,Zhiyong Zhou,Zhouyu Liu,Mengni Yu,Yu Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph-based DL is explored for map generalization; GraphSAGE best for aggregation link prediction, but precise node movement remains hard, indicating aggregation is more complex than simplification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance automated map generalization by applying graph neural networks to two core tasks (simplification and aggregation) within a unified framework, leveraging spatial relationships in building footprints.

Method:  comparative evaluation of GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE on multi-scale building datasets. Simplification reframed as node movement prediction; aggregation reframed as link prediction. Assessment focuses on accuracy and the relative difficulty of tasks, with notes on data imbalance and post-processing requirements.

Result:  GraphSAGE provides relatively stronger performance for link prediction (aggregation). Node movement prediction is challenging across architectures. Aggregation appears more complex than simplification, suggesting current DL approaches struggle to capture higher-order spatial relationships. Limitations include data imbalance and need for post-processing to complete generalized outputs.

Conclusion:  The work offers methodological directions for integrating graph-based DL into map generalization, highlighting both promise and current limitations. It points to aggregation as the harder task and the need for further research to address spatial relationship modeling and practical post-processing.

Abstract: Map generalization remains one of the fundamental tasks in cartography, especially for the simplification and aggregation of complex building footprints. This study presents the first exploratory application of graph-based deep learning to both tasks, reformulating simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a unified graph learning framework. We evaluate representative graph neural network architectures (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets, showing that GraphSAGE demonstrates relative strengths in link prediction accuracy, while also revealing persistent challenges in precise node movement prediction. Beyond quantitative performance, the results highlight that aggregation poses greater complexity and challenges than simplification, underscoring the difficulty of capturing higher-level spatial relationships in map generalization with current deep learning approaches. Although limitations such as data imbalance and the need for post-processing remain, the study provides valuable insights and methodological directions for advancing automated map generalization with deep learning approaches.

</details>


### [245] [Low-Energy Reduced RISC-V Instruction Subset Processor for Tsetlin Machine Inference at the Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19964)
*Chanda Gupta,Sanidhya Bhatia,Shaurya Priyadarshi,Himani Panwar,Rishad Shafik,Sudip Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A domain-specific, reduced-ISA RISC-V microprocessor for Tsetlin Machine inference is proposed, achieving substantial energy savings and faster inference while maintaining or improving accuracy relative to BNN baselines across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While TM offers energy-efficient, logic-based inference suitable for edge AI, existing co-processor designs are tightly coupled to host systems and lack programmability. A flexible, programmable TM accelerator is desirable.

Method: Design of both a baseline RV32IM core and a reduced-ISA TM-oriented core using RISC-V modularity; instruction profiling to guide ISA simplification; datapath/control-path simplifications tailored to TM inference; evaluation on multiple datasets with BNN as a baseline for comparison.

Result: TM-based cores achieved up to 88.18% accuracy on CIFAR-2 (vs. 60.0% for BNN); execution time reduced up to 98%; energy consumption reduced by an average of 29.7x across tested datasets.

Conclusion: A domain-specific, reduced-ISA TM processor delivers programmable, energy-efficient edge inference with competitive or superior accuracy and substantial performance gains compared with BNN baselines, validating the approach for TM-oriented edge AI workloads.

Abstract: Tsetlin Machine (TM) is a logic-based machine learning approach that relies on simple bitwise operations and finite-state automata, which makes it attractive for edge AI deployments. Recent work has focused on co-processor and accelerator designs based on Tsetlin Machines (TMs). Although these designs achieve high performance, they typically depend on tightly coupled interfaces, microcode-style programming, and external host processors, limiting flexibility and ease of programming. In this work, we present a domain-specific RISC-V microprocessor architecture and design flow tailored for TM inference. Leveraging the modular structure of RISC-V, we design a reduced instruction subset processor that retains programmability while targeting improved performance and lower energy consumption for TM workloads. Instruction profiling is employed to guide instruction reduction, followed by datapath and control path simplifications tailored to TM inference. Both the baseline RV32IM core and the proposed reduced core are evaluated across multiple datasets and compared with Binarized Neural Networks (BNNs), which serve as a hardware-efficient baseline due to their reliance on bitwise operations during inference. Results show that TM achieves comparable or higher accuracy (e.g., up to 88.18% on CIFAR-2 compared to 60.0% for BNN) while reducing execution time by up to 98% across multiple datasets. Furthermore, the proposed design achieves an average $29.7\times$ reduction in energy consumption, demonstrating its effectiveness for programmable and efficient edge AI systems.

</details>


### [246] [The Token Is a Group Element: On Lie-Algebra Attention over Matrix Lie Groups](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20547)
*Przemyslaw Musialski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel attention mechanism where tokens are bare elements of a matrix Lie group; attention scores are a closed-form log-proximity measure, yielding invariant, equivariant attention without representation theory, demonstrated on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable exact, invariant attention on geometric data by using group elements as tokens and a canonical log-based distance, avoiding learned kernels and representation-theoretic machinery.

Method: Represent each token as a group element g_i in a matrix Lie group G. Define pairwise score s_ij = -||log(g_i^{-1} g_j)||^2/τ using the log map, with no feature payload or action ρ(g). This yields diagonal G-equivariance and cocycle property; applicable to non-compact groups like Aff(2).

Result: The closed-form score matches a learned MLP kernel on invariants and outperforms it on SE(2), using 50–80x fewer score parameters; a vector-token baseline breaks invariance by 5–12 orders of magnitude.

Conclusion: Lie-Algebra Attention offers a canonical, representation-free attention mechanism for matrix Lie groups, extending to full affine groups and preserving invariances without irreps or Clebsch–Gordan machinery.

Abstract: We place the attention token on the group: a token is an element $g_i$ of a matrix Lie group $G$ -- a bare transformation, with no feature payload and no external action $ρ(g)$ carrying it. To our knowledge this is the first attention construction whose tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements: their score is the closed-form algebra norm of the relative pose rather than a learned kernel, and it reaches the affine full-frame groups that every irrep- or surjective-exp-based method must exclude. We call it Lie-Algebra Attention. Once tokens are group elements, the rest follows with none of the usual representation-theoretic machinery. The relative geometry of a pair is canonical, $g_i^{-1} g_j$, so the pairwise invariant $w_{ij} = \log(g_i^{-1} g_j)$ is intrinsic rather than designed; equivariance under the diagonal $G$-action is tautological, and the cocycle condition holds automatically. The attention score is the negative squared algebra norm, $s_{ij} = -\|\log(g_i^{-1} g_j)\|_λ^2/τ$: the canonical proximity kernel under a block-weighted Frobenius inner product, with no irreducible representations, spherical harmonics, Clebsch-Gordan products, or learned kernel. The construction applies to any matrix Lie group on a chosen logarithm chart containing the relative poses, including the non-compact non-abelian affine groups with scale and shear that no vector-token attention method reaches: neither the irrep tradition nor surjective-exp methods. Three sequence-completion experiments, on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2), bear this out: the closed-form score matches a learned MLP kernel on the same invariant and outperforms it on SE(2), using 50 to 80x fewer score parameters, while a vector-token baseline breaks invariance by five to twelve orders of magnitude.

</details>


### [247] [Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19984)
*Juntian Huang,Jurgen Kurths,Ying Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KARC uses explicit basis-function expansions inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem to replace recurrent reservoirs, enabling closed-form training, and achieving competitive performance with traditional reservoir computing, including on PDE benchmarks, while enabling integration with diffusion models for text-to-image generation; it forms a principled bridge between reservoir computing and Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of conventional reservoir computing (limited long-range dependency capture, hyperparameter sensitivity, costly recurrence) by introducing a lightweight, expressive alternative with efficient training.

Method: Introduce Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing (KARC) by substituting trainable reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions guided by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem; demonstrate that KARC is a lightweight design of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) and permits efficient closed-form training; compare to existing reservoir computing on challenging benchmarks (e.g., PDEs) and show integration with generative diffusion models for text-to-image generation.

Result: KARC achieves comparable cost while outperforming existing reservoir computing methods on challenging benchmarks, including PDEs; it can be integrated with diffusion models for text-to-image generation.

Conclusion: The work establishes a principled bridge between reservoir computing and Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, enabling efficient and high-fidelity forecasting of dynamical systems through a lightweight, expressive architecture with closed-form trainable components.

Abstract: Reservoir computing offers a lightweight framework for forecasting dynamical systems but may struggle to capture long-range dependencies due to limited representational capacity. Conventional reservoir computing recurrently uses trainable reservoirs with hyperparameter sensitivity, while the next-generation reservoir computing removes recurrence at the cost of rapidly growing feature dimensions. Here, we develop Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing (KARC), which replaces reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem. We rigorously show that KARC is a lightweight design of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs), preserving the potential expressive capacity of KANs while admitting efficient closed-form training of reservoir computing. At comparable cost, KARC outperforms existing reservoir computing methods on challenging benchmarks including partial differential equations. It can also be integrated with generative diffusion models for text-to-image generation. This work thus establishes a principled bridge between reservoir computing and KANs, enabling efficient and high-fidelity dynamical system forecasting.

</details>


### [248] [Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR): Function-Preserving SVD Compression for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19993)
*Nico Harder,Daniel Becking,Karsten Mueller,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A layer-local SVD-based LLM compression framework (AIR) uses a backward-signal influence metric to guide low-rank approximations via a single ALS sweep, improving perplexity at reduced parameters and providing FLOP, memory, and latency gains, while being compatible with end-to-end methods (e.g., AIR+LoRA).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need efficient, accurate LLM compression that preserves performance while reducing parameters and compute. Existing methods can require substantial calibration data and may not exploit backward influence signals; AIR introduces a backward-signal influence metric to steer low-rank factorization.

Method: AIR performs a single closed-form alternating least squares (ALS) sweep starting from an activation-aware optimum of SVD-LLM(W). It integrates an influence metric element-wise under a monotone-descent guarantee, is layer-local, and orthogonally composable with end-to-end methods like LoRA.

Result: AIR improves perplexity over SVD-LLM(W) by more than 18% at <=60% parameter retention, matches quality with ~90% less calibration data, and yields gains in FLOPs, peak memory, and per-token latency. Alone, AIR exceeds ACIP; AIR+LoRA outperforms ACIP.

Conclusion: Air is an effective, complementary, layer-local SVD-based compression framework for LLMs that leverages backward influence signals to enable strong compression with minimal calibration data and compatibility with existing end-to-end approaches, delivering tangible efficiency gains.

Abstract: We present Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR), an SVD-based LLM compression framework that guides each weight matrix's low-rank approximation with a backward-signal influence metric. Starting from the activation-aware optimum of SVD-LLM(W), AIR runs a single closed-form alternating least squares (ALS) sweep that integrates influence element-wise under a monotone-descent guarantee. AIR is layer-local and composes orthogonally with end-to-end methods: alone it exceeds ACIP, and AIR+LoRA outperforms it further. AIR improves perplexity over SVD-LLM(W) by >18% at <=60% parameter retention, matches its quality with ~90% less calibration data, and turns parameter savings into FLOP, peak-memory, and per-token latency gains.

</details>


### [249] [Connect the Dots: Training LLMs for Long-Lifecycle Agents with Cross-Domain Generalization Via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20002)
*Yanxi Chen,Weijie Shi,Yuexiang Xie,Boyi Hu,Yaliang Li,Bolin Ding,Jingren Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A general RL-based framework to train LLMs to 'Connect the Dots' across long task sequences, enabling self-updating context for long-lifecycle agents; includes end-to-end RL architecture, dedicated tasks/environments, and proof-of-concept implementations; shows potential cross-domain generalization and releases code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable LLMs to operate as autonomous agents over extended lifecycles by learning to update their internal context from experiences and improve future performance.

Method: End-to-end RL with long rollout sequences that interleave solve-task and update-context episodes; design of tasks/environments to elicit the meta-capability; GRPO-style RL with fine-grained credit assignment; empirical evaluation on in-domain/out-of-domain settings; open-source release.

Result: Empirical results validate the efficacy of end-to-end RL training in the CoD setting and show potential for out-of-distribution generalization within training domains, across domains, and to Ralph-loop settings; proof-of-concept implementations illustrate practicality.

Conclusion: CoD provides a general, extensible framework for training LLMs to acquire and exploit a long-horizon meta-capability, bridging multiple lines of prior work and enabling new avenues for AI agents; code released for reproducibility and further research.

Abstract: This work presents a general framework for training large language models (LLMs) to "Connect the Dots" (CoD), a meta-capability required by long-lifecycle agents: as an LLM-based AI agent gets deployed in an environment, it solves a long sequence of tasks while continuously exploring the environment, learning from its own experiences, and iteratively self-updating its context about the environment, thereby achieving progressively better performance on future tasks conditioned on the updated context. Major components of the CoD framework include: (1) algorithm design and infrastructure for end-to-end reinforcement learning (RL) with long rollout sequences interleaving solve-task and update-context episodes; (2) tasks and environments for incentivizing and eliciting the targeted meta-capability in LLMs during training, as well as for faithfully measuring progress during evaluation. We present proof-of-concept implementations of the CoD framework, including a GRPO-style RL algorithm with fine-grained credit assignment, as well as tasks and environments tailored to the targeted meta-capability (rather than domain-specific LLM capabilities or standard task-by-task RL). Empirical results validate the efficacy of end-to-end RL training in the CoD setting, and demonstrate the potential for out-of-distribution generalization -- within the training domains, across different domains, and from CoD to Ralph-loop settings -- of the elicited meta-capability. Our investigation of CoD connects several lines of prior works, and opens up new opportunities for advancing LLMs and AI agents. To facilitate further research and applications, we release our implementations at \url{https://github.com/agentscope-ai/Trinity-RFT/tree/research/cod/examples/research_cod}.

</details>


### [250] [StreamKL: Fast and Memory-Efficient KL Divergence for Boosting Attention Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20005)
*Guangda Liu,Yiquan Wang,Chengwei Li,Wenhao Chen,Jing Lin,Yiwu Yao,Danning Ke,Wenchao Ding,Jieru Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StreamKL introduces a fused GPU primitive for computing KL divergence between attention distributions without quadratic materialization, dramatically reducing memory and IO, enabling long-context distillation with speedups up to 43x forward and 14x backward, and memory footprint from O(N_Q N_K) to O(1).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the quadratic memory/IO bottleneck in attention distillation (KL divergence between distributions) at long sequence lengths.

Method: Develop online coupled KL formulation, single-pass forward kernel streaming Q-K tiles through on-chip SRAM; backward pass recomputes attention probabilities tile-by-tile; specialized GPU kernels with optimizations.

Result: Up to 43x forward, 14x backward speedups; memory footprint of attention distillation reduced to O(1) on GPU memory; enables long-context distillation on a single GPU.

Conclusion: StreamKL provides a practical, memory-efficient, and fast primitive for attention KL divergence in distillation tasks, enabling long-context applications and potentially broader impact on related KD and sparse-attention training.

Abstract: Attention distillation, which trains one attention distribution to match another by minimizing their Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, is widely used in knowledge distillation, model compression, continual learning, and sparse-attention LLM training. However, existing approaches materialize both attention distributions before computing the KL reduction, incurring $O(N_QN_K)$ memory and IO costs that become prohibitive at long context lengths. We present StreamKL, the first fused GPU primitive for attention KL divergence that eliminates this quadratic materialization. StreamKL derives a novel online formulation for the coupled two-distribution KL reduction, enabling a single one-pass forward kernel that streams query-key tiles through on-chip SRAM. For the backward pass, StreamKL recomputes attention probabilities tile-by-tile, avoiding storage of quadratic intermediates. We further design and implement efficient GPU kernels with dedicated optimizations. Experiments show StreamKL delivers up to $43\times$ and $14\times$ speedups over baseline methods in the forward and backward passes, respectively. Most importantly, StreamKL reduces the extra HBM footprint of attention distillation from $O(N_QN_K)$ to $O(1)$, enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.

</details>


### [251] [VIMPO: Value-Implicit Policy Optimization for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20008)
*Zhewei Kang,Aosong Feng,Sergey Levine,Dawn Song,Xuandong Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VIMPO is a critic-free policy optimization that derives a policy-implied value function from KL-regularized RL optimality conditions for autoregressive generation, enabling a PPO-style actor update and improved credit assignment without training a critic; it outperforms GRPO on RLVR benchmarks, especially under noisy rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the trade-off between simple critic-free training and fine-grained credit assignment in verifiable reward reinforcement learning for large language models.

Method: Derive a value recurrence from KL-regularized RL optimality; express value via policy-reference log-ratios; anchor by the terminal condition that no future reward remains; implement a simple value loss without a critic; yield a critic-free actor advantage enabling a PPO-style update.

Result: On mathematical RLVR benchmarks (MATH-500, AIME 2024/2025, OlympiadBench), VIMPO improves over GRPO, with larger gains on competition-style evaluations; robustness under noisy rewards.

Conclusion: Policy-implied value optimization provides finer credit assignment while retaining the practical simplicity of critic-free training, yielding consistent gains in verifiable-reward RL for LLMs.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become a central tool for improving the reasoning ability of large language models, but current methods face a trade-off between simplicity and credit assignment. Group-relative methods such as GRPO avoid training a critic, but typically assign a trajectory-level advantage to every token. Actor-critic methods provide denser learning signals, but require a learned value function with its own training instability. We introduce VIMPO, a critic-free policy optimization method that derives a policy-implied value function from the optimality conditions of KL-regularized reinforcement learning. For autoregressive generation, the resulting value recurrence can be written in terms of policy-reference log-ratios and anchored by the terminal condition that no future reward remains at the end of a trajectory. This gives a simple value loss that incorporates outcome-level verifiable rewards without training a critic. The same derivation also yields a critic-free actor advantage, allowing VIMPO to separate reward incorporation through the value loss from policy improvement through a PPO-style actor update. On mathematical RLVR benchmarks, VIMPO improves over GRPO across MATH-500, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and OlympiadBench, with especially larger gains on competition-style evaluations. Under noisy rewards, VIMPO retains a consistent advantage over GRPO, suggesting that policy-implied value optimization can provide finer credit assignment while preserving the practical simplicity of critic-free training.

</details>


### [252] [Self-Adaptive Scale Handling for Forecasting Time Series with Scale Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20010)
*Xu Zhang,Zhengang Huang,Yunzhi Wu,Xun Lu,Erpeng Qi,Yunkai Chen,Zhongya Xue,Peng Wang,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a self-Adaptive Scale-handling (AS) module for time series forecasting with scale heterogeneity, combining Scale Calibrating and Scaling Selection to adapt per input; improves performance across real-world fund sales datasets while preserving semantic discriminability and reducing inverse-scaling errors; plug-and-play into existing TSF models; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world time series often exhibit scale heterogeneity across series, making joint modeling challenging. Global normalization can suppress low-scale signals, while window-based scaling can destroy semantic discriminability and amplify inverse-scaling errors. A per-input adaptive scaling mechanism is needed to preserve semantics and improve data utilization.

Method: AS comprises two components: Scale Calibrating (SC), which learns and calibrates prior mean scaling factors via neural networks; Scaling Selection (SS), which decides whether to apply the calibration or retain the original scaling to avoid over-calibration. The module is designed to be integrated into popular TSF architectures.

Result: Empirical evaluation on real-world fund sales datasets from Ant Fortune and Alipay shows that AS consistently improves model performance across different TSF architectures. The authors provide code and dataset at a public GitHub repository.

Conclusion: AS offers a simple, effective plug-and-play approach for handling scale heterogeneity in TSF, preserving semantic discriminability while reducing inverse-scaling errors, and demonstrating broad applicability to real-world industrial data.

Abstract: Current time series forecasting (TSF) research predominantly focuses on scale-homogeneous data, where different time series share similar numerical magnitude ranges. However, in real-world industrial scenarios such as financial product sales, different time series often differ by orders of magnitude (scale heterogeneity). Since these series share similar temporal patterns, joint modeling is desirable for better data utilization, yet existing scaling methods either compress low-scale signals (global normalization) or destroy semantic discriminability and amplify inverse-scaling errors (window-based scaling). This paper proposes a self-Adaptive Scale-handling (AS) module that learns adaptive scale factors tailored to each input, preserving semantic discriminability while reducing inverse-scaling errors. AS consists of Scale Calibrating (SC), which calibrates prior mean scaling factors through neural networks, and Scaling Selection (SS), which decides whether to apply calibration or retain the original factor, avoiding over-calibration. Experiments on real-world fund sales datasets from Ant Fortune and Alipay show that AS seamlessly integrates into popular TSF models and consistently improves their performance. The code and dataset are available at the link https://github.com/Meteor-Stars/ASTSF.

</details>


### [253] [Hierarchical Control in Multi-Agent Games: LLM-based Planning and RL Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20014)
*Jannik Hösch,Alessandro Sestini,Florian Fuchs,Amir Baghi,Joakim Bergdahl,Konrad Tollmar,Jean-Philippe Barrette-LaPierre,Linus Gisslén*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hierarchical LLM-augmented RL system orchestrates pretrained RL skills for multi-agent coordination, achieving competitive performance with hand-crafted rules and higher perceived believability than end-to-end training in 2v2 King of the Hill.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of scaling reinforcement learning to complex multi-agent tasks by leveraging a centralized, high-level planner (LLM) to coordinate pre-trained skills, reducing the reliance on end-to-end training and manual rule design.

Method: A pretrained large language model acts as a centralized strategic controller selecting among specialized RL skill policies for a team of agents; RL policies handle reactive low-level execution. Evaluation in a 2v2 King of the Hill environment against behavior tree (BT) and flat RL baselines, plus a user study.

Result: LLM+RL achieved task performance statistically equivalent to hand-crafted BT (46.4% vs 51.5% win rate, p=0.103); both BT and LLM+RL significantly outperformed Flat RL. User study (n=15) found 60% perceived LLM+RL as most human-like (p=0.027).

Conclusion: Pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills to achieve competitive multi-agent coordination and higher perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved strong performance in sequential decision-making, yet scaling to complex multi-agent environments remains challenging due to sparse rewards, large state-action spaces, and the difficulty of learning coordinated strategies.
