We propose a hierarchical architecture where a pretrained large language model (LLM) acts as a centralized strategic controller that selects among specialized RL skill policies for a team of agents, while RL policies handle reactive low-level execution. We evaluate this hybrid system in a competitive 2v2 King of the Hill environment against behavior tree (BT) and \emph{``Flat''} RL (end-to-end training without skill decomposition) baselines. The LLM+RL system achieves task performance statistically equivalent to hand-crafted BT (46.4\% vs 51.5\% win rate, $p=0.103$) while both significantly outperform Flat RL trained without skill decomposition. A user study ($n=15$) reveals that 60\% of participants perceive LLM+RL agents as the most human-like ($p=0.027$), citing behavioral adaptability and tactical variability. These results demonstrate that pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills, achieving competitive multi-agent coordination and superior perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

</details>


### [254] [Adaptive Distance-Aware Trunk Deep Operator Learning for Long-Span Roadway Bridges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20015)
*Bilal Ahmed,Diab W. Abueidda,Waleed El-Sekelly,Tarek Abdoun,Mostafa E. Mobasher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive-trunk DeepONet for localized response prediction in long-span bridges, combining a KNN-driven learning-domain with distance-aware trunk features, a stiffness-informed Schur complement reconstruction, and a reduced-order shell model for scalable training. Validated on a benchmark and Mussafah Bridge; achieves FEM-level accuracy (<5% error) with ~60x total time reduction and up to 10^4x faster inference (excluding reconstruction). Enables rapid full-field responses, influence lines, and influence surfaces under arbitrary vehicle loading for digital twins.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-span bridges exhibit highly localized responses under vehicular loads, causing expensive repeated FE analyses for tasks like influence surface generation and digital twin development. Existing SciML approaches often fail to capture localized effects accurately, limiting scalable, fast predictions.

Method: Develop an adaptive-trunk DeepONet that dynamically constructs a load-dependent learning domain via KNN to focus on influence zones; enrich the trunk with distance-aware features encoding load-node geometry; integrate a physics-based full-field reconstruction using a stiffness-informed Schur complement to extend adaptive-node predictions to the full structure; generate training data with a reduced-order equivalent shell model to preserve global behavior while reducing cost.

Result: FEM-level accuracy with relative errors <5% on benchmark and Mussafah Bridge. Total response evaluation time, including reconstruction, reduced by ~60x. Excluding reconstruction, AD-DeepONet inference is up to 10^4x faster than FEM. Enables rapid generation of full-field responses, influence lines, and influence surfaces under arbitrary vehicle loading.

Conclusion: The proposed AD-DeepONet framework effectively balances local accuracy and global consistency for large-scale bridge analysis and digital twin applications, offering substantial speedups and the ability to rapidly generate key response metrics under flexible loading scenarios.

Abstract: Long-span roadway bridges exhibit highly localized structural responses under vehicular loading, making repeated FE analysis computationally expensive for applications such as influence surface generation and structural digital twins. Existing SciML approaches struggle to accurately capture these localized responses. To address this challenge, this study proposes an adaptive-trunk DeepONet for localized structural response prediction in large-scale bridge systems. The framework dynamically constructs a load-dependent learning domain using a KNN strategy, allowing the network to focus on structural influence zones. The trunk network is further enhanced using distance-aware features that encode the geometric relationship between the load and structural nodes. A physics-based full-field reconstruction is incorporated through a stiffness-informed Schur complement formulation, enabling predictions at adaptive nodes to be extended to the entire structural domain. To enable scalable training, response data are generated using a reduced-order equivalent shell model that preserves the dominant global behavior while significantly reducing computational cost. The proposed framework is validated on both a benchmark bridge model and the real-world Mussafah Bridge. Results show that the method achieves FEM-level accuracy with relative errors below 5%, while reducing the total response evaluation time (including full-field reconstruction) by approximately 60x; excluding the post-processing reconstruction step, the AD-DeepONet inference is up to four orders of magnitude faster than FEM. In addition, the framework enables rapid generation of full-field responses, influence lines, and influence surfaces under arbitrary vehicular loading configurations, demonstrating strong potential for large-scale bridge analysis and digital twin applications.

</details>


### [255] [Exploring the potential of AlphaEarth and TESSERA embeddings for Fine-scale Local Climate Zone Mapping: A case study across five cities in Switzerland](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20034)
*Htet Yamin Ko Ko,Clement Atzberger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Embedding-based LCZ mapping with EO foundation model embeddings (TESSERA, AlphaEarth) and an attention U-Net improves upscaling to 10 m over traditional S1S2 in five Swiss cities; TESSERA performs best; cross-year transfer remains challenging; higher-quality reference data yields the largest accuracy gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-scale urban climatic analyses require high-resolution LCZ maps, but many cities rely on coarse ~100 m LCZ data. EO foundation-model embeddings offer a path to upscaling with reduced preprocessing and feature engineering.

Method: Compare precomputed embeddings from TESSERA, AlphaEarth, and Sentinel-1/2 composites for LCZ mapping in five Swiss cities using an attention-based U-Net to upscale to 10 m. Three experiments assess multi-city transferability, impact of higher-resolution reference data, and temporal robustness to phenology changes.

Result: IoU scores: 0.59–0.69 and 0.77–0.82 in the first two experiments. TESSERA consistently outperforms both S1S2 and AlphaEarth. Cross-year transferability remains an open challenge. Embeddings enable scalable, reproducible LCZ mapping and reduce preprocessing; higher-quality reference data is the strongest lever for accuracy gains.

Conclusion: EO foundation-model embeddings, combined with a location-aware attention U-Net, show strong potential to deliver fine-scale LCZ maps with improved regional transferability and scalability; data quality enhances gains most significantly.

Abstract: Understanding urban spatial morphology is critical for climate modeling, risk assessment, and sustainable urban design, and Local Climate Zone (LCZ) mapping provides the basic framework for this. However, many cities still use coarse ~100-m resolution LCZ records, which are unsuitable for fine-scale urban research. In this study, precomputed embeddings from TESSERA (Feng et al., 2025) and AlphaEarth (Brown et al., 2025) are compared to traditional Sentinel-1/2 (S1S2) composites in five Swiss cities to see if they can upscale coarse LCZ maps to 10-m resolution using an attention-based U-Net. Three experiments assess multi-city transferability, the impact of higher-resolution reference data, and temporal robustness to year-to-year phenology changes. We find that all datasets achieve strong performance with test data Intersection-over-Union (IoU) ranging from 0.59-0.69 and 0.77-0.82 in the first two experiments. TESSERA consistently outperforms both S1S2 and AlphaEarth across both settings As expected, we find that the transfer of embedding-based models from one year to another remains an open challenge. Overall, however, our results demonstrate the promising potential of embeddings derived from EO foundation models to reduce time consuming preprocessing, respectively, manual feature engineering tasks and to guide a universal deep learning-based LCZ mapping workflow. When combined with a simple location-aware attention U-Net architecture, the embeddings enhance regional transferability and scalability, supporting the development of comprehensive and reproducible fine-scale LCZ maps for global urban climate applications Improving reference data quality remains the strongest lever for further accuracy gains.

</details>


### [256] [Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis using a Multimodal Approach with 3D MRI and PET](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20037)
*Loukas Ilias,Anthi-Maria Vozinaki,Christos Ntanos,Dimitris Askounis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multimodal MRI-PET AD diagnosis framework using 3D CNN features with adaptive fusion (concat, GMU, gated self-attention) and a sparsely gated MoE classifier; demonstrates input-adaptive performance and interpretability via Grad-CAM; GMU and self-attention outperform simple concatenation, and MoE is essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address robustness to patient/site heterogeneity and computational inefficiency in multimodal AD diagnosis by moving beyond static fusion and single-computation models, employing input-adaptive routing (MoE) and attention-based fusion, with Grad-CAM for interpretability.

Method: Extract 3D CNN features from MRI and PET; implement three fusion strategies (concatenation, GMU, gated self-attention); train a sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) classifier with input-adaptive routing; use Grad-CAM to visualize disease-related regions; evaluate on three binary tasks: NC vs. MCI, MCI vs. AD, NC vs. AD.

Result: GMU achieved 80.46% (NC vs. MCI) and 95.47% (NC vs. AD); gated self-attention achieved 82.08% on MCI vs. AD; ablations show removing MoE degrades accuracy; results support the value of input-adaptive, multimodal modeling of MRI and PET for AD diagnosis.

Conclusion: Input-adaptive fusion and MoE routing improve multimodal AD diagnostic performance over static fusion, highlighting complementary MRI-PET information and maintaining interpretability via Grad-CAM.

Abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is an irreversible neurodegenerative disorder and a leading cause of death worldwide. Early diagnosis plays an important part especially at the Mild Cognitive Impairment stage, where timely intervention can help slow its progression before it advances to AD. Neuroimaging data, like Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) scans, can help detect brain changes early by providing structural and functional brain changes related to the disease. Yet, many multimodal models still fuse MRI and PET with static concatenation and apply identical computation to all subjects, which limits robustness to patient/site heterogeneity and can waste computation. To address these limitations, we present the first study of combining 3D convolutional feature extractors with three fusion strategies - concatenation, Gated Multimodal Unit (GMU), and gated self-attention - and a sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) classifier that performs input-adaptive routing, activating only the most informative experts per case. Finally, we utilize Grad-CAM to visualize disease-related regions, ensuring model interpretability. Experiments are performed across three binary classification tasks (NC vs. MCI, MCI vs. AD, and NC vs. AD). Results show that GMU achieves accuracies of 80.46 % (NC vs. MCI) and 95.47 % (NC vs. AD), while gated self-attention attains 82.08 % on MCI vs. AD. Ablations show that removing the MoE consistently degrades accuracy across all tasks. These findings underscore the value of input-adaptive, multimodal modeling for AD diagnosis by leveraging the complementary nature of MRI and PET.

</details>


### [257] [Comparative Study of Neural Surrogate Architectures for Autoregressive Prediction of Internal Battery States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20053)
*Gihyun Lee,Thorben Menne,Simon Olma,Jakob Hilgert,Sangyoung Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: U-Net with multi-scale spatial inductive bias outperforms other autoregressive surrogates for DFN; achieves ~3% final-step nRMSE across internal states after 300 steps and 5.38x faster than the DFN solver.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the computational bottleneck of the Doyle-Fuller-Newman model by learning a generalizable, real-time-capable surrogate that captures state-evolution dynamics across operating conditions for battery management systems and digital twins.

Method: Systematic comparison of four neural architectures (MLP, ResNet, U-Net, FNO) as autoregressive state-transition operators with current conditioning; unified training framework using multi-step unrolling; evaluation over a wide range of operating conditions; analysis of the impact of spatial inductive bias.

Result: U-Net achieved a mean final-step nRMSE of ~3% across all internal state variables after 300-step autoregressive rollouts; observed 5.38x speed-up relative to the numerical DFN solver.

Conclusion: Spatial inductive bias is a key determinant of surrogate performance for DFN state observability; U-Net provides the best balance of accuracy and efficiency among the studied architectures, advancing surrogates for real-time BMS and digital twins.

Abstract: The Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model resolves internal electrochemical states in lithium-ion batteries with high fidelity. However, the numerical solution of its governing equations is computationally prohibitive for real-time deployment, limiting scalability from individual cells to pack and fleet-scale applications. While machine learning surrogates can substantially reduce inference latency through GPU acceleration, most existing approaches learn solution approximations tied to specific operating conditions rather than learning generalizable state-evolution dynamics. This work presents a systematic comparison of four neural network architectures (MLP, ResNet, U-Net, FNO) formulated as autoregressive state-transition operators that predict full DFN internal states across a wide range of operating conditions. To ensure a controlled architectural comparison, all models are trained under a unified framework using multi-step unrolling and current-conditioning, isolating the impact of spatial inductive bias. Results demonstrate that the U-Net's multi-scale feature hierarchy achieves a mean final-step nRMSE of 3% averaged across all internal state variables after 300-step autoregressive rollouts, while providing a 5.38x speed-up over the numerical solver. These findings highlight spatial inductive bias as a critical determinant of surrogate performance, advancing the development of surrogates for internal state observability for next-generation battery management systems and digital twins.

</details>


### [258] [PaAno+: Multiscale Encoding and Cross-Variable Attention for Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20055)
*Youji Zhu,Hongbing Wang,Wenchao Liu,Xiaodong Liu,Xiangguang Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PaAno offers a lightweight, patch-based anomaly detector for time series, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with efficient computation by using multiscale convolutional backbones, cross-scale adaptive attention, cross-variable fusion attention, and a self-supervised pretext task with triplet loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the computational burden of Transformer- and large-model-based anomaly detectors while improving feature extraction and cross-variable dependency modeling for multivariate time series.

Method: A patch-oriented encoder with a multiscale convolutional feature extractor, cross-scale adaptive attention with residual connections, a cross-variable fusion attention module, a temporal patch-window sorting pretext task, and triplet loss for embedding discrimination.

Result: On the TSB-AD benchmark, PaAno achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy for both univariate and multivariate tasks, with significant gains in metrics such as VUS-PR, and maintains low computational footprint suitable for real-time deployment on resource-limited devices.

Conclusion: PaAno demonstrates that a carefully designed lightweight, patch-based representation can match or surpass Transformer/large-model baselines in time-series anomaly detection while offering practical efficiency for edge deployment.

Abstract: Time-series anomaly detection has significant practical value for industrial and medical monitoring, as well as other critical domains. Current Transformer- and large-model-based detection approaches incur excessive computational overhead, while existing lightweight alternatives are constrained by insufficient feature extraction and inadequate modeling of dependencies across multivariate variables. To mitigate the above drawbacks, this study develops a lightweight, efficient anomaly detection model, dubbed PaAno, within the patch-oriented representation learning paradigm. In the encoder module, a multiscale feature-extraction backbone is constructed using convolutional kernels with differentiated receptive fields to capture hierarchical temporal characteristics; subsequent cross-scale adaptive attention aggregation, combined with residual connection optimization, further stabilizes feature representation learning. A cross-variable fusion attention module is embedded to explicitly characterize inter-variable correlations, empowering the model to identify anomalous patterns amid intricate operational conditions. Moreover, a novel pretext task based on temporal patch-window sorting is customized to uncover intrinsic structural properties of time series, and triplet loss is leveraged to optimize the patch embedding space for enhanced feature discrimination. Extensive experiments on the TSB-AD benchmark demonstrate that the proposed PaAno achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy on both univariate and multivariate tasks, yielding significant performance gains across evaluation metrics, including VUS-PR, relative to the original PaAno. Leveraging a compact network design, the presented model achieves favorable computational efficiency, enabling deployment on resource-limited terminals for real-time anomaly inference.

</details>


### [259] [What Makes Effective Supervision in Latent Chain-of-Thought: An Information-Theoretic Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20075)
*Xinghao Chen,Chak Tou Leong,Wenjin Guo,Jian Wang,Wenjie Li,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent CoT faces gradient attenuation and representational drift; two supervision axes (Trajectory and Space) and a move from geometric imitation to mutual information maximization; Unified Latent Probe (ULP) measures MI between latent trajectories and steps; results show Information-Performance Binding and superiority of generative reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diagnose why latent chain-of-thought fails to robustly reason and provide a principled supervision framework for latent reasoning.

Method: Information-theoretic analysis of Latent CoT; decompose supervision into Trajectory (dense stepwise signals) and Space (semantic structure of latent space); compare rigid geometric compression with generative reconstruction; introduce Unified Latent Probe (ULP) to quantify mutual information between latent trajectories and reasoning steps; empirical experiments linking information fidelity to reasoning performance.

Result: Rigid geometric compression collapses the reasoning space while generative reconstruction preserves information capacity; ULP correlates with reasoning accuracy; evidence of Information-Performance Binding; MI-maximization supervision improves latent reasoning; code released.

Conclusion: Propose shifting from geometric imitation toward mutual information maximization for latent reasoning supervision; provide a principled framework and practical tools (ULP) and code to guide latent CoT design.

Abstract: Latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) internalizes reasoning within continuous hidden states, offering a promising alternative to verbose discrete reasoning traces. However, robust latent reasoning remains difficult because outcome supervision provides weak learning signals and leaves latent trajectories prone to semantic drift. In this work, we analyze Latent CoT from an information-theoretic perspective and identify this failure as a dual collapse: gradient attenuation along the optimization path and representational drift in the latent space. We further decompose process supervision into two complementary dimensions: Trajectory Supervision, which injects dense stepwise reasoning signals, and Space Supervision, which preserves the semantic structure of the latent manifold. Our analysis shows that rigid geometric compression can collapse the reasoning space, whereas generative reconstruction provides a more flexible semantic anchor that better preserves information capacity. To measure these effects, we introduce the Unified Latent Probe (ULP), which quantifies the mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps. Experiments reveal a clear Information-Performance Binding: reasoning accuracy depends on the information fidelity preserved in the latent chain. These findings provide a principled framework for latent reasoning supervision and suggest shifting from geometric imitation toward mutual information maximization. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/EIT-NLP/Supervision-in-Latent-CoT}{this repository}.

</details>


### [260] [Sensorimotor World Models: Perception for Action via Inverse Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20104)
*Petr Ivashkov,Randall Balestriero,Bernhard Schölkopf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We propose a sensorimotor world model (SMWM) trained end-to-end with inverse dynamics regularization, yielding stable, action-aligned latent representations suitable for offline planning in 2D/3D tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Perception-for-action theories argue representations should reflect action relevance, while JEPA-style latent world models risk collapse when optimized solely for predictability. There is a need for compact, controllable latent states learned from high-dimensional observations without heavy training tricks.

Method: Introduce SMWM, a latent world model trained end-to-end with inverse dynamics regularization. The regularizer forces latent states to preserve the action information behind transitions, biasing representations toward controllable environment aspects and away from distractors, without requiring frozen encoders, EMA, or other complex regularizers.

Result: SMWM yields stable latent world models from offline, reward-free trajectories, producing compact, interpretable latent spaces and competitive planning performance across simple 2D and 3D control tasks.

Conclusion: Inverse dynamics regularization provides a single, effective mechanism to prevent representation collapse and align latent states with controllable actions, enabling practical end-to-end sensorimotor world modeling for planning.

Abstract: Perception for action suggests that representations of the world should be shaped not by visual fidelity alone, but by their relevance for actions. At the same time, latent JEPA-style world models advocate learning compact predictive states from high-dimensional observations to facilitate the prediction of future states, but end-to-end training of these models is nontrivial because representations may collapse if our only goal is to construct a latent state that is easy to predict. We introduce a sensorimotor world model (SMWM): a latent world model trained end-to-end with inverse dynamics regularization. This single regularizer addresses both issues: it prevents representation collapse and induces action-aligned representations. By forcing latent states to preserve information about the action underlying a transition, it biases the model toward the controllable degrees of freedom of the environment while discarding uncontrollable distractors. This yields stable latent world models trained from offline, reward-free trajectories, without frozen encoders, exponential moving averages, or complex latent regularizers. Empirically, SMWM learns compact, interpretable latent spaces and enables competitive planning performance across simple 2D and 3D control tasks.

</details>


### [261] [Quantile of Means: A Bonus-Free Ensemble Method for Minimax Optimal Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20107)
*Asaf Cassel,Aviv Rosenberg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a count-free, quantile-based ensemble method for finite-horizon MDPs, achieving optimal variance-dependent regret bounds and grounding ensemble exploration in reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL exploration relies on brittle count-based uncertainty estimates that are hard to compute in practice and lack strong theoretical justification for ensemble methods. There is a need for principled, scalable exploration with theoretical guarantees.

Method: Extend an ensemble-based approach from Multi-Armed Bandits to finite-horizon MDPs by using a quantile-based, count-free uncertainty estimation within ensembles to guide exploration.

Result: The proposed method achieves optimal variance-dependent regret bounds, providing a theoretical justification for ensemble-based exploration in RL.

Conclusion: The work offers a solid theoretical foundation for ensemble-based exploration in RL and presents a practical, scalable alternative to traditional count-based uncertainty methods.

Abstract: Optimal Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms typically rely on carefully constructed count-based uncertainty estimates to drive exploration. Although theoretically sound, such estimates are hard to compute in practical settings and therefore offer limited insight for designing exploration heuristics. Meanwhile, ensembling has emerged as a practical approach, but remains without theoretical justification. Building on a recent ensemble-based method for Multi-Armed Bandits, we propose a quantile-based ensemble method for finite-horizon Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). Our simple count-free approach achieves optimal variance-dependent regret bounds, providing theoretical grounding for ensemble-based exploration in RL.

</details>


### [262] [When Calibration Fails the Vulnerable Hospital: Federated Conformal Risk Control via Risk-Curve Shrinkage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20115)
*Nafis Fuad Shahid*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A shrinkage-based federated conformal risk control (CRC) protocol improves worst-case site coverage without inflating prediction sets excessively; pooling CRC across sites underperforms for individual hospitals, while per-site CRC inflates sets by ~83x. The method sends only site risk curves (G scalars) to a central server, which computes shrinkage-regularized thresholds per site using a single hyperparameter n0 to trade coverage and efficiency; leave-one-site-out analysis selects n0 (e.g., 19) achieving 2.7/20 violations at 2.0x stretch. Finite-sample correction is essential; Lagrangian optimization is harmful; marginal CRC guarantee is preserved under site-mixture assumption; validated on FeTS-2022 data (1,251 subjects, 20 institutions) with four targets and three seeds, no patient data leaves sites.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable distribution-free, reliable segmentation-quality guarantees in federated, multi-institution settings with heterogeneous site performance, while preserving clinical utility and protecting privacy.

Method: Propose shrinkage-based federated CRC: each site sends only its empirical risk curve (G scalars) to a central server, which computes a shrinkage-regularized threshold per site. A single hyperparameter n0 trades worst-case coverage vs. prediction-set efficiency. Leave-one-site-out analysis guides n0 (e.g., n0=19). Compare with pooled CRC and per-site CRC; show finite-sample correction is essential; show marginal guarantee via site mixture; ensure privacy by not sharing raw data.

Result: Pooling across sites yields good average coverage but fails coverage at ~40% of sites; per-site CRC restores coverage but inflates prediction sets ~83x; shrinkage-based CRC achieves improved worst-case coverage with manageable set sizes; with n0=19, violations 2.7/20 at 2.0x stretch; direct Lagrangian optimization concentrates risk on vulnerable hospitals; finite-sample correction term is essential (removing triples violations). Validation across four targets and three seeds; no patient-level data leaves sites.

Conclusion: The shrinkage-based federated CRC protocol provides distribution-free, site-aware coverage guarantees with a tunable trade-off between worst-case coverage and set efficiency, preserving a marginal CRC guarantee under site mixtures and demonstrating practical viability on real multi-institution brain-tumor data.

Abstract: Conformal risk control (CRC) provides distribution-free guarantees on segmentation quality by calibrating a prediction-set threshold on held-out data. In federated deployments, the standard approach pools calibration scores across sites into a single threshold. We provide the first quantification, on real multi-institutional brain tumor data (FeTS-2022, 1,251 subjects, 20 institutions), showing that this naive pooled CRC protects the average hospital but violates coverage at 40% of individual institutions, with the worst site exceeding the target false-negative rate by 7.8 percentage points. The naive alternative, per-site local CRC, largely restores coverage but inflates prediction sets by 83x, rendering them clinically useless. We propose a shrinkage-based federated CRC protocol: each site transmits only its empirical risk curve (G scalars) to a server, which computes a shrinkage-regularized threshold per site. A single hyperparameter n0 smoothly trades worst-case coverage for prediction-set efficiency; leave-one-site-out sensitivity analysis identifies n0=19, achieving 2.7/20 violations at 2.0x stretch. We further show that direct Lagrangian optimization of coverage budgets fails, concentrating risk on vulnerable hospitals, and that the finite-sample correction term is essential: removing it triples violations. The marginal CRC guarantee is preserved by construction under the stated site-mixture assumption; per-site coverage is validated across four targets with three seeds. No patient-level images, masks, or per-volume scores leave any site.

</details>


### [263] [Multi-Modal Contrastive Learning for Implicit Earth Embeddings via Location Tying](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20167)
*Jonathan Hecht,Lukas Arzoumanidis,Ziyue Li,Youness Dehbi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two multimodal contrastive learning architectures (MELT and SALT) extend self-supervised pre-training for geospatial tasks beyond two modalities by leveraging unpaired data. They perform on par with the best two-modality baseline (SATCLIP) across four tasks, but adding more modalities often yields diminishing returns; the location encoder remains the bottleneck. MELT offers more stable training and a stronger foundation for scaling than SALT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scarcity of high-quality ground-truth data for spatial prediction. While contrastive learning with a single additional modality is common, expanding to multiple modalities using unpaired geospatial data could enhance representations if the objective and encoder capacity align with diverse signals.

Method: Introduce two multimodal contrastive architectures: MELT (Multimodal Embedding via Location Tying) and SALT (Sequential Alternating Location Training). Both extend to multiple modalities by incorporating unpaired geospatial data and aim to align representations across modalities through a contrastive objective. They compare against a strong two-modality baseline (SATCLIP) across four downstream tasks.

Result: Both MELT and SALT are technically viable and achieve performance on par with SATCLIP (the strongest two-modality baseline) across four downstream tasks. Increasing the number of modalities does not reliably improve performance; peak benefit is reached early in the contrastive objective, regardless of modality diversity or pre-training volume.

Conclusion: The bottleneck lies in the choice of location encoder rather than modality richness or training scale. MELT offers more stable training than SALT and provides a solid foundation for future scaling of multimodal geospatial pre-training.

Abstract: Spatial prediction tasks are often limited by a lack of high-quality labelled ground-truth observations. To overcome this challenge, self-supervised pre-training is a possible solution, with contrastive learning dominant for location encoders. Those approaches usually align geographic coordinates with just one additional modality. We propose two multimodal contrastive learning architectures: Multimodal Embedding via Location Tying (MELT) and Sequential Alternating Location Training (SALT). These architectures expand this framework beyond two modalities by utilising unpaired geospatial data. Both methods are technically viable and match the performance of the strongest two-modality baseline (SATCLIP) across four downstream tasks. However, increasing the number of modalities does not consistently improve performance, suggesting that the chosen location encoder is the main limitation - the contrastive objective reaches its peak early, regardless of modality diversity or pre-training volume. MELT provides more stable training than SALT and presents a stronger foundation for future scaling.

</details>


### [264] [Predicting gestational age at birth in the context of preterm birth from multi-modal fetal MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20172)
*Diego Fajardo-Rojas,Megan Hall,Daniel Cromb,Mary A. Rutherford,Lisa Story,Emma C. Robinson,Jana Hutter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-modal fetal MRI ML pipeline estimates gestational age at birth by combining imputation, feature selection, and regression; evaluated on 333 controls and 93 preterm cases with cross-validation; modest predictive performance but demonstrates feasibility and potential for a classification step.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preterm birth is a major contributor to neonatal mortality and morbidity. Predicting gestational age at birth from MRI features could aid risk stratification and clinical management. The study aims to move beyond classification to regression/prediction using a pipeline that handles missing data and selects informative features.

Method: A pipeline including data imputation, feature selection, and regression models applied to comprehensive multi-modal morphological and functional fetal MRI data. Dataset comprises 333 control and 93 preterm cases. They perform stratified 10-fold cross-validation, report R2, MAE, and classification metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) after categorizing GA at birth into term vs preterm. An ablation study validates the pipeline design. Key features identified include cervical length and placental T2* statistics. Code is provided publicly.

Result: R2 = 0.13; mean absolute error (MAE) = 2.74 weeks. Classification metrics across folds: accuracy = 0.77, sensitivity = 0.59, specificity = 0.82. Ablation supports design choices. Findings suggest feasibility of predicting GA at birth using multi-modal fetal MRI plus ML, though performance is modest. The work positions GA-at-birth prediction as a proof of concept; larger cohorts planned for finer preterm stratification; code available at the cited GitHub repository.

Conclusion: This work demonstrates the feasibility of predicting gestational age at birth from multi‑modal fetal MRI using an ML pipeline, transitioning from prior classification-only approaches. While initial performance is modest, it provides a proof of concept and a foundation for scaling up with larger cohorts and refined feature sets to enable clinically actionable GA-at-birth predictions.

Abstract: Preterm birth is associated with significant mortality and a risk for lifelong morbidity. The complex multifactorial aetiology hampers accurate prediction and thus optimal care. A pipeline consisting of bespoke machine learning methods for data imputation, feature selection, and regression models to predict gestational age (GA) at birth was developed and evaluated from comprehensive multi-modal morphological and functional fetal MRI data from 333 control cases and 93 preterm birth cases. The GA at birth predictions were classified into term and preterm categories and their accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity were reported. An ablation study was performed to further validate the design of the pipeline. Performance was evaluated using stratified 10-fold cross-validation. The pipeline achieves an R2 score of 0.13 and a mean absolute error of 2.74 weeks. It also achieves a 0.77 accuracy, 0.59 sensitivity, and 0.82 specificity across folds. The predominant features selected by the pipeline include cervical length and statistics derived from placental T2* values. The confluence of fast, motion-robust and multi-modal fetal MRI techniques and machine learning prediction allowed the prediction of the gestation at birth. This information is essential for any pregnancy. To the best of our knowledge, preterm birth had only been addressed as a classification problem in the literature. Therefore, this work provides a proof of concept. Future work will increase the cohort size to allow for finer stratification within the preterm birth cohort. Our code is available at https://github.com/dfajardorojas/ml-for-preterm-birth-.

</details>


### [265] [Computational Methods and Challenges in Cell-Free DNA Analysis for Multi-Cancer Early Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20174)
*Nicko Starkey,Marcin W. Wojewodzic,Krzysztof Rzecki*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A systematic review (2022-2025) of cfDNA-based MCED computational methods, focusing on fragmentomics and epigenetic signals, comparing classical ML, deep learning (autoencoders), and multimodal ensembles; finds ensembles most ready but calls for standardization; outlines challenges and open problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthesize progress in cfDNA-based multicancer early detection (MCED) methods to guide research and clinical translation, highlighting signal types, modeling approaches, and readiness.

Method: Literature survey of 2022–2025 works; categorize methods by signal type (fragmentomics, epigenetics), modeling approach (statistical, machine learning, deep learning including autoencoders), assess interpretability, validation strategies, and clinical readiness; identify challenges (technical, computational, methodological) and open problems; evaluate the promise of multimodal ensembles.

Result: Findings indicate that multimodal ensemble methods offer the strongest potential for clinical integration and highest readiness. However, standardization of evaluation protocols and reporting is crucial for fair cross-study comparisons and progress tracking. The field notes benefits from leveraging fragmentomics (e.g., fragment length, end motifs) and epigenetic features (e.g., methylation) in a combinatorial manner, with DL approaches (e.g., autoencoders) enabling representation learning, and classical ML providing interpretable baselines. Validation strategies are heterogeneous, and clinical readiness varies across approaches.

Conclusion: The review concludes that multimodal ensembles are the most promising path to clinical translation, but achieving robust, reproducible performance requires standardized evaluation pipelines, transparent reporting, and rigorous cross-cohort validation, followed by careful assessment of interpretability and integration into clinical workflows.

Abstract: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) is a promising avenue for non-invasive multicancer early detection (MCED), in that, it can enable multiple cancer detection simultaneously from a single blood draw, with particular sensitivity to cancers that currently lack established screening programs. Here we review the computational methods developed between 2022 and 2025 for cfDNA-based MCED. We focus on how fragmentomics and epigenetic features are extracted and analyzed to detect cancer at early stages. We first briefly outline the biological basis of cfDNA signals, then review classical statistical and machine learning approaches alongside deep learning frameworks including autoencoder-based models. For each method we discuss biological interpretability, validation strategy, and readiness for clinical integration. Furthermore, we categorize the current challenges into technical, computational, and methodological while outlining open problems in the field. This review shows that multimodal ensemble approaches have the strongest promise for clinical integration and the highest readiness. However, for better assessment of future work and side-by-side comparison, standardization of evaluation protocols and reporting results will be crucial.

</details>


### [266] [Effective Dimension Governs Generalization in Quantum Kernel Vision Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20183)
*Jian Xu,Delu Zeng,John Paisley,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A single, measurable quantity—the effective dimension d_eff of the quantum feature kernel—unifies two empirical phenomena in quantum vision models: (i) more entanglement and more uniform entanglement improve generalization, and (ii) injecting quantum noise can improve test accuracy. The paper provides a spectral analysis of quantum-kernel vision models, showing how entanglement structure and noise move d_eff, with overfitting linked to ridge-like regularization via d_eff contraction. It offers an exact kernel decomposition under depolarization, analyzes amplitude damping, and demonstrates kernel capacity bounds and risk decomposition. Empirically, d_eff contraction correlates with improved accuracy; amplitude damping lifts accuracy up to ~13% in a sweet spot, with noise acting as spectral filtering. The work reframes reported anecdotes as manifestations of d_eff, guiding design of quantum-vision models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain two puzzling empirical observations in quantum-vision models—(a) larger or more uniformly distributed entanglement aiding generalization, and (b) noise that can improve test accuracy—through a unified, quantitative principle rather than ad-hoc explanations.

Method: Study quantum-kernel vision models (quantum feature maps + kernel classifiers) using a spectral perspective. Derive an exact decomposition of the depolarized kernel K_p, analyze amplitude damping, establish a kernel-machine capacity bound, and perform a capacity/alignment risk decomposition. Validate the monotone contraction of d_eff empirically in experiments up to 12 qubits; use the depolarizing family to confirm kernel decomposition and explore behavior across entanglement/noise settings.

Result: d_eff serves as a unifying driver: depolarization drives d_eff toward 1; amplitude damping contracts d_eff and can raise test accuracy (up to ~13%) at an inverted-U sweet spot; noise injection aligns with spectral-filtering effects predicated by d_eff; the monotone contraction is observed empirically in entangled setups; a one-parameter depolarizing family provides exact, constructible collapse of d_eff and supports the kernel decomposition to machine precision.

Conclusion: Two previously reported anecdotes about quantum vision models can be understood as consequences of a single spectral quantity, d_eff. This provides a design principle: tune entanglement and noise to control d_eff, using contraction in overfitting regimes as a regularizer and spectral filtering to improve generalization.

Abstract: Recent quantum vision models-quantum vision transformers and quantum convolutional networks-report two striking but unexplained empirical phenomena: (i) ansatze with more, or more uniformly distributed, entanglement generalize better, and (ii) injecting quantum noise can improve test accuracy rather than degrade it. These observations are currently treated as curiosities, discovered by grid search and explained, if at all, by hand. We show that both are manifestations of a single, measurable quantity: the \emph{effective dimension} $d_{\rm eff}$ of the (noise-shaped) quantum feature kernel. Working primarily with quantum-kernel vision models-a quantum feature map read out by a kernel classifier-we give a spectral account in which entanglement structure and quantum noise are two knobs that move $d_{\rm eff}$; in an overfitting regime, contracting $d_{\rm eff}$ acts as ridge-like regularization. We analyze the mechanism: an \emph{exact} decomposition of the depolarized kernel $K_p=(1-p)^2K+\tfrac{p(2-p)}{D}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$ with $d_{\rm eff}(K_p)\to1$, a contraction result (and its boundary) for amplitude damping, a kernel-machine capacity bound, and a capacity/alignment risk decomposition; the monotone contraction operative in our entangled experiments is verified empirically, not proven in general. Along the one-parameter depolarizing family the collapse is instead exact by construction; we use it only to confirm the kernel decomposition to machine precision and at up to $12$ qubits, not as evidence for $d_{\rm eff}$. Amplitude damping contracts $d_{\rm eff}$ and lifts test accuracy by up to $+13\%$ along an inverted-U sweet spot; the effect's sign flips between the over- and under-fitting regimes; noise injection matches an explicit spectral-filtering frontier. Our results organize two reported anecdotes into a single measurable principle for designing quantum-vision models.

</details>


### [267] [Learner-based Concept Drift Detection: Analysis and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20216)
*Md Moman Ul Haque Khan,Samira Sadaoui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical and empirical survey of concept drift characteristics and drift detectors across synthetic and real data, assessing performance under abrupt and gradual drifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary data streams due to concept drift degrade predictive performance; timely drift detection is essential for robust decision-making.

Method: Theoretical analysis of concept drift characteristics; categorization of drift detectors; empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world data streams exhibiting diverse drift types, including abrupt and gradual changes.

Result: Provides a framework for understanding drift behavior and detector performance; benchmarks multiple detectors across contexts; highlights strengths, weaknesses, and applicability of detectors in different streaming scenarios.

Conclusion: Enhances understanding of concept drift characteristics and drift detector behavior; offers guidance on detector selection and design for diverse streaming contexts; notes gaps and avenues for future research.

Abstract: Machine learning algorithms deployed for evolving streaming environments must handle the non-stationary data distributions, commonly referred to as concept drift. The presence of concept drift poses a major challenge for many real-world applications because it can severely degrade their predictive performance, hindering their ability to support robust decision-making. Consequently, the timely and efficient detection of drift events is critical for sustaining high accuracy over time. This study examines theoretically the concept drift characteristics and numerous drift detection algorithms across several categories. Furthermore, we evaluate their performance on both synthetic and real-world datasets exhibiting diverse streaming scenarios and drift characteristics, such as abrupt and gradual changes. This study aims to enhance understanding of the complex notion of concept drift characteristics and behavior of drift detectors, along with their applicability to diverse contexts.

</details>


### [268] [Boundary Embedding Shaping with Adaptive Contrastive Learning for Graph Structural Disentanglement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20283)
*Jiaqing Chen,Zidu Yin,Yichao Cai,Yuhang Liu,Zhen Zhang,Dong Gong,Javen Qinfeng Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning plug-in for GNNs that selectively suppresses boundary-region entanglement to reduce spurious structural noise and improve classification and link prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph structural entanglement causes semantically irrelevant neighbors to contaminate node embeddings, especially for nodes near class boundaries, blurring decision boundaries and destabilizing predictions. Existing robust GNNs treat all nodes uniformly, neglecting boundary vulnerabilities.

Method: BES is an adaptive contrastive learning module that plugs into GNNs and targets boundary-region entanglement by selectively suppressing spurious structural noise at decision boundaries with minimal parameter perturbation.

Result: Empirically, BES consistently improves boundary discrimination and outperforms existing methods. It increases GCN node classification accuracy on average by 3.3% (up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and achieves superior accuracy in link prediction.

Conclusion: BES provides a lightweight, boundary-aware enhancement for GNNs, yielding consistent gains in both node classification and link prediction by mitigating boundary-region structural noise with minimal model changes.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) excel at aggregating neighbor information for classification, yet their performance is hindered by graph structural entanglement, where spurious correlations from semantically irrelevant neighbors contaminate node embeddings. This challenge is most acute for nodes near class boundaries in the embedding space, where amplified structural noise blurs decision boundaries and destabilizes predictions. Existing robust GNN methods largely treat all nodes uniformly, ignoring boundary vulnerabilities. In this paper, to improve classification performance, we tackle graph structural disentanglement by identifying boundary-region entanglement as the primary bottleneck and propose Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning GNN plug-in module that selectively suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries with minimal model parameter perturbation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BES consistently improves boundary discrimination and outperforms existing leading methods. Notably, BES boosts GCN performance by an average of 3.3% in node classification (up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and achieves superior accuracy in link prediction.

</details>


### [269] [Integrating national forest inventory, airborne lidar, and satellite imagery for wall-to-wall mapping of forest structure with computer vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20291)
*Luke J. Zachmann,David D. Diaz,Vincent A. Landau,Chelsey Walden-Schreiner,Tony Chang,Nathan E. Rutenbeck,Katharyn A. Duffy,Kiarie Ndegwa,Andreas Gros,Scott Conway,Guy Bayes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A nationwide 10 m satellite-based forest structure model trained on LiDAR samples estimates canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter, enabling annual wall-to-wall forest attributes that extend dynamic range and reduce regression-to-mean biases for planning and wildfire management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for wall-to-wall, annually updated forest attribute maps across large landscapes to support management and wildfire risk planning. Disparate data sources with varying vintages and quality cause confounding in operational planning; existing passive-sensor models suffer from saturation and bias.

Method: Develop a satellite-based forest structure model trained on LiDAR-derived samples and apply it across the contiguous United States to concurrently estimate five attributes at 10 m resolution (Ccover, H, biomass, BA, QMD). The model aims to maintain predictive capability across sparse to dense canopies and to mitigate saturation and regression-to-mean effects.

Result: The model extends the usable dynamic range beyond typical passive-sensor saturation, reduces regression-to-mean biases, and provides coherent wall-to-wall, annual 10 m attribute maps for forest and wildfire planning across CONUS.

Conclusion: VibrantForests offers a coherent, annually updated, high-resolution multi-attribute forest information foundation that addresses a key limitation in large-area planning, enabling improved forest management and wildfire risk assessment.

Abstract: Remote sensing is increasingly relied upon to deliver actionable science for forest and wildfire risk management across large landscapes. Wall-to-wall, annually updated maps are a persistent need for effective forest management. Many planning systems and data collections combine disparate data sources with different purposes, vintages, and prediction quality, which leads to confounding behavior in operational planning systems. We introduce the VibrantForests framework, developed and applied to map forest attributes and provide a coherent foundation for effective forest and wildfire planning. VibrantForests includes a satellite-based forest structure model trained on lidar-derived samples and applied across the contiguous United States to concurrently generate estimates of canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground live tree biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter at 10-meter resolution. We demonstrate predictive capability spanning the full spectrum of forest conditions ranging from sparse-canopy/low-biomass to dense-canopy/high-biomass. Results show that our model extends the range at which saturation is commonly encountered in comparable passive-sensor models, and reduces regression-to-mean behavior that commonly produces overestimation of forest attributes in small/sparse conditions and underestimation in large/dense conditions. The VibrantForests framework addresses a key limitation in large-area forest and wildfire planning by delivering coherent wall-to-wall estimates of management-relevant attributes at annual cadence and 10m resolution.

</details>


### [270] [Shifting-based Optimizable Linear Relaxations for General Activation Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20292)
*Philipp Kern,László Antal,Erika Ábráham,Carsten Sinz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A generic, shifting-based framework (SLiR) for linear relaxations of neural activations, parameterized by slope and offset via a shifting procedure, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or critical points, yielding sound upper/lower bounds and tighter relaxations across many activations, improving verification coverage by up to 7.8x over state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Verification of neural networks typically relies on hand-crafted, activation-specific relaxations, which are labor-intensive and impede scalability to new or complex activations. A unified, automated approach would reduce manual effort and expand verifiable properties.

Method: SLiR parameterizes linear relaxations of activation functions by their slope and computes the offset through a shifting procedure to maintain sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain. The method relies on a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points to determine feasible relaxations, enabling broad applicability to various activation functions and efficient optimization.

Result: Experimental results show SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8 times more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: SLiR provides a broadly applicable, sound, and efficient relaxation framework that reduces manual effort in activation-specific relaxations and increases verification coverage, with strong empirical performance across diverse activation functions.

Abstract: The use of neural networks (NNs) is rapidly increasing, including in safety- and security-critical domains. To provide formal guarantees about NN behavior, many verification methods rely on optimizable linear relaxations of activation functions. However, existing techniques depend on hand-crafted relaxations for each activation function. Extension to state-of-the-art activation functions therefore requires substantial manual effort. In contrast, our approach SLiR (Shifting-based Linear Relaxations) is broadly applicable, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points. SLiR parameterizes relaxations by their slope and computes the corresponding offset via a shifting procedure that ensures sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain, enabling efficient optimization while maintaining correctness. Our experiments show that SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [271] [Recurrent neural networks approximate continuous functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20325)
*Valentin Abadie,Clemens Hutter,Helmut Bölcskei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fixed-architecture RNNs can uniformly approximate any continuous function on [-1,1] using time as a resource; introduces TMNU to implement polynomial approximation with fixed weights and hidden dimension; provides both upper convergence rates and minimax lower bounds on runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To remove the conventional network-size scaling in universal approximation and instead rely on extended runtime of a fixed network, thereby exploring the trade-off between architectural complexity and computation time.

Method: Introduce Turing machine with neural units (TMNU) as an intermediary model; show that its algorithmic power can be simulated by a ReLU RNN with fixed weights and a fixed hidden dimension; construct explicit bounds on weights and hidden states; derive convergence rates tied to polynomial approximation schemes.

Result: Any continuous f on [-1,1] can be uniformly approximated by the time evolution of a single fixed-weight ReLU RNN; convergence rates match underlying polynomial approximation rates; provide minimax lower bounds proving runtime is a necessary resource in this fixed-network setting.

Conclusion: Establishes a fixed-network, time-asymptotic approximation framework, linking algorithmic computation to RNN dynamics; TMNU serves as a bridge between classical approximation and neural computation, and runtime becomes a fundamental resource to achieve accuracy.

Abstract: Classical approximation theorems ask for a new neural network whenever the target accuracy is improved. This paper studies the opposite possibility: can the network be chosen once and for all, and can accuracy be bought only by letting it run longer? We prove that this is possible for every continuous function on [-1,1]. More precisely, each such function is uniformly approximated by the time evolution of a single ReLU recurrent neural network with fixed weights and fixed hidden dimension. The mechanism behind the construction is a new intermediate model, the Turing machine with neural units (TMNU). This model retains the algorithmic freedom needed to implement polynomial approximation schemes, while remaining rigid enough to be simulated by RNNs with explicit bounds on hidden dimension and weight magnitude. The resulting convergence rates reflect the underlying polynomial approximation rates. We complement the construction with minimax lower bounds showing that runtime is not merely a proof artifact, but an unavoidable resource in this fixed-network approximation paradigm.

</details>


### [272] [Quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold networks for PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20326)
*Xiang Rao,Yuxuan Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QCPIKAN is a quantum-classical physics-informed network that solves PDEs by integrating Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers with parameterized quantum circuits, achieving exponential convergence of high-frequency errors and reduced numerical dispersion, validated on porous-media seepage PDEs with superior performance to prior quantum-classical PINNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve PDE solvers by leveraging hybrid quantum-classical models to address high-frequency error behavior and numerical dispersion in porous-media PDEs; extend physics-informed learning with quantum components.

Method: Hybrid QCPIKAN architecture: Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers plus parametric quantum circuits; physics-informed loss enforcing physical constraints; theoretical support from approximation theory; tested on single-phase flow, component transport, and two-phase flow in porous media.

Result: Demonstrates exponential attenuation of high-frequency errors and mitigated dispersion; achieves higher global accuracy, better local error control, accurate dynamic evolution tracking, and precise displacement-front localization relative to existing quantum-classical PINNs.

Conclusion: QCPIKAN offers a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs, combining quantum-classical advantages with rigorous physics constraints.

Abstract: We develop QCPIKAN, the first quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold network designed to solve partial differential equations (PDEs). Built upon Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits, this hybrid framework embeds physical constraints into the training loss to enforce physical consistency. Our theoretical investigations grounded in approximation theory prove that this design accelerates high-frequency error convergence to an exponential rate and effectively mitigates numerical dispersion. We validate the framework across three typical seepage scenarios in porous media, including single-phase flow, component transport and two-phase flow. Compared with existing quantum-classical physics-informed neural networks, QCPIKAN achieves superior performance in global prediction accuracy, local error control, dynamic evolution tracking and displacement front localization. This work provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs.

</details>


### [273] [Constrained hybrid modelling to predict microbial dynamics and organic matter turnover in soil systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20329)
*Paul Collart,Juergen Gall,Andrea Schnepf,Holger Pagel,Lars Doorenbos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid framework to infer biokinetic parameters for a soil OM turnover model from metagenome-derived traits using a neural network with ecological constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve process-based soil models by embedding genomic information, addressing the data-to-parameter gap in microbial ecology crucial for predicting carbon cycling and climate responses.

Method: A neural-network mapping from genomic trait data to biokinetic parameters, integrated with ecological theory constraints to ensure realistic dynamics, applied to synthetic and real metagenome datasets, including unobserved state variables.

Result: Outperforms multiple baselines, effectively learns dynamics of unmeasured components, and remains robust with small training datasets.

Conclusion: Presents the first hybrid approach linking genome-derived traits to process-based soil models, enabling more accurate parameterization under data scarcity and aiding prediction of soil carbon dynamics.

Abstract: Soil microorganisms control organic matter cycling and largely determine how soil systems can cope with and mitigate climate change and environmental threats. Representing microbial dynamics in process-based soil models is therefore critical to predict carbon cycling in soils, albeit highly challenging to inform from data. One promising approach to improve their parametrisation is the integration of genomic data, yet modelling the complex and unknown relationship between genomes and the processes the microbes are driving is an unsolved problem. In this work, we present the first hybrid modeling framework for deriving biokinetic parameter values of a process-based soil organic matter turnover model from metagenome-inferred functional traits based on DNA sequencing data. Our model predicts biokinetic parameters of the process-based model from genomic trait data with a neural network and integrates constraints from ecological theory and literature to ensure realistic behavior, even of non-observed state variables. We evaluate our method on synthetic genomic trait datasets of varying complexity and on real data, showing that our approach improves performance over multiple baselines and learns the dynamics of unmeasurable components of the process-based model effectively, even for small training datasets.

</details>


### [274] [Critical Percolation as a Synthetic Data Model for Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20347)
*Aryeh Brill,Tom Ingebretsen Carlson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a scalable, analytically tractable synthetic dataset family based on critical mean-field percolation clusters in high-dimensional space, enabling principled interpretability evaluation via hierarchical latent variables and linear decodability from neural activations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current interpretability benchmarks lack hierarchical, multi-scale structure. Critical percolation provides fractal, power-law clusters with known exponents, offering a realistic yet tractable testbed for probing how networks capture multi-scale structure.

Method: Define hierarchical functions on percolation clusters. Latent taxonomy variables generate targets. Use a mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence to sample a random tree and corresponding hierarchical latent decomposition in near-linear time, enabling data generation at arbitrary scale. The data model is analytically tractable with fixed critical exponents that constrain properties without hyperparameter tuning.

Result: Probing experiments show that ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural activations. The dataset exhibits sparsity, self-similarity, and power-law statistics, while maintaining analytical tractability.

Conclusion: Critical percolation provides a principled, scalable benchmark for interpretability research, combining realistic multi-scale structure with analytic guarantees to facilitate evaluation of latent-variable discovery and attribution methods.

Abstract: Neural networks learn features that reflect the hierarchical, multi-scale structure of natural data. Synthetic datasets used to evaluate interpretability methods typically lack this structure, limiting their value as realistic toy models. To close this gap, we introduce a family of synthetic datasets consisting of hierarchical functions defined on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in a high-dimensional data space. The percolation data consists of sparse, low-dimensional fractal clusters with a power-law size distribution. Latent variables modeling a taxonomic hierarchy generate each data point's target value. The data model is analytically tractable with known critical exponents that fix its properties without requiring hyperparameter tuning. We leverage a mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence to propose an almost linear-time algorithm to jointly sample a random tree and its hierarchical latent decomposition, enabling data generation at arbitrary scale. Using probing experiments, we find that the model's ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations. Together, sparsity, self-similarity, power-law statistics, and analytical tractability make critical percolation a principled testbed for interpretability research.

</details>


### [275] [On the Variance of Temporal Difference Learning and its Reduction Using Control Variates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20357)
*Hsiao-Ru Pan,Bernhard Schölkopf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TD variance can be asymptotically bounded by Monte Carlo estimators in the phased tabular setting; shorter-horizon updates reduce variance for a fixed sample count; Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) acts as a regression-adjusted control variate providing tighter variance bounds in the large-sample limit; numerical results illustrate these behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncover the mechanisms by which TD learning reduces variance, compare TD with MC, and evaluate how DAE influences variance through control variates, with implications for sample efficiency and estimator design.

Method: The paper conducts a theoretical analysis in a phased setting with tabular representation to derive an asymptotic upper bound on TD variance in terms of MC estimators, analyzes the effect of update horizon on variance for a fixed sample budget, and reframes Direct Advantage Estimation as a regression-adjusted control variate. It also includes numerical experiments in carefully designed environments to illustrate the variance behavior of the estimators.

Result: TD variance is shown to be asymptotically bounded above by MC estimators; shorter horizon updates incur less variance for a fixed number of samples; DAE yields a tighter variance bound than TD in the large-sample limit; numerical experiments corroborate the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: Variance reduction in TD arises from effectively aggregating across more independent trajectories; using MC-like bounds clarifies the limit behavior, and DAE provides a principled route to tighter variance bounds via a regression-adjusted control variate. The results offer guidance for estimator choice and algorithm design to improve sample efficiency in RL.

Abstract: We analyze the variance of temporal difference (TD) learning using the phased setting with tabular representation, and show that one of the mechanisms behind its ability to reduce variance is by effectively aggregating over a larger number of independent trajectories. Based on this insight, we demonstrate that (1) the variance of TD is asymptotically bounded from above by Monte Carlo (MC) estimators, and (2) shorter horizon updates incurs less variance for a fixed number of samples. Beyond TD, we show that Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE), a method for estimating the advantage function, can be seen as a type of regression-adjusted control variate, which achieves a tighter bound on the variance compared to TD in the large-sample limit. Finally, we numerically illustrate the behaviors of these estimators with carefully designed environments.

</details>


### [276] [Train, Retrieve, or Both? A Four-Arm Head-to-Head for Correct Statutory Citation on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20359)
*Ali Asaria,Tony Salomone,Deep Gandhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid retrieval with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) provides the best exact-match citation accuracy for Ontario RTA citations among four evaluated configurations, while base zero-shot and SFT-only underperform. Retrieval is essential to prevent hallucinations; the SFT+RAG hybrid yields 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinations, outperforming a larger pipeline, but the aspirational 0.70 target is not reached. Results are preliminary on a small eval set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether fine-tuning alone suffices for legal-citation tasks or whether a hybrid SFT+RAG approach improves factual and exact-statute citations, addressing the information needs of self-represented tenants and legal aid.

Method: Four-arm head-to-head evaluation of Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct variants: base zero-shot; LoRA SFT-only; RAG-only; and SFT+RAG hybrid. Use citation-precision (section+subsection) as exact-match metric on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set. Compare against larger retrieval pipelines; analyze how recall and hallucinations interact with model configuration.

Result: The SFT+RAG hybrid achieves the highest exact-match score (~0.481) with zero hallucinated citations. The base model cannot cite the RTA; SFT-alone mis-recalls sections. RAG-only is hampered by high-recall candidate sets, but retrieval is essential to eliminate hallucinations by construction. A cheap bge-small hybrid matches or outperforms a pipeline using larger embedder and cross-encoder reranker. More data or larger training sets do not improve performance in this setting.

Conclusion: Hybrid SFT+RAG is effective at reducing hallucinations and improving robust statutory citation in this context, though the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target remains unmet. The results are preliminary on a small eval set and require further data, larger-scale validation, and potentially refinements. Practically, a lightweight hybrid approach can satisfy user needs without relying on heavy specialized retrieval.

Abstract: Self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff need to be pointed at the provision of law that actually governs a question, with a correct statutory citation. We study this task on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (RTA) and its core regulation, asking the operator's question empirically: is fine-tuning enough, or is hybrid retrieval needed? We run a four-arm head-to-head on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, and an SFT+RAG hybrid), scored on citation exact-match (section+subsection) over a small, human-verification-pending real eval set. The base model cannot cite the RTA and SFT-only mis-recalls sections; retrieval is essential and drives hallucination to zero by construction; and the SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations. Its edge comes from SFT making provision selection more robust to the higher-recall candidate sets that hurt zero-shot RAG. Notably, this cheap bge-small hybrid matches or beats a pipeline built on bigger, specialized retrieval models (a larger embedder and a cross-encoder reranker), and a larger/improved training set does not help either: strong statutory-citation performance here does not require specialized retrieval models or more data. The artifact zeroes hallucination and clears the lift-over-base bar but does not reach the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target. All results are on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set and are reported as preliminary.

</details>


### [277] [Judging to Improve: A De-biased VLM-as-3D-Judge Protocol for Single-Image 3D Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20364)
*Ali Asaria,Tony Salomone,Deep Gandhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A debiased cross-model VLM judge is specialized via lightweight adaptation to optimize a furniture asset generator; the protocol shows parity with a strong base but does not surpass it, highlighting the need for stronger signals and careful evaluation design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether a VLM-based judging signal can be specialized to guide an open generator without human labels, and whether such specialization yields gains for a furniture asset class.

Method: Create a training judge (distinct from an evaluation judge) to break circularity; apply position-bias correction; fix three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, reference-free judging that rewards clean-but-wrong outputs); calibrate with clear-gap win-rate evidence; use public models and data with lightweight parameter-efficient adaptation (PEFT); measure across six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep; use independent base samples to gauge learnable preferences; rely on quality-contrastive construction for signaling.

Result: Most targeted method (conditioner repair under severe degradation) achieves parity with the base (0.50 win-rate) but no method exceeds 65% win-rate; independent base samples show essentially no learnable preference (0.94 order-flip rate); overall, matching the strong base with cheap adaptation is possible but exceeding it requires stronger signals and data; clean inputs saturate the judge; conditioning repair moves geometry; win-rates are directional at n=8 objects; the protocol is informative and reusable.

Conclusion: The proposed protocol and calibration methods enable reuse for evaluating VLM-guided optimization; cheap adaptation can match a strong public base but does not surpass it, implying that more than lightweight PEFT and public data are needed for improvement.

Abstract: A companion study established a de-biased, cross-model VLM-as-3D-judge that reliably ranks single-image-to-3D mesh quality where cheap geometry and CLIP proxies fall short. This paper asks: can that judge's preferences specialize a strong open generator, TRELLIS, on one asset class (furniture), cheaply and without human labels? Taking the judge from ranking to optimization is where the work lives. Pushing a VLM judge into the training and evaluation loop exposes failure modes ranking never triggered, so our contribution is an optimization-grade hardening of the judge: a training judge (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) held distinct from an evaluation judge (InternVL3-8B) to break circularity; position-bias correction; and fixes for three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, and reference-free judging that rewards clean-but-wrong outputs), with calibration evidence (clear-gap win-rate 0.83-1.0; base-vs-base ~0.5). Using this protocol as an independent evaluator, and working only from public models and data with lightweight parameter-efficient adaptation, we find our methods match the strong base rather than exceed it. Independent base samples carry essentially no learnable preference (0.94 order-flip rate), so signal must be engineered by quality-contrastive construction. Across six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep, the most targeted - conditioner repair under severe degradation - reaches parity (0.50) with the base, while no method clears the >=65% win-rate target. The result is mechanistic: clean inputs saturate the judge, flow-DIT fine-tuning washes out through the sampler, and conditioning repair is the locus that moves geometry. Win-rates are directional at n=8 objects. Matching a strong public-data base with cheap adaptation is itself informative: exceeding it needs more than lightweight PEFT on public data, and the judge protocol is reusable.

</details>


### [278] [CRAX: Fast Safe Reinforcement Learning Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20376)
*Tristan Tomilin,Mourad Boustani,Mickey Beurskens,Thiago D. Simão*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRAX is a fast, scalable safe RL benchmarking suite built on MuJoCo XLA (MJX) with JAX; it achieves ~100x speedups over CPU baselines, provides six environments across three difficulty levels and agent-specific tasks, and reveals no single safe-RL method dominates while highlighting curriculum learning and safety transfer as beneficial.


<details>
