  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the computational bottleneck of high-fidelity 3D safety RL benchmarks, enabling large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping in safety-critical domains like robotics and autonomous driving.

Method: Develop CRAX on MJX with vectorized operations and hardware acceleration; provide six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks, each with three difficulty levels; evaluate six safe RL methods and analyze curriculum learning and safety transfer.

Result: Significant speedups (~100x) over CPU-based benchmarks; no single safe RL method dominates across all tasks; trade-offs exist between performance and safety; curriculum learning across difficulties and safety transfer improve performance in harder settings.

Conclusion: CRAX offers a scalable, diverse safe-RL benchmark platform that enables rapid experimentation and evaluation of safety-performance trade-offs; curricula and transfer mechanisms can bolster performance in challenging tasks.

Abstract: Safety is a core concern for deploying reinforcement learning (RL) agents in real-world domains such as robotics and autonomous driving. While benchmarks have been central to progress in RL, existing safety benchmarks with high-fidelity 3D physics remain computationally slow, limiting large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping. To address this gap, we propose CRAX (Constrained RL Accelerated with JAX). Built on top of the MuJoCo XLA (MJX) physics engine with realistic 3D dynamics, CRAX leverages vectorized operations and hardware acceleration, yielding up to ~100x speedups over comparable CPU-based safety benchmarks. The benchmark features six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks, each spanning three difficulty levels. Evaluating six popular safe RL methods shows that no single approach dominates across all tasks, and reveals the trade-offs between performance and safety. We find that curriculum learning across difficulty levels and safety transfer can improve performance over direct training in harder settings.

</details>


### [279] [Towards Modality-imbalanced Federated Graph Learning: A Data Synthesis-based Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20382)
*Zhengyu Wu,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Zekai Chen,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedMGS tackles modality imbalance in multimodal federated graph learning via graph-aware latent semantic synthesis, using three components to recover missing modal semantics; yields up to 17.41% gains across four tasks with strong efficiency-performance tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modality imbalance occurs at both client level (missing entire modalities) and node level (missing features). Existing studies largely address graph-agnostic or centralized settings, hindering practical deployment; there is a need to recover missing semantic information directly in the representation space to reduce variance and preserve privacy.

Method: Introduce FedMGS with three core components: (1) availability-aware graph encoder to shield local structure from missing modalities, (2) prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer to establish cross-client semantic anchors for missing modalities, (3) reliability-calibrated semantic fusion to regulate recovered latent representations before prediction. Formalizes modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis.

Result: Extensive experiments on four tasks show FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines, achieving gains up to 17.41% with favorable efficiency–performance tradeoffs.

Conclusion: FedMGS effectively solves modality imbalance in MM-FGL by synthesizing missing semantic information within the representation space, improving alignment with the data distribution and stabilizing learning under missing modalities.

Abstract: MultiModal Federated Graph Learning (MM-FGL) offers a natural collaborative training paradigm, but its practical deployment is challenged by two granularities of modality imbalance. Client-level imbalance occurs when certain clients lack entire modalities, while node-level imbalance occurs when individual nodes exhibit missing visual or textual attributes. While several relevant studies exist, our investigation reveals that they predominantly target graph-agnostic or centralized scenarios, rendering them difficult to adapt directly. To address these challenges, we formalize modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as an implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis problem. This paradigm recovers missing modal semantics directly within the representation space, thereby maximizing alignment with the original data's semantic distribution and mitigating the high variance induced by missing modalities. To this end, we propose FedMGS (Federated Modality-aware Graph Synthesis), which integrates three core components. The availability-aware graph encoder prevents missing modalities from contaminating local structural propagation. The prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer establishes cross-client semantic anchors for unavailable modalities. The reliability-calibrated semantic fusion mechanism regulates the impact of recovered latent representations prior to predictive readout. Extensive experiments on four tasks show that FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines with gains up to 17.41% with best efficiency-performance tradeoff.

</details>


### [280] [The Significance of Style Diversity in Annotation-Free Synthetic Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20400)
*Zahra Abbasiantaeb,Zeno Belligoli,Omar Essam,Mohammad Aliannejadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for generating high-utility synthetic dialogue data for intent classification without human annotations, using intent definitions, two axes of topic/style attributes for diversity, and two post-hoc stylization models (Univ, Exam); LLM-based filtering; achieves up to 93.3% of performance with labeled data; style diversity is more impactful than topic diversity; generation-time style integration outperforms post-hoc styling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation:  Industrial settings often lack labeled seed data for intent classification. The work aims to eliminate dependence on human-annotated data by leveraging only intent definitions to produce diverse, high-quality synthetic dialogues.

Method: Generate synthetic dialogues solely from intent definitions, introducing two attributes: topic and style, to boost diversity. Apply two post-hoc stylization models (Univ, Exam) to synthetic utterances. Use an LLM as a judge to filter data quality. Evaluate on industrial and public datasets.

Result: Synthetic data achieves up to 93.3% of the performance of models trained on human-annotated data. Style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for utility. Incorporating style attributes during generation is more effective than post-hoc styling.

Conclusion: Incorporating style diversity at generation time substantially enhances synthetic data utility for intent classification and reduces reliance on human annotations; prioritize style control during generation over topic variation and favor in-generation styling over post-processing.

Abstract: Generating high-utility synthetic data for intent classification typically requires human-annotated seed data, which is often unavailable in fast-paced industrial settings. In this paper, we propose a framework for synthetic dialogue generation that works entirely without human-annotated data, relying solely on intent definitions. Our proposed dialogue generation framework utilizes two different types of topic and style attributes to improve data diversity. Also, we propose two novel post-hoc stylization models called Univ and Exam to transform synthetic LLM-generated utterances into more varied, human-like linguistic styles. To enhance data quality, we utilize an LLM-as-a-judge filtering process. Experimental results on both industrial and public datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves up to 93.3% of the performance obtained using human-annotated training data. Crucially, the findings reveal that style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for synthetic data utility, as it prevents models from learning spurious stylistic correlations. Furthermore, the study shows that incorporating style attributes during the generation process is more effective than post-hoc style adaptation.

</details>


### [281] [Direct Advantage Estimation for Scalable and Sample-efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20411)
*Hsiao-Ru Pan,Bernhard Schölkopf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends Direct Advantage Estimation to partial observability and replaces explicit transition models with discrete latent dynamics, preserving sample efficiency on Atari while reducing computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DAE's gains rely on full observability and learned transition probabilities, which are expensive and impractical for high-dimensional, partially observed tasks. Extending DAE to POMDPs and reducing computational overhead are needed for real-world applicability.

Method: Provide a minimal-extension theoretical framework for DAE in partially observable domains; introduce a discrete latent dynamics model that learns p(z'|z,a) to approximate environment transitions; integrate this with the DAE update; evaluate on the Arcade Learning Environment.

Result: DAE maintains its sample efficiency under partial observability; performance scales with function approximator capacity; discrete latent dynamics deliver computational savings by avoiding full transition modeling; Atari experiments demonstrate viability.

Conclusion: The approach broadens the applicability of DAE to realistic, partially observable settings; discrete latent dynamics offer scalable, efficient transition modeling; future work could explore richer latent structures and broader benchmarks.

Abstract: Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) has been shown to improve the sample efficiency of deep reinforcement learning algorithms. However, its reliance on full environment observability limits its applicability in realistic settings, and its requirement to model transition probabilities incurs substantial computational overhead for high-dimensional observations. In the present work, we address both limitations. First, we extend the theoretical framework of DAE to partially observable domains with minimal modifications. Second, we reduce its computational complexity by introducing discrete latent dynamics models that efficiently approximate transition probabilities. We evaluate our approach on the Arcade Learning Environment and find that DAE scales effectively with function approximator capacity while retaining high sample efficiency.

</details>


### [282] [Pseudo-Feature Padding: A Lightweight Defense Against False Data Injection in Power Grids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20415)
*Farhin Farhad Riya,Shahinul Hoque,Yingyuan Yang,Jinyuan Sun,Kevin Tomsovic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight, model-agnostic defense for CPS-DNNs against FDIA via randomized input padding with pseudofeature values from input statistics, boosting robustness with minimal performance impact; validated on IEEE 14/30/118/300-bus state estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CPS-DNNs are vulnerable to false data injection attacks (FDIA); attackers can evade detection, and CPS-specific characteristics challenge conventional defenses. A deployable, architecture-agnostic solution is needed to enhance robustness without extensive modifications.

Method: Augment inputs with a dedicated padding layer that appends pseudofeature values derived from the inputs' statistical distribution. This increases input dimensionality in a randomized, data-aware manner, making adversarial perturbations non-transferable and less effective. The core model remains unchanged (lightweight, model-agnostic).

Result: Empirical evaluation on IEEE 14/30/118/300-bus systems for state estimation shows significant robustness gains under adversarial settings with negligible degradation in standard performance. The approach mitigates attacks that bypass traditional defenses.

Conclusion: Input-padding with data-derived pseudofeatures offers a practical, deployable defense for CPS-DNNs against FDIA, maintaining accuracy while enhancing security, and is suitable for real-world CPS deployments.

Abstract: Deep Neural Networks DNNs have achieved remarkable accuracy in various tasks including their application in CyberPhysical Systems CPS for detecting False Data Injection Attacks FDIA during critical operations However the unique infrastructure of CPS makes DNNs vulnerable to exploitation by attackers aiming to evade detection Additionally the distinct nature of CPS presents challenges for conventional defense mechanisms against FDIA This paper proposes an innovative defense framework that strengthens DNNs against such attacks by introducing an additional input layer that performs padding in the input samples using pseudofeature values derived from the inputs statistical distribution This padding increases the input dimensionality in a randomized and dataaware manner making adversarial attacks computationally infeasible due to the nontransferable nature of crafted perturbations and the unpredictability of the padded structure Our method is lightweight modelagnostic and requires no modifications to the core architecture making it highly deployable in realworld CPS settings We evaluated our framework on critical power grid applications such as state estimation using the IEEE 14bus 30bus 118bus and 300bus systems Experiments under adversarial settings demonstrate that our padding strategy significantly improves model robustness with negligible impact on performance and effectively mitigates attacks that would otherwise bypass conventional defenses

</details>


### [283] [On the Redundancy of Timestep Embeddings in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20416)
*José A. Chávez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Timesteps may be unnecessary: removing explicit timestep embeddings in diffusion models can retain or improve generation quality and be justified by theory under certain conditions; time-agnostic models on CelebA/CIFAR-10 match or surpass conditioned baselines in FID, precision, and recall, suggesting implicit noise-scale inference from corrupted inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the necessity of explicit temporal conditioning in diffusion models and explore whether simpler, time-agnostic architectures can achieve comparable or superior results while potentially improving efficiency.

Method: Theoretical analysis showing conditions under which the global minimizer of the diffusion training objective is attained without explicit timestep conditioning. Empirical ablations on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures with timesteps embeddings removed, trained on CelebA and CIFAR-10. Comparisons against conditioned baselines across metrics such as FID, precision, and recall. Investigation into whether the model can infer noise scales from the corrupted input under specific assumptions.

Result: Time-agnostic models preserve structural fidelity and can even outperform conditioned counterparts on selected metrics. The global training objective can reach its minimizer without explicit timestep conditioning under certain conditions. Models appear to implicitly infer noise scales from inputs, reducing the need for temporal conditioning.

Conclusion: Challenging the standard reliance on explicit temporal conditioning, the work points toward more efficient, time-agnostic diffusion architectures that maintain or improve performance, motivating broader exploration of implicit noise-scale inference and simplified conditioning schemes.

Abstract: Diffusion models rely heavily on explicit timestep embeddings to modulate the denoising process across various noise scales. In this work, we challenge the necessity of these temporal signals by analyzing their impact on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures. Beyond empirical evidence, we provide a theoretical framework demonstrating that, under certain conditions, the global minimizer of the diffusion training objective can be achieved without explicit timestep conditioning. Our findings reveal a surprising robustness when timestep embeddings are completely removed. Extensive ablation studies on the CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets show that these time-agnostic models can maintain high structural fidelity and even surpass their conditioned counterparts in competitive metrics, including FID, precision, and recall. Our analysis suggests these architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from the corrupted input under specific assumptions, rendering explicit temporal conditioning redundant. This study challenges long-standing temporal conditioning paradigms and paves the way for more efficient and structurally focused generative architectures.

</details>


### [284] [Neural network surrogates with uncertainty quantification for inverse problems in partial differential equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20417)
*Christian Jimenez-Beltran,Aretha L. Teckentrup,Antonio Vergari,Konstantinos C. Zygalakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A neural-network surrogate, DeepGaLA, provides uncertainty-aware predictions for DE solvers to enable scalable Bayesian inference in inverse problems; its fidelity is diagnosed via delayed-acceptance MCMC; it matches Gaussian-process surrogates in accuracy, scales better with parameter dimension, and supports DE constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian inverse problems require repeated, costly forward-model evaluations. Surrogates that quantify predictive uncertainty help prevent overconfident inferences when training data are limited, enabling scalable inference for complex differential-equation models.

Method: Introduce DeepGaLA, a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that yields uncertainty-aware predictions. The surrogate can incorporate differential-equation constraints, including nonlinear settings. Use a short delayed-acceptance MCMC run to diagnose the fidelity of surrogate-induced posterior approximations. Compare performance against established Gaussian-process surrogates across numerical experiments and test scalability with parameter dimensionality.

Result: DeepGaLA achieves forward-model accuracy comparable to Gaussian-process surrogates while remaining more efficient as parameter dimension grows. It provides uncertainty quantification to reduce overconfident inferences with limited training data and can incorporate DE constraints, including nonlinear ones.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates like DeepGaLA offer scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems, combining neural expressivity with principled uncertainty handling and compatibility with DE constraints.

Abstract: Inverse problems for differential equations arise throughout science and engineering, where one seeks to infer unknown model parameters from noisy or incomplete observations. Traditional numerical methods for these problems are often computationally expensive, particularly in Bayesian settings where evaluating the likelihood becomes costly for complex forward models and high-dimensional parameter spaces. To address this challenge, we introduce DeepGaLA, a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that provides uncertainty-aware predictions, reducing overconfident inference when training data are limited. To evaluate the fidelity of the surrogate-induced posterior approximations in practice, we show that a short run of delayed-acceptance Markov chain Monte Carlo can serve as an effective diagnostic. Across a range of numerical experiments, DeepGaLA delivers forward-model approximations with accuracy comparable to established Gaussian-process surrogates, while better maintaining efficiency as parameter dimension grows. Moreover, it can incorporate differential-equation constraints, including in nonlinear settings. Overall, these results indicate that uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates can enable scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems.

</details>


### [285] [Sparsity, Superposition, and Forgetting: A Mechanistic Study of Representation Retention in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20431)
*Jan Wasilewski,Jędrzej Kozal,Michał Woźniak,Bartosz Krawczyk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A controlled toy-world framework for continual learning (CL) that disentangles forgetting mechanisms by manipulating sparsity, feature superposition, and task-level capacity; uses SINDy to link retention dynamics to exposure; reports three findings on how superposition, sparsity, and capacity interact with forgetting; provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real CL datasets conflate many factors, making it hard to identify why forgetting occurs. A synthetic, controllable environment with ground-truth latent features and tunable task properties enables observable, testable mechanisms of retention, interference, and capacity allocation.

Method: Construct a synthetic generator–separator pipeline with ground-truth latent features. Create tasks with tunable sparsity and feature overlap. Define measurable quantities for representation strength and directional superposition. Analyze retention dynamics by fitting sparse dynamical models (SINDy) that relate retention, superposition, and exposure history. Perform a task-level analysis via effective rank to characterize how representational capacity is allocated across tasks.

Result: Three takeaways: (1) Superposition tends to increase over time with transient dips at task boundaries, suggesting boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher feature sparsity induces more superposition yet does not inevitably cause forgetting; when representations remain strong, forgetting can be reduced despite overlap. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes. Overall, overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation, challenging the simplistic view that more superposition always leads to more forgetting.

Conclusion: The toy analysis yields falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL, enabling targeted investigation of forgetting mechanisms in a controlled setting.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) systems often forget previously acquired knowledge, yet the mechanisms driving forgetting remain hard to isolate in practice because real datasets entangle many factors. We present a controlled, toy-world framework that makes these mechanisms observable and testable. Using a synthetic generator-separator pipeline, we define ground-truth latent features, build tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap, and introduce measurable quantities for representation strength and superposition (directional overlap among features). We then study retention dynamics-the temporal change of representation strength by fitting sparse dynamical relations (via SINDy) between retention, superposition, and exposure history. A complementary task-level analysis based on effective rank characterizes how representational capacity is allocated across tasks. Our controlled experiments yield three takeaways. (1) Superposition tends to increase over time with transient dips at task boundaries, suggesting boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher feature sparsity induces more superposition yet does not inevitably cause forgetting; when representations remain strong, forgetting can be reduced despite overlap. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes. Together, these results nuance the common intuition that more superposition leads to more forgetting by showing that overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation. Our toy analysis provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL.

</details>


### [286] [Evolutionary Two-Stage Hyperparameter Optimization Strategies for Physics-Informed Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20442)
*Fedor Buzaev,Dmitry Efremenko,Egor Bugaev,Andrei Ermakov,Denis Derkach,Daria Pugacheva,Fedor Ratnikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-stage evolutionary optimization for PINN hyperparameters: quick, low-fidelity screening of configurations via truncated-epoch training, followed by full training of the best candidates using standard optimizers. Demonstrates improved mean error under fixed compute on Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz equations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs exhibit unstable convergence and severe sensitivity to architecture and optimization hyperparameters due to a non-convex, multi-term physics-informed loss. Outer-loop hyperparameter tuning is a noisy, black-box problem where gradient-based methods struggle. Evolutionary algorithms offer robust, population-based exploration over heterogeneous hyperparameters.

Method: Stage 1: Run low-fidelity PINN trainings with truncated epochs to rapidly evaluate many configurations; treat hyperparameters (architecture, optimizer, loss weights, learning rate schedule, etc.) as a black-box search problem. Stage 2: Fully train the top-performing configurations with standard gradient-based optimizers to refine solutions. The outer-loop optimization is guided by an evolutionary algorithm that balances exploration and exploitation within a fixed computational budget.

Result: Across three PDE problems (Advection, Klein-Gordon, Helmholtz), the two-stage EA approach consistently surpasses standard PINN training, achieving significantly lower mean error under constrained compute.

Conclusion: A two-stage evolutionary strategy improves PINN robustness and accuracy within fixed budgets by decoupling rapid exploration from thorough exploitation. The approach is effective across diverse PDEs, though its performance hinges on the fidelity of the low-cost screening and the design of the evolutionary operators; further studies could explore hyperparameter sensitivity, budgets, and scalability.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) solve Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws into neural network training. However, their performance suffers from unstable convergence, training plateaus, and strong sensitivity to architectural and optimization hyperparameters due to the highly non-convex and multi-term structure of the physics-informed loss. In this setting, the outer-loop hyperparameter search is a noisy and black-box optimization problem over heterogeneous parameters, where classical local or gradient-based strategies are easily trapped in suboptimal regions. Evolutionary algorithms, with their population-based exploration and ability to handle mixed, non-differentiable search spaces, provide a more robust mechanism for discovering promising configurations. We propose and investigate a two-stage approach based on evolutionary algorithms that combines exploration and exploitation parts of PINNs training to improve solution accuracy and robustness under fixed computational budgets. In the first stage, we perform low-fidelity training runs with truncated epochs to rapidly screen candidate configurations, treating hyperparameter selection as a black-box outer-loop problem. In the second stage, only the most promising candidates are fully trained with standard gradient-based optimizers to refine the solution. Evaluated on three popular problems, namely Advection, Klein-Gordon and Helmholtz equations, our method consistently outperforms standard training and achieves significantly lower mean error within constrained computational resources.

</details>


### [287] [Data Bias Mitigation under Coverage Constraints & The Price of Fairness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20461)
*Bruno Scarone,Alfredo Viola,Renée J. Miller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An intersectional bias mitigation framework using coverage constraints and an integer linear program (ILP) to ensure representation of intersectional subgroups, trading small bias for data efficiency. It defines the price of fairness as the minimum data modification cost under a given fairness tolerance and demonstrates performance preservation across classifiers with coverage constraints being essential for downstream accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of principled intersectional bias measures and the underrepresentation of intersectional subgroups in training data; provide a data-governance-friendly, legally aware tool that quantifies the trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification costs.

Method: Extend a bias mitigation framework by incorporating coverage constraints to ensure sufficient representation for all groups, including intersectional subgroups. Formulate bias mitigation as an integer linear program over mitigation strategies and introduce the price of fairness as the minimum data modification cost as a function of fairness tolerance.

Result: Empirical evaluation on public datasets shows preserved predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers when using coverage-constrained mitigation. Coverage constraints are essential to maintaining downstream ML performance, and the approach provides data-efficient bias mitigation with tunable fairness.

Conclusion: Incorporating coverage constraints yields a practical, data-efficient approach to intersectional bias mitigation, with a formal ILP framework and a quantifiable price of fairness to guide policy and data-governance decisions.

Abstract: Machine learning models have been shown to exhibit discriminatory outcomes or degraded performance for individuals at the intersection of multiple sensitive attributes, such as race and gender. This stems in part from two interrelated challenges: the lack of principled measures for quantifying bias (potentially intersectional), and insufficient representation of intersectional subgroups in training data. We extend a recent bias mitigation framework to incorporate coverage constraints that enforce sufficient representation across groups, including intersectional subgroups. Since achieving exactly zero bias for all groups may not be data efficient (meaning it may require large amounts of data), our solution trades small approximation errors in bias for greater data efficiency while satisfying coverage constraints. We also formulate bias mitigation as an integer linear program that optimizes over all mitigation strategies, and characterize the price of fairness, the minimum data modification cost, as a function of fairness tolerance. This is essential both for legal compliance, where regulations may mandate specific fairness thresholds, and for data governance, enabling practitioners to make informed trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification (particularly, data purchasing) costs. We evaluate our techniques on publicly available datasets, demonstrating that bias mitigation via our framework preserves predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers, and that coverage constraints, while motivated by statistical considerations, are essential for preserving downstream ML performance.

</details>


### [288] [Agentic Symbolic Search: Characterizing PDEs Beyond Hand-crafted Expressions, Meshes, and Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20467)
*Zongmin Yu,Liu Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASYS proposes a prior-guided, differentiable symbolic-search framework to discover and refine analytical PDE representations by evolving symbolic structures and fitting parameters, yielding interpretable formulas for problems where closed forms exist or can be approximated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To shift PDE solution understanding from numeric/black-box outputs to interpretable analytical structures, leveraging prior theory and accumulated experience through automated inductive bias.

Method: Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS): an agent translates PDE theory, public constraints, and search history into differentiable symbolic programs. Evolutionary search refines symbolic forms, while gradient-based optimization fits continuous parameters. The approach injects inductive bias and aims to recover known analytical forms or construct new analytical approximations, evaluated on five PDE problems.

Result: ASYS yields interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen–Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller–Segel chemotactic blow-up. It recovers existing analytical forms when available and constructs analytical approximations for problems lacking closed forms, demonstrated across five problems spanning bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing.

Conclusion: ASYS demonstrates a new paradigm for PDE solution characterization, offering interpretable analytical forms discovered automatically, complementing handcrafted analysis, mesh-based numerics, and neural approximations.

Abstract: Mathematicians understand a PDE solution through mathematical structures rather than tables of computed values. Historically, this has been the product of mathematical analysis, carried out by hand for each problem individually. Neither numerical simulation nor neural networks produce those structures directly. We propose Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS), a prior-guided framework in which an agent translates PDE theory, public problem constraints, and accumulated search experience into testable differentiable symbolic programs. The mathematical forms are refined under evolutionary search, while their continuous parameters are fit by gradient-based optimization. This makes the search an automated form of inductive-bias injection rather than blind symbolic regression. For problems with known analytical forms, ASYS recovers these forms naturally; for other problems, ASYS constructs analytical approximations which can guide mathematicians toward further analysis. In our experiments, across five problems spanning bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing, ASYS produces interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel chemotactic blow-up, in settings where no closed-form description was previously available. ASYS shows the possibility of a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions, beyond handcrafted analytical solutions, mesh-based numerical solutions, and neural network approximations.

</details>


### [289] [UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20474)
*Inesh Chakrabarti,David Limpus,Aditi Ghai Rana,Bowen Bao,Spandan Tiwari,Thiago Crepaldi,Ashish Sirasao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: 4-bit KV-cache with rotation/codebook quantization for context-heavy, multi-turn agents; achieves 3.47x (late rounds) and 2.3x (all rounds) faster P50 TTF and 1.63x higher throughput vs FP8 baseline, aided by AMD GPU optimizations (UltraQuant).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context, multi-turn agent workloads create heavy pressure on the KV cache due to reused long prefixes and concurrency limits. The work frames a 4-bit KV caching approach to jointly optimize task quality, cache residency, and serving throughput.

Method: Adopts 4-bit KV caching anchored by TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization (quality anchor) and vLLM FP8 KV caching (deployment anchor). Presents practical design choices (asymmetric K/V handling, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, block-scale variants) and GPU-focused serving optimizations (optimized decode-attention kernels, UltraQuant FP4 path with FP8 queries, FP4 KV tensors, UE8M0 scales, native scaled-MFMA on CDNA4).

Result: On long-context multi-turn workloads, UltraQuant yields 3.47x faster P50 time-to-first-token in late rounds (2.3x across all rounds) and 1.63x throughput improvement over the FP8 KV baseline.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that a carefully engineered 4-bit KV-cache pipeline—with rotation, quantization, and GPU-accelerated kernels—substantially reduces latency and increases throughput for context-heavy, multi-turn agents, validating 4-bit caching as a practical deployment strategy.

Abstract: Context-heavy agents place unusual pressure on the key-value (KV) cache: long prefixes are reused across many short turns, while concurrency determines whether the serving system can keep GPUs utilized. We study 4-bit KV-cache compression for this setting, using TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization as a quality anchor and vLLM FP8 KV caching as the deployment anchor. We report three contributions. First, we frame 4-bit KV caching around multi-round agent workloads where task quality, cache residency, and serving throughput must be measured jointly. Second, we describe the practical design choices needed to make the 4-bit path robust, including asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, and block-scale variants. Third, we present serving optimizations on AMD GPUs, including optimized decode-attention kernels and UltraQuant, an FP4 approximation path that uses FP8 queries, FP4 KV tensors, UE8M0 group scales, and native scaled-MFMA support on CDNA4. On a long-context, multi-turn agentic workload, UltraQuant cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in the cache-pressured late rounds (2.3x across all rounds) and raises output throughput by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline.

</details>


### [290] [Marginal Advantage Accumulation for Memory-Driven Agent Self-Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20475)
*Mingyu Yang,Keye Zheng,Congchao Cheng,Yujie Liu,Xingkang Lu,Fan Jiang,Yefei Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAA introduces cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation for batch-style trace distillation via alignability and comparability; uses EMA to accumulate differential signals, semantic identity merging; achieves strong empirical results across benchmarks while reducing token cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of cross-batch evidence accumulation in batch-style trace distillation, which prevents distinguishing consistently effective operations from coincidental hits.

Method: Proposes two structural conditions (alignability and comparability). Introduces Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA): constructs differential signals to enable cross-batch comparability, accumulates signed evidence per operation via EMA, and ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging; implemented as a post-processing architecture.

Result: Empirically strong: best results in 14/16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models; consistently outperforms batch-level distillation baselines and matches or exceeds online alternatives in most settings; reduces optimization-phase token consumption by ~75%.

Conclusion: MAA provides a principled cross-batch evidence-accumulation framework for batch distillation, enabling reliable identification of stable operations and significant token-cost reduction; potential for generalization to similar batch-based distillation tasks.

Abstract: In batch-style trace distillation, the same memory operation may receive contradictory feedback across different batches. Existing methods lack a cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation mechanism, making it impossible to distinguish stably effective operations from accidental hits. This paper formalizes the requirement as two structural conditions, alignability and comparability, and proposes Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA). MAA constructs differential signals to make them comparable across batches, accumulates signed evidence per operation via EMA, and ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging. As a post-processing architecture, MAA achieves the best results in 14 out of 16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models, consistently outperforming existing batch-level distillation baselines and matching or surpassing online alternatives in most settings, while reducing optimization-phase token consumption by approximately 75%.

</details>


### [291] [Contagion Networks: Evaluator Bias Propagation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20493)
*Zewen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Contagion Networks to quantify how evaluator biases propagate among LLM agents; reports measurable contagion (Gamma_3), propagation regimes, and mitigation via larger evaluator committees; open-source framework released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluator biases in LLM-based multi-agent systems can cascade through interactions, undermining overall system evaluation. A formal, measurable framework is needed to understand propagation dynamics and to design effective mitigations.

Method: 3-agent experiment with DeepSeek-chat; three evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based); compute Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_N (Gamma_3) and spectral radius rho(Gamma_N); compare across homogeneous vs cross-model setups; vary evaluator committee size k from 1 to 3; quantify contagion reduction; benchmark against prior MM-EPC results; release open-source framework.

Result: Observed gamma values in [0.157, 0.352], indicating consistent propagation of evaluator biases between agents, even with the same underlying model. Identified three propagation regimes governed by rho(Gamma_N). Homogeneous-model agents exhibit contagion coefficients 3–5x weaker than cross-model coefficients reported in MM-EPC (gamma ~0.85–1.3), placing them in the suppression regime. Increasing evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%. Open-source Contagion Network framework released.

Conclusion: Evaluator biases propagate through interacting LLM agents with measurable contagion dynamics; larger evaluator committees effectively mitigate contagion; cross-model interactions are more prone to contagion; framework enables systematic study and mitigation of evaluation biases in multi-agent LLM systems.

Abstract: When large language models serve as evaluators in multi-agent systems, their systematic evaluation biases propagate through the agent network. We introduce Contagion Networks, a formal framework for measuring how evaluator biases spread across interacting LLM agents. In a controlled 3-agent experiment using DeepSeek-chat with three distinct evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based), we measure the Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_3 and find that evaluator biases consistently propagate between agents (gamma in [0.157, 0.352]), even within the same underlying model. We identify three propagation regimes governed by the spectral radius rho(Gamma_N), and demonstrate that homogeneous-model agents produce contagion coefficients 3-5x weaker than cross-model coefficients observed in prior work (MM-EPC: gamma approx 0.85-1.3), placing them in the suppression regime. We show that increasing evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%, providing an actionable mitigation strategy. We release the open-source Contagion Network experimental framework.

</details>


### [292] [Execution-State Capsules: Graph-Bound Execution-State Checkpoint and Restore for Low-Latency, Small-Batch, On-Device Physical-AI Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20537)
*Liang Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces execution-state capsules and FlashRT to enable graph-bound, stateful snapshot/restoration for on-device, low-latency LLM serving, offering sub-millisecond restoration and GPU speedups over cold prefill, as a complementary alternative to traditional KV-cache reuse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the latency-bound regime of on-device LLM serving where frequent branching, resets, and re-entries require full-state reuse beyond KV fragments.

Method: Propose a white-box kernel runtime (FlashRT) that executes captured graph plans on contiguous static buffers, enabling graph-bound execution-state capsules that snapshot/restore/fork/rollback the entire execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolutions, MTP, and metadata.

Result: Demonstrates byte-exact capsule restores on RTX 5090; token-identical under greedy decode; sub-millisecond GPU-resident snapshot/restore; TTFT speedup grows from 3.9x (2k tokens) to 27x (16k tokens); correctness holds on Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark.

Conclusion: Execution-state capsules complement traditional KV-cache serving by providing a latency-first mechanism for explicit execution-state reuse, and are effective across edge and data-center platforms.

Abstract: Mainstream LLM serving systems reuse prefix work mainly through paged or radix key-value (KV) caches. This is highly effective for high-throughput, high-concurrency serving, but it manages only one positional fragment of execution state: the KV cache. We study the opposite regime: low-latency, small-batch, on-device physical-AI serving, where interactive LLM agents, speech systems, and robot policies repeatedly branch, reset, interrupt, and re-enter under tight responsiveness budgets. We introduce execution-state capsules, a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism for the complete restorable state at a committed boundary. FlashRT is a white-box, backend-facing kernel runtime whose evaluated NVIDIA CUDA backend runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. Because the live state is a closed set of named buffers, a capsule can snapshot, restore, fork, or roll back the whole execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata. This moves reuse from token-addressed KV fragments to graph-bound execution-state boundaries. On an RTX 5090, capsule restore is byte-exact at the stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode. A KV-only ablation diverges, showing that recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond, and TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens. On Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark, the same correctness and structural properties hold. Capsules are not a replacement for high-throughput KV-cache serving; they define a complementary latency-first serving point for explicit execution-state reuse.

</details>


### [293] [Multi-Task Bayesian In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20538)
*Qingyang Zhu,Eric Karl Oermann,Kyunghyun Cho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that uses priors as in-context prefixes. A transformer learns to adapt predictions across prior families, achieving oracle-like accuracy with substantial speed gains, and showing robustness to distribution shifts in both synthetic and real-world spatiotemporal data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exact Bayesian inference is often intractable and existing amortized approaches struggle with adapting to new priors or distribution shifts. There is a need for scalable, robust predictive inference that can generalize across priors and high-dimensional latent structures.

Method: Introduce a multi-task in-context learning (ICL) framework where prior information is represented as a prefix of in-context data. Train a transformer on sequences of prior and target tasks to learn to adapt predictive distributions across families of priors (i.e., amortized hierarchical Bayesian inference).

Result: The method matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster. It remains robust under out-of-meta-distribution priors and priors with high-dimensional latent structure. Demonstrated on a real-world spatiotemporal temperature benchmark.

Conclusion: This work supports scalable, robust amortized Bayesian inference by leveraging ICL with explicit prior representations, enabling rapid adaptation to new priors and complex latent structures across task families.

Abstract: Bayesian predictive inference provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification, data efficiency, and robust generalization. However, exact inference is often intractable, and scalable approximations may remain computationally expensive or require restrictive modeling assumptions that degrade predictive performance. Prior-Data Fitted and in-context models have recently emerged as an amortized alternative by learning to map datasets directly to predictive distributions, but existing approaches are tightly coupled to the support of the training prior and lack explicit mechanisms for adapting to new priors at test time, resulting in limited robustness under distribution shift. We introduce a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that explicitly represents prior information as a prefix of in-context datasets. A transformer trained on sequences of prior and target tasks learns to adapt its predictions across families of priors. On a suite of evaluations with increasing difficulty, including out-of-meta-distribution priors and priors with high-dimensional latent structures, our method matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster. We further demonstrate its practical relevance on a real-world spatiotemporal temperature prediction benchmark. Code is available at https://github.com/martianmartina/multi-task-bayesian-icl/.

</details>


### [294] [Predictability as a Fine-Grained Measure for Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20546)
*Linda Lu,Karthik Sridharan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework called privacy via predictability quantifies privacy leakage as the attacker’s gain in predicting sensitive information after seeing outputs, given compromised data. It is generally incomparable to differential privacy (DP), but in a worst-case setting it implies mutual-information DP. The paper develops a GMM-based asymptotic analysis for predictability with data from a stationary, ergodic, mixing process, and derives a predictability-calibrated output perturbation for empirical risk minimization (ERM), offering a complementary, fine-grained privacy control alongside DP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DP’s worst-case guarantees can be overly conservative, causing large accuracy loss. A finer-grained, attacker-model-aware privacy metric is needed to capture realistic leakage from partial or compromised data.

Method: Define unpredictability as the incremental predictive gain for unknown individuals after observing the algorithm’s output, conditional on compromised data. Use generalized method of moments (GMM) to analyze asymptotics under stationary, ergodic, mixing processes. Derive a perturbation scheme for ERM outputs calibrated by predicted leakage.

Result: Reveal that predictability and DP are generally incomparable; in the extreme compromised-data scenario with binary sensitive queries, predictability implies mutual-information DP. Provide a framework to quantify leakage for specific attacker knowledge and queries, plus a practical, predictability-calibrated perturbation for ERM.

Conclusion: Predictability offers a flexible, attacker-model-specific privacy metric that complements DP. It enables finer-grained control of privacy-utility tradeoffs and can be used alongside DP to tailor protection to particular sensitive information and adversary models.

Abstract: Differential privacy (DP) ensures rigorous individual-level privacy guarantees against even the most knowledgeable attackers, but its worst-case nature can impose a costly privacy-accuracy tradeoff. We introduce privacy via predictability, a fine-grained framework that explicitly incorporates the attacker's core knowledge, a compromised portion of the dataset generated by a stochastic process, and a specified family of queries. Predictability measures privacy leakage as the incremental gain in an attacker's ability to predict sensitive information about unknown individuals after observing the algorithm's output, beyond what can already be inferred from the compromised data. We show that predictability and DP are generally incomparable: each can be small while the other is large. However, in the worst-case regime where all but one individual is compromised, and all binary queries are considered sensitive, predictability implies mutual-information DP. More generally, predictability provides a finer-grained privacy metric tailored to specific sensitive information and specific attacker models. We introduce a general framework, using the generalized method of moments (GMM), to analyze asymptotic predictability when the compromised data is generated by a stationary, ergodic, mixing process. Using this analysis, we derive a predictability-calibrated output perturbation scheme for ERM. Our approach is complementary to DP and can be used alongside DP to provide fine-grained privacy control.

</details>


### [295] [Optimal Deterministic Multicalibration and Omniprediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20557)
*Georgy Noarov,Aaron Roth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deterministic minimax-optimal multicalibration achieving the known rate ~O(ε^{-3}), previously only attainable by randomized predictors; extends to outcome indistinguishability and yields deterministic omnipredictors/panpredictors with optimal sample complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibrated predictions across multiple group weightings are desirable for trustworthy ML. The open question was whether randomization is necessary to achieve minimax-optimal sample complexity for ε-multicalibration; resolving this clarifies fundamental limits and broadens practical applicability.

Method: Constructs a minimax-optimal deterministic algorithm for ε-multicalibration that outputs a deterministic predictor. generalizes the approach to produce deterministic predictors satisfying outcome indistinguishability (OI) for finite or finitely covered test collections. Applies these ideas to obtain deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity.

Result: The paper achieves minimax-optimal ~O~(ε^{-3}) sample complexity for ε-multicalibration with a deterministic predictor, and extends to deterministic OI predictors for finite/finitely covered tests, yielding deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal rates.

Conclusion: Resolves the open problem on whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration, establishing that determinism suffices. Provides a unified framework for deterministic predictors across multicalibration, OI, omniprediction, and panpredictors, with potential impact on trustworthy ML and fairness-related applications.

Abstract: A model is multicalibrated on a collection of group weights $G$ if it is calibrated -- i.e. unbiased even conditional on its prediction -- not just overall, but also after reweighting contexts by each $g \in G$. It is a useful property for many downstream applications and is a basic desideratum of trustworthy machine learning. Before this work, all predictors known to attain the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity rate for $\varepsilon$-multicalibration were randomized, while deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity. Whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration was explicitly asked by [CLNR26] and implicitly in several prior works.
  We resolve this open problem by giving a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor. We then generalize the algorithm to produce optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability (OI) with respect to finite or finitely covered collections of tests. As an application, this also gives deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity, resolving open problems posed by [OKK25] and [BHHLZ25].

</details>


### [296] [How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20560)
*Joshua Engels,Callum McDougall,Bilal Chughtai,Janos Kramar,Senthoran Rajamanoharan,Cindy Wu,Arthur Conmy,Asic Q Chen,Jean Tarbouriech,Min Ma,Brendan O'Donoghue,João Gabriel Lopes de Oliveira,Rohin Shah,Neel Nanda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiffusionGemma’s diffusion-based reasoning initially seems less transparent, but introducing an interpretable token bottleneck reduces variable- transparency gap to 1.1× Gemma 4 without sacrificing performance; algorithmic transparency remains harder. Diffusion-specific phenomena emerge in interpretability studies, while monitorability remains comparable to Gemma 4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess how computation in continuous latent spaces affects two facets of reasoning transparency (variable and algorithmic) in diffusion-based LLMs, and evaluate practical monitorability.

Method: Decompose transparency into variable transparency (visibility of intermediate states) and algorithmic transparency (ability to reconstruct the reasoning process). Quantify opaque serial depth; implement an interpretable token bottleneck to route information between denoising steps; compare to Gemma 4. Perform interpretability case studies to identify diffusion-specific effects (non-chronological reasoning, token/sequence smearing, intermediate-context reasoning). Assess monitorability for downstream tasks.

Result: Opaque serial depth is reduced from naive diffusion levels to a near-parity of Gemma 4 (1.1×) when using a token bottleneck with no performance loss. Algorithmic transparency remains more challenging for diffusion due to pervasive token updates. Case studies reveal diffusion-specific phenomena. Monitorability of DiffusionGemma is comparable to Gemma 4.

Conclusion: Interpretable bottlenecks can restore variable-transparency in diffusion models without harming performance, while algorithmic transparency remains harder; diffusion-specific interpretability effects exist; monitorability remains viable, guiding safe use and debugging of diffusion-based LLMs.

Abstract: LLM reasoning transparency is a critical affordance for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse and misalignment, and debugging surprising model behaviors. However, DiffusionGemma performs a larger fraction of its computation in a continuous latent space; does this make its reasoning less transparent? We study this question by decomposing transparency into two components: variable transparency, whether we understand intermediate snapshots of a model's computational state; and algorithmic transparency, whether we can use these snapshots to reconstruct the process by which the model arrived at its outputs. Naively, DiffusionGemma has poor variable transparency: its opaque serial depth, the amount of serial computation that occurs in between interpretable model states, seems at first 28.6X higher than the corresponding autoregressive Gemma 4 model. However, we show that we can map the information flowing between denoising steps through an interpretable token bottleneck with no decrease in downstream performance. Treating these intermediate states as interpretable reduces the opaque serial depth to just 1.1X that of Gemma 4. Algorithmic transparency is harder for diffusion models than for autoregressive models because all token predictions in the canvas can change at every denoising step, giving the model the power to implement complicated distributed algorithms during the denoising process. To begin bridging this gap, we conduct a suite of interpretability case studies, uncovering initial evidence of novel diffusion-specific phenomena such as non-chronological reasoning, token and sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning. Finally, we test monitorability, a key application of transparency that measures whether model outputs are useful for downstream tasks. We find that DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [297] [Semi-Automatic Correction of 3D Tubular Structure Skeletons via Component-Wise MST and Filtered Delaunay Triangulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19949)
*Ruoxuan Yang,Chuan Li*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Semi-automatic correction of topological artifacts in 3D skeletons by user-guided path finding. Uses component-wise MST for local propagation and filtered 3D Delaunay graph to bridge gaps, producing an ordered centerline that fits into post-processing workflows. Implemented in C++ with Libigl; qualitative brain-vessel results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automatic skeletonization often yields topological artifacts (spurious connections, fragmentation) that are difficult to fix automatically. A minimal-interaction correction tool can accelerate editing while preserving plausible centerlines.

Method: Given a user-selected source and target point, the method traces a path by combining (i) component-wise minimum spanning trees for stable local propagation and (ii) a filtered 3D Delaunay edge graph to bridge gaps and handle ambiguous junctions. Candidate steps are ranked by a score incorporating direction continuity, spatial proximity, component consistency, and progress toward the target. Output is an ordered polyline (edge sequence) suitable as a correction to be integrated into downstream skeleton post-processing. Implemented in C++ with Libigl; interactive viewer; demonstrated on brain vessel datasets with typical crossing and dotted artifacts.

Result: Qualitative demonstrations on brain vessel datasets show correction of common artifacts (crossing, dotted). The approach is lightweight and intended as a building block for more comprehensive interactive correction pipelines in biomedical imaging and related domains.

Conclusion: The proposed semi-automatic method yields plausible centerline corrections with minimal user input and can integrate into existing skeletonization workflows. Validation is qualitative; further quantitative evaluation and integration with broader pipelines would strengthen its utility.

Abstract: Skeletonization of tubular structures from 3D imaging is essential for tasks such as morphometric analysis, transport or flow simulation, and procedural planning in domains including vascular networks, plant root systems, and neural connectomes. However, automatic skeleton extraction often introduces topological artifacts, such as erroneous connections between nearby branches and fragmented centerlines caused by noise or missing data. Correcting these artifacts manually can be time-consuming and error-prone, especially when precise interaction is required.
  We present a semi-automatic correction method that reconstructs a plausible centerline connection from minimal user input. Given a user-selected source and target point, our method traces a path by combining (i) component-wise minimum spanning trees for stable local propagation and (ii) a filtered 3D Delaunay edge graph for bridging gaps and handling ambiguous junctions. Candidate steps are ranked using a score that accounts for direction continuity, spatial proximity, component consistency, and target-directed progress. The output is an ordered polyline (or edge sequence) that can be used as a suggested correction and integrated into downstream skeleton post-processing workflows.
  We implement the system in C++ with an interactive viewer based on Libigl and demonstrate representative qualitative results on brain vessel datasets, including correction of typical "crossing" and "dotted" artifacts. While our current validation is qualitative, the method is lightweight and serves as a practical building block toward more comprehensive interactive correction pipelines in biomedical imaging and related domains.

</details>


### [298] [Quadratic Forms for Measuring Geometric Trees in 3-dimensional Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20096)
*Yossi Bokor Bleile,Emanuele Cortinovis,Herbert Edelsbrunner,Shota Uka*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Proposes a hexplot visualization and a quadratic-form–based metric to quantify the directional spread of tree-like geometric graphs in R^3, enabling visualization, measurement, and statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the processes shaping tree-like structures by applying geometric and topological tools to quantify their directional spread.

Method: Apply the theory of quadratic forms to measure directional spread of 3D geometric graphs and introduce the hexplot model, with a metric derived from the Fisher metric on the standard triangle, for visualization and statistics.

Result: Introduces the hexplot model and its metric, enabling visualization and quantitative analysis of directional spread for tree-like geometric graphs in three-dimensional space, and supports data collection of relevant statistics.

Conclusion: The hexplot framework provides a new, principled approach for analyzing directional spread in tree-like geometric graphs by integrating quadratic-form theory with Fisher-metric-inspired geometry for visualization and statistics.

Abstract: Tree-like structures appear in many areas of science, and their shapes can help understand the underlying processes they drive or that give rise to them.
  By thinking of these structures as geometric graphs in $\mathbb{R}^3$, we gain access to tools from computational geometry and topology to study them.
  In this paper, we adopt the theory of quadratic forms to measure the directional spread of geometric graphs, and we introduce the hexplot model -- equipped with a metric derived from the Fisher metric on the standard triangle -- to visualize, measure, and collect statistics.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [299] [Physical Atari: A Robust and Accessible Platform for Real-time Reinforcement Learning on Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19357)
*Khurram Javed,Joseph Modayil,Gloria Kennickell,Richard S. Sutton,John Carmack*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A low-cost, robust platform (Physical Atari) for real-world reinforcement learning experiments by integrating a Robotroller, Atari Devbox, and accessible hardware; built for under $1,000 and capable of weeks-long, reusable trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between simulation and real-world RL by providing an affordable, robust, and reproducible hardware/software platform to study learning directly on physical robots and to understand how small distribution shifts affect policy performance.

Method: Hardware/software design for Physical Atari: Robotroller acts on an Atari CX40+ controller; Atari Devbox renders the environment frame and reward from the Arcade Learning Environment; off-the-shelf camera and desktop computer integrate into a complete system. Robustness achieved by bearing-based motion to reduce wear and high-frequency servo monitoring with stress-limiting. Accessibility achieved via consumer 3D-printed parts and components under $1,000 total cost. System validated through weeks of continuous RL experiments on robots.

Result: Demonstrates that reinforcement learning algorithms can learn directly on robots using Physical Atari. Shows that even small distribution shifts between learning and deployment can significantly degrade policy performance, underscoring the importance of on-device adaptation for strong robotic performance.

Conclusion: Physical Atari is a robust, affordable, and accessible platform for conducting real-world RL research. It enables sustained experiments on physical robots and highlights the critical role of on-device adaptation to maintain performance under deployment variability.

Abstract: We built a robot called the Robotroller that actuates an Atari CX40+ controller and a device called the Atari Devbox that renders the game frame and the reward signal from the Arcade Learning Environment on a screen. The Robotroller and the Atari Devbox, together with an off-the-shelf camera and a desktop computer, constitute a system that can be used to study reinforcement learning algorithms in the physical world. We call the full system Physical Atari. In this paper, we detail the key decisions that make Physical Atari a robust and accessible platform. To make the system robust, we designed the Robotroller so that all movement is done through bearings, which reduces wear. Additionally, we wrote software that monitors the state of the servos at a high frequency and intervenes to limit stress. To make the system accessible, we used affordable off-the-shelf components and parts that can be manufactured using consumer 3D printers. Physical Atari can be built for under $1,000 and has been used for weeks of non-stop reinforcement learning experiments without any mechanical failures. We used it to validate that reinforcement learning algorithms can learn directly on robots and show that even small distribution shifts between learning and deployment can significantly degrade the performance of policies. Our results underscore the importance of on-device adaptation for strong performance on robots.

</details>


### [300] [WorkBenchMark: A LEGO-Based Assembly Benchmark with an Assembly-by-Disassembly Baseline for the Smart Manufacturing League](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19358)
*Wenbo Ma,Daniel Swoboda,Matteo Tschesche,Till Hofmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces WorkBenchMark, a LEGO Duplo-based robotic assembly benchmark with 400 tasks across four complexity tiers; shows planning-based pipeline outperforms a modern vision-language-action baseline; resources will be openly released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where end-to-end learning struggles to integrate low-level manipulation with high-level symbolic reasoning under physical constraints, and to provide a standardized, open benchmark for robotic assembly research.

Method: Develop LEGO Duplo-based tasks (400 tasks across four tiers); implement open-vocabulary perception; propose Assembly-by-Disassembly baseline; develop planning-based pipeline; compare against vision-language-action baseline; provide open-source simulation environment and baseline.

Result: The planning-based pipeline outperforms the vision-language-action baseline across all tiers, demonstrating robustness across task difficulty; 400 tasks covered; improved reliability under physical constraints.

Conclusion: The benchmark and resources will be released openly to support the robotic assembly community, highlighting the value of planning-based approaches with perceptual grounding over end-to-end methods.

Abstract: We introduceWorkBenchMark, a LEGO Duplo-based robotic assembly benchmark motivated by the RoboCup Smart Manufacturing League. Robotic assembly couples low-level manipulation with task-level symbolic reasoning under physical constraints, a combination that current end-to-end learning methods do not yet solve reliably. The benchmark provides 400 tasks across four complexity tiers. We provide an open-vocabulary perception, Assembly-by-Disassembly baseline solution. Our planning-based pipeline outperforms a modern vision-language-action approach across all tiers. The benchmark, simulation environment, and baseline implementation will be released openly to support the broader robotic assembly community.

</details>


### [301] [3D Scene Graphs: Open Challenges and Future Directions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19383)
*Dennis Rotondi,Francesco Argenziano,Sebastian Koch,Nathan Hughes,Martin Buechner,Johanna Wald,Lukas Rosenberger Schmid,Daniele Nardi,Abhinav Valada,Liam Paull,Federico Tombari,Luca Carlone,Kai O. Arras*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Unified review of 3D Scene Graphs (3DSGs): formalizes a common framework, analyzes modeling choices (nodes/edges, hierarchy, dynamics, affordances), surveys construction pipelines from sensory data, and evaluates downstream tasks; provides guidelines, open challenges, and a community resource website.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The 3DSG field is fragmented across communities with differing definitions, pipelines, and evaluation protocols, hindering method comparison and real-world deployment.

Method: Comprehensive survey that (1) formalizes 3DSGs under a common definition, (2) analyzes constituent modeling choices (nodes/edges, hierarchy, dynamic scenes, affordance extensions), (3) reviews construction pipelines from raw sensory data and standard terminologies, and (4) examines downstream applications and evaluation strategies, culminating in guidance and a dedicated community website.

Result: A unified formalization and taxonomy for 3DSGs, synthesis of existing formulations and pipelines, critical assessment of evaluation strategies, and a set of open challenges and recommendations, accompanied by a community resource site (https://3dscenegraphs.com/).

Conclusion: 3DSGs offer powerful, expressive scene representations but remain fragmented; standardization in definitions, representations, and evaluation is essential for robust real-world deployment, with emphasis on dynamic, affordance-aware, and cross-domain extensions and a centralized community resource.

Abstract: 3D Scene Graphs (3DSGs) have emerged as a powerful representation for spatial AI by combining geometric grounding with semantic and relational abstractions of the environment. Their expressiveness has made them relevant to a broad range of problems in robotics and computer vision, including manipulation, navigation, task planning, scene understanding, and many others. However, the field remains fragmented: different communities adopt distinct formulations, construction pipelines, and evaluation protocols, making it difficult to compare methods, identify common assumptions, and assess remaining challenges for robust real-world deployment. This survey provides a unified and critical review of 3DSGs, with particular emphasis on open challenges and future directions. We first formalize 3DSGs under a common definition and analyze the principal modeling choices that characterize existing formulations, including node and edge attributes, hierarchical structure, dynamic scene representations, and affordance-aware extensions. We then review how 3DSGs are built from raw sensory observations, discussing the most common terminologies, conventions, and techniques. Finally, we examine downstream applications and evaluation strategies, from intrinsic graph quality to task-level performance. To support the community, we also provide a dedicated website that organizes and extends the surveyed content, accessible at https://3dscenegraphs.com/.

</details>


### [302] [DiffusionVS: A Generative Framework for Robust Visual Servoing Based on Diffusion Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19397)
*Hongkang Cui,Rui He,Haoyao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-policy-based visual servoing method with online, interactive data collection improves robustness and generalization; achieves near-perfect sim performance and strong real-world results, and generalizes to other visual servoing networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address trajectory jitter and error accumulation in regression-based visual servoing due to noise sensitivity and distribution shifts; enhance temporal consistency and robustness through diffusion-based sequence prediction and data augmentation.

Method: Inputs are normalized image coordinates of observed tag corners; actions are camera velocities generated via conditional denoising in a diffusion-policy framework; online training expands data diversity through interactive data collection; diffusion-based module is shown to improve other visual servoing architectures.

Result: Simulation success rates approaching 100%; real-world experiments ~93% success; improved generalization and robustness; diffusion mechanism demonstrated as broadly applicable across different visual servoing systems.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based strategy enhances visual servoing by providing temporal-consistent action sequences, robust generalization, and broad applicability to existing networks when integrated with the diffusion module.

Abstract: Visual servoing is a fundamental technique in robotic manipulation and navigation. Regression-based visual servoing frequently experiences trajectory jitter as a result of noise-sensitive single-step mappings and the accumulation of errors during distribution shifts. In contrast, Diffusion Policy maintains temporal consistency by predicting action sequences and improves robustness through implicit data augmentation.
  This paper presents a novel diffusion-based servoing method. Based on Diffusion Policy, the proposed approach uses normalized image coordinates of observed tag corners as input and generates camera velocity through conditional denoising. To overcome the generalization limitations of models trained on static datasets, an online training paradigm is adopted, continuously expanding the diversity of training data through interactive experience collection. This strategy substantially enhances both the performance and generalization capability of the model. Comprehensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving success rates of nearly 100\% in simulation and 93\% in physical experiments. Beyond the specific pipeline, we further validate the generality of the diffusion mechanism. Experiments show that existing visual servoing networks consistently achieve improved performance when integrated with our diffusion-based module. These results indicate that the proposed strategy possesses broad applicability and can enhance various visual servoing systems beyond the specific architecture presented here.

</details>


### [303] [Playful Agentic Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19419)
*Junyi Zhang,Jiaxin Ge,Hanjun Yoo,Letian Fu,Zihan Yang,Yaowei Liu,Raj Saravanan,Shaofeng Yin,Justin Yu,Dantong Niu,Zirui Wang,Roei Herzig,Ken Goldberg,Yutong Bai,David M. Chan,Ion Stoica,Angjoo Kanazawa,Jiahui Lei,Haiwen Feng,Trevor Darrell*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Playful agentic robot learning (RATs) uses self-directed play to build a persistent code skill library, enabling reuse of skills for downstream tasks with improved performance and transfer, without additional finetuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current code-as-policy systems are task-driven and rely on explicit instructions for reusable skills; aim to make skill acquisition continual and autonomous via play.

Method: RATs generate exploratory tasks, plan/execute robot-code policies, verify progress, diagnose failures, retry with dense feedback, and distill successful executions into a persistent library; tests reuse skills by retrieving from the library into downstream inference contexts; evaluations on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces; cross-task transfer to RoboSuite and real-world without finetuning.
