Result: Significant gains on held-out downstream tasks (20.6 pp on LIBERO-PRO, 17.0 pp on MolmoSpaces) compared to CaP-Agent0; retrieval-based improvements of 8.9 and 8.8 points on RoboSuite and real-world transfer without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Play-based continual skill learning via RATs yields transferable, reusable code-policy skills that improve performance and transfer in robot-learning benchmarks; supports plug-in skill reuse across different Code-as-Policy agents.

Abstract: Current agentic robot systems can write executable Code-as-Policy programs, observe feedback, and revise behavior across multiple attempts, but they remain largely task-driven: reusable skills are acquired only after explicit instructions. We study Playful Agentic Robot Learning, where an embodied coding agent uses self-directed play as a continual skill-learning stage before downstream tasks arrive. We introduce RATs, Robotics Agent Teams designed for play-time skill acquisition. During play, RATs proposes novel yet learnable exploratory tasks, plans and executes robot-code policies, verifies intermediate progress, diagnoses failures, retries with dense, step-level feedback, and distills successful executions into a persistent code skill library. At test time, the agent reuses relevant skills from this frozen library to help solve new tasks. Experiments in LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces show that play-learned skills improve held-out downstream tasks over no-play and random-play baselines, with 20.6 and 17.0 percentage-point gains over CaP-Agent0 on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces, respectively. Moreover, the learned skills can be plugged into other inference-time Code-as-Policy agents by simply retrieving them into the context, improving RoboSuite and real-world transfer by 8.9 and 8.8 points, respectively, without finetuning the underlying model.

</details>


### [304] [Simulating Robotic Locomotion in Sand: Resistive Force Theory in an Open-Source Physics Engine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19504)
*Ryan Walker Brown,Laura K. Treers,Kathryn A. Daltorio*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: 3D Granular RFT integrated into MuJoCo enables sand locomotion simulation; achieves ~20% accuracy for hexapod walking distance and foot sinkage; provides open-source tool for design in granular media.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resistive force theory approximates ground reactions in sand but is not integrated in common 3D physics engines; there is a need for stable, efficient simulation to study legged locomotion on granular substrates.

Method: Implement 3D granular RFT in MuJoCo; validate across scenarios; analyze effects of end-effector shape, speed, and loading; compare simulated outcomes (walking distance and foot sinkage) to experimental data.

Result: The simulations predict walking distance and foot sinkage within 20% of experimental results for a 12-DOF hexapod; key trends with shape, speed, and loading are preserved; the tool is open source and can aid robot design for granular media.

Conclusion: 3D RFT in a standard physics engine provides a practical, approximate substrate for planning and evaluating legged robots on sand; despite inherent approximations, it enables rapid exploration and design improvements.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Resistive Force Theory (RFT) enable approximation of ground reaction forces for locomotion in sand without the computational expense of modeling interactions with individual grains. However, these tools have been absent in 3D physics engines commonly used for robot simulation. We explore if resistive force approximations are sufficient, when integrated with standard dynamics calculations, to provide a stable substrate for a freely walking robot. To determine this, we implement 3D Granular Resistive Force Theory (3D RFT) in a physics simulation engine, MuJoCo. We verify simulations in multiple scenarios to demonstrate that key trends due to end effector shape, speed, and loading are preserved. Our implementation predicts walking distance and foot sinkage of a 12-Degree of Freedom hexapod robot within 20\% of experiments in sand. While RFT has inherent approximations, the open source tool described here has potential to help develop new and improved robot designs to traverse granular media substrates.

</details>


### [305] [Proprioceptive Invariant State Estimation for Humanoid Robots on Non-Inertial Ground](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19512)
*Falak Mandali,Zijian He,Yan Gu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Invariant EKF-based proprioceptive state estimation for humanoids on non-inertial ground using stance-foot constraints; achieves fast convergence and accurate relative base pose with only foot-mounted IMUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimate base position and velocity relative to moving ground without external sensors, enabling robust operation on dynamic terrains using only onboard proprioception.

Method: InEKF with right-invariant measurement model, leveraging foot-mounted IMUs and kinematic constraints at the stance foot to handle ground-induced nonlinearities; full proprioception; observability analysis for relative pose/velocity on non-inertial ground.

Result: Significant improvements: 96% faster convergence and 80% reduction in position error compared to existing InEKFs; on uni-axially rotating ground walking achieves <9 cm error for initial error up to 1 m; experiments on Digit robot standing/squatting on swaying/pitching ground.

Conclusion: Demonstrates viability of proprioceptive InEKF for humanoids on dynamic ground; effective use of invariance properties and stance-foot constraints; potential impact on robust legged locomotion under ground motion.

Abstract: This paper presents an invariant extended Kalman filtering (InEKF) approach for real-time state estimation of humanoid robots operating on non-inertial ground using only onboard proprioceptive sensing. The proposed approach estimates the robot's base position and velocity relative to the moving ground frame without requiring direct measurements of ground motion or externally mounted sensors. By exploiting kinematic constraints at the stance foot through foot-mounted IMUs, the filter accounts for ground-induced nonlinearities in the process and measurement models while remaining fully proprioceptive. The estimator is formulated to admit a right-invariant measurement model, enabling favorable error dynamics under large initial uncertainties. Observability analysis establishes conditions under which the robot's relative base position and velocity are observable with respect to the non-inertial ground frame. Experiments with the Digit humanoid robot standing and squatting atop a swaying and pitching ground showcase a 96% speedup in convergence rate and an 80% reduction in position estimate errors over existing InEKFs. Walking experiments on a uni-axially rotating ground achieve an average estimation error of less than 9 cm for an initial error of up to 1 m.

</details>


### [306] [A Categorial and Sheaf-Theoretic Semantics for Autonomic Component Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19525)
*Manuel Hernández,Eduardo Sánchez-Soto*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A high-level proposal to model SCEL-based distributed systems as sheaves over a topological space, where components are points, ensembles are open sets, and distributed knowledge forms the data; information flow is 'gluing' of local data and failures are obstructions detected via sheaf cohomology, recasting verification as geometric analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of reasoning about global, emergent properties in large-scale, decentralized autonomous systems. SCEL’s current operational semantics are limited for structural analysis; a category-theoretic/sheaf-theoretic framework promises compositional, global insight.

Method: Develop a multi-layered mathematical model using category theory and sheaf theory. Model a society of SCEL-described robots as a sheaf on a topological space: components as points, ensembles as open sets, and distributed knowledge as the sheaf data. Interpret information sharing as a gluing operation; quantify failures as topological obstructions via sheaf cohomology; discuss implications for verification and system design.

Result: A theoretical framework proposing to recast verification and analysis of SCEL-like distributed systems as geometric/topological analysis. The work outlines a path toward structural insights and compositional reasoning, with potential theorems and examples to illustrate the mapping, though empirical validation and concrete case studies are not specified.

Conclusion: If formalized, the approach could provide a principled, geometry-based foundation for analyzing robustness and failure modes in autonomous systems. Challenges include specifying the appropriate topology and sheaf structure for SCEL constructs and ensuring tractability for large systems; further work is needed to demonstrate practical verification benefits.

Abstract: The proliferation of large-scale, decentralized systems of autonomous agents, such as swarms of robots and networked cyber-physical systems, presents a formidable challenge to traditional formal methods. The Software Component Ensemble Language (SCEL) offers a formal model for such systems, but its operational semantics is not ideal for reasoning about global, structural, and emergent properties. This report proposes a new, multi-layered mathematical model for SCEL using category theory and sheaf theory. We argue that a society of robots described in SCEL can be formally modeled as a sheaf on a topological space, where components are points, ensembles are open sets, and distributed knowledge forms the sheaf's data. In this framework, computational processes like information sharing become equivalent to the sheaf-theoretic operation of "gluing" local data. System failures can then be understood and quantified as topological obstructions, measurable by sheaf cohomology. This approach transforms the verification of a complex distributed system into the analysis of the geometry of a mathematical object, providing deep, structural insights for the design of robust autonomic systems.

</details>


### [307] [SCAN-Planner: Spatial Collision-Aware Local Planning for Route-Guided Long-Range Quadruped Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19555)
*Han Zheng,Zhe Chen,Yiwen Fu,Ming Yang,Tong Qin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SCAN-Planner: a spatial collision-aware local planner for quadrupeds using a yaw-aware twin-cylinder footprint, projected A* on a ground-following surface, and a robot-centric sliding map for safe, long-range navigation in cluttered 3D environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current local planning for quadrupeds often uses isotropic inflation or 2D maps, which leads to conservative motion and poor handling of overhanging obstacles in 3D scenes; there is a need for long-range, collision-aware planning that accounts for full body geometry and 3D structures.

Method: 1) Use a yaw-aware twin-cylinder footprint to model the elongated quadruped for whole-body collision checks in an inflated 3D occupancy map. 2) Apply a projected A* search on an interpolated ground-following surface with z-gradient suppression to avoid horizontal obstacles while maintaining vertical stability. 3) Employ a robot-centric sliding map with boundary fallback for high-resolution local collision checking and recovery from local dead ends.

Result: Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate safe, smooth, and efficient trajectories in dense clutter, 3D unstructured scenes, stair traversal, and long-range navigation tasks.

Conclusion: SCAN-Planner integrates geometry-aware collision checking, far-range planning, and robust local maps to enable reliable long-range quadruped navigation in challenging 3D environments.

Abstract: Quadruped robots are increasingly expected to navigate through narrow passages, cluttered indoor scenes, and large-scale 3D unstructured environments. Existing local planners commonly approximate the robot using isotropic geometric inflation or rely on planar and elevation-map representations, leading to conservative motion in tight spaces and limited reasoning about overhanging structures. This letter presents SCAN-Planner, a spatial collision-aware local planning framework for long-range quadruped navigation. A yaw-aware twin-cylinder footprint is used to model the elongated robot body, enabling whole-body collision evaluation through sparse queries in an inflated 3D occupancy map. We further introduce a projected A* search that generates collision-free guidance on an interpolated ground-following surface, with z-gradient suppression to avoid obstacles horizontally while maintaining vertical stability. For large-scale deployment, a robot-centric sliding map with boundary fallback provides high-resolution local collision checking and recovery from local dead ends. Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that SCAN-Planner generates safe, smooth, and efficient trajectories in dense clutter, 3D unstructured scenes, stair traversal, and long-range navigation tasks.

</details>


### [308] [pdSTL: Probabilistic Differentiable Signal Temporal Logic for Stochastic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19561)
*Bennett Dogbey,Hemanth Manjunatha*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: pdSTL unifies probabilistic semantics with differentiable robustness over belief trajectories, enabling end-to-end optimization under uncertainty; uses interval-valued semantics and LSTM-style unfolding to provide differentiable, linear-time monitoring; validated on obstacle avoidance, lane changes, and real quadcopter flights, outperforming deterministic differentiable STL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots in uncertain environments require specifications that are both probabilistic and differentiable for gradient-based optimization; existing STL extensions either lack differentiability or ignore belief-space uncertainty.

Method: Introduce interval-valued probabilistic semantics; propagate bounds through the STL syntax tree; formulate temporal robustness as a recurrent unfolding of STL operators (LSTM-style), enabling linear-time, differentiable monitoring suitable for end-to-end trajectory optimization.

Result: Simulations and real Crazyflie experiments under aerodynamic disturbances show pdSTL provides probabilistic guarantees and maintains larger safety margins, with more efficient optimization and better safety performance than deterministic differentiable STL.

Conclusion: pdSTL offers a principled differentiable framework for probabilistic STL on belief trajectories, enabling robust gradient-based planning under uncertainty; future work could extend richer stochastic models, scalability, and integration with planning pipelines.

Abstract: Autonomous robots operating in uncertain environments must satisfy complex temporal and safety specifications despite stochastic dynamics and sensing noise. While Signal Temporal Logic (STL) offers robustness measures for gradient-based optimization, existing extensions either lack differentiability or ignore belief-space uncertainty. We introduce pdSTL (probabilistic differentiable Signal Temporal Logic), a framework that unifies probabilistic semantics with differentiable robustness over belief trajectories. pdSTL employs interval-valued probabilistic semantics to compute conservative satisfaction bounds, propagated compositionally through the STL syntax tree. We formulate the temporal robustness evaluation as a recurrent, LSTM-style unfolding of STL operators, enabling linear-time, differentiable monitoring suitable for end-to-end trajectory optimization. We validate pdSTL on simulated obstacle avoidance, lane-change maneuvers, and real-world Crazyflie quadcopter flight experiments under aerodynamic disturbances. Results demonstrate that pdSTL achieves efficient optimization with formal probabilistic guarantees, significantly outperforming deterministic differentiable STL in maintaining safety margins under real-world uncertainty.

</details>


### [309] [One Demo is Worth a Thousand Trajectories: Action-View Augmentation for Visuomotor Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19586)
*Chuer Pan,Litian Liang,Dominik Bauer,Eric Cousineau,Benjamin Burchfiel,Siyuan Feng,Shuran Song*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A data augmentation framework for visuomotor policies using fisheye eye-in-hand demonstrations, Gaussian Splatting-based 3D scene editing for unseen objects, and trajectory optimization to generate collision-free, view-consistent trajectories and render novel views; improves success under distribution shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visuomotor policies are brittle to changes in initial configuration and unseen obstacles; existing data augmentation falls short of producing realistic, physically consistent observations and actions, limiting generalization without extensive data collection.

Method: 1) Collect real-world eye-in-hand demonstrations with a single fisheye camera. 2) Generate visually realistic fisheye image sequences and corresponding physically feasible action trajectories. 3) Introduce a Gaussian Splatting formulation adapted to wide FoV fisheye cameras to reconstruct and edit 3D scenes with unseen objects. 4) Use trajectory optimization to produce smooth, collision-free, view-rendering-friendly action trajectories. 5) Render visual observations from novel viewpoints. 6) Evaluate in simulation and real-world manipulation tasks, in both the original scene and augmented scene with obstacles.

Result: The augmentation framework improves success rates for various manipulation tasks in both the original scene and obstacle-augmented scenes, demonstrated in simulation and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: The approach yields robust visuomotor policies by enriching training data with realistic fisheye observations and physically feasible trajectories across novel views; Gaussian Splatting for wide-FOV fisheye cameras is a key contribution enabling effective 3D scene editing and rendering.

Abstract: Visuomotor policies for manipulation have demonstrated remarkable potential in modeling complex robotic behaviors, yet minor alterations in the robot's initial configuration and unseen obstacles easily lead to out-of-distribution observations. Without extensive data collection effort, these result in catastrophic execution failures. In this work, we introduce an effective data augmentation framework that generates visually realistic fisheye image sequences and corresponding physically feasible action trajectories from real-world eye-in-hand demonstrations, captured with a portable parallel gripper with a single fisheye camera. We introduce a novel Gaussian Splatting formulation, adapted to wide FoV fisheye cameras, to reconstruct and edit the 3D scene with unseen objects. We utilize trajectory optimization to generate smooth, collision-free, view-rendering-friendly action trajectories and render visual observations from corresponding novel views. Comprehensive experiments in simulation and the real world show that our augmentation framework improves the success rate for various manipulation tasks in both the same scene and the augmented scene with obstacles requiring collision avoidance.

</details>


### [310] [Safe, Real-Time Active Model Discrimination and Fault Diagnosis for Nonlinear Systems via Differentiable Reachability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19590)
*Xinpei Ni,Melkior Ornik,Glen Chou,Samuel Coogan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A real-time, safe active fault diagnosis framework for uncertain nonlinear systems using interval reachability and differentiable optimization to discriminate among multiple candidate models with safety guarantees, achieving sub-50 ms discrimination across high-dimensional robotic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for fast, robust fault diagnosis and model discrimination under disturbances while guaranteeing safety in real-time control of nonlinear systems.

Method: Output-feedback, time-varying policy optimization with a finite horizon safety constraint; uses interval over-approximations of reachable state/output sets; encodes diagnosability as a differentiable objective that penalizes overlap between reachable outputs of candidate models; online solution via gradient-based optimization using JAX and differentiable reachability primitives.

Result: Demonstrates reliable model discrimination in under 50 ms across up to 11 fault modes on diverse platforms (simulated quadrotor, fighter-jet model, differential-drive robot, quadrupedal navigation); outperforms baselines in discrimination success rate and speed; provides formal safety guarantees.

Conclusion: The approach delivers fast, scalable, and safe model discrimination for uncertain nonlinear systems in real time, with strong empirical performance across multiple high-dimensional robotic platforms.

Abstract: We present a safe, real-time algorithm for active fault diagnosis and model discrimination for uncertain continuous-time nonlinear systems with process and measurement disturbances. Given a finite set of candidate models representing nominal and faulty modes, including actuator and sensor faults, we formulate an output-feedback, time-varying policy optimization problem that (i) robustly enforces state-input safety constraints over a finite horizon and (ii) drives the system to produce sampled measurements consistent with at most one model, enabling deterministic diagnosis. To solve this problem in real time, we develop a tractable approximation using interval over-approximations of reachable state and output sets, and encode diagnosability via a differentiable objective that penalizes overlap between the reachable output sets of possible models. The resulting optimization is solved efficiently online with gradient-based methods using JAX and differentiable reachability primitives. We evaluate our method on sensor and actuator fault diagnosis (up to 11 fault modes) in several high-dimensional nonlinear robotic systems, including a simulated quadrotor and fighter-jet model, a hardware differential-drive robot, and quadrupedal navigation. Across these case studies, our approach achieves reliable model discrimination in under 50 ms, outperforming baselines in discrimination success rate and speed while providing formal safety guarantees.

</details>


### [311] [Fail-RAG : A Retrieval Augmented Generation Informed Framework for Robot Failure Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19598)
*Ameya Salvi,Jie Hu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Fail-RAG is a Retrieval Augmented Generation framework for detecting failures in warehouse robotics. It embeds and retrieves failure images and context against a failure database and uses Vision-Language Models to analyze failures via an instruction template. Evaluated on fixed arms and a mobile manipulator across five operation types, it improves failure detection accuracy by about 25 percentage points versus off-the-shelf VLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unforeseen/variable failures in dynamic warehouse environments hinder rule-based detection; robust, scalable detection requires leveraging multi-modal signals and retrieval-based reasoning.

Method: Construct Fail-RAG: embed failure imagery and contextual data, retrieve similar cases from a failure database via similarity search, and apply Vision-Language Models guided by an instruction template to analyze failures. Evaluate in both simulation and real hardware (fixed-arm and mobile manipulator) on multiple warehouse tasks; compare against off-the-shelf VLM baselines.

Result: Achieves ~25 percentage point higher failure detection accuracy on average across five robot operation types compared to using off-the-shelf VLMs; demonstrates effectiveness for real-world failure detection.

Conclusion: Fail-RAG provides a more robust failure-detection approach for autonomous warehouse robots by combining retrieval-augmented reasoning with vision-language analyses, showing strong potential for deployment in dynamic environments.

Abstract: Industry automation is witnessing an evolution in robotics driven by both technological breakthroughs and societal changes: progress towards generalist robots, embodied and physical artificial intelligence (AI), and increasing labor shortage in manufacturing.An intelligent autonomous robot needs to not only act according to planned motions but also react to any unexpected events. In this study, we focus on such unexpected events in warehouses where robots are used for material handling. Specifically, we refer to any unexpected events as failures and develop methods to detect robot operations related failures. Rule-based detection methods may break since the form of failures could change due to the dynamic nature of both environments and tasks. We propose 'Fail-RAG', a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)-based failure detection framework where failure images and context information are embedded and queried against a failure database by calculating their similarities. Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are further used to analyze failures and provide details by following our instruction template. We evaluated the performance of Fail-RAG by conducting both simulation and physical experiments using fixed robot arms and a mobile manipulator for multiple tasks that are common in warehouse automation. Fail-RAG achieved 25 percentage point higher failure detection accuracy on average across five types of robot operations compared to using off-the-shelf VLMs, indicating its effectiveness for real-world failure detection.

</details>


### [312] [Formal Verification of Learned Multi-Agent Communication Policies via Decision Tree Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19632)
*Ahmad Farooq,Kamran Iqbal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: End-to-end safety verification framework for learned multi-agent communication policies via distilling neural policies into interpretable decision trees, enabling formal verification with PRISM; validated on VQ-VIB policies for 5-7 drones; achieves high fidelity, strong safety guarantees, and faster verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety guarantees for MARL policies in safety-critical robotics, addressing the opacity of neural policies and the difficulty of applying formal methods to deep learning-based coordination.

Method: Four-stage pipeline: (1) domain-specific feature extraction from agent observations, (2) decision-tree distillation achieving 97.9% ± 1.2% fidelity to neural policies, (3) automated translation to PRISM probabilistic model checker specifications with full feature-to-state-variable correspondence, (4) compositional verification of PCTL properties using pairwise decomposition with union-bound aggregation and empirical neighbor modeling. Evaluation on Vector-Quantized Variational Information Bottleneck (VQ-VIB) policies for multi-drone coordination with 5–7 agents, verifying 18 temporal properties across safety, liveness, and cooperation; discrete VQ-VIB messages show fidelity advantages and faster verification (3–4x).

Result: Verified 18 temporal properties; 88.9% property satisfaction; all five safety thresholds met (collision probability 0.3% vs. 1% threshold). Monte Carlo validation shows verified safety properties transfer to original neural policies with <=0.6 percentage-point deviation (95% CI). Discrete VQ-VIB messages yield +11.6 to +13.6 percentage-point fidelity over continuous methods, enabling 3–4x faster verification.

Conclusion: The framework delivers empirically validated safety verification for distilled policy abstractions, providing a practical bridge between deep MARL and formal safety workflows for multi-robot deployment.

Abstract: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) enables agents to develop coordination strategies through emergent communication, but neural policies lack the formal safety guarantees required for safety-critical robotic deployment in drone swarms and autonomous vehicle fleets. We present the first end-to-end framework for safety verification of learned multi-agent communication policies through policy abstraction: neural policies are distilled into interpretable decision trees, then formally verified, with empirical validation confirming that verified safety properties transfer to original networks. Our four-stage pipeline consists of domain-specific feature extraction from agent observations, decision tree distillation achieving 97.9% +/- 1.2% fidelity to neural policies, automated translation to PRISM probabilistic model checker specifications with complete feature-to-state-variable correspondence, and compositional verification of Probabilistic Computation Tree Logic (PCTL) properties via pairwise decomposition with union-bound aggregation and empirical neighbor modeling. Evaluating Vector-Quantized Variational Information Bottleneck (VQ-VIB) policies for multi-drone coordination with 5-7 agents, we verify 18 temporal logic properties across safety, liveness, and cooperation, achieving 88.9% property satisfaction with all five safety thresholds satisfied (0.3% collision probability vs. 1% threshold). Monte Carlo validation of original neural policies confirms that verified safety properties transfer with <=0.6 percentage-point deviation (95% CI). Discrete VQ-VIB messages provide +11.6 to +13.6 percentage-point fidelity advantages over continuous methods, enabling 3-4x faster verification. Our framework provides empirically validated safety verification for distilled policy abstractions, serving as a practical bridge between deep MARL and formal safety workflows for multi-robot deployment.

</details>


### [313] [CTS-MoE: Implicit Terrain Adaptation via Mixture-of-Experts for Perceptive Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19633)
*Francisco Affonso,Matheus P. Angarola,Ana Luiza Mineiro,Aditya Potnis,Marcelo Becker,Girish Chowdhary*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CTS-MoE: a perception-gated dense mixture-of-experts actor with multi-critic heads for multi-task legged locomotion under discontinuous terrain; end-to-end concurrent training; perception-only routing at deployment; improves task specialization and reduces interference vs monolithic baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discontinuous terrain requires anticipatory maneuvers; multi-task RL faces a tension between sharing and separation. Without proper separation, policies may interfere via conflicting rewards; existing approaches trade off specialization vs generalization.

Method: Dense mixture-of-experts (MoE) actor with perception-based gating to compose shared behaviors; a multi-critic with task-specific value heads to prevent interference; single-stage concurrent teacher-student training that handles partial observability and avoids sequential distillation; task labels used only during training; deployment routing relies solely on perception (no high-level selector or terrain classifier).

Result: Experiments on a Unitree Go1 in simulation and hardware show task-aware specialization, with lower tracking error and higher success rates than monolithic baselines, across seen and unseen terrains.

Conclusion: CTS-MoE enables task-aware sharing with minimized interference, enabling terrain adaptation without extra selectors and with effective handling of partial observability; demonstrates practical gains in real-world locomotion tasks.

Abstract: Perceptive legged locomotion over discontinuous terrain (e.g., stairs, gaps, and obstacles) requires adaptive behavior, as a single conservative gait cannot produce the anticipatory maneuvers needed for abrupt topology changes. Cast as multi-task reinforcement learning, this problem introduces a tension between sharing and separation. Tasks use a common locomotion base but have conflicting rewards, so a policy must share behavior while avoiding value interference. Prior work addresses only one side, with monolithic policies sacrificing specialization and hierarchical sub-policies sacrificing generalization across transitions and unseen terrain. We propose CTS-MoE, which combines a dense mixture-of-experts actor with perception-based gating to compose shared behaviors and a multi-critic with task-specific value heads to prevent interference. The model is trained end-to-end in a single-stage concurrent teacher-student setup that handles partial observability and avoids sequential distillation, with task labels used only during training. At deployment, routing depends solely on perception, allowing terrain adaptation without a high-level selector or terrain classifier. Experiments on a Unitree Go1 in simulation and on hardware across seen and unseen terrains show task-aware specialization, with lower tracking error and higher success rates than monolithic baselines. Project Website: https://cts-moe.github.io/ .

</details>


### [314] [Scaling Self-Play for End-to-End Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19641)
*Luke Rowe,Roger Girgis,Rodrigue de Schaetzen,Daphne Cornelisse,Alaap Grandhi,Felix Heide,Eugene Vinitsky,Christopher Pal,Liam Paull*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Gigapixel enables scalable self-play for end-to-end driving from pixels; uses self-play DAgger with a privileged RL teacher and perception adaptation to real sensors; achieves competitive results on HUGSIM and NAVSIM-v2 and shows scalable gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end driving models trained on offline human demonstrations suffer from limited state coverage and lack of closed-loop feedback, making them brittle and prone to compounding errors; a scalable, pixel-level self-play paradigm is needed to improve robustness and data efficiency.

Method: Introduce Gigapixel, a high-throughput batched driving simulator with perspective rendering that renders a simplified bounding-box world at 50k agent steps per second. Train pixel-based policies in self-play using on-policy distillation from a privileged RL teacher (self-play DAgger). Bridge sim-to-real gap with lightweight perception adaptation to real-world sensor data.

Result: Policies trained in Gigapixel and adapted to real-world sensors achieve competitive performance on HUGSIM and NAVSIM-v2 benchmarks without human trajectory supervision. Scaling self-play yields proportional gains in policy performance, demonstrating practicality and scalability of self-play for end-to-end models.

Conclusion: Self-play is a practical and scalable strategy for training end-to-end autonomous driving policies, with positive sim-to-real transfer via perception adaptation.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving models are typically trained on offline human-demonstration datasets that provide limited state coverage and often no closed-loop feedback, making them prone to compounding errors when deployed in closed-loop and brittle to long-tail agent interactions. To overcome these limitations, we propose an alternative strategy for training end-to-end driving models: large-scale self-play directly from pixels in simulation. While prior self-play approaches have shown promising transfer to real-world driving, they typically assume vectorized Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) observations that are incompatible with end-to-end policies operating directly on sensor observations. To this end, we introduce Gigapixel, a high-throughput batched driving simulator with perspective rendering, enabling scalable self-play directly from pixel observations. Rather than targeting compute-costly photorealistic sensor simulation, Gigapixel renders a simplified bounding-box world that preserves essential scene structure while achieving throughput at 50k agent steps per second. Since direct pixel-space self-play RL is prohibitively sample-inefficient at end-to-end model scale, we propose self-play DAgger training: we train pixel-based policies in self-play via on-policy distillation from a privileged RL teacher. To bridge the sim-to-real gap, we subsequently transfer the self-play trained policies to real-world sensor data through lightweight perception adaptation. Policies trained in Gigapixel and adapted to real-world sensor data achieve competitive performance on the HUGSIM and NAVSIM-v2 benchmarks without human trajectory supervision. Moreover, scaling self-play training yields proportional gains in policy performance, establishing self-play as a practical and scalable strategy for training end-to-end models.

</details>


### [315] [DF-ExpEnse: Diffusion Filtered Exploration for Sample Efficient Finetuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19656)
*Calvin Luo,Chen Sun,Shuran Song*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DF-ExpEnse introduces an exploration-driven fine-tuning framework for pretrained generative control policies. It builds an expressive, tractable candidate set from multimodal models and uses an ensemble of critics to select actions that balance policy quality with exploration. In fleet settings, cross-agent communication enables coordinated exploration. It integrates with existing RL fine-tuning pipelines and shows consistent sample-efficiency gains across manipulation and locomotion tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve online data efficiency for fine-tuning pretrained generative control policies by promoting informative exploration. Leverage multimodal generative models to build diverse candidate actions and use ensemble critics to balance action quality with exploration drive. Extend to multi-agent fleets for collaborative exploration.

Method: Construct a multimodal candidate action set from the generative control policy. Use an ensemble of critics to evaluate actions based on a balance between expected return (quality) and exploration interest. Select the action that optimizes this balance. In fleet scenarios, enable cross-agent communication to coordinate exploration. Seamlessly integrate with existing policy fine-tuning pipelines that use RL.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows consistent improvements in sample-efficiency during fine-tuning across manipulation and locomotion tasks compared with naive fine-tuning and other action-selection schemes.

Conclusion: DF-ExpEnse provides a practical, plug-and-play exploration method for pretrained generative control policies that improves data efficiency and supports coordinated exploration in multi-agent robotics, with broad applicability to various manipulation and locomotion tasks.

Abstract: A natural recipe for intelligent robotic decision-making is initializing from pretrained generative control policies, which have summarized offline experience, and adapting them to self-collected online experience. We present DF-ExpEnse, an exploration technique that improves the quality of online experience collection, thus increasing finetuning sample-efficiency. DF-ExpEnse leverages the multimodal modeling capabilities of the generative control policy to create an expressive and tractably evaluatable candidate set. It then utilizes an ensemble of critics to identify the action that best balances quality with high exploration interest. In fleet settings, DF-ExpEnse further enables cross-agent communication to facilitate collaborative exploration as a group. DF-ExpEnse can be seamlessly integrated with existing strategies that finetune pretrained generative control policies via reinforcement learning. We experimentally validate consistent sample-efficiency benefits through DF-ExpEnse across a variety of manipulation and locomotion tasks, compared to default finetuning and alternative action selection schemes. Project can be found at https://df-expense.github.io.

</details>


### [316] [Safe Local Navigation for Ackermann-Steered Robots in Unmapped Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19672)
*Christian Schaible,Shahin Sirouspour*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Local safe-navigation framework for Ackermann-steered robots in unmapped environments: select the safest heading by maximizing open space, enforce obstacle-separating bounding lines via convex quadratic optimization, and track with a feedback-linearizing controller; claims faster computation and safer paths; open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable safe, efficient local navigation for mobile robots without a global map or goal, prioritizing obstacle clearance and computational speed.

Method: Detect local obstacles and choose the heading along the direction of the largest open space. Construct left/right bounding lines around the vehicle by solving a convex quadratic program that maximizes clearance to obstacles. Optionally impose constraints to keep lines parallel and smooth transitions from prior steps. Use a feedback-linearizing controller to regulate distance to the bounding lines, enabling tracking of a local reference path that preserves safety.

Result: Experimental results show safer navigation paths and significantly shorter computation times compared with some exploration-based planners; open-source code provided amid the implementation.

Conclusion: The framework improves safety and computational efficiency for local navigation of Ackermann vehicles in unknown environments by combining obstacle-aware heading selection, convex optimization for obstacle separation, and feedback-linearization-based path tracking.

Abstract: A control framework is proposed for safe local navigation of mobile robots equipped with Ackermann steering in unmapped environments where a global goal is absent. Based on local obstacle detections, the safest heading angle is determined along the direction of the largest open space ahead of the vehicle. Guided by this direction, bounding lines are constructed on the left and right sides of the vehicle to achieve obstacle separation. These bounding lines are obtained by solving a convex quadratic optimization that maximizes vehicle-to-obstacle clearance. Optionally, conditions are imposed on the bounding lines to preserve parallelism and smooth abrupt changes from prior control steps. A feedback-linearizing controller is then used to regulate the vehicle's distance from one or both bounding lines, effectively enabling tracking of a local reference path that preserves safety through obstacle clearance maximization. Open-source code is included for the application of this control scheme. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method produces safer navigation paths with significantly shorter computation times, compared to some existing exploration-based planners.

</details>


### [317] [ForEnt: A Multi-Modal Dataset for Characterizing Quadruped Robot Entrapments in Forest Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19675)
*Natapat Kirdwichai,Danesh Tarapore*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ForEnt introduces a multi-modal forest entrapment dataset for quadruped robots, enabling benchmarking and development of entrapment detection and robust locomotion in forest environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forest environments present unique entrapment risks for legged robots; there is a lack of dedicated datasets to study failure modes like vine entanglement, hindering robust autonomy and rapid benchmarking.

Method: Collect a multi-modal dataset (RGB-D, LiDAR, proprioception, third-person video) with the low-cost Unitree Go2 across 8 forest sites in Southampton Common Woodlands, UK, covering ~1.7 km in 11 sequences and recording 69 entrapment events; provide synchronized sensor streams and labeled entrapment events for reproducible benchmarking.

Result: The dataset records 69 entrapment events, enabling analysis of terrain factors contributing to entrapment and benchmarking of entrapment detection strategies across multi-modal data; first dedicated resource for forest entrapment in quadrupeds, facilitating method evaluation.

Conclusion: ForEnt lowers the barrier to robust quadruped forest deployments by providing a labeled, multi-modal dataset for entrapment analysis and detection benchmarking, supporting future research in failure-aware locomotion and recovery strategies.

Abstract: Legged robots are increasingly deployed in forests for ecological surveying and monitoring, yet their autonomy is often interrupted consequent to the challenges posed in traversing forest environments. Forest entrapments, for example, when a robot's legs are ensnared in vines or other vegetation, result in loss of stability and toppling. Such events not only disrupt the mission and require manual intervention, but also risk damage to the robot hardware. To address the absence of a dedicated dataset to investigate these failure modes in forest environments, we present ForEnt, a multi-modal dataset collected with the low-cost Unitree Go2 quadruped across eight forest sites in the Southampton Common Woodlands, UK. For our dataset, over approximately 1.7 km of traversals in 11 sequences were conducted, yielding 69 recorded entrapment events. ForEnt includes time-synchronized RGB-D images, LiDAR scans, proprioceptive data, and third-person video, enabling analysis of terrain factors contributing to entrapment and providing labeled sensor streams for reproducible benchmarking. By supporting the evaluation of entrapment detection strategies, ForEnt lowers the barrier to developing robust quadruped robot deployments in challenging forest environments.

</details>


### [318] [Route-Constrained Robust Fusion Estimation for MEMS/GNSS Integrated Navigation of Unmanned Ground Vehicles in GNSS Degraded Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19687)
*Jingzhi Cui,Chao Zhang,Yuliang Mao,Shaolin Lü,Dongmei Li,Huan Che,Rong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A route-constrained EKF approach for GNSS-denied localization of unmanned ground vehicles using map-guided alignment of dead reckoning with road segments to generate pseudo-position observations; includes practical controls and shows improved drift suppression in tunnel scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cumulative localization drift during GNSS outages in structured road environments undermines mission reliability; integrating road-level constraints via a map-aware state estimator can maintain continuity and accuracy.

Method: During GNSS outage, align historical dead reckoning (DR) trajectory with local segments extracted from a high-definition map using a 2D rigid transformation to obtain a route-referenced position. Treat this as a pseudo-position observation and feed it into an Extended Kalman Filter update. Introduce route-level constraints within a unified estimation framework. Additional engineering strategies: trigger control, matching quality validation, route offset compensation, and single update correction limiting.

Result: Experiments in long tunnel, multi-segment tunnel, and curved tunnel show effective suppression of DR drift, reduced risk of large deviations, and improved localization continuity and road-level usability.

Conclusion: The proposed route-constrained state estimation framework successfully mitigates localization drift during GNSS outages and enhances road-level localization robustness; practical strategies improve real-world applicability.

Abstract: To address cumulative localization drift of unmanned ground vehicles in structured road environments under severe Global Navigation Satellite System signal occlusion, this paper proposes a robust route-constrained state estimation method. During periods without satellite signals, the proposed method establishes the correspondence between the historical dead reckoning trajectory and local segments of the mission route extracted from a high-definition map, and estimates a route-referenced position via a two-dimensional rigid transformation. The estimated position is then formulated as a pseudo-position observation and incorporated into an Extended Kalman Filter update. In this way, route constraints at the road level can be continuously injected into a unified state estimation framework, thereby suppressing position deviation relative to the mission route while indirectly improving azimuth estimation. To enhance practical applicability, engineering strategies, such as trigger control, matching quality validation, route offset compensation, and single update correction limiting, are further introduced. Experiments in three representative scenarios, including a long tunnel, a multi-segment tunnel, and a curved tunnel, show that the proposed method effectively suppresses error accumulation during satellite outages, reduces the risk of large maximum deviation, and improves localization continuity and road-level usability.

</details>


### [319] [Comparative Study on Agility, Efficiency, and Impact Absorption of Bipedal Robots with Active Toes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19699)
*Joong-Gil Kim,Wontae Ye,Geunwoo Cho,Seong-Ho Yun,Se-Hyoung Cho,Yong-Jae Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Active toes in a 14-DOF biped robot improve efficiency and agility in simulation; toe-equipped robot shows 17.5% CoT reduction, 5% lower heel-strike GRF, and significant agility gains at 1.33 m/s.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify benefits of human-like toes in robots through high-fidelity simulation and fair RL training to determine their impact on agility, efficiency, and impact absorption.

Method: 14-DOF biped robot with active toes; high-fidelity simulation with coupled transmissions and accurate power modeling; minimal RL reward; identical training for toe-on and toe-off; metrics: CoT, GRF, path deviation.

Result: CoT reduced by 17.5%; heel-strike GRF reduced by 5%; agility: average path deviation -25%, max path deviation -34% at 1.33 m/s.

Conclusion: Active toes provide measurable improvements; results support integrating toes for efficiency and agility gains; methodology ensures fair comparison.

Abstract: Human legs exhibit high efficiency, agility, and impact absorption, with toes playing a crucial role in these capabilities. While many attempts have been made to implement human-like toes in robots, they have not fully replicated human characteristics nor rigorously validated their benefits. We propose a 14-DOF biped robot emulating human toes' lightweight, high-torque, robust nature. To quantitatively analyze the effectiveness of the active toes in terms of agility, efficiency, and impact absorption, we developed a high-fidelity simulation training environment that reflects actual actuators with coupled transmissions and accurate power consumption. To ensure a fair comparison between configurations with and without active toes, we designed a minimal RL reward function and applied an identical training procedure to both. The simulation results indicate that, at 1.33 m/s walking, the toe-equipped robot reduced CoT by 17.5% and heel-strike GRF by 5.0% compared with the toe-ablation configuration. On the agility test, average and maximum path deviation decreased by 25.0% and 34.0%, respectively.

</details>


### [320] [A Differentiable Composite Approximation Framework for Autonomous Underwater Vehicle Maneuvering Modeling from Sea-Trial Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19711)
*Aobo Wang,Aifei Xia,Zihao Wang,Lizhu Hao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A differentiable composite-approximation framework combines polynomial-basis and data-adaptive bases for AUV maneuvering; gradient-based co-calibration with current compensation improves field-data predictions vs traditional baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between polynomial-based and data-driven models by jointly learning a differentiable predictor that blends predefined and adaptive basis functions, while accounting for ocean-current effects in field data.

Method: A differentiable predictor that couples a polynomial-basis component with a data-adaptive component; gradient-based co-calibration with a sensitivity-aware mechanism to bound polynomial updates and a neural residual to capture remaining nonlinearities; current estimation/compensation to produce current-free targets for training and rollout.

Result: On sea-trial data from a 7-meter AUV under multiple maneuvers, the method yields better recursive trajectory and velocity predictions than polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior hybrid baselines.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of field-data-based AUV maneuvering modeling using a differentiable composite approach and current compensation, with practical applicability to onboard autonomous navigation.

Abstract: Field-based modeling from onboard measurements can produce autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) maneuvering models that reflect real operating characteristics. From an approximation perspective, conventional maneuvering models use predefined constraint polynomial bases, whereas data-driven models use data-adaptive bases. Motivated by this basis-function view, this paper presents a differentiable composite-approximation formulation, in which the polynomial-basis component and the data-adaptive basis component are treated as differentiable parts of a single predictor and calibrated jointly. A gradient-based co-calibration method is developed for full-scale AUV maneuvering prediction, where a sensitivity-aware mechanism regulates bounded polynomial updates while the neural residual captures remaining nonlinear discrepancies under a shared prediction objective. To account for ocean-current effects in field data, a turning-motion-based current estimation and compensation procedure is incorporated to construct current-compensated learning targets for training and rollout. The framework is evaluated using sea-trial data collected from a 7-meter AUV under multiple maneuvering conditions. Results show that the proposed method improves recursive trajectory and velocity prediction compared with polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior hybrid baselines, demonstrating its applicability to field-data-based AUV maneuvering modeling.

</details>


### [321] [Bidirectional Tutoring for Developmental Motor Learning in Robots: Co-Developed Interaction Dynamics Support Stable Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19728)
*Rui Fukushima,Jun Tani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Bidirectional tutoring with a humanoid robot using a free-energy principle (FEP) neural network with generative replay leads to more consistent, staged generalization in motor tasks, outperforming unidirectional tutoring and requiring less tutor guidance over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether bidirectional, socially grounded tutoring constrains robot motor learning and enhances coherence and generalization, leveraging the robot's prior experiences as priors in co-developed trajectories.

Method: Two experiments with a physical humanoid robot performing object manipulation: (1) human-robot bidirectional interaction and (2) an AI tutor with an adaptive intervention mechanism to control tutoring. The learning framework is a developmental, FEP-based neural network extended with generative replay, enabling stable sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes.

Result: Across both settings, bidirectional tutoring produced consistent behaviors and stage-wise generalization; the robot progressively required less tutor guidance, indicating that prior experiences constrained learning and aided generalization.

Conclusion: Bidirectional tutoring, as an embodied and socially grounded approach, provides an effective scaffold for developmental motor learning in robots, promoting coherence and progressive autonomy.

Abstract: Infants are well known to develop their motor skills through dense interaction with caregivers. Although such social interaction is crucial for human development, motor-skill learning in robots is often treated as a unidirectional process in which robots passively receive demonstrations from tutors. This overlooks a key property of social interaction: it is inherently bidirectional, with tutor and learner dynamically adapting to each other. In such interactions, the robot's past experiences may function as prior constraints that shape the dynamics of their co-developed trajectories. We hypothesize that bidirectional tutoring allows such constraints to guide the formation of consistent behavioral patterns that preserve behavioral coherence and support generalization, whereas unidirectional interaction lacks such constraints and leads to broader, less consistent behavioral patterns. To examine this hypothesis, we conducted two experiments with a physical humanoid robot performing an object manipulation task: one involving human-robot interaction and another employing an AI tutor interacting with the real robot through an adaptive intervention mechanism designed to examine whether similar effects would emerge under more controlled conditions. We implement the developmental learning framework using a free-energy-principle-based neural network extended with generative replay, which supports stable sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes. Across both settings, bidirectional tutoring fostered consistent behaviors and stage-wise generalization, while the robot gradually required less tutor guidance. These results suggest that bidirectional tutoring, as an embodied and socially grounded approach, provides an effective scaffold for developmental motor learning in robots.

</details>


### [322] [VOiLA: Vectorized Online Planning with Learned Diffusion Model for POMDP Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19729)
*Marcus Hoerger,Rishikesh Joshi,Rahul Shome,Ian Manchester,Hanna Kurniawati*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VOiLA learns task-agnostic POMDP models via conditional diffusion for online planning, then distills diffusion samplers into compact feedforward generators and integrates them with GPU-accelerated VOPP. It yields large sampling-cost reductions (≈1e3×) and matches or surpasses R-Critic methods using <10% of training data, with strong generalization and successful sim-to-real execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planning under uncertainty with POMDPs is powerful but hampered by the difficulty of obtaining faithful models for real-world problems; there is a need for data-efficient, real-time capable generative POMDP models that can be leveraged by online planners.

Method: Learn transition and observation samplers using conditional diffusion models; learn observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates; distill diffusion samplers into compact feedforward generators; integrate with Vectorized Online POMDP Planner (VOPP) to exploit GPU parallelization for online planning.

Result: Sampling cost reduced by up to nearly three orders of magnitude; VOiLA achieves equal or better performance than Recurrent Soft Actor Critic with <10% training data and exhibits superior generalization to unseen configurations; physical robot experiments show success in 10/10 runs using models learned from simulated data.

Conclusion: VOiLA demonstrates practical, data-efficient learned generative POMDP models enabling real-time online planning for robotics through diffusion-based samplers and their distillation, with strong sim-to-real transfer and robust performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: Planning under uncertainty is an essential capability for autonomous robots. The Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) provides a powerful framework for such a capability. Although POMDP-based planning has advanced significantly, its application to real-world problems is often limited by the difficulty of obtaining faithful POMDP models. We present Vectorized Online planning wIth Learned diffusion model for POMDP Agents (VOiLA), a framework that learns task-agnostic POMDP models for online planning under uncertainty. VOiLA learns transition and observation samplers using conditional diffusion models and learns observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates. To enable efficient online planning, the diffusion samplers are distilled into compact feedforward generators and integrated with Vectorized Online POMDP Planner (VOPP), an online POMDP planner designed to leverage GPU parallelization. Experimental results indicate the distillation strategy reduces sampling cost by up to nearly three orders of magnitude, making learned generative POMDP models practical for online planning. Evaluation of VOiLA on three benchmark problems indicate that VOiLA achieves equal or better performance than Recurrent Soft Actor Critic while using less than 10% training data, and generalizes much better to unseen environment configurations. Physical robot evaluation indicates VOiLA uses the models learned using only simulated data and generates a policy that successfully accomplish the task in 10 of 10 runs.

</details>


### [323] [Temporal Self-Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19752)
*Yinsen Jia,Boyuan Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TSIL uses temporally efficient trajectories discovered during learning as self-supervision to improve long-horizon manipulation; achieves better efficiency and robustness without heavy reward shaping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward shaping with dense rewards can cause agents to exploit shortcuts; rare efficient behaviors may be forgotten. Temporal efficiency offers a scalable, autonomous supervision signal for RL.

Method: Mine fast, successful trajectories during learning and convert them into reusable supervision. Use configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets derived from those trajectories and apply efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning to preserve and replay efficient behaviors.

Result: Evaluated on 15 long-horizon manipulation tasks; TSIL improves learning efficiency, task-completion efficiency, revisitation of fast strategies, and robustness to unstable training.

Conclusion: The temporal structure of successful behavior provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for RL beyond manual reward shaping, suggesting broader applicability.

Abstract: Long-horizon robot manipulation policies trained with reward shaping can still exploit dense rewards through inefficient interaction, while rare efficient behaviors may be forgotten during training. We argue that temporal efficiency itself provides a powerful and underutilized source of self-supervision for reinforcement learning. We introduce Temporal Self-Imitation Learning (TSIL), a reinforcement learning framework that mines temporally efficient successful trajectories generated during learning and converts them into reusable supervision for future policy improvement. TSIL progressively refines learning using configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets derived from fast successful trajectories, while preserving and replaying efficient behaviors through efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning. Across 15 distinct long-horizon manipulation tasks, TSIL consistently improves learning efficiency, task-completion efficiency, revisitation of fast successful behaviors, and robustness to unstable training conditions. More broadly, our results suggest that the temporal structure of successful behavior itself provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for reinforcement learning beyond manually engineered reward shaping alone.

</details>


### [324] [Data Standards for Humanoid Robotics: The Missing Infrastructure for Physical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19769)
*Shaoshan Liu,Xiugong Qin,Xuan Wu,Xuan Xia,Ning Ding,Jialu Liu,Jie Tang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes ISO-based data standards as foundational infrastructure for physical AI in humanoid robotics, emphasizing embodied, coherent, and shareable data with lifecycle metadata and domain-specific grammars.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses data non-cumulative issues, data silos, and high collection costs; argues that embodied interaction data must be interpretable, shareable, and reusable as AI moves into bodies.

Method: Synthesis anchored in the development of ISO/WD 26264-1 (Humanoid robot datasets—Part 1: General requirements) within ISO/TC 299/WG 16; argues for a general standard plus capability-specific parts outlining domain grammar and lifecycle infrastructure.

Result: Three insights: (1) Humanoid robot data are embodied interaction data with relational context among body, action, task, scene, trace, and outcome; (2) Value depends on physical coherence; multimodal streams must have inspectable timing, frames, calibration, kinematics, units, and synchronization; (3) Bottlenecks are data scarcity and non-cumulative data due to costs, silos, and inconsistent evaluation.

Conclusion: Standards should provide horizontal infrastructure for lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, and traceability, while capability-specific parts define domain grammar for manipulation, locomotion, HRI, cognition, and future humanoid capabilities; data standards must evolve to structure physical interaction as AI moves off screens into bodies.

Abstract: The scalability of humanoid robots will depend not only on models and hardware, but also on whether physical experience can accumulate across robots, tasks, organizations, and time. Drawing on the authors' work in developing ISO/WD 26264-1, Humanoid robot datasets -- Part 1: General requirements, within ISO/TC 299/WG 16, this article argues that data standards are becoming foundational infrastructure for Physical AI. We develop three insights. First, humanoid robot data is embodied interaction data, not a collection of isolated digital samples; a useful dataset must preserve the relationship among robot body, action, task, scene, execution trace, and outcome. Second, its value depends on physical coherence: multimodal streams are reusable only when timing, coordinate frames, calibration, kinematics, units, and synchronization assumptions remain inspectable. Third, the main bottleneck is not only data scarcity, but non-cumulative data caused by high collection costs, data silos, and inconsistent evaluation. We argue that humanoid robot data standards address these bottlenecks by making embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable. A general standard should provide horizontal infrastructure for lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, and traceability, while capability-specific parts should define domain grammar for manipulation, locomotion, human-robot interaction, cognition, and future humanoid capabilities. As AI moves from screens into bodies, data standards must evolve from organizing digital information to structuring physical interaction.

</details>


### [325] [Start Right, Arrive Right: Asynchronous Execution via Initial Noise Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19774)
*Trong-Bao Ho,Quang-Tan Nguyen,Thien-Loc Ha,Gia-Binh Nguyen,Viet-Thanh Nguyen,Long Dinh,Minh N. Vu,Duy M. H. Nguyen,An Thai Le,Ngo Anh Vien*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PAINT is a training-free method for asynchronous inference in action chunking for flow-based policies. It selects an appropriate initial noise (via backward Euler inversion) to ensure prefix-consistent next action chunks, avoiding controller retraining or trajectory steering. A repainting rule constructs the final chunk. Demonstrated to improve execution consistency and task performance across 12 simulated benchmarks and 6 real-world manipulation tasks across various embodiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latency in multi-step flow-based action generation during asynchronous execution causes inconsistencies at chunk boundaries. Prior work uses trajectory steering to align with the executed prefix; this work reframes the problem as selecting an initial noise to achieve prefix consistency.

Method: Backward Euler inversion is used to find the initial noise that yields a coherent next chunk when the unmodified flow ODE runs. A repainting rule constructs the final chunk from the generated trajectory. The approach is training-free, requires no gradients, retraining, or policy modification.

Result: PAINT improves execution consistency and task performance across 12 simulated benchmarks and 6 real-world manipulation tasks, covering single-arm, bimanual, and humanoid embodiments.

Conclusion: Asynchronous inference for flow-based action chunks can be effectively reframed as a noise-selection problem. PAINT provides a simple, training-free solution that yields coherent, high-quality action chunks without modifying the policy or requiring gradients.

Abstract: Action chunking enables robot policies to produce temporally coherent behavior, but generating multi-step action sequences with flow-based policies incurs latency that is incompatible with real-time control. Under asynchronous execution, the robot continues executing the current chunk while the next one is generated, causing even minor delays to create inconsistencies at chunk boundaries. Existing methods address this problem by steering generation toward the already executed action prefix. We instead show that prefix consistency can be achieved by selecting an appropriate initial noise before generation begins, allowing the unmodified flow ODE to produce a coherent next chunk. This reframes asynchronous inference as a noise selection problem rather than a trajectory steering problem. We introduce \textbf{PAINT}, a training-free method that finds this noise via backward Euler inversion and constructs the final chunk through a repainting rule. In summary, \texttt{PAINT} requires no gradients, retraining, or policy modification; yet it improves execution consistency and task performance across \textit{12 simulated benchmarks} and \textit{6 real-world manipulation tasks} spanning single-arm, bimanual, and humanoid embodiments. Website: ~\href{https://paint-action-chunking.github.io}{\texttt{https://paint-action-chunking.github.io}}.

</details>


### [326] [EquiVLA: A General Framework for Rotationally Equivariant Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19784)
*Thien-Loc Ha,Quang-Tan Nguyen,Trong-Bao Ho,Long Dinh,Minh Duc Nguyen,Gia-Binh Nguyen,Pham Tri Quang,Minh N. Vu,Duy M. H. Nguyen,An Thai Le,Ngo Anh Vien*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EquiVLA introduces a general SO(2)-equivariant Vision-Language-Action framework for robot manipulation, combining EquiPerceptor and EquiActor to achieve end-to-end equivariance from observations to action sequences; it yields notable gains across sim and real tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA policies lack geometric inductive biases, leading to high data requirements for generalizing across in-plane rotations. Injecting SO(2) equivariance aims to improve data efficiency and generalization in manipulation tasks.

Method: EquiVLA builds an end-to-end SO(2)‑equivariant VLA model by (i) EquiPerceptor that produces approximately SO(2)‑equivariant visual representations from frozen ViT features, and (ii) EquiActor, an exactly SO(2)‑equivariant flow‑matching Diffusion Transformer action head. The framework is architecture‑agnostic with a frozen vision‑language backbone and a diffusion transformer for action. Instantiated on GR00T N1.5 and evaluated on multiple LIBERO suites, CALVIN ABCD→D, and five real‑robot tasks on Mobile ALOHA.

Result: On LIBERO, 92.6% average success (vs. 78.1% baseline); CALVIN average sequence length 4.03 (vs. 3.45); real-robot success improved from 54% to 72%.

Conclusion: EquiVLA establishes an approximate SO(2) equivariance chain from camera observations to action sequences, yielding significant improvements in both simulation and real-robot manipulation, and highlighting the benefits of geometric inductive biases in VLA models.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for generalist robot manipulation, yet they lack geometric inductive biases: policies trained at specific orientations require substantially more data to generalize across rotational configurations. We present \textsc{EquiVLA}, the first general framework for end-to-end $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant VLA models, applicable to any architecture coupling a frozen vision-language backbone with a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. \textsc{EquiVLA} introduces \textsc{EquiPerceptor}, which produces approximately $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant visual representations from frozen ViT features; and \textsc{EquiActor}, an exactly $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. Together, they establish an approximate $\mathrm{SO}(2)$ equivariance chain from camera observations to predicted action sequences. Instantiated on GR00T~N1.5 and evaluated across four LIBERO suites, CALVIN ABCD$\to$D, and five real-robot tasks on Mobile ALOHA, \textsc{EquiVLA} achieves $92.6\%$ average success on LIBERO (vs. $78.1\%$ baseline), an average sequence length of $4.03$ on CALVIN (vs. $3.45$), and improves real-robot success from $54\%$ to $72\%$.

</details>


### [327] [TIDY: Thermal Infrared Image Denoising via Wavelet Domain Entropy and Directional Stripe Index](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19813)
*Tai Hyoung Rhee,Dong-Guw Lee,Ayoung Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TIDY is a lightweight wavelet-domain denoiser for thermal infrared (TIR) imagery trained on real clean-noisy data, addressing stochastic and fixed-pattern noise for indoor robustness and online robotics deployment while preserving structure and speed (~34 Hz).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor TIR data suffer from severe stochastic/fixed-pattern noise and low thermal contrast, hindering estimation tasks. Existing denoisers trade accuracy for speed and are often trained on synthetic noise, limiting robustness for robotics deployments.

Method: Formulate denoising in the wavelet domain to explicitly separate noise from structure. Train a lightweight model on real clean-noisy TIR pairs. Introduce two loss terms—Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index—to suppress stochastic noise and stripe artifacts. Achieve fast inference (~34 Hz) with reduced spatial complexity.

Result: TIDY yields improved robustness under severe indoor corruption and zero-shot scenarios, with consistent gains in downstream robotics tasks such as thermal inertial odometry and monocular depth estimation."

Conclusion: A fast, robust TIR denoiser for indoor robotics that leverages wavelet-domain disentanglement and novel wavelet-based losses. Provides practical online deployment capability and access to code/dataset.

Abstract: Thermal infrared (TIR) imaging has been a popular choice for field robotics due to its robust perception capability under low light visual degradation, but it suffers from severe stochastic and fixed-pattern noise that breaks downstream estimation. This noise is intensified indoors due to low thermal contrast and uniform temperature distributions, contributing to the relative lack of indoor TIR deployments. Existing TIR denoising methods exhibit a poor accuracy-efficiency tradeoff, either too slow for online deployment required in robotics or insufficiently robust to severe degradation, while typically being trained on synthetic noise. Addressing these problems, we propose TIDY, a lightweight wavelet-domain denoiser trained on real clean-noisy TIR data. By reformulating TIR denoising in the wavelet domain, TIDY explicitly disentangles noise from structural content, enabling targeted suppression with reduced spatial complexity, significantly improving inference speed over prior methods (~34Hz). TIDY introduces two new metrics, Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index, as complementary loss terms to explicitly suppress stochastic noise and stripe artifacts. Across severe indoor corruption and zero-shot settings, TIDY improves robustness and yields consistent gains in downstream robotics tasks including thermal inertial odometry and monocular depth estimation. Code and dataset is available at: https://github.com/williamrheeth/TIDY

</details>


### [328] [World Engine: Towards the Era of Post-Training for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19836)
*Tianyu Li,Li Chen,Caojun Wang,Haochen Liu,Kashyap Chitta,Zhenjie Yang,Yuhang Lu,Naisheng Ye,Yihang Qiu,Yufei Wang,Luoxi Zou,Jiaxin Peng,Jin Pan,Zhaoyu Su,Andrei Bursuc,Shengbo Eben Li,Andreas Geiger,Peng Su,Hongyang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A post-training approach for autonomous driving using World Engine to synthesize high-stakes interactions from real logs, enabling reinforcement-based fine-tuning that aligns policies with safety constraints, achieving substantial safety improvements beyond data scaling alone; released code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world driving datasets lack sufficient safety-critical, long-tail events, limiting reliability of end-to-end policies. A scalable method is needed to learn from rare interactions without real-world risk.

Method: World Engine reconstructs high-fidelity interactive environments from logs and extrapolates them into realistic safety-critical variations. These synthetic interactions are used for post-training reinforcement learning to enforce safety constraints on driving policies.

Result: On nuPlan-based benchmarks, the approach substantially reduces failures in rare safety-critical scenarios and yields larger gains than merely increasing pre-training data. In production-scale deployment, it reduces simulated collisions and improves on-road testing performance.

Conclusion: Post-training on synthesized safety-critical interactions provides a scalable and effective pathway to safer autonomous driving, complementing and surpassing data scaling alone; codebase and training pipeline are publicly released.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles must operate safely in the real world, where errors can have severe consequences. Although modern end-to-end driving policies excel in routine scenarios, their reliability is limited by the scarcity of safety-critical ``long-tail'' events in real driving datasets. These rare interactions define the practical safety boundary of the learned policy, yet they are difficult to collect at scale in the real world. Here we show that this fundamental limitation can be addressed by post-training pre-trained driving models on synthesized high-stakes interactions. We introduce World Engine, a generative framework that reconstructs high-fidelity interactive environments from real-world logs and systematically extrapolates them into realistic safety-critical variations. This paradigm enables reinforcement-based post-training to align policies with safety constraints, circumventing the physical risks inherent in real-world exploration. On a public benchmark built on nuPlan, World Engine substantially reduces failures in rare safety-critical scenarios and yields significantly larger gains than scaling pre-training data alone. Furthermore, when deployed on a production-scale autonomous driving system, the resulting policy reduces simulated collisions and demonstrates measurable improvements in on-road testing, showing that post-training on synthesized, safety-critical interactions offers a scalable and effective pathway to safer autonomous driving. The full codebase suite, including training, is released to the public.

</details>


### [329] [MMD-SLAM: Structure-Enhanced Multi-Meta Gaussian Distribution-Guided Visual SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19874)
*Fan Zhu,Ziyu Chen,Peichen Liu,Yifan Zhao,Zhisong Xu,Hui Zhu,Hongxing Zhou,Sixun Liu,Chunmao Jiang*
