Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MMD-SLAM introduces structure-enhanced Visual SLAM using Atlanta World priors and a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation, augmented by point-line fusion and a Gaussian evolution strategy, achieving state-of-the-art tracking and mapping results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D Gaussian Splatting SLAM methods underutilize structural information, leading to poorer rendering quality and inconsistent maps; leveraging Atlanta World priors provides robust geometric cues for improved mapping.

Method: A three-component approach: (1) point-line fusion for pose optimization with 3D line segments to strengthen constraints; (2) Multi-Meta Gaussian representation with dominant directions encoding AW priors; (3) Gaussian evolution strategy adapting to scene geometry for global optimization that uses structural cues.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art performance in tracking accuracy and mapping quality; 48.56% reduction in ATE RMSE on ScanNet; 5.71% PSNR improvement on Replica vs MonoGS.

Conclusion: Integrating AW-based structure priors and line-based constraints into a Gaussian-based SLAM framework significantly improves robustness and photorealistic mapping, validating the usefulness of structural priors in SLAM.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has significantly boosted novel view synthesis and high-fidelity scene reconstruction, expanding the potential of 3DGS-based Visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) methods. However, most existing systems fail to fully exploit the underlying structural information, which limits rendering quality and often leads to inconsistent maps. To address these limitations, we propose MMD-SLAM, a structure-enhanced Visual SLAM framework that leverages the Atlanta World (AW) assumption to guide a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation for photorealistic mapping. First, we introduce a point-line fusion strategy for pose optimization, where 3D line segments are incorporated to improve tracking robustness and provide additional constraints for mapping. Second, we design a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation with dominant directions, explicitly encoding structural priors from the AW hypothesis. Finally, we propose a Gaussian evolution strategy that adapts to scene geometry and incorporates structural cues into global optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that these innovations enable MMD-SLAM to achieve state-of-the-art performance in both tracking accuracy and mapping quality. e.g., our method achieves a 48.56% reduction in ATE RMSE on ScanNet and a 5.71% improvement in PSNR on Replica, compared with MonoGS.

</details>


### [330] [One-to-Two Acting: A Novel Framework for Single-arm Agent Action Expansion to Dual Arms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19897)
*Youbin Yao,Nieqin Cao,Mingyan Li,Yan Ding,Fuqiang Gu,Chao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ExS2D leverages single-arm supervision to enable efficient dual-arm manipulation via hierarchical task expansion and LLM-driven coordination, achieving a 54.4% reduction in steps and maintaining success rates, validated in simulation and four real tasks with few-shot data and zero bimanual demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dual-arm manipulation offers higher throughput but requires costly bimanual demonstrations; learning from single-arm supervision with structured task expansion can bridge this gap.

Method: Three-stage framework: (1) extract structured subtasks with temporal precedence from textual instructions; (2) ground subtasks to executable actions via observation-based subtask-guided mapping; (3) use a multimodal LLM-driven coordinator for precedence-aware, collision-free dual-arm allocation and synchronized planning.

Result: In simulation, average execution steps reduced by 54.4% with comparable success rate to a single-arm baseline. Real-robot experiments across four tasks show reliable dual-arm execution from few-shot single-arm data and without any bimanual demonstrations.

Conclusion: ExS2D demonstrates data-efficient pathways to dual-arm execution from single-arm supervision, offering substantial efficiency gains and practical applicability, with potential extension to more tasks and stronger safety guarantees.

Abstract: Dual-arm manipulation can improve throughput via parallel execution, but collecting bimanual demonstrations for training is costly and difficult. We present ExS2D, a hierarchical action expansion framework that enables dual-arm manipulation from single-arm supervision. ExS2D first generates structured subtasks from textual instructions while explicitly capturing temporal precedence. It then grounds each subtask into executable actions through subtask-guided action mapping in observation. Finally, precedence-aware action allocation and synchronized planning are performed by a multimodal large language model driven coordinator to select collision-free dual-arm executions. Simulation experiments demonstrate that ExS2D reduces the average execution steps by 54.4% while maintaining a comparable success rate to a single-arm baseline. Real-robot experiments on four tasks further demonstrate the reliability of ExS2D for dual-arm execution under few-shot single-arm samples, while using zero bimanual demonstrations.

</details>


### [331] [Co-policy: Responsive Human-Robot Co-Creation for Musical Performances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19914)
*Xuetao Li,Wenke Huang,Mang Ye,Zijian Liu,Jinhua Xie,Jifeng Xuan,Miao Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces Co-policy: a framework for grounded semantic intent, constrained musical variation, and visuomotor execution in human-robot musical co-creation, enabling low-latency, physically plausible robot responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between semantic musical understanding and real-time, physically executable robot actions for embodied AI music co-creation; improve alignment, timing, and adaptability.

Method: Ground semantics with pre-inference anchors and a fine-tuned Qwen-vl planner (F-Qwen) to create co-creation plans from speech, seeds, and visuals; implement a Gaussian-Mixture Visuomotor Policy (GMP) as a conditional mixture-density policy mapping target notes and visual context to robot actions in a single forward pass; evaluate on real-robot chimes with ablations and expert assessments against diffusion-policy baselines.

Result: Enhanced intent alignment, execution accuracy, and response frequency; demonstrated advantages of physically grounded action generation over ablated baselines and standard diffusion policies.

Conclusion: Physically grounded action generation is essential for effective embodied human-AI musical co-creation, with GMP enabling rapid, multimodal, and constrained robotic responses.

Abstract: Art has long stood as a pivotal expression of human creativity. Embodied artificial intelligence offers a route for generative models to participate in that creativity through physical action rather than disembodied digital content. In robotic music co-creation, it is challenging to connect semantic musical understanding with real-time and physically executable performance. We present Co-policy, a framework for human-robot musical co-creation that separates semantic intent grounding, constrained musical variation, and visuomotor execution. To ground musical semantics, Co-policy uses pre-inference semantic anchors and a fine-tuned Qwen-vl planner (F-Qwen) to transform speech, live musical seeds, and visual observations into structured co-creation plans. To support low-latency execution, Co-policy introduces a Gaussian-Mixture Visuomotor Policy (GMP), implemented as a conditional mixture-density policy that maps target notes and visual context to multimodal robot actions in a single forward pass. Unlike robotic playback systems that merely reproduce user-specified notes, Co-policy generates complementary musical responses under both musical and physical constraints. Real-robot chime experiments, ablations, and expert evaluation show improved intent alignment, execution accuracy, and response frequency over diffusion-policy and ablated baselines, supporting physically grounded action generation as a key requirement for embodied human-AI co-creation.

</details>


### [332] [Deep-Unfolded Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19920)
*Hunter Kuperman,Minchan Jung,Rahul V. Ghosh,Alex Oshin,Evangelos A. Theodorou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Deep Coordinator is a deep-unfolding framework that learns to dynamically adjust ADMM-DDP hyperparameters for distributed robotics optimization, using unsupervised learning to avoid degenerate supervised training; it delivers 6.18–9.44x speedups and scales to 8x larger systems while maintaining trajectory quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter tuning in distributed optimization for multi-agent robotics is brittle and problem-specific; adaptive, solve-time tuning can improve runtime and performance.

Method: Unroll a fixed number of ADMM-DDP iterations into a neural network; learn functions between layers that map optimizer state to next penalty parameters; use unsupervised learning to train the model; test on fleets of cars and quadrotors.

Result: Trajectories of comparable quality; 6.18-9.44x faster than conventional solvers; performance preserved when deployed to systems up to 8x larger than training.

Conclusion: First deep-unfolding framework to adapt penalty parameters of a non-convex optimizer at solve-time; demonstrates substantial runtime gains and scalability; unsupervised learning mitigates degenerate training.

Abstract: Distributed optimization is a highly scalable and structurally transparent technique to solve multi-agent robotics problems; however, such methods often suffer from the need for highly-specialized, problem-specific hyperparameter tunings. In this work, we propose Deep Coordinator, a deep-unfolding framework that learns to dynamically adjust the hyperparameters of ADMM-DDP, a popular distributed solver for robotics tasks, at solve-time in response to optimizer performance. Our architecture consists of unrolling a fixed number of ADMM-DDP iterations into a neural network with learnable functions between layers mapping the optimizer state to the next hyperparameters. To the best of our knowledge, Deep Coordinator is the first deep-unfolding framework to adapt the penalty parameters of a non-convex optimizer at solve-time; we show that the mainstream supervised approach can yield degenerate solutions when training such models, and propose an unsupervised learning scheme. On simulations with fleets of cars and quadrotors, Deep Coordinator produces trajectories of comparable quality 6.18-9.44x faster than conventional solvers. Furthermore, Deep Coordinator retains its performance benefits when deployed to systems up to 8x larger than trained on.

</details>


### [333] [SWAP: Symmetric Equivariant World-Model for Agile Robot Parkour](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19928)
*Kaixin Lan,Ze Wang,Hongyi Li,Lei Jiang,Chaojie Fu,Chengkai Su,Choi Lam Wong,Yongbin Jin,Hongtao Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SWAP introduces an equivariant symmetric world model that encodes left-right symmetry in both the world model and actor-critic networks, improving data efficiency and generalization for legged locomotion; achieves real-world parkour records and zero-shot transfer to varied terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce redundancy in latent representations caused by encoding symmetric interactions separately and to leverage geometric regularities via symmetry priors to improve learning efficiency and policy performance.

Method: End-to-end SWAP framework incorporating symmetry/equivariance into the world model and policy networks; uses group-equivalent architectures to enforce left-right symmetry and tests on a real quadruped performing parkour with generalization to mirrored terrains and diverse outdoor environments.

Result: Real-world experiments achieving 2.13 m gap jump and 1.63 m platform climb; robust geometric generalization to unseen mirrored terrains; zero-shot transfer across diverse outdoor environments.

Conclusion: Embedding symmetry equivariance is an effective structural prior that enhances data efficiency and generalization for learned legged locomotion, enabling more ambitious physical tasks.

Abstract: While latent world models enable the proactive predictions required for extreme parkour, their purely data-driven nature forces them to redundantly encode left-right symmetric interactions as independent patterns. This inflates the learning burden and hinders the capture of geometric regularities, restricting the latent space's efficiency for downstream policies. To address this, we propose SWAP, an end-to-end equivariant symmetric world model. This framework embeds symmetry directly into both the world model and the actor-critic networks. In real-world tests, the robot leaps across a 2.13 m gap and climbs a 1.63 m platform, breaking records for quadruped parkour. Furthermore, the framework exhibits robust geometric generalization to unseen mirrored terrains and exceptional zero-shot transferability across diverse outdoor environments. These results demonstrate that symmetry equivariance is an effective structural prior for pushing the physical boundaries of learned legged locomotion.

</details>


### [334] [Motor Angular Speed Preintegration for Multirotor UAV State Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19929)
*Matěj Petrlík,Filip Novák,Robert Pěnička,Martin Saska*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A UAV state estimation method (MAS-LO) uses preintegrated accelerations from motor speeds to propagate state, minimizing IMU reliance, fusing with LiDAR via a motor-based preintegration factor in a factor-graph framework; shows substantial accuracy gains over LIO-SAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IMU measurements in UAVs are degraded by propeller-induced vibrations, limiting precision; need a high-frequency, vibration-resilient alternative for accurate state estimation.

Method: Preintegrate accelerations derived from motor speeds; propagate state using these preintegrated values; introduce a motor-speed preintegration factor for graph optimization; fuse with LiDAR in MAS-LO; open-source.

Result: Compared to LIO-SAM, MAS-LO achieves 28% better position accuracy, 65% better velocity accuracy, 14% lower measurement lag, and robust performance to parameter mismatches.

Conclusion: MAS-LO provides a more precise, robust state estimator for agile UAV flights and demonstrates the viability of motor-speed-based preintegration as a replacement or supplement to IMU measurements in factor-graph-based odometry; open-source implementation.

Abstract: A precise state estimate is crucial for a tight feedback control that enables agile and near-obstacle flights of UAVs. The state-of-the-art methods fuse slow pose measurements with high-frequency inertial measurements to obtain a precise state estimate. However, the inertial measurements from the IMU onboard the UAV are degraded by vibrations from spinning propellers and the precision of the estimated state suffers. We propose a novel approach based on the preintegration of accelerations obtained from motor speeds. We show that the accelerations obtained in this manner can be used for state propagation on their own to achieve better precision without including the IMU. Further, we propose a factor composed of the preintegrated motor speeds that can be directly employed in factor graph optimization frameworks. We combine our factor with LiDAR measurements into the proposed Motor Angular Speed LiDAR Odometry (MAS-LO) algorithm for precise state estimation, which we open-source. Lastly, we evaluate the estimation precision against a state-of-the-art inertial algorithm LIO-SAM to show 28% improvement in position and 65% in velocity estimation accuracy, 14% lower measurement lag, and high robustness to wrong parameter values.

</details>


### [335] [Evaluation of Augmented Reality-based Intuitive Interface for Robot-Assisted Transesophageal Echocardiography: A User Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19971)
*Xiu Zhang*,Matteo Di Mauro*,Sofia Breschi,Angela Peloso,Emiliano Votta,Arianna Menciassi,Elena De Momi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Three interfaces for robot-assisted TEE were evaluated: 3D visualization with tip-level control best; significantly improved spatial accuracy and reduced orientation error; supports AR-based interfaces for safer, more intuitive robotic TEE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the complexity, fatigue, and radiation exposure of manual TEE by developing intuitive, spatially-awareness-enhancing interfaces for robot-assisted TEE.

Method: Robotic TEE platform with electromagnetic tracking and a virtual simulator. Three interfaces tested: 2D joint-level (2D-JI), 3D joint-level (3D-JI), and 3D tip-level (3D-TI). N=36 participants performed standardized navigation tasks to reproduce target echocardiographic views. Outcomes: position/orientation errors, completion time, NASA-TLX workload.

Result: 3D visualization substantially improved spatial accuracy (median position error down from 13 mm to 3 mm; orientation error halved vs 2D). Tip-level interaction yielded a further 50% reduction in orientation error and reduced inter-user variability compared with joint-level control. Overall, 3D-TI was the most effective and ergonomic interface.

Conclusion: AR-enhanced visualization combined with intuitive tip-level control can enhance performance and procedural safety in robotic TEE, supporting integration into future systems; further validation in clinical settings is warranted.

Abstract: TransEsophageal Echocardiography (TEE) is essential for diagnosing and guiding Structural Heart Disease (SHD) interventions. However, manual TEE manipulation demands significant operator expertise, is physically demanding, and exposes clinicians to radiation when performed alongside fluoroscopy. Robotic-assisted TEE systems have been introduced to improve probe handling and reduce operator fatigue, yet the design of intuitive and effective user interfaces remains an open challenge. This study presents and evaluates a model-enhanced, Augmented Reality (AR)-based intuitive interface for robot-assisted TEE, designed to improve spatial awareness and control intuitiveness. A robotic TEE platform integrated with electromagnetic tracking and a virtual simulator was used to compare three user interfaces differing in visualization and interaction modalities: 2D jointlevel (2D-JI), 3D joint-level (3D-JI), and 3D tip-level (3D-TI). Thirty six participants performed standardized navigation tasks to reproduce target echocardiographic views, with performance assessed via position and orientation errors, completion time, and NASA-TLX workload scores. Results show that 3D visualization significantly improved spatial accuracy, reducing median position error from 13 mm to 3 mm and halving the orientation error compared with the 2D interface. Tip-level interaction yielded a further 50% reduction in orientation error and reduced interuser variability relative to joint-level control. Overall, the 3D-TI configuration, combining immersive visualization with direct tip-level control, proved the most effective and ergonomic interface, supporting the integration of AR-based visualization and intuitive control paradigms into next-generation robotic TEE systems to enhance operator performance and procedural safety.

</details>


### [336] [Tri-Info: Generalizable, Interpretable Failure Prediction for VLA Models via Information Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19998)
*Jinghan Yang,Yunchao Zhang,Wang Yuan,Haolun Wan,Jiaming Zhang,Zhengyang Hu,Yanchao Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Tri-Info offers three information-theoretic signals to detect VLA model failures: action diversity, temporal consistency, and state-action coupling, enabling cross-domain failure detection with high accuracy and interpretable diagnostics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA systems can cause harmful, unpredictable behavior; reliable, interpretable failure detection is essential and should generalize beyond training environments.

Method: Formalize VLA control as a closed-loop information pipeline and derive Triple Information-theoretic (Tri-Info) signals that quantify diversity of actions, temporal consistency of action sequences, and coupling to state transitions.

Result: Tri-Info matches strong baselines in-domain and transfers across architectures, environments, and sim-to-real, achieving 83% accuracy on real-world tasks where prior detectors fail; provides interpretable diagnostics of failure modes.

Conclusion: Tri-Info is a simple yet powerful, generalizable detector for VLA failures with interpretable diagnostics that generalize across domains and real-world deployment.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are increasingly deployed across diverse tasks, yet they remain black boxes whose physical interactions can cause irreversible harm, making generalizable and interpretable failure detection essential. We observe that successful and failed rollouts carry systematically different information-theoretic signatures. Building on this, we formalize VLA control as a closed-loop information pipeline and derive the Triple Information-theoretic (Tri-Info) signals that capture whether actions remain diverse, temporally consistent, and coupled to state transitions. Across six VLA models and three benchmark environments, Tri-Info matches the strongest baselines in-domain. Moreover, Tri-Info transfers across architectures, environments, and the sim-to-real gap without retraining, reaching 83\% accuracy on real-world tasks where prior detectors collapse to chance. This establishes Tri-Info as a simple yet powerful method that not only detects failures with strong cross-domain generalization, but also delivers interpretable diagnostics of the underlying failure modes.

</details>


### [337] [A Neuromorphic Reinforcement Learning Framework for Efficient Pathfinding in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20031)
*Junzhe Xu,Zecui Zeng,Lusong Li,Yuetong Fang,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Ultra-low-power RMFS pathfinding by converting an ANN policy to an SNN via hard-label distillation, enabling event-driven, sparse computation with neuromorphic hardware; achieves substantial energy and latency gains while preserving policy quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RMFS pathfinding under dynamic changes and real-time constraints; conventional methods are expensive; RL shows promise but deployment on constrained hardware is challenging.

Method: Train full-precision ANN using collision-allowing strategy to densify informative trajectories; convert to SNN using hard-label knowledge distillation to mitigate output distribution mismatch; implement event-driven inference triggered by sparse events; end-to-end pipeline to neuromorphic hardware.

Result: Hardware experiments show up to 11,281x energy savings and approx 2x latency reduction vs GPU baseline; decision quality comparable to original policy.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that neuromorphic inference is practical and energy-sustainable for large-scale RMFS operations.

Abstract: Dynamic environmental changes, confined workspaces, and stringent real-time constraints make pathfinding in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) a challenging problem for conventional search- and rule-based methods, which typically suffer from high computational complexity and long decision latency. While reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful alternative, deploying learned policies with extreme energy efficiency on resource-constrained hardware remains an open challenge. We present SDQN-RMFS, an end-to-end framework that achieves high-fidelity deployment of an RL-trained policy from a full-precision artificial neural network (ANN) through to a neuromorphic chip. By computing only when triggered by sparse events, this framework unlocks ultra-low-power RMFS pathfinding. Our full-stack pipeline operates as follows: an ANN policy is first efficiently trained via a collision-allowing strategy to densify informative trajectories, and then converted into a spiking neural network (SNN) via a hard-label knowledge distillation approach. This effectively addresses the output distribution mismatch, preserving policy capability across the ANN-to-SNN pipeline while substantially reducing inference latency. Hardware experiments demonstrate up to 11,281$\times$ energy savings and a nearly two-fold reduction in latency compared to a high-performance GPU baseline, while maintaining decision quality on par with the original trained policy. These results establish physical neuromorphic inference as a practical and energy-sustainable pathway for large-scale RMFS operations.

</details>


### [338] [MirrorDuo: Reflection-Consistent Visuomotor Learning from Mirrored Demonstration Pairs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20048)
*Zheyu Zhuang,Ruiyu Wang,Giovanni Luca Marchetti,Florian T. Pokorny,Danica Kragic*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MirrorDuo uses reflection-based data augmentation for 6-DoF imitation learning, generating mirrored demonstrations to double data efficiency and enable cross-workspace transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce data collection costs and improve generalization to workspace variations in image-based behavior cloning and diffusion policies by leveraging symmetry between workspace sides.

Method: Construct mirrored counterparts by transforming images, proprioception, and 6-DoF end-effector actions; can be used as augmentation or as a structural prior for reflection-equivariant networks; exploits overlap between original and mirrored domains; effective when demonstrations are evenly distributed across both workspace sides, and capable of transferring skills to the mirrored workspace with few or zero target demonstrations.

Result: With balanced demonstrations across both sides, achieves significantly improved performance at the same data budget; when demonstrations exist on only one side, enables efficient skill transfer to the mirrored workspace with as few as zero or five demonstrations in the target arrangement.

Conclusion: MirrorDuo offers a practical, data-efficient approach to exploiting workspace symmetry in imitation learning, compatible with standard pipelines and reflection-equivariant architectures.

Abstract: Image-based behaviour cloning leverages demonstrations captured from ubiquitous RGB cameras. However, it remains constrained by the cost of collecting diverse demos, especially for generalizing across workspace variations. We propose MirrorDuo, a reflection-based formulation that operates on image, proprioception, and full 6-DoF end-effector action tuples, generating a mirrored counterpart for each original demonstration, effectively achieving "collect one, get one for free". It can be applied as a data augmentation strategy for existing learning pipelines, such as standard behaviour cloning or diffusion policy, or as a structural prior for reflection-equivariant policy networks. By leveraging the overlap between the original and mirrored domains, MirrorDuo achieves significantly improved performance under the same data budget when demonstrations are evenly distributed across both sides of the workspace. When demonstrations are confined to one side, MirrorDuo enables efficient skill transfer to the mirrored workspace with as few as zero or five demos in the target arrangement.

</details>


### [339] [VFILC: Accurate Frequency Extrapolations in Imitation Learning via Sampling Frequency ILC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20056)
*Nozomu Masuya,Toshiaki Tsuji,Sho Sakaino*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A VFILC framework extends variable-frequency imitation learning with iterative learning control to extrapolate variable-speed motions, achieving substantial frequency-error reduction and improved accuracy over VFIL, including notable gains in wiping and shaking tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NN imitation methods for variable-speed motion struggle to extrapolate beyond trained speeds and often yield unstable or unpredictable results; VFIL enables speed extrapolation but in an open-loop manner leading to frequency errors at high speeds.

Method: Combine VFIL (variable-frequency imitation learning) with iterative learning control (ILC) implementing both feedforward (VFIL) and feedback correction to adjust frequency errors; evaluate on three tasks (wiping, shaking, mixing) and compare against VFIL.

Result: The method successfully extrapolates speeds and reduces frequency errors across tasks. Feedback in particular reduces frequency errors by 81% in wiping and 50% in shaking at double the training speed, compared with VFIL alone; also shows 27% accuracy improvement over VFIL at interpolated frequency for a contact-rich mixing task affected by complex friction.

Conclusion: VFILC effectively enables reliable extrapolation and improves accuracy for variable-speed imitation tasks by leveraging a feedback-corrected ILC approach, with substantial gains in frequency error reduction in extrapolated regimes.

Abstract: Conventional neural network (NN)-based imitation learning methods for variable-speed motion either restricted their scope to interpolated speeds, or generated unpredictable motions when extrapolating beyond trained velocity ranges. Variable-frequency imitation learning (VFIL) enabled extrapolations of speeds by linking the NN model's sampling frequency to the motion frequency, whereas its open-loop configuration caused frequency errors, especially in the extrapolated high-frequency settings. This study proposes variable-frequency imitation learning with iterative learning control (VFILC) based on a combination of VFIL and iterative learning control (ILC) with both feedforward and feedback parts, the former taking advantage of VFIL and the latter adjusting the frequency errors. The experimental results showed that the proposed method successfully and accurately extrapolated motion speeds and reduced frequency errors in all three tasks, and that the feedback especially reduced the frequency errors by a remarkable 81% in the wiping task and 50% in the shaking task, both compared to simple feedforward VFIL, when extrapolating at double the average speed in the training data. The proposed method also improved accuracy by 27% compared with VFIL even at an interpolated frequency for a contact-rich mixing task affected by complex friction traits.

</details>


### [340] [Pose6DAug: Physically Plausible Multi-view Object Swapping for Robot Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20118)
*Jonghoon Lee,Seong Hyeon Park,Byungwoo Jeon,Minha Lee,Jinwoo Shin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Pose6DAug proposes a failure-driven data augmentation framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies that syntheses multi-view, physically plausible demonstrations by swapping the manipulated object in a policy’s successful episodes, anchored by a temporally coherent 6D pose trajectory. This enables targeted exposure to failure modes without new data collection and improves generalization to novel objects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA policies struggle to generalize to out-of-distribution objects with different appearance or geometry. Collecting multi-view teleoperation data for all failure cases is costly and time-consuming, hindering scalable generalization.

Method: From successful episodes, Pose6DAug swaps the manipulated object while preserving the action trajectory. To maintain multi-view consistency and physical plausibility, the method operates in 3D by anchoring a target object with an explicit mesh driven by a temporally coherent 6D pose trajectory, yielding consistent renderings across all camera views. The augmented data is used to fine-tune the VLA policy, avoiding additional data collection.

Result: Fine-tuning on the proposed augmented data yields a 16.5% relative improvement in success on novel objects compared to a state-of-the-art baseline, while maintaining in-distribution performance.

Conclusion: Multi-view and physically consistent augmentation via 3D pose-based object swapping is a practical and scalable route to improve VLA generalization to unseen objects.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies have shown strong potential for general-purpose manipulation, yet they often fail on novel, out-of-distribution objects whose appearance or geometry deviates from the training distribution. The standard remedy is to collect multi-view teleoperation data for every failure case, but this scales poorly in both cost and time. We introduce Pose6DAug, a failure-driven data augmentation framework that turns a policy's own successful episodes into targeted demonstrations for its failure modes, without any new data collection. Our key insight is that each successful episode already encodes a physically valid action trajectory together with calibrated multi-view observations. By swapping only the manipulated object while preserving this trajectory, we obtain new and physically grounded demonstrations. However, naive 2D video editing breaks multi-view consistency and physical plausibility, particularly under heavy occlusion and egocentric viewpoints. Our method instead operates directly in 3D, anchoring the target object with an explicit mesh driven by a temporally coherent 6D pose trajectory, ensuring geometrically consistent renderings across all camera views. Fine-tuning a VLA on data augmented by our method improves success rates by 16.5% relative to the state-of-the-art baseline on novel objects, while preserving in-distribution performance. These results show that multi-view and physically consistent augmentation is a practical path to scalable VLA generalization.

</details>


### [341] [Dual-Agent Framework for Cross-Model Verified Translation of Natural-Language Protocols into Robotic Laboratory Platform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20120)
*Hyeonna Choi,Jung Yup Kim,Hyuneui Lim,Seunggyu Jeon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Agent-based protocol translation converts natural-language microplate experiments into executable robotic commands using a Parser Agent, rule-based mapping, and LLM Validation Agent; evaluated on ELISA protocols and demonstrated end-to-end Bradford assay on a robotic platform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the semantic gap between natural-language biological protocols and automated laboratory robots, especially for microplate experiments requiring complex well/sample/reagent mappings and precise dispensing.

Method: Parser Agent converts NL protocols into structured representations; rule-based mapping engine deterministically enforces robotic platform constraints to generate device-level commands; heterogeneous LLM Validation Agent checks completeness, parameter accuracy, and execution order, with a self-correction loop through structured feedback; evaluation across a sweep of 7 Parsers and 3 Validators on randomly selected ELISA protocols to study effects of model scale and Validator type on translation accuracy and pass rates under cross-model verification; comparison against end-to-end LLM mapping to assess accuracy-latency trade-offs; end-to-end Bradford assay quantification on a microplate demonstrated on a robotic platform to validate real-world execution.

Result: Framework narrows the semantic gap, reveals the accuracy-latency trade-off, and provides insights into how model scale and validator type affect translation accuracy and pass rates; achieves end-to-end autonomous execution from NL protocols to physical experiments (Bradford assay).

Conclusion: A flexible, scalable approach for translating natural-language experimental protocols into executable microplate-automation commands; potential applicability to other assays; robustness enhanced by validation and self-correction loops, though trade-offs between speed and accuracy persist.

Abstract: Biological experiment protocols are written in natural language, whereas automation systems rely on predefined control commands, creating a semantic gap that limits autonomous execution. Microplate-based automatic experiments are particularly challenging due to the need to simultaneously control well mapping, sample-reagent combinations, replicate placement, and parallel dispensing. This study proposes an agent-based protocol translation framework that converts natural-language microplate-based protocols into executable control commands for a robotic laboratory platform. A Parser Agent formalizes the natural-language protocol into a structured representation, and a rule-based mapping engine deterministically incorporates the operational constraints of the robotic laboratory platform to generate device-level control commands. A heterogeneous LLM Validation Agent verifies completeness, parameter accuracy, and execution order, and triggers a self-correction loop with structured feedback when errors are detected. A sweep involving 7 Parsers and 3 Validators on randomly selected ELISA protocols evaluates how model scale and Validator type affect translation accuracy and pass rates under cross-model verification. The accuracy-latency trade-off is further verified by comparing the rule-based mapping of the proposed framework with LLM end-to-end direct mapping. Finally, Bradford assay-based protein quantification using a microplate was demonstrated on a robotic laboratory platform, validating end-to-end autonomous execution from natural-language protocols to real-world experiments. The proposed framework provides a flexible approach to narrowing the semantic gap between natural-language protocols and microplate-based self-driving laboratories.

</details>


### [342] [Frequency-Aware Flow Matching for Continuous and Consistent Robotic Action Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20135)
*Jianing Guo,Fangzheng Chen,Zihao Mao,Wong Lik Hang Kenny,Zhenhong Wu,Yu Li,Yishuai Cai,Yuanpei Chen,Yikun Ban,Kai Chen,Qi Dou,Yaodong Yang,Xianglong Liu,Huijie Zhao,Simin Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FAFM enables continuous, temporally smooth actions by transforming action sequences to the DCT frequency domain, performing flow matching on coefficients, and reconstructing actions with cosine basis; it handles heterogeneous input frequencies with no extra parameters and improves robustness and performance across benchmarks and real hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete action chunks used in prior flow-matching methods are brittle to varying control frequencies and can cause temporally inconsistent, unstable actions. A method that yields continuous actions and respects frequency variations is needed.

Method: 1) Convert discrete action sequences to DCT coefficients to enable frequency-domain flow matching. 2) Perform flow matching on DCT coefficients. 3) Reconstruct continuous actions via cosine basis expansion. 4) Impose a Sobolev-type smoothness regularization by constraining the first-order temporal derivative to suppress high-frequency changes. 5) Applicable to standalone flow-matching policies and vision-language action models; no additional network parameters.

Result: FAFM yields higher success rates, richer multimodal expressivity, smoother trajectories, faster convergence, and robustness to mechanical biases and mixed-frequency inputs across synthetic toy benchmarks, obstacle avoidance, LapGym, LIBERO; demonstrated on a real Franka robot; code available.

Conclusion: FAFM provides a simple, parameter-free enhancement to flow matching that enables continuous, temporally-consistent actions and robust performance across varied frequencies and tasks, broadening applicability of flow-based manipulation methods.

Abstract: Flow matching has emerged as a standard paradigm for robotic manipulation owing to its strong expressive power for modelling complex, multimodal action distributions, alongside similar approaches like diffusion policy. However, existing methods rely on discretized action chunks, making them brittle to demonstrations collected at heterogeneous control frequencies and prone to temporally inconsistent actions that degrade control stability. In this paper, we propose Frequency-Aware Flow Matching (FAFM), which outputs continuous, temporally consistent actions. To handle heterogeneous frequency input, we transform discrete action sequences into the frequency domain with the discrete cosine transform (DCT), perform flow matching over the resulting coefficients, and reconstruct continuous actions via cosine basis expansion. To generate temporally consistent actions, we regularize the first-order temporal derivative to promote smooth actions. This corresponds to a Sobolev-type constraint that suppresses high-frequency errors and discourages abrupt action changes. Our FAFM is simple, introduces no additional network parameters and applies to standalone flow-matching policies and vision-language action models. Across synthetic toy benchmark, obstacle avoidance, LapGym, and LIBERO, FAFM improves success rates, multimodal expressivity, motion smoothness, convergence speed, robustness to mechanical bias and mixed-frequency input. These gains are consistent when deployed on a real-world Franka robot. Code available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/FAFM.

</details>


### [343] [Robust Assembly State Reasoning from Action Recognition for Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20150)
*James Fant-Male,Roel Pieters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study compares five state-tracking methods for assembling-state inference from HAR inputs across two diverse datasets, revealing no universal best method. NN and HMM perform well under low variability, while logic-based approaches offer robustness in other cases; modeling action durations improves performance for repeated actions without extra sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HAR-based tracking of assembly state in human-robot collaboration is challenging in realistic scenarios due to variability and noise; understanding which state-tracking methods are robust across tasks guides practical deployment.

Method: Systematic evaluation of five state-tracking approaches (covering logic-based, HMM, NN, and others) using two diverse datasets. Testing with simulated inputs at varying noise levels and realistic HAR-model inputs to assess robustness and task-dependence. Investigation of the role of modeling expected action duration.

Result: Optimal method depends on the task; NN and HMM perform well in tasks with limited variability, while logic-based methods are more robust in other scenarios. Methods that model action duration are important for repeated actions when no additional sensing is available.

Conclusion: State-tracking method selection should be task-specific; incorporating duration modeling can improve performance, particularly for repeated actions with limited sensing; consider robustness across noise and variability when choosing an approach.

Abstract: Human Action Recognition (HAR) is frequently investigated in Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) research to understand what actions have been performed and hence the state of a collaborative task. Accurately tracking an assembly state from HAR is however not fully investigated, and in realistic scenarios is not a trivial task. This research systematically investigates and compares methods for tracking assembly state using action recognition inputs. Investigations using two diverse datasets and five state tracking approaches, including logic-based, Hidden Markov Model (HMM), and neural network (NN) methods, show that optimal approaches are not uniform across different tasks and that different methods fail under different circumstances. Testing is performed using both simulated inputs with varying noise levels and realistic inputs from a HAR model. Results show NN and HMM methods can perform well in tasks with limited variability, but for other scenarios logic-based approaches can be more robust. Methods which model expected action duration are also important for tasks with repeated actions where no additional sensing is provided.

</details>


### [344] [Belt-Finger: An Affordable Soft Belt-Driven Gripper for Dexterous In-Hand Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20193)
*Boya Zhang,Andreas Zell,Georg Martius*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A parallel-gripper upgrade adds three in-hand DoFs via a double-soft-belt finger module, preserving opening/closing while enabling translation, pitch, and roll; compatible with MPC and teleoperation, it improves dexterity and task feasibility over standard parallel grippers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parallel grippers are simple and reliable but lack in-hand mobility, forcing large arm movements and limiting dexterous manipulation in confined spaces.

Method: Attach a double-soft-belt finger module to a standard parallel gripper to realize three in-hand DoFs (translation, pitch, roll) while maintaining basic opening/closing; integrate with two control pipelines: (1) model predictive control for in-hand manipulation of known objects; (2) a lightweight teleoperation interface enabling simultaneous arm and gripper control (total 10 DoFs).

Result: Empirical results across challenging manipulation tasks in teleoperation, MPC, and learned policies show consistent improvements in dexterity and task feasibility compared with a conventional parallel gripper.

Conclusion: The gripper preserves the reliability and precision of parallel grippers while adding inexpensive, user-friendly in-hand motion capabilities, broadening the range of feasible manipulations in constrained environments.

Abstract: Parallel-jaw grippers are the default manipulator choice in robotics because they are simple, robust, and inexpensive. Their limited in-hand mobility, however, often forces large arm motions and restricts dexterous manipulation in confined workspaces. We present a parallel-gripper upgrade: a double-soft-belt-based finger module that preserves standard opening/closing while adding three in-hand degrees of freedom (DoF): translation, pitch, and roll. The mechanism is deliberately kept simple and engineered for inexpensive manufacturing and straightforward integration, preserving the reliability and precise control of traditional parallel grippers while greatly broadening the range of manipulation capabilities. To demonstrate the utility of the added DoFs, we integrate the gripper in two control pipelines. First, we adapt a model predictive controller for in-hand manipulation of known objects. Second, we introduce a lightweight teleoperation interface that enables simultaneous control of the robot arm and gripper (10 DoFs total) with minimal hardware. Across a suite of challenging manipulation tasks executed via teleoperation, MPC, and trained policies, the proposed gripper consistently improves dexterity and task feasibility compared to a conventional parallel gripper

</details>


### [345] [Stable Transformer-Actor-Critic Model Predictive Control: A Contraction Analysis Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20197)
*Antonio Marino,Valerio Modugno,Marco Cognetti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Formal robustness guarantees for Transformer-based Actor-Critic MPC using δISS and contraction theory; certifiably robust policy via a training regularizer; validated on a nonlinear drone with target-reaching and obstacle avoidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensure closed-loop stability and robustness for sequence-based learning components inside MPC pipelines, addressing non-convexity and safety concerns in complex dynamical systems.

Method: Prove global incremental Input-to-State Stability (δISS) for Transformer networks. Use Riemannian contraction theory to analyze the interconnected dynamics between the physical plant and the predictive neural network. Integrate the derived bounds as a training regularizer to yield a certifiably robust policy within a Transformer-Actor-Critic MPC framework.

Result: A certifiably robust policy is produced. Validation on a nonlinear 3D drone model demonstrates capability in target-reaching and obstacle-avoidance maneuvers.

Conclusion: The work provides a rigorous pathway to endow learning-based MPC with formal robustness guarantees via δISS and contraction-based analysis, potentially improving safety in autonomous robotics and other uncertain nonlinear control tasks.

Abstract: Actor-Critic Model Predictive Control (MPC) effectively addresses complex, non-convex control problems, but guaranteeing the closed-loop stability of sequence-based learning models within these pipelines remains challenging. This paper introduces a novel Transformer-Actor-Critic MPC architecture with formal robustness guarantees. First, we prove that Transformer networks can satisfy global incremental Input-to-State Stability ($δ$ISS). We then leverage Riemannian contraction theory to analyze the interconnected dynamics between the physical plant and the predictive neural network. Finally, we integrate these theoretical bounds as a training regularizer to yield a certifiably robust policy. The framework is validated on a nonlinear 3D drone model executing target-reaching and obstacle-avoidance maneuvers.

</details>


### [346] [FlowMaps: Modeling Long-Term Multimodal Object Dynamics with Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20209)
*Francesco Argenziano,Miguel Saavedra-Ruiz,Sacha Morin,Charlie Gauthier,Daniele Nardi,Liam Paull*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlowMaps is a latent flow matching model that predicts multimodal future object locations in continuous 3D space, conditioned on past human interactions, to improve dynamic object navigation. It generalizes to unseen environments and outperforms state‑of‑the‑art in 600+ episodes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots in household environments must reason about how objects move over time due to human routines. Current methods struggle to reliably link present observations to previously seen objects in changing environments; exploiting spatio-temporal regularities can enhance navigation and search.

Method: FlowMaps learns implicit spatio-temporal dependencies by modeling multimodal distributions over future object locations in continuous 3D space. It uses a latent flow matching framework, conditioning on past human interactions to predict likely location changes and generalizes to unseen environments with similar routines.

Result: Empirically, FlowMaps outperforms state-of-the-art approaches across more than 600 episodes in a dynamic Object Navigation task, in both simulated and real-world settings.

Conclusion: Modeling object dynamics with continuous, multimodal spatio-temporal distributions improves robotic search and navigation in changing household environments and supports generalization across environments sharing similar object routines; code and materials are provided by the authors.

Abstract: Joint spatial and temporal understanding of 3D scenes is a crucial requirement for robots deployed in everyday household environments. Such agents must not only comprehend and navigate spatial layouts, but also reason about how these spaces evolve over time. In particular, humans interact with objects daily, causing them to change position throughout the environment and making it difficult for robots to reliably associate current observations with previously seen objects. However, these interactions are not random: human habits and routines induce spatio-temporally consistent patterns in object locations, which robotic agents can potentially learn and then exploit for downstream tasks such as navigation. To this end, we introduce FlowMaps, a latent flow matching model for estimating multimodal distributions over the future locations of dynamic objects in a continuous 3D space. By learning the implicit dependencies among objects and their temporal evolution, FlowMaps predicts likely changes in object locations conditioned on past human interactions, while supporting generalization across previously unseen environments that share similar object routines. To demonstrate the utility of this method, we deploy FlowMaps in a downstream dynamic Object Navigation task in both simulated and real-world environments. Across more than 600 episodes, FlowMaps outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, showing that modeling object dynamics through continuous, multimodal spatio-temporal distributions improves robotic search and navigation in changing household environments. Code and additional material is available at https://fra-tsuna.github.io/flowmaps/.

</details>


### [347] [Mobile Target Search with Imperfect Perception: A Partially Observable Stochastic Game Theoretical Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20232)
*Hanzheng Zhang,Shu Liang,Shuyu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: POSG-based framework for mobile search under perception uncertainty; introduces a detectability criterion via stochastic recurrence; provides a server-assisted distributed algorithm using aggregative potential games and KL-divergence-based target prediction; simulations validate effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model and solve robust mobile search with imperfect perceptions (false alarms, misses, jamming) where adversarial evasion and bounded mobility interact.

Method: Formulate as a partially observable stochastic game (POSG) with grid-shaped area and bounded mobility. Introduce a detectability concept and sufficient criteria via stochastic recurrence analysis. Develop a server-assisted distributed algorithm exploiting aggregative potential game structure for searchers and a KL-divergence-based reduction for target prediction.

Result: Derives sufficient detectability conditions and presents a distributed algorithm whose performance is demonstrated through numerical simulations, validating the detectability analysis.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled approach to guaranteed detection under perception uncertainty and scalable distributed search, with simulations supporting efficacy and indicating directions for future work.

Abstract: This paper investigates mobile target search under imperfect perceptions caused by sensor limitations, malicious jamming, or communication noise. Searchers and targets operate in a grid-shaped area with bounded mobility, leading to a dynamic interplay between search and evasion. To capture this adversarial interaction under imperfect perceptions, we adopt the partially observable stochastic game (POSG) approach, which generalizes partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) by incorporating target intelligence. To handle false alarms and missed detections caused by perceptual uncertainties, we propose a novel detectability concept to determine whether a search strategy guarantees eventual detection, and provide sufficient detectability criteria based on stochastic recurrence analysis. We further develop a server-assisted distributed algorithm that utilizes the aggregative potential game structure for searchers and a KL-divergence-based reduction for target prediction. Numerical simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm and support the detectability analysis.

</details>


### [348] [Finetuning Vision-Language-Action Models Requires Fewer Layers Than You Think](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20246)
*Gia-Binh Nguyen,Trong-Bao Ho,Thien-Loc Ha,Khoa Vo,Philip Lund Møller,Quang T. Nguyen,Long Dinh,Tuan Dam,Vu Duong,Tung M. Luu,Trung Le,Tran Nguyen Le,Minh Vu,An Thai Le,Ngan Le,Daniel Sonntag,James Zou,Jan Peters,Duy M. H. Nguyen,Ngo Anh Vien*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Structural compression of Vision-Language-Action models via Centered Kernel Alignment removes redundant layers, achieving up to 50% depth reduction with training-time and inference speedups while preserving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce compute burden of large VLA models without expensive retraining; reveal and exploit layer-wise redundancy in continuous control policies.

Method: Training-free structural compression using a single forward pass to compute Centered Kernel Alignment and identify redundant layer features; remove twin layers to halve depth; apply to VLM backbone and policy head; then fine-tune the compressed model.

Result: 40-50% faster training; up to 30% faster real-time inference; maintains or surpasses full-scale base model performance; validated on LIBERO, RoboCasa, SimplerEnv and 10 real-world tasks across 4 robotic embodiments.

Conclusion: VLAs can be effectively compressed by reducing depth via redundancy detection, offering a compute-efficient paradigm for scalable robot learning without compromising performance.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models pre-trained on massive video-robot datasets have revolutionized robotic manipulation, yet their multi-billion parameter architectures impose prohibitive computational burdens during downstream fine-tuning and real-time inference. In this work, we reveal a highly non-trivial architectural characteristic of these continuous control foundation policies (e.g., pi_0, GR00T-N1.5): despite being trained on diverse physical trajectories, they exhibit severe layer-wise representational redundancy. To exploit this, we introduce a structural compression pipeline that is entirely training-free, bypassing the need of existing methods to load full-scale models to learn optimized token reductions or dynamic layer selectors. Instead, using only a single forward pass via Centered Kernel Alignment to identify redundant layer features, we remove twin layers to permanently compress the model depth by up to 50% across both the VLM backbone and the continuous control policy head. Downstream fine-tuning of this streamlined architecture yields a dual acceleration benefit: a 40-50% reduction in training time and up to 30% faster real-time inference, while matching or exceeding full-scale base model performance. We comprehensively validate our method across three simulation benchmarks (LIBERO, RoboCasa, SimplerEnv) and 10 diverse real-world manipulation tasks across 4 unique robotic embodiments. These results prove that advanced VLAs require significantly fewer layers than previously assumed, offering a highly compute-efficient paradigm for scalable robot learning.

</details>


### [349] [Efficiently Linking Real Scenes with Synthetic Data Generation for AI-based Cognitive Robotics and Computer Vision Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20272)
*Paul Koch,Vivek Chavan,André Sers,Adem Karakurt,Paul Hofmann,Mohamad Zaher Ziadeh,Jörg Krüger*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Survey of AI vision for cognitive robotics, highlighting current limits/trends; outlines ongoing work to bridge sim-to-real by connecting training-data generation with simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need efficient training data and AI architectures to overcome domain gaps, precision limits, and scalability for industry/household robotics.

Method: Literature synthesis of state-of-the-art limits/trends; description of ongoing work to bridge the sim-to-real gap by linking training-data generation to simulations.

Result: Provides conceptual analysis and a proposed approach; no empirical results reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: Bridging the simulation-to-reality domain gap through training-data generation is a key direction for robust AI vision in cognitive robotics.

Abstract: AI vision models are a driving factor for the potential use case scenarios of cognitive robotics within in the industry and household applications. A large array of methods from semantic environment analysis towards 6D and grasping pose estimation have been proposed based on the latest AI achievements. However, such advancements require further strong and efficient methods w.r.t. training data and AI-architectures, which are capable in synergy to tackle current challenges, precision limits, and scalability beyond domain gaps. In this paper, we discuss these current limits and trends in the related state-of-the-art which are challenging those. Further we discuss our current work in progress on bridging the domain gap between simulations and real world applications by linking those in the training data generation.

</details>


### [350] [Co-VLA: Coordination-Aware Structured Action Modeling for Dual-Arm Vision-Language-Action Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20285)
*Yandong Wang,Jiaqian Yu,Xiongfeng Peng,Lu Xu,Yamin Mao,Weiming Li,Jaewook Yoo,Dongwook Lee,Daehyun Ji,Mingbo Zhao,Chao Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Co-VLA adds explicit coordination priors to Vision-Language-Action models via a Structured Action Expert and Latent-Aware Controller for bimanual manipulation, yielding improved tight-coordination performance, robust OOD generalization, and faster task completion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end implicit coordination is insufficient for tightly coupled bimanual tasks; explicit coordination priors are needed for reliability, interpretability, and safety.

Method: Replace monolithic action head with a Structured Action Expert (SAE); introduce a coordination-aware loss shaping shared and residual latents into task-structure; a Latent-Aware Controller (LAC) modulates synchronization strength, execution asymmetry, smoothness, and safety in real time; compatible with standard control pipelines without force/impedance control; evaluated in simulation and real-world.

Result: Significant gains: 27% absolute improvement in success rate for tight coordination tasks; OOD real-world performance more than doubles (13% to 27%); task completion time reduced by up to 25%.

Conclusion: Explicit coordination priors via SAE and real-time LAC enhance reliability, interpretability, and performance of VLA-based bimanual manipulation; the approach generalizes across simulation and real-world settings and maintains compatibility with standard control stacks.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models show strong capabilities in single and dual-arm robotic manipulation. Prior works show coordinated bimanual behaviors can emerge from end-to-end learning, leveraging large vision-language backbones with continuous action prediction. However, as bimanual tasks become tightly coupled and execution constraints become critical, implicit coordination alone is insufficient to ensure reliable, interpretable, and stable behavior.
  In this work, we propose Co-VLA, a coordination-aware bimanual manipulation framework introducing explicit structural priors into VLA models. We instantiate our method on a state-of-the-art vision-language backbone by replacing its monolithic action head with a Structured Action Expert (SAE) designed for bimanual coordination. Specifically, we introduce explicit structure at the action generation level with a modular coordination-aware loss that shapes shared and residual latents according to task-specific structures. The shared latent encodes task-level coordination intent, while residual latents capture execution adjustments for each arm.
  At deployment, a Latent-Aware Controller (LAC) interprets the learned representations to modulate synchronization strength, execution asymmetry, smoothness, and safety constraints in real time. LAC operates at the joint-command level and remains compatible with standard control pipelines without requiring force or impedance control. Experiments across simulation and real-world benchmarks show Co-VLA significantly outperforms monolithic baselines, achieving a 27% success rate gain in tight-coordination tasks, more than doubling performance in OOD real-world scenarios (from 13% to 27%), and reducing task completion time by up to 25%.

</details>


### [351] [Towards 3D karst underwater scene reconstruction from rotating sonar data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20322)
*Georgios Evangelos Margaritis,Lionel Lapierre,Simon Rohou,Zhi Yan,Andreas Nüchter,François Goulette*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A pipeline combining continuous-time SLAM and a two-stage deep learning surface reconstruction method to reconstruct underwater karst conduits from sonar data, addressing drift and sparsity to produce an immersive 3D mesh for hydrogeological analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Karst aquifers are critical freshwater resources but are hard to map due to complex subsurface geometry, sparse/noisy sonar data, and navigation drift, hindering accurate 3D reconstructions.

Method: Integrates continuous-time SLAM to correct trajectory drift from sonar profiler data with a two-stage deep learning approach for surface reconstruction, yielding an immersive, navigable 3D mesh.

Result: Produces an immersive and navigable 3D mesh suitable for hydrogeological analysis, enabling better visualization and interpretation of underwater karst conduits.

Conclusion: The combined SLAM + DL pipeline addresses data sparsity and drift in underwater karst mapping, improving accuracy and usability of 3D reconstructions for hydrogeological study.

Abstract: Karst aquifers provide critical freshwater resources but pose significant hazards due to their complex and poorly understood subsurface geometry. Mapping these environments is challenging because sonar data from underwater exploration is sparse and noisy, while navigation estimates suffer from drift limiting standard 3D reconstruction methods. We present a pipeline for reconstructing underwater karst conduits from a sonar profiler. We combine a continuous-time SLAM approach to correct trajectory drift with a novel two-stage deep learning method for surface reconstruction, producing an immersive and navigable 3D mesh for hydrogeological analysis.

</details>


### [352] [Autonomous Driving with Priority-Ordered STL Specifications Under Multimodal Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20336)
*Taha Bouzid,Shuhao Qi,Mircea Lazar,Sofie Haesaert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Uncertainty-aware trajectory planning with a lexicographic ordering of STL specifications under uncertainty, implemented via MPPI control, demonstrated to handle conflicting objectives efficiently in simulation with multi-modal uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In safety-critical autonomous driving, multiple requirements (safety, comfort, traffic-rule compliance) must be prioritized because not all can be satisfied simultaneously, and prediction uncertainty about other agents increases the difficulty of satisfying them all. A principled, uncertainty-robust prioritization is needed.

Method: Introduce a predefined lexicographic ordering over Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications that remains valid under uncertainty. Integrate this ordering with Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control to perform planning under multi-objective tradeoffs and uncertainty.

Result: Simulation studies show the framework efficiently manages conflicting objectives under realistic multi-modal uncertainty, enabling prioritized, uncertainty-aware trajectory planning.

Conclusion: The proposed lexicographic, uncertainty-aware STL-based prioritization framework embedded in MPPI provides a principled approach to safe and comfortable trajectory planning under uncertainty, with potential for real-time application and extension to various STL specs.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles must plan trajectories that satisfy a multitude of requirements on safety, passenger comfort, and compliance with traffic rules. However, in safety-critical scenarios, it is not always possible to satisfy all requirements simultaneously, necessitating their prioritization based on importance. At the same time, in these safety-critical scenarios, the uncertainty in trajectory predictions of the surrounding traffic, such as other vehicles and pedestrians, should be explicitly accounted for. In this work, we propose an uncertainty-aware trajectory planning framework that incorporates a predefined lexicographic ordering over Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications that stays valid under uncertainty. We implement this formulation with Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control and we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on simulation scenarios, showing that our framework efficiently handles conflicting objectives under realistic multi-modal uncertainty.

</details>


### [353] [An Infrastructure-less, Control-Independent Solution to Relative Localisation of a Team of Mobile Robots using Ranging Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20365)
*Paolo Golinelli,Tommaso Faraci,Daniele Fontanelli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Decentralised, anchor-less cooperative localisation for robot teams using a multi-hypothesis Bayesian framework, relying on odometry, sparse ranging, and local communication; robust to temporary unobservability and enabling group-informed estimates without fixed infrastructure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust team localization in unstructured environments without fixed infrastructure, rapid deployment, and minimal system requirements.

Method: A multi-hypothesis Bayesian filter that maintains the full set of feasible location estimates. The approach is fully decentralised, uses only local odometry, sparse inter-agent ranging, and short-range communication, and does not require controlling robot motion to ensure observability. Information sharing allows agents to benefit from the entire group's estimates even with partial connectivity.

Result: Claims robustness under transient unobservable conditions and that agents benefit from the group's estimates via information sharing, even when the network is only partially connected.

Conclusion: Anchor-less, fully decentralised cooperative localisation that operates with minimal sensing/communication, offering robustness and fast deployment for robot teams without fixed infrastructure.

Abstract: The ability to localise teams of robots is essential for applications ranging from robotic fleets in unstructured environments to cooperative control and navigation tasks. In such contexts, fixed infrastructure is often unavailable, deployments must be fast and flexible, and system requirements must be minimal. We present a decentralised cooperative localisation algorithm that addresses all these challenges at once. The method is anchor-less, fully decentralised, and, unlike most existing approaches, does not require controlling the robots motion to ensure team observability. It relies only on local odometry, sparse inter-agent ranging measurements, and short-range communication, all of which are widely available in practice. The algorithm adopts a multi-hypothesis Bayesian framework that maintains the entire set of feasible solutions, ensuring robustness under transient unobservable conditions. Moreover, through information sharing, each agent benefits from the estimates of the entire group, even in partially connected conditions.

</details>


### [354] [CoLI: A Reproducible Platform for Continuum Robot Learning via Monolithic 3D Printing and Isomorphic Teleoperation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20389)
*Ziyuan Tang,Chenxi Xiao**

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Open-source continuum robot with monolithic, multi-material 3D-printed compliant arm, an isomorphic teleoperation interface for direct actuator mapping, and support for imitation-learning autonomy to enable reproducible benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reproducibility and usability barriers in continuum robotics due to complex fabrication, challenging kinematic modeling, and opaque control interfaces.

Method: Hardware: monolithic compliant arm fabricated via multi-material 3D printing that simplifies assembly. Control: isomorphic teleoperation with direct actuator-level mapping eliminating explicit kinematic modeling and providing a singularity-free mapping. Autonomy extension: imitation-learning-based control. Evaluation: hardware characterization and manipulation tasks.

Result: Demonstrates a reproducible, learning-ready continuum robot platform; hardware characterization and task experiments show viability, enabling accelerated algorithm development and systematic benchmarking.

Conclusion: The design provides a readily reproducible, learning-ready continuum robot platform that lowers barriers to adoption and benchmarking in the continuum robotics community.

Abstract: Continuum robots offer strong potential for manipulation tasks due to their high degrees of freedom, compliant structures, and operational safety. However, their adoption in both research and practical applications has been hindered by reproducibility issues arising from complex fabrication and assembly processes, challenging kinematic modeling, and a lack of intuitive control interfaces. To address these challenges, we present a novel open-source continuum robot design. The platform features a simplified fabrication pipeline enabled by multi-material 3D printing, allowing the arm to be fabricated as a monolithic compliant structure with minimal assembly. Control is achieved through an isomorphic teleoperation interface that establishes a direct actuator-level mapping, eliminating the need for explicit kinematic modeling and providing a singularity-free mapping. Building on this hardware design, the platform further supports imitation-learning-based autonomous control. The proposed system is evaluated through hardware characterization and a set of manipulation tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the platform provides a reproducible, learning-ready continuum robot system, accelerating algorithmic development and systematic benchmarking for the continuum robotics community.

</details>


### [355] [Agentic AutoResearch forSpace Autonomy: An Auditable, LLM-Driven Research Agent for Aerospace Control Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20394)
*Amit Jain,Richard Linares*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An LLM-driven AutoResearch loop autonomously refines aerospace control policies with a credibility layer that certifies results against seed noise, deployed policies onboard, and demonstrated on Clohessy-Wiltshire rendezvous and keep-out docking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve reliability and reproducibility of learned spacecraft control policies by automating architecture/hyperparameter exploration while guarding against seed-noise artifacts.

Method: An LLM acts as offline research agent that reads problem description and run history, suggests a single edit to the training script, executes it, and logs outcomes. A credibility layer verifies results via per-problem seed-noise measurement, reseeded verification of the best configuration, and leave-one-out pruning. The loop is applied to two aerospace control problems and the trained policy is deployed onboard while the model does not operate the vehicle.

Result: In both problems, the audited policy clears seed noise by many standard deviations; an undirected search fails to achieve similar improvements. For the docking problem, undirected search yields no feasible policy, while the learned policy avoids the keep-out zone on every seed.

Conclusion: AutoResearch provides a robust, audit-enabled autonomous research loop for developing aerospace control policies, yielding more reliable improvements than unguided searches and enabling deployment of validated policies onboard; the credibility checks help prevent spurious improvements due to seed noise.

Abstract: Spacecraft guidance, navigation, and control functions are increasingly realized as learned policies distilled from expert solvers. Developing such a policy is itself a research process: an investigator selects an architecture and hyperparameters, runs experiments, and must determine whether an apparent improvement is genuine or merely seed noise. This paper presents AutoResearch, a framework in which a large language model autonomously drives that loop for aerospace control problems, coupled with a credibility layer, built into the loop, that certifies each reported result against the problem's own measured seed noise. The language model serves only as the offline research agent that develops the control policy; the trained policy it produces is then deployed onboard the spacecraft, while the model itself never operates the vehicle. At each iteration the agent reads a plain-language problem description and the run history, proposes a single edit to the training script, executes it, and logs the outcome. No reported result is credited until it passes the same three checks: measured per-problem seed noise, reseeded verification of the best configuration, and leave-one-out pruning of the agent's edits. The same loop is applied, unchanged, to two aerospace control problems: a Clohessy-Wiltshire relative rendezvous and a safety-constrained collision-avoidance docking past a keep-out zone, each calibrated against a known optimal control benchmark. In both, the audited policy clears the measured seed noise by many standard deviations; an undirected search over the same parameters does not. On the docking problem the gap becomes categorical: undirected search yields no feasible policy, while the learned policy stays outside the keep-out zone on every seed.

</details>


### [356] [LIT-GS: LiDAR-Inertial-Thermal Gaussian Splatting for Illumination-Robust Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20424)
*Shikuan Shi,Chunran Zheng,Jiaming Xu,Tianyong Ye,Tao Yu,Yukang Cui*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces LIT-GS, a LiDAR-inertial-thermal Gaussian Splatting framework that uses LiDAR-derived planes as explicit constraints to improve pose/structure refinement and Gaussian optimization, boosting robustness in illumination-changing and texture-deficient scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB photometric cues are unreliable in thermal or low-texture environments; existing LIV Gaussian mapping struggles under illumination changes; integrating LiDAR geometry and plane priors can stabilize geometry and rendering.

Method: Leverages LIV visual map points as confidence-aware cross-modal anchors to establish thermal-LiDAR associations; adds weighted LiDAR point-to-plane residuals into bundle adjustment for joint pose/3D point refinement under weak thermal supervision; introduces a LiDAR-plane-regularized differentiable splatting objective to align rendered points with locally observed planes, mitigating surface thickening and drift.

Result: Empirical results on both proprietary sequences and public datasets show consistent improvements in geometric accuracy and rendering quality over state-of-the-art LIV-based Gaussian Splatting baselines, with particular gains in challenging lighting conditions.

Conclusion: By fusing LiDAR-derived plane geometry into pose/structure optimization and the Gaussian splatting objective, LIT-GS enhances robustness to lighting and texture deficiencies, reducing drift and surface thickening in thermal imagery while maintaining real-time rendering capability.

Abstract: Gaussian Splatting has enabled real-time neural rendering, yet existing LiDAR-inertial-visual (LIV) Gaussian mapping pipelines remain fragile under illumination changes and texture-deficient scenes due to their reliance on RGB photometric cues.
